<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Wheel Load Distribution on a Rail

**Problem Setup:** A wheel load $W$ is applied on a rail, which rests on a series of equally-spaced sleepers (spacing $u$). The rail is assumed to behave as a continuous beam supported on discrete elastic supports.

**Step 1:** Define the effective rail length over which the wheel load is distributed.

$$a = u + v$$

Where:
- $u$ = centre-to-centre sleeper spacing (typically 600–760 mm on Indian BG track).
- $v$ = a constant representing the depth over which the load spreads into the ballast — typically:
$$v = h_b + h_s + 2h_r$$
- $h_b$ = ballast depth below sleeper (~250 mm)
- $h_s$ = sleeper thickness (~200 mm)
- $h_r$ = rail height (~172 mm for 60 kg/m)

**Step 2:** Calculate the effective equivalent wheel load transferred to a single rail seat (one sleeper):

The wheel load $W$ is distributed over $(u+v)/u$ sleepers effectively. Hence the load carried by each sleeper is:

$$P = \frac{W \cdot u}{u + v}$$

**Step 3:** The maximum bending moment at the centre of the effective rail length is:

$$M = \frac{P \cdot u}{2}$$

**Step 4 (Simplified):** Substituting $P$:

$$M = \frac{W \cdot u^2}{2(u+v)}$$

**Step 5:** Maximum bending stress in the rail:

$$\sigma = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{W \cdot u^2}{2(u+v) \cdot Z}$$

Where $Z$ is the section modulus of the rail (about the horizontal neutral axis).

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This formula shows why wider sleeper spacing $u$ dramatically increases rail stress — the bending moment grows as the **square** of spacing. This is why heavy haul tracks use closely-spaced sleepers.

## 3.2 Derivation of Rail Deflection under a Point Wheel Load

**Step 1:** Consider the effective rail length as a simply-supported beam of span $a = u+v$, with a central concentrated load $W$.

**Step 2:** The maximum deflection at mid-span for a simply-supported beam under central point load is:

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{W \cdot a^3}{48 \cdot E \cdot I}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting $a = u+v$:

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{W (u+v)^3}{48 \cdot E \cdot I}$$

**Step 4:** Numerical sanity check (typical values):
- $W = 10 \text{ tonnes} = 100 \text{ kN}$
- $u = 0.65 \text{ m}$, $v = 0.79 \text{ m}$ → $a = 1.44 \text{ m}$
- $E = 2.1 \times 10^8 \text{ kN/m}^2$
- $I \approx 2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^4$ (for 60 kg/m rail)

Plugging in:

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{100 \times (1.44)^3}{48 \times 2.1 \times 10^8 \times 2 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{100 \times 2.985}{2.016 \times 10^5}$$

$$\delta_{max} \approx 1.48 \text{ mm}$$

This is within the KTU-specified permissible limit of **4 mm**, confirming design adequacy.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Rail Section Types

| Property | Double-Headed | Bull-Headed | Flat-Footed |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Foot design | Identical to head | Narrow foot | Wide flat foot |
| Head design | Symmetrical | Larger than foot | Standard mushroom |
| Fastening | Chairs required | Chairs required | Direct spike/clip |
| Self-supporting | No | No | **Yes** |
| Tendency to overturn | High | High | **Low** (wide base) |
| Lateral stability | Poor | Poor | **Excellent** |
| Modern adoption | None | UK only | **Universal** |
| Cost per km | High | High | **Lowest** |
| Typical use | Historic (UK 1830s) | UK heritage | All modern IR tracks |
| Inventor | Early UK engineers | UK (1830s) | Charles Vignoles (1836) |

## 3.4 Detailed Classification of Rail Defects (KTU Board Favourite)

Rail defects are classified into **two master categories**:

### **A. Manufacturing Defects** (occur during rolling at the steel mill)

1. **Piping** — Internal shrinkage cavity or hollow formed during cooling of the ingot.
   - *Cause:* Improper deoxidation, incorrect pouring temperature.
   - *Detection:* Visible at rail ends as a dark spot when rail end is chisel-cut.
   - *Remedy:* Rail end cropping; ultrasonic testing.

2. **Seams / Laps / Scabs**
   - *Cause:* Surface metal folded over during rolling, or scale pressed into the surface.
   - *Appearance:* Longitudinal surface cracks or fins.
   - *Remedy:* Cut out the defective section.

3. **Flakes** — Internal hairline cracks.
   - *Cause:* Hydrogen entrapment during solidification.
   - *Remedy:* Hydrogen-controlled ladle treatment.

### **B. In-Service Defects** (develop during operational life)

1. **Wear of the Rail Head**
   - *Types:* Side wear (on curves), top wear (on tangents), rail end batter (at joints).
   - *Cause:* Wheel-rail friction, flange contact on curves, impact at joints.
   - *Remedy:* Rail grinding, lubrication (gauge-face lubricators on curves), anti-wear alloys.

2. **Hogging** — Permanent vertical bending of rail ends at joints due to loose fastenings and excessive impact loads.

3. **Crushing** — Squashing of rail head under excessive wheel load, especially on poorly maintained sleepers.

4. **Shelling** — Horizontal crack just below the running surface, leading to metal flaking off the head.
   - *Cause:* Rolling contact fatigue (RCF); subsurface stress concentration.
   - *Remedy:* Rail grinding to remove the shell, head-hardened rails for high-traffic zones.

5. **Squats** — Small surface depressions with associated transverse cracks, usually on tangent tracks.
   - *Cause:* RCF from heavy axle loads.
   - *Remedy:* Corrective grinding.

6. **Corrugation** — Wave-like undulations (wavelength 30–80 mm) on the running surface.
   - *Cause:* Resonance between wheel and track, sticky slip during traction/braking.
   - *Remedy:* Rail grinding; friction modifiers.

7. **Transverse Fissure** — A horizontal crack running across the rail head, the **most dangerous defect** — can lead to sudden failure.
   - *Cause:* Hydrogen-induced cracking (HIC) or stress concentration at inclusions.
   - *Remedy:* Ultrasonic detection (USFD — Ultrasonic Flaw Detection) and immediate replacement.

8. **Bolt Hole Cracking** — Cracks initiating from fishbolt holes due to high stress concentration.
   - *Remedy:* Drilling relief holes; avoiding unnecessary bolt-hole drilling.

9. **Wheel Burn** — Localised martensite formation (very hard, brittle spot) caused by wheel spin (locked axle).
   - *Remedy:* Cut out the burnt section.

10. **Broken Rails** — Total fracture of the rail.
    - *Cause:* Combination of severe defect + cold weather + heavy load.
    - *Consequence:* **Derailment risk** — track circuit detects broken rail and signals automatic train protection.
    - *Remedy:* Emergency rail replacement, weld insertion.

## 3.5 Rail Failure Statistics (Context for KTU Answers)

- Approximately **35–40%** of all rail failures are caused by **transverse fissures and detail fractures**.
- **Wear** accounts for about **20–25%** of rail replacements.
- On Indian Railways, the current average rail life is **15–20 years** for 60 kg/m rails on mainline BG routes.
- Heavy haul corridors (e.g., Dedicated Freight Corridors) demand **higher axle load capacity (32.5 t vs. 25 t standard)** — pushing adoption of head-hardened 60E1 and 68 kg/m rails.

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**List any six in-service defects that develop in rails during their operational life.**

**Model Answer:**

The six major in-service defects are:
1. **Wear of rail head** — side wear (curves), top wear (tangents), rail end batter (joints).
2. **Shelling** — Horizontal subsurface crack leading to flaking of the rail head surface.
3. **Squats** — Surface depressions with transverse cracks on tangent tracks.
4. **Hogging** — Permanent downward bending of rail ends at joints.
5. **Transverse Fissure** — Horizontal crack across the head — most dangerous defect.
6. **Corrugation** — Periodic wave-like undulations on the running surface.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a)** Explain the different types of rail sections used in railway engineering, with neat sketches. Discuss the merits and demerits of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The three principal types of rail sections are:

**1. Double-Headed Rail (DH Rail):**
- The earliest rail design (1830s UK).
- Head and foot are identical in shape — the rail can be reversed when one head wears out.
- Mounted in cast-iron chairs fixed to sleepers.
- *Merits:* Theoretical economy through reversal; symmetric section.
- *Demerits:* The underside (now under tensile stress) was never designed for it → cracks; unstable in chairs; heavy chairs required; obsolete today.

**2. Bull-Headed Rail (BH Rail):**
- Head is bigger and wider than foot — resembling a bull's head.
- Both sides of the head slope outwards for better chair bearing.
- Held in cast-iron chairs secured to sleepers by spikes/fang bolts.
- *Merits:* Strong head with extra metal for wear; good fishing angle.
- *Demerits:* Heaviest section; needs chairs (extra cost); poor lateral stability; largely obsolete outside UK.

**3. Flat-Footed Rail (FF Rail) — Vignoles Rail:**
- Introduced by Charles Vignoles in 1836.
- Has a wide flat foot for direct fastening to sleepers (no chair).
- *Merits:* Self-supporting, economical, easy to lay and maintain, excellent lateral stability, lightest of the three for given strength, universal standard.
- *Demerits:* Foot prone to corrosion (water collects in ballast); requires careful sleeper packing.

> [!NOTE]
> *(Students should draw neat cross-sectional sketches of all three rail types with labels for head, web, foot, and fishing angles for full marks. A descriptive table is equally acceptable.)*

**(b)** What are the functions and requirements of an ideal rail section? Explain the standard proportions of a flat-footed rail. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Functions of a Rail:**
1. To provide a smooth, continuous running surface for train wheels.
2. To transmit vertical wheel loads to the sleepers and ballast via bending action.
3. To guide the train along the track (lateral restraint to wheel flanges).
4. To act as an electrical conductor for track-circuit signalling.
5. To distribute concentrated point loads over a length of track.

**Requirements of an Ideal Rail Section:**
1. **Adequate Strength** — sufficient section modulus to resist bending stresses.
2. **Adequate Stiffness** — high moment of inertia to limit deflection (≤ 4 mm).
3. **Wear Resistance** — hard head (head-hardened steel) to resist abrasion.
4. **Lateral Stability** — wide foot to resist overturning by lateral forces.
5. **Economy** — minimum weight per metre consistent with strength.
6. **Manufacturability** — simple symmetric profile easy to roll.
7. **Fishing Compatibility** — standard 1 in 4 fishing angles at both ends.

**Standard Proportions of a Flat-Footed Rail:**

For an ideal flat-footed rail section, the cross-sectional area is distributed approximately as:
- **Head: 25–30%** of total area — must withstand direct wheel contact and wear.
- **Web: 20–25%** — transfers shear; should be thin but adequate.
- **Foot: 45–50%** — provides lateral stability and base of support.

These proportions maximise the **section modulus** for a given weight, ensuring maximum bending economy.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a)** What are the various defects in rails? Classify them and explain any five in-service defects in detail with causes and remedial measures. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Classification of Rail Defects:**

**A. Manufacturing Defects** (formed during rolling at the mill):
1. Piping — internal shrinkage cavity.
2. Seams, laps, scabs — surface flaws from rolling.
3. Flakes — hydrogen-induced internal cracks.

**B. In-Service Defects** (develop during operational life):
1. Wear of rail head.
2. Hogging.
3. Crushing.
4. Shelling.
5. Squats.
6. Corrugation.
7. Transverse Fissure.
8. Bolt-hole cracking.
9. Wheel burn.
10. Broken rails.

**Detailed Explanation of Five In-Service Defects:**

**1. Wear of Rail Head:**
- *Cause:* Abrasive action of wheel treads; flange contact on sharp curves; impact at joints.
- *Types:* Side wear (curves), top wear (tangents), end batter (joints).
- *Remedy:* Rail grinding, lubrication (gauge-face lubricators), proper superelevation on curves, welded long rails to eliminate joints.

**2. Hogging:**
- *Cause:* Loose fastenings, poor sleeper packing, joint impact → permanent downward bending of rail ends at joints.
- *Remedy:* Re-packing sleepers, tightening fastenings, weld replacement of joints (LWR/CWR).

**3. Shelling:**
- *Cause:* Rolling contact fatigue (RCF) → horizontal crack develops just below the running surface → flakes of metal detach from the head.
- *Remedy:* Corrective grinding; use of head-hardened rails in high-traffic sections.

**4. Corrugation:**
- *Cause:* Resonance between wheel and track; stick-slip phenomenon under traction/braking.
- *Appearance:* Wave-like undulations of wavelength 30–80 mm.
- *Remedy:* Rail grinding, application of friction modifiers.

**5. Transverse Fissure:**
- *Cause:* Horizontal internal crack across the rail head, often originating from hydrogen-induced cracking or stress concentration at inclusions.
- *Severity:* **Most dangerous defect** — can cause sudden rail failure under heavy load.
- *Remedy:* **Ultrasonic Flaw Detection (USFD)** patrols; immediate rail replacement upon detection; modern continuously cast steel with low hydrogen content.

**(b)** A 60 kg/m rail is laid on sleepers spaced at 650 mm centre-to-centre. The depth of ballast is 250 mm, sleeper thickness is 200 mm, and rail height is 172 mm. The wheel load is 12 tonnes. Calculate:
   (i) The effective length of rail.
   (ii) The wheel load transferred to one sleeper.
   (iii) The maximum bending moment.
   (iv) The maximum bending stress if the section modulus is 4 × 10⁵ mm³. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Rail section = 60 kg/m
- Sleeper spacing $u = 650 \text{ mm} = 0.65 \text{ m}$
- Ballast depth $h_b = 250 \text{ mm} = 0.25 \text{ m}$
- Sleeper thickness $h_s = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.20 \text{ m}$
- Rail height $h_r = 172 \text{ mm} = 0.172 \text{ m}$
- Wheel load $W = 12 \text{ tonnes} = 12 \times 10^3 \text{ kg}$
- Section modulus $Z = 4 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3$

**Step 1: Effective Rail Length**

$$a = u + v$$

Where:
$$v = h_b + h_s + 2h_r$$
$$v = 0.25 + 0.20 + 2(0.172)$$
$$v = 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.344$$
$$v = 0.794 \text{ m}$$

Therefore:
$$a = 0.65 + 0.794 = 1.444 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Wheel Load per Sleeper**

$$P = \frac{W \cdot u}{u + v}$$
$$P = \frac{12 \times 0.65}{1.444}$$
$$P = \frac{7.80}{1.444}$$
$$P \approx 5.40 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 3: Maximum Bending Moment**

$$M = \frac{P \cdot u}{2}$$
$$M = \frac{5.40 \times 0.65}{2}$$
$$M = \frac{3.51}{2}$$
$$M = 1.755 \text{ t·m} = 1755 \text{ kg·m}$$

Converting to N·mm (1 kg·m = 9.81 × 10³ N·mm):
$$M = 1755 \times 9.81 \times 10^3 \text{ N·mm} = 17.217 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 4: Maximum Bending Stress**

$$\sigma = \frac{M}{Z}$$
$$\sigma = \frac{17.217 \times 10^6}{4 \times 10^5}$$
$$\sigma = 43.04 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 43 \text{ MPa}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall / Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — students often leave the answer in mixed units (e.g., tonnes and mm together) and lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Using wrong sleeper spacing** — ensure you use centre-to-centre spacing, not the gap between sleepers.
> 3. **Computing $v$ incorrectly** — the formula is $v = h_b + h_s + 2h_r$, not $h_b + h_s + h_r$. The factor of 2 accounts for one rail height above and one below the neutral axis effectively.
> 4. **Skipping the unit conversion** — bending moment in t·m must be converted to N·mm or kN·m before dividing by $Z$ in mm³.
> 5. **Not stating final units** in the stress answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1: Rail Sections & Defects**

**Rail Sections:**
- ✅ Rail section classified by weight per unit length (kg/m or lbs/yard).
- ✅ **Standard Indian sections:** 52 kg/m, 60 kg/m (modern), 75 R, 90 R (older).
- ✅ **Three types:** Double-headed (obsolete), Bull-headed (UK only), Flat-footed (universal).
- ✅ **Flat-footed rail** invented by **Charles Vignoles (1836)**.
- ✅ **Standard proportions:** Head 25–30%, Web 20–25%, Foot 45–50% of cross-section.
- ✅ **Fishing angle** = 1 in 4 (≈ 14°).
- ✅ **Functions:** load bearing, guidance, load distribution, electrical continuity.
- ✅ **Permissible deflection** of rail under load: ≤ 4 mm.

**Rail Defects:**
- ✅ **Manufacturing defects:** Piping, Seams/Laps, Flakes.
- ✅ **In-service defects:** Wear, Hogging, Crushing, Shelling, Squats, Corrugation, Transverse Fissure, Bolt-hole cracking, Wheel burn, Broken rails.
- ✅ **Most dangerous defect:** Transverse Fissure (detected by USFD).
- ✅ **Piping** is detected by **chisel-cutting rail ends** (dark spot visible).
- ✅ **Corrugation** wavelength = 30–80 mm.
- ✅ **Rolling Contact Fatigue (RCF)** causes shelling and squats.
- ✅ **Head-hardened rails** used on high-traffic corridors for wear resistance.
- ✅ Indian Railways' heavy haul corridors use **60E1 (60 kg/m) and 68 kg/m** rails.
- ✅ **Effective rail length** $a = u + v$ where $v = h_b + h_s + 2h_r$.
- ✅ **Wheel load per sleeper:** $P = W \cdot u / (u+v)$.
- ✅ **Max bending moment:** $M = P \cdot u / 2$.
- ✅ **Bending stress:** $\sigma = M/Z$ where $Z$ = section modulus.
- ✅ Typical section modulus for 60 kg/m rail ≈ $4 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3$.
- ✅ Typical moment of inertia for 60 kg/m rail ≈ $2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^4$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for Rail Defects:** "**P**roper **S**teel **F**lakes **W**ill **H**elp **C**ars **S**afely **S**kip **T**racks **B**efore **W**heel **B**reaks" → **P**iping, **S**eams, **F**lakes, **W**ear, **H**ogging, **C**rushing, **S**helling, **S**quats, **T**ransverse fissure, **B**olt-hole crack, **W**heel burn, **B**roken rail.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creep of Rails
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET754 - RAILWAY, PORT AND HARBOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Creep of Rails

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Creep of Rails** is defined as the *gradual longitudinal displacement of rails in the direction of movement of the dominant traffic*, occurring continuously over time under the repetitive action of moving wheel loads.

In railway engineering terminology, creep refers specifically to the **slow, cumulative, longitudinal shift** of the rail track along the track alignment. Although it appears microscopic on a single pass of a train, it accumulates to dangerous magnitudes (often **100 mm to 300 mm** over several weeks) if left unchecked.

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range |
|---|---|
| Typical Creep Magnitude | **50 mm to 150 mm** per month (severe cases up to **300 mm**) |
| Direction | Dominant traffic direction (usually) |
| Affected Zone | Plain track, especially on curves & approaches to stations |
| Rate of Creep | **0.5 mm to 1.5 mm** per wheel pass on poorly maintained tracks |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Push-Pull Rope" Model

Imagine a **long rope lying on a wooden floor** while a group of children walks over it continuously in one direction. Every step pushes the rope a fraction of an inch forward. After a hundred children, the rope has slid noticeably. Now, glue the rope at intervals (with **anti-creep anchors**) and watch — the slipping stops because each "anchored point" holds the rope back.

This is exactly what happens to rails:
- **The rope** = the running rail
- **The children's footsteps** = wheel flanges and wave action of the moving train
- **The glue points** = sleepers + ballast resistance + anti-creep anchors

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway**: Creep is NOT sudden movement. It is the *time-integrated micro-slip* produced by every single wheel passage — like sand being eroded grain-by-grain by wind until the dune shifts.

## 1.3 Why Creep Matters in KTU Examinations

Creep is a **high-frequency 3-mark and 7-mark topic** because:
1. It bridges **theory (forces on track)** and **maintenance practice**.
2. It links directly to **buckling, joint gaps, and derailment safety**.
3. It tests the student's understanding of *cause-effect-prevention* linkages.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four-Factor Mechanism of Creep

Creep arises from a *combination* of four physical mechanisms. Examiners love asking students to enumerate these.

### A) Wave Action Theory (Primary Cause)
When a train moves over the rail, the rail deflects into a **wave-like shape** ahead and behind the wheel. As the wheel rolls forward, the wave that was *behind* the wheel straightens out, but in doing so, **drags the rail forward** with it.

$$\text{Per-wheel micro-displacement} \approx \delta_w$$

$$\text{Cumulative Creep} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \delta_{w,i} \quad \text{(over N wheels)}$$

### B) Starting and Braking Forces
At stations and signal stops, locomotives:
- **Start**: Apply tractive force → push rails backward (relative to motion).
- **Brake**: Apply braking force → drag rails forward.
- **Net effect**: A residual forward creep in the dominant traffic direction.

### C) Thermal Expansion & Contraction
Rails expand on hot days and contract on cold nights. Because the **ballast and sleepers resist this movement unevenly**, the rail "walks" in one direction, leaving **uneven expansion gaps** at joints.

$$L_t = L_0 (1 + \alpha \cdot \Delta T)$$

Where:
- $L_t$ = rail length at temperature $t$
- $L_0$ = rail length at reference temperature
- $\alpha$ = coefficient of linear expansion (steel $\approx$ **$11.5 \times 10^{-6} / °C$**)
- $\Delta T$ = temperature differential

### D) Yawing and Hunting of Locomotives
Modern locomotives (especially on curves) undergo **lateral oscillations (hunting)** and **yawing**, generating lateral thrusts that also push rails longitudinally.

## 2.2 Causes — Complete Enumerated List (KTU Favorite)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic — "DOTS BICPS"** to remember the 8 causes:
> - **D** – Deflection of rails under load
> - **O** – Oscillations (Yawing/Hunting) of locomotives
> - **T** – Thermal expansion/contraction
> - **S** – Starting & braking forces
> - **B** – Bad drainage & weak formation
> - **I** – Insufficient ballast / poor boxing
> - **C** – Curved tracks (lateral thrust)
> - **P** – Poor sleeper spacing (gauge face wear)
> - **S** – Soft murrum / weak subgrade

## 2.3 Effects of Creep — The "Damage Chain"

If creep is unaddressed, the following damage chain triggers:

1. **Sleepers tilt out-of-square** → skew (non-rectangular sleeper spacing).
2. **Spacing of sleepers becomes non-uniform** → some areas over-crowded, some gapped.
3. **Expansion gaps at joints become unequal** → tight joints (summer → buckling risk) or open joints (winter → pull-apart risk).
4. **Kinks form at joints** → impact loads accelerate.
5. **Points and crossings displaced** → interlocking failure risk.
6. **Gauge & alignment disturb** → derailment risk.
7. **Rail ends battered** → weld/bolt fatigue.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Thermal expansion of rail | $\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$ | mm | Computes gap change at joints |
| 2 | Steel expansion coefficient | $\alpha_{steel} = 11.5 \times 10^{-6}$ | per °C | Standard rail steel constant |
| 3 | Expansion gap provision | $e = \alpha \cdot L \cdot (T_{max} - T_{ref})$ | mm | At rail joint during laying |
| 4 | Wave-action creep per pass | $\delta_w \approx \dfrac{W \cdot a^3}{3 E I}$ | mm | Elastic beam deflection analogy |
| 5 | Creep resistance per anchor | $F_a = \mu \cdot N$ | N | Anchors must exceed this |
| 6 | Required anchor spacing | $s_a = \dfrac{R_a}{F_a}$ | m | Anti-creep layout design |
| 7 | Lame's creep constant (empirical) | $C = \dfrac{\text{Total creep (mm)}}{\text{Days} \times \text{Train passes/day}}$ | mm/pass | Field measurement correlation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Lame's Constant**: $C$ is purely empirical and varies from **0.1 to 0.5 mm per train axle passage** depending on track maintenance quality.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Creep Knowledge |
|---|---|
| **Track Maintenance Planning** | Decides frequency of de-stressing operations |
| **LWR / CWR Design** | Determines anchor spacing in long welded rails |
| **High-Speed Rail (HSR)** | Creep amplifies differential expansion in CWR |
| **Curve Design** | Anti-creep plates mandatory on curves < 3° |
| **Safety Audits** | Unequal expansion gaps are key derailment predictors |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Creep Resistance from Elastic Wave Theory

The dominant mechanism is the **wave-action theory**. We treat the rail as a beam on elastic foundation (Winkler model) and compute the longitudinal drag force exerted by the ballast and sleepers on the rail per wheel passage.

### Step 1: Idealize the Rail as a Beam on Elastic Foundation

Let the rail rest on a continuous elastic foundation of modulus $k$ (N/mm/mm). Under a moving wheel load $P$, the rail deflects into a wavy shape. The differential equation of equilibrium is:

$$E I \frac{d^4 y}{dx^4} + k \cdot y = q(x)$$

Where:
- $E$ = Young's modulus of rail steel $\approx$ **$2.1 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$**
- $I$ = Moment of inertia of rail section (e.g., 52 kg rail → $I \approx 1.55 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$)
- $q(x)$ = distributed reaction
- $y$ = vertical deflection

### Step 2: Compute the Characteristic Length

The characteristic length $\lambda$ of the deflection wave is:

$$\lambda = \sqrt[4]{\frac{4 E I}{k}}$$

Typical values: $k \approx 5$ to $20 \text{ N/mm/mm}^2$, giving $\lambda \approx 800$ mm to $1100$ mm for 52 kg rail.

### Step 3: Vertical Deflection under a Point Load

For a concentrated wheel load $P$ at $x = 0$:

$$y(x) = \dfrac{P \lambda}{8 E I} \cdot e^{-\vert x \vert / \lambda} \left(\cos \dfrac{\vert x \vert}{\lambda} + \sin \dfrac{\vert x \vert}{\lambda} \right)$$

### Step 4: Slope of Deflection (Energy Gradient)

The slope $\frac{dy}{dx}$ near the wheel represents the longitudinal "pull" direction. Integrating along the deformed wave gives the net micro-displacement per wheel passage:

$$\delta_w = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} f(y,\lambda)\,dx$$

For a simplified Winkler half-space, the practical engineering approximation is:

$$\delta_w \approx \dfrac{P}{2 \lambda k}$$

### Step 5: Force Resisted by Ballast (Anti-Creep Reaction)

The longitudinal resistance offered by the ballast per unit length of rail is:

$$r_b = \mu \cdot N$$

Where:
- $\mu$ = coefficient of friction between sleeper bottom and ballast $\approx$ **0.6 to 0.8** (sharp ballast)
- $N$ = effective vertical reaction distributed per unit length

For a standard PSC sleeper spacing of $s = 600$ mm with axle load 22.5 tonnes (220 kN):

$$N = \dfrac{220{,}000}{600} \approx 366.7 \text{ N/mm}$$

$$r_b = 0.7 \times 366.7 \approx 256.7 \text{ N/mm}$$

### Step 6: Anti-Creep Anchor Spacing Derivation

For an anchor to hold the rail, the creep force per unit length $f_c$ must be balanced:

$$F_c = f_c \cdot s_a$$

For equilibrium:

$$F_a \geq F_c$$

$$s_a \leq \dfrac{F_a}{f_c}$$

Where:
- $F_a$ = Holding capacity of one anti-creep anchor (typically **5 kN to 10 kN**)
- $f_c$ = creep force per unit length (typically **10 N/mm to 30 N/mm** for heavy haul)

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**

Given:
- $F_a = 8 \text{ kN} = 8000 \text{ N}$
- $f_c = 20 \text{ N/mm}$

Find anchor spacing $s_a$:

$$s_a = \dfrac{8000}{20} = 400 \text{ mm}$$

But standard practical spacing is on every **3rd to 5th sleeper** (every **1.8 m to 3.0 m**), so anchors are placed in clusters.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Creep Mechanism in Tabular Form

| Mechanism | Trigger | Force Direction | Magnitude Indicator |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wave action | Moving wheel | Forward drag | $P/(2\lambda k)$ |
| Starting tractive | Acceleration | Backward push (then rebound) | $\mu \cdot W \cdot a/g$ |
| Braking | Deceleration | Forward drag | $f_b \cdot m$ |
| Thermal expansion | $\Delta T > 0$ | Bidirectional | $\alpha L \Delta T$ |
| Yawing/Hunting | Lateral oscillation | Lateral + longitudinal coupling | Empirical |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Creep Mechanism — Block-Level Functional Architecture

Below is the functional architecture of creep generation, from wheel load input to rail displacement output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Wheel Load Input"] --> B["Rail Bending Wave Formation"]
    B --> C["Longitudinal Drag Force Generated"]
    C --> D{"Anti Creep Anchor Present?"}
    D -- Yes --> E["Force Absorbed by Anchor"]
    E --> F["Rail Held in Position"]
    D -- No --> G["Rail Slides Forward"]
    G --> H["Sleeper Skew Develops"]
    H --> I["Unequal Joint Gaps Form"]
    I --> J["Track Buckling or Pull Apart Risk"]
    F --> K["Safe Track Geometry Maintained"]

    subgraph CausesBlock["CREEP CAUSES"]
        A1["Wave Action Theory"] --> A
        A2["Starting and Braking Forces"] --> A
        A3["Thermal Expansion"] --> A
        A4["Yawing and Hunting"] --> A
    end
```

## 4.2 Anti-Creep Treatment Strategy — Architecture Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Prevention Level 1: Design"] --> P2["Prevention Level 2: Maintenance"]
    P2 --> P3["Prevention Level 3: Monitoring"]

    P1 --> P1a["Adequate Ballast Section"]
    P1 --> P1b["Proper Drainage Provision"]
    P1 --> P1c["Quality Sleeper Spacing"]

    P2 --> P2a["Anti Creep Anchors at Spacing"]
    P2 --> P2b["Ballast Boxing and Packing"]
    P2 --> P2c["Tightening of Fish Bolts"]

    P3 --> P3a["Creep Post Monitoring"]
    P3 --> P3b["Periodic Gauge and Alignment Checks"]
    P3 --> P3c["De stressing Operations"]
```

## 4.3 Creep Damage Chain — Failure Progression Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Stage 1: Micro Slip per Wheel"] --> S2["Stage 2: Accumulated Displacement 50mm"]
    S2 --> S3["Stage 3: Sleeper Skew Visible"]
    S3 --> S4["Stage 4: Unequal Joint Gaps"]
    S4 --> S5["Stage 5: Kink Formation at Joints"]
    S5 --> S6["Stage 6: Buckling Risk in Summer"]
    S5 --> S7["Stage 6 Alt: Pull Apart Risk in Winter"]
    S6 --> S8["Stage 7: Derailment Risk"]
    S7 --> S8
```

## 4.4 Cross-Section of Anti-Creep Anchor Installation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R["Rail Foot"] --> AN["Anti Creep Anchor Clip"]
    AN --> SL["Sleeper Top Surface"]
    SL --> BS["Ballast Cushion Layer"]
    BS --> SG["Subgrade Formation"]

    AN -. "Holds rail foot firmly against sleeper via spring tension" .- R
    SL -. "Distributes anchor reaction" .- BS
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define creep of rails. State any three causes of creep.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Creep of rails is defined as the longitudinal movement of rails in the direction of the dominant traffic, occurring gradually and cumulatively due to the repeated passage of wheel loads.

**Three Causes (2 Marks — 1 Mark each for any two, full credit for three):**
1. Wave action of rails caused by the moving wheel loads.
2. Starting and braking forces of locomotives at stations and gradients.
3. Thermal expansion and contraction of rails due to temperature variation.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip**: Examiners allocate 1 mark for the definition and 2 marks for listing causes. Writing the *mechanism* in 1 line (e.g., "wave action drags the rail forward") earns the 2nd mark for each cause.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**List four ill effects of creep of rails.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. Sleepers get displaced from their square position, leading to skew sleeper alignment.
2. Spacing of sleepers becomes non-uniform.
3. Expansion gaps at rail joints become unequal, leading to buckling in summer and pull-apart in winter.
4. Kinks form at the joints, causing rough riding and accelerated deterioration of the track.

> [!NOTE]
> A single-line *consequence* listing is sufficient — but writing the *consequence → damage* chain (e.g., "non-uniform spacing → gauge distortion") earns an extra **0.5 mark** for depth.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> Students frequently lose marks by:
> - Confusing **creep** with **buckling** (buckling is a *consequence* of creep + thermal stress).
> - Omitting the **direction** in the definition.
> - Writing causes *without mechanisms*.
> - Confusing **anti-creep anchors** with **anti-buckling anchors** (they serve different but related purposes).

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q.A (a)** Explain the phenomenon of creep of rails. Discuss in detail the various causes of creep. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(1) Phenomenon Explanation (3 Marks):**

Creep is the slow, continuous, longitudinal displacement of rails in the direction of dominant traffic. It arises because every wheel passage applies both vertical and horizontal forces on the rail. The rail deflects into a wave shape; as the wheel rolls on, the trailing half of the wave straightens and drags the rail forward by a microscopic distance. Over thousands of wheel passages, this accumulates to several centimeters per month.

The displacement is generally measured using **creep posts** fixed at the ends of rails and surveyed periodically against a stable reference peg.

**(2) Causes in Detail (4 Marks):**

| Cause | Mechanism |
|---|---|
| Wave action theory | Deflection wave straightens behind the wheel and pulls the rail forward. |
| Starting forces | Locomotive tractive effort pushes rails backward initially; rebound wave drags forward. |
| Braking forces | Brake shoes apply tangential force dragging rails in direction of motion. |
| Thermal expansion | Expansion gaps close unevenly due to resistance from sleepers and ballast. |
| Yawing/hunting of locomotive | Lateral oscillations induce coupled longitudinal components. |
| Soft formation / poor drainage | Ballast settles unevenly, reducing anchorage. |

**(3) Magnitude Indicators:** Creep of 50–150 mm/month is considered moderate; > 200 mm/month is severe.

---

**Q.A (b)** Describe the effects of creep of rails. Explain the various measures adopted to prevent creep. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(1) Effects of Creep (3 Marks):**
1. Sleeper skew — sleepers lose their right-angle position with rails.
2. Non-uniform sleeper spacing.
3. Unequal expansion gaps → buckling in summer, pull-apart in winter.
4. Displacement of points and crossings.
5. Difficulty in maintaining correct gauge, alignment, and level.
6. Kinks at joints → rough riding, fish-plate failure.

**(2) Preventive Measures (4 Marks):**

| Preventive Measure | Engineering Logic |
|---|---|
| Adequate and clean ballast | Increases longitudinal resistance |
| Proper drainage | Prevents ballast weakening |
| Anti-creep anchors (every 3rd–5th sleeper) | Mechanical restraint on rail |
| Tight fish bolts and joint maintenance | Holds rail ends against movement |
| Steel sleepers on high-creep stretches | Greater frictional anchorage |
| Boxing of ballast to sleeper top | Adds passive resistance from shoulder |
| Use of bearing plates and elastic fastenings | Modern elastic fastenings (e.g., Pandrol) resist creep |
| De-stressing of LWR/CWR | Periodic re-tensioning to neutralize creep |

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q.B (a)** What are the different methods of measurement of creep of rails? Describe any one method in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(1) Methods of Measurement (3 Marks):**

| Method | Principle |
|---|---|
| Creep Post Method | Reference posts at rail ends; periodic measurement of distance |
| String Line Method | String stretched between fixed reference marks |
| Survey Theodolite Method | Optical surveying from a fixed benchmark |
| Paint Marks on Rail Foot | Visual marker lines to detect shift |
| Strain Gauge / Digital Sensor | Modern continuous monitoring |

**(2) Detailed Method — Creep Post Method (4 Marks):**

**Setup:**
- Two concrete posts are installed: one at the *fixed reference point* (e.g., a level crossing, bridge abutment, or any non-movable structure) and the other at the *rail end* being monitored.
- The fixed reference post is anchored firmly in the subgrade, away from the track influence zone.

**Procedure:**
1. Mark the rail foot at the **center of the post** using a paint mark.
2. After a fixed interval (say 7 days), re-measure the offset of the rail-foot mark from the post center using a steel tape or vernier scale.
3. Record the displacement, direction, and date.

**Calculation:**

$$\text{Creep per day} = \dfrac{D_2 - D_1}{t_2 - t_1} \quad \text{mm/day}$$

Where $D_1, D_2$ are successive readings and $t_2 - t_1$ is the time interval.

**Limitation:**
- Manual and time-consuming.
- Subject to human error in alignment of measurement axis.

---

**Q.B (b)** A railway track exhibits a creep of **120 mm over 30 days** under **40 train passages per day** on a tangent track. Compute:
(i) Lame's creep constant
(ii) The expected creep over 60 days if train frequency increases to 50 passages/day
**(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Total creep $C_t = 120$ mm over 30 days
- $N_1 = 40$ passages/day
- $\Delta t_1 = 30$ days

**(i) Lame's Creep Constant (3 Marks):**

$$C = \dfrac{C_t}{N_1 \times \Delta t_1}$$

$$C = \dfrac{120}{40 \times 30} = \dfrac{120}{1200} = 0.1 \text{ mm per passage}$$

**(ii) Expected Creep over 60 days at 50 passages/day (4 Marks):**

$$C_{new} = C \times N_2 \times \Delta t_2$$

$$C_{new} = 0.1 \times 50 \times 60$$

$$C_{new} = 300 \text{ mm}$$

**Interpretation:** A 25% increase in traffic frequency leads to a **150% increase in cumulative creep** (from 120 mm to 300 mm) over the same doubled period — a non-linear escalation justifying stringent anti-creep maintenance on high-traffic corridors.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** Always carry forward the *units* explicitly. Writing "$0.1 \text{ mm/passage}$" earns more marks than writing just "$0.1$".

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision Checklist — Print Before Exam:**

- [ ] **Definition**: Creep = *longitudinal* movement of rail *in direction of dominant traffic*.
- [ ] **Magnitude**: 50–150 mm/month typical; up to 300 mm in severe cases.
- [ ] **Wave action theory** is the *primary* cause — rail wave straightens behind the wheel and pulls the rail forward.
- [ ] **Five key causes**: Wave action, starting/braking, thermal expansion, yawing/hunting, weak ballast.
- [ ] **Eight-effect damage chain**: Skew sleepers → uneven spacing → unequal gaps → kinks → buckling/pull-apart → derailment risk.
- [ ] **Anti-creep anchors** placed every **3rd to 5th sleeper** (≈ 1.8 m to 3.0 m spacing).
- [ ] **Creep measurement**: Creep post method is the standard field technique.
- [ ] **Lame's Constant**: $C = \dfrac{\text{Total creep}}{\text{Passages} \times \text{Days}}$ — empirical, **0.1 to 0.5 mm/passage**.
- [ ] **Steel sleeper** offers highest creep resistance; **wooden sleeper** the lowest.
- [ ] **Pandrol clips and elastic fastenings** are modern anti-creep solutions for LWR/CWR.
- [ ] **Direction**: Creep is in the *direction of traffic* on tangent track; reverse possible on steep up-gradients.
- [ ] **Remedial hierarchy**: (1) Maintenance → (2) Anchors → (3) De-stressing → (4) Sleeper replacement.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Theories - Measurement of Creep - Prevention of Creep
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET754 - RAILWAY, PORT AND HARBOR ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Theories - Measurement of Creep - Prevention of Creep # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] **RAIL CREEP — KTU Standard Definition** > **Rail Creep** is defined as the *gradual longitudinal displacement of rails* in the track relative to the sleepers, occurring either in the direction of traffic (forward creep) or against the direction of traffic (backward creep). It is essentially the slow, continuous, and irreversible movement of the rail along the track alignment due to dynamic wheel-rail interaction, braking, traction, and wave action. In more rigorous track engineering terms: $$\text{Creep} = \frac{\Delta L_{longitudinal}}{L_{rail}} \quad [\text{unitless, or mm/m}]$$ The **allowable creep** as per Indian Railway Standards (IRS) is generally limited to **150 mm** before corrective re-setting of the track is mandated. On broad gauge and high-speed corridors, this limit is even tighter. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] **Think of rail creep like this...** > Imagine pushing a long bamboo stick slowly across a bed of sand using repeated gentle taps from one end. Each tap nudges the stick a tiny bit forward. Although any single nudge is barely visible, after thousands of cycles, the stick has crawled noticeably forward. > > Similarly, **every passing train wheel** delivers microscopic longitudinal kicks to the rail. Over months and years, these kicks accumulate, and the rail literally *crawls* longitudinally on the sleeper bed — this is creep. A **real-world railway analogy**: Place a long steel ruler on a stack of textbooks. Strike one end repeatedly with a pen. With each strike, the ruler slides a fraction of a millimeter forward. Now replace the textbooks with sleepers and ballast, the ruler with a rail, and the pen strikes with passing train wheels — you have rail creep. --- ## 1.3 Why Creep Matters — Engineering Significance | Parameter | Typical Value / Standard | |---|---| | Allowable Creep Limit (BG Mainline) | **≤ 150 mm** | | Allowable Creep Limit (High-speed corridors) | **≤ 75 mm** | | Normal Creep Rate (well-maintained track) | **1–3 mm/day** | | Excessive Creep Rate (poor track) | **> 5 mm/day** | | Standard Rail Length (BG) | **13.00 m** (shorter) / **25.00 m** (longer) | | Standard Sleeper Spacing (BG Mainline) | **0.60 m** (centres) | --- ## 1.4 Key Effects of Creep (Why it is undesirable) > [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner Focus — Effects of Creep are frequently asked for 3–7 marks.** > 1. **Sleepers tilt out of square** (skewing), disturbing sleeper spacing. > 2. **Gauge and alignment distortion** at curves and approaches. > 3. **Loss of expansion gap** at rail joints — causes *buckling* in summer. > 4. **Excessive opening of joints** — causes *pull-apart* in winter and rough riding. > 5. **Disturbs ballast packing**, reducing lateral resistance. > 6. **Damages switch points, crossings, and level-crossing panels** due to displaced stock rail alignment. > 7. **Misalignment of interlocking** in yards and station throats. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Classical Theories of Rail Creep The longitudinal force system that drives creep is the cumulative result of millions of micro-interactions. Three classical theories attempt to explain the *mechanism* of creep generation: --- ### 2.1.1 THEORY 1 — WAVE MOTION THEORY (Shamanovsky's Theory) > [!IMPORTANT] **Most widely accepted theory for creep.** **Core Concept:** The rail, treated as an **infinite beam resting on a continuous elastic foundation** (Winkler foundation), undergoes a transverse deflection wave as each wheel load passes. **Step-by-Step Mechanics:** 1. As a wheel approaches a rail section, the rail deflects **downwards** ahead of the wheel. 2. The rail behind the wheel begins to **rebound upward**. 3. This deflection pattern travels as a **transverse wave** along the rail. 4. Due to the wave's asymmetry (larger forward slope, gentler rear slope), particles on the rail surface experience a **net forward displacement** per wave cycle. 5. Mathematically: the *forward wave* is of greater amplitude than the *backward wave* → **net longitudinal forward movement**. **Mathematical Representation (simplified):** For a beam on elastic foundation, the deflection $y(x,t)$ follows: $$y(x, t) = \frac{P \lambda}{2k} \cdot e^{-\lambda \vert x - vt \vert} \cdot \left[\cos(\lambda (x - vt)) + \sin(\lambda (x - vt))\right]$$ Where: - $P$ = Wheel load - $\lambda$ = Characteristic coefficient $= \sqrt[4]{\dfrac{k}{4EI}}$ - $k$ = Foundation modulus (ballast stiffness) - $E$ = Young's modulus of rail steel - $I$ = Moment of inertia of rail - $v$ = Train speed The longitudinal creep component is proportional to $\lambda$, and therefore to **train speed** and **wheel load**. > [!NOTE] **Key Insight:** Wave motion theory predicts that creep rate **increases with train speed and axle load** — which matches field observations on high-speed corridors. --- ### 2.1.2 THEORY 2 — PERCUSSION THEORY (Theory of Impact at Joints) **Core Concept:** Applies mainly to **jointed track** (rails with fish-plated joints). **Step-by-Step Mechanics:** 1. When a wheel approaches a rail joint, it traverses the **expansion gap** ($e$) first. 2. The wheel strikes the receiving rail with a **vertical impact** $F_v$. 3. The wheel contact geometry is such that impact is rarely purely vertical — there is a slight **inclination** at the wheel-rail interface. 4. This inclined impact resolves into: - **Vertical component** ($F_v$) — handled by sleeper/ballast. - **Horizontal forward component** ($F_h$) — pushes the rail longitudinally. 5. Each joint impact = one forward nudge on the rail. 6. With millions of wheel passages per year, **cumulative creep is significant**. $$F_h = F_v \cdot \tan(\alpha)$$ Where $\alpha$ = angle of inclination of impact force from vertical (typically $1°$–$3°$). > [!WARNING] **KTU Pitfall:** Students often write that percussion occurs at *every* point on the rail. It is specifically a **joint phenomenon** and explains why **Long Welded Rails (LWR)** show *much less* creep than jointed track. --- ### 2.1.3 THEORY 3 — DRAG THEORY (Theory of Friction) **Core Concept:** Explains creep as the **reaction** to wheel slip during traction and braking. **Step-by-Step Mechanics:** 1. **During Acceleration:** Driving wheels rotate faster than the train's translation. They tend to slip **backward** relative to the rail. By Newton's third law, friction on the rail is **forward** → rail creeps **forward**. 2. **During Braking:** Braked wheels tend to lock and skid. They slip **forward** relative to the rail. Friction on the rail is **backward** → rail creeps **backward**. 3. **During Steady Running:** Creep is minimal; only wave action contributes. > [!NOTE] **Field Verification:** Drag theory correctly explains why, in predominantly **braking zones** (approach to stations, down-gradients), rails often creep **backward**, contrary to the wave theory prediction. --- ### 2.1.4 Comparative Summary of Theories | Feature | Wave Motion Theory | Percussion Theory | Drag Theory | |---|---|---|---| | **Predominant Location** | Whole track | Joints only | Whole track | | **Dominant Driver** | Train speed & load | Joint impact | Traction/Braking | | **Direction Predicted** | Forward | Forward | Depends on accel/braking | | **Effect of LWR** | Reduced | Eliminated | Unchanged | | **Acceptance** | Most widely accepted | Valid for jointed track | Valid supplementary theory | --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Description | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $C = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0 \cdot t}$ | Creep rate (longitudinal displacement per unit length per day) | mm/m/day | | 2 | $\lambda = \sqrt[4]{\dfrac{k}{4EI}}$ | Wave theory characteristic coefficient | $\text{m}^{-1}$ | | 3 | $y(x,t) = \dfrac{P\lambda}{2k} e^{-\lambda\vert x-vt\vert}[\cos+\sin]$ | Beam-on-elastic-foundation deflection | m | | 4 | $F_h = F_v \cdot \tan\alpha$ | Horizontal impact at joint (Percussion) | N | | 5 | $N_{AC} = \dfrac{n_{sleepers}}{2}$ | Number of anti-creepers (one per alternate sleeper) | count | | 6 | $C_{allowable} \leq 150$ | Permissible creep on BG main line | mm | | 7 | $E_{joint} = L \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Required expansion gap | mm | | 8 | $\Delta T_{max} = T_{rail,max} - T_{rail,at\ laying}$ | Maximum temperature differential | °C | | 9 | $L_{effective} = 2 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2EI \cdot \Delta T \cdot \alpha \cdot F_s}{A}}$ | Breathing length of LWR | m | | 10 | $R_{ballast} = \mu_b \cdot W_{sleeper}$ | Ballast resistance force | N | **Where:** - $\alpha$ = coefficient of thermal expansion of steel $= 11.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ per } °C$ - $\mu_b$ = ballast friction coefficient $= 0.6$–$0.8$ - $W_{sleeper}$ = weight of sleeper + load on it - $F_s$ = safe thermal stress in rail $= 800$–$1100 \text{ kg/cm}^2$ - $A$ = cross-sectional area of rail - $E = 2.1 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$ for rail steel --- ## 2.3 Real-World Utility > [!NOTE] **Engineering Application Mapping** > - **Wave Motion Theory** → Used in **dynamic track stiffness modelling**, **LWR (Long Welded Rail) design**, and **high-speed rail sleepers**. > - **Percussion Theory** → Drives **fish-plate design**, **joint sleeper design**, and **maintenance planning for jointed track**. > - **Drag Theory** → Critical for **locomotive braking system design**, **traction motor control**, and **EMU suburban track layout** where frequent accel/braking occurs. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Creep Rate from Measurement Data > **Given (Typical KTU Numerical):** A 13 m long rail panel is observed to move longitudinally by **8 mm in 7 days** as measured between two reference pegs at its ends. Calculate: > 1. Creep per metre of rail per day > 2. Total creep in 30 days (extrapolated) > 3. Comment on whether corrective action is required per IRS limits. --- ### Step 1 — Identify Given Data $$L_0 = 13.00 \text{ m}, \quad \Delta L = 8 \text{ mm}, \quad t = 7 \text{ days}$$ ### Step 2 — Apply the Creep Formula The creep rate $C$ is defined as longitudinal displacement per unit rail length per unit time: $$C = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0 \cdot t}$$ ### Step 3 — Substitute Values $$C = \frac{8 \text{ mm}}{13.00 \text{ m} \times 7 \text{ days}}$$ $$C = \frac{8}{91} \text{ mm/m/day}$$ $$\boxed{C \approx 0.0879 \text{ mm/m/day}}$$ ### Step 4 — Convert to mm/day (cumulative for the panel) $$C_{panel} = \frac{\Delta L}{t} = \frac{8}{7} = 1.143 \text{ mm/day}$$ ### Step 5 — Extrapolate to 30 Days $$\Delta L_{30} = C_{panel} \times 30 = 1.143 \times 30$$ $$\boxed{\Delta L_{30} \approx 34.3 \text{ mm}}$$ ### Step 6 — Decision Per IRS Standard The **allowable creep** before re-setting = **150 mm**. Since projected creep $\approx 34.3 \text{ mm} < 150 \text{ mm}$, **no immediate corrective action** is required. However, **monitoring should continue weekly**, and *anti-creepers should be installed preventatively* if the section lies on a steep gradient or sharp curve. --- ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Beam on Elastic Foundation (Wave Theory Support) The classical differential equation for a beam of infinite length resting on a continuous elastic (Winkler) foundation, subjected to a moving concentrated load $P$, is: $$EI \cdot \frac{\partial^4 y}{\partial x^4} + k \cdot y(x,t) = P \cdot \delta(x - vt)$$ Where: - $EI$ = flexural rigidity of the rail - $k$ = modulus of subgrade reaction (ballast stiffness), typically $5000$–$15000 \text{ kN/m}^2$ - $y(x,t)$ = vertical deflection at position $x$ and time $t$ - $\delta$ = Dirac delta function (concentrated load model) - $v$ = velocity of the moving load ### Step 1 — Seek Steady-State Solution Assume $y(x,t) = Y(x - vt) = Y(\xi)$, where $\xi = x - vt$. By chain rule: $$\frac{\partial^4 y}{\partial x^4} = Y^{(4)}(\xi)$$ ### Step 2 — Define Characteristic Coefficient $$\lambda = \sqrt[4]{\frac{k}{4EI}}$$ ### Step 3 — Standard Solution Form $$Y(\xi) = \frac{P\lambda}{2k} \cdot e^{-\lambda\vert\xi\vert} \left[\cos(\lambda\xi) + \sin(\lambda\xi)\right]$$ This gives a **damped sinusoidal wave** moving with the wheel. ### Step 4 — Compute Maximum Deflection (Under Wheel) At $\xi = 0$ (under the load): $$Y_{max} = Y(0) = \frac{P\lambda}{2k} \cdot [1 + 0] = \frac{P\lambda}{2k}$$ Substituting $\lambda$: $$Y_{max} = \frac{P}{2k} \cdot \sqrt[4]{\frac{k}{4EI}}$$ $$\boxed{Y_{max} = \frac{P}{2\sqrt{2}} \cdot \sqrt[4]{\frac{1}{k^3 EI}}}$$ ### Step 5 — Compute Net Longitudinal Displacement per Wave Cycle The asymmetry of the wave (steep front, gentle rear) gives a net forward displacement $\Delta_{net}$ over one wavelength $\lambda^{-1}$: $$\Delta_{net} \propto \frac{P^2 \cdot v}{k \cdot EI}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] **Engineering Interpretation:** The longitudinal creep per train passage is: > - **Directly proportional** to the **square of axle load** $P^2$. > - **Directly proportional** to **train speed** $v$. > - **Inversely proportional** to **track stiffness** $k$ and **rail bending stiffness** $EI$. This is why **heavy, high-speed trains on soft track ballast** cause the most severe creep problems. --- ## 3.3 Numerical on Percussion Force (Theory 2) **Given:** A wheel impact at a rail joint delivers vertical force $F_v = 120$ kN. The impact is inclined at $\alpha = 2.5°$ to the vertical. Find the horizontal forward component $F_h$. ### Step 1 — Recall Formula $$F_h = F_v \cdot \tan(\alpha)$$ ### Step 2 — Substitute $$F_h = 120{,}000 \times \tan(2.5°)$$ ### Step 3 — Evaluate $$\tan(2.5°) = 0.04366$$ $$F_h = 120{,}000 \times 0.04366$$ $$\boxed{F_h \approx 5239 \text{ N} \approx 5.24 \text{ kN}}$$ ### Step 4 — Interpretation This 5.24 kN horizontal kick occurs at **every joint impact**, and there can be **tens of thousands of joint impacts per day** on a busy BG line. Cumulative effect → measurable creep. --- ## 3.4 Anti-Creeper Quantity Calculation **Given:** A 1 km stretch of BG track with sleeper spacing = 0.60 m. Anti-creepers are provided on alternate sleepers. ### Step 1 — Number of Sleepers per km $$n_{sleepers} = \frac{1000}{0.60} = 1667 \text{ sleepers/km (approx.)}$$ ### Step 2 — Number of Anti-Creepers (Alternate Sleepers) $$N_{AC} = \frac{n_{sleepers}}{2} = \frac{1667}{2} \approx 834 \text{ anti-creepers/km}$$ ### Step 3 — Number per Rail Length (13 m panel) $$N_{AC,panel} = \frac{13.0}{0.60 \times 2} = \frac{13.0}{1.20} \approx 11 \text{ anti-creepers per 13 m rail}$$ ### Step 4 — For Both Rails $$N_{total} = 2 \times 11 = 22 \text{ anti-creepers per 13 m rail panel}$$ $$\boxed{N_{total} = 22 \text{ anti-creepers per 13 m rail}}$$ > [!NOTE] **KTU Examiner Note:** On curves and gradients, anti-creeper density is doubled (one per sleeper). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Conceptual Map of Rail Creep ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Rail Creep Phenomenon] --> B[Causes / Theories] A --> C[Measurement Methods] A --> D[Prevention Methods] A --> E[Effects on Track] B --> B1[Wave Motion Theory
Bending waves under moving load] B --> B2[Percussion Theory
Joint impact horizontal kick] B --> B3[Drag Theory
Friction from accel or braking] B --> B4[Pumping of Sleepers
Loose ballast ejection] C --> C1[Creep Indicator Posts
Reference monuments] C --> C2[Measuring Chord Method
Two rail darts with steel tape] C --> C3[Periodic Survey
Weekly or fortnightly readings] D --> D1[Anti Creepers
Bearing plate anchors] D --> D2[Ellis Anti Creeper
Spring steel anchor] D --> D3[Adequate Sleepers
Higher density per km] D --> D4[Long Welded Rails
Eliminate joint percussion] D --> D5[Proper Ballast Section
Boxing and consolidation] E --> E1[Sleeper Skewing] E --> E2[Joint Gap Distortion] E --> E3[Switch Misalignment] E --> E4[Buckling Risk in Summer] B1 --> F[Forward Creep Dominant] B2 --> F B3 --> G[Forward or Backward] E1 --> H[Track Geometry Failure] E2 --> H E3 --> H E4 --> H ``` --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Wave Motion Theory Sequence ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant W as Wheel Load participant R as Rail Beam participant S as Sleeper and Ballast participant F as Foundation W->>R: Step 1: Approach causes forward deflection R->>R: Step 2: Wave begins to form W->>R: Step 3: Rail under wheel fully deflected R->>S: Step 4: Vertical load transmitted S->>F: Step 5: Elastic reaction from ballast R->>R: Step 6: Rebound wave behind wheel R-->>W: Step 7: Asymmetric wave creates NET FORWARD displacement Note over R: Cumulative effect = Rail Creep Forward ``` --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Anti-Creeper Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT[Track Loading] L1[Wheel Impact Vertical] L2[Wheel Impact Horizontal] L3[Thermal Stress] end subgraph ANCHOR[Anti Creeper System] AC1[Bearing Plate Anchor] AC2[Ellis Anchor] AC3[Spring Type Anchor] AC4[Fair Type Anchor] end subgraph SLEEPER[Sleeper Interface] S1[Wooden Sleeper] S2[Concrete Sleeper] S3[Steel Sleeper] end subgraph OUTPUT[Resistance Mechanism] O1[Friction Grip on Rail Base] O2[Mechanical Lock to Sleeper] O3[Elastic Restoring Force] end L1 --> ANCHOR L2 --> ANCHOR L3 --> ANCHOR ANCHOR --> SLEEPER ANCHOR --> OUTPUT SLEEPER --> O1 SLEEPER --> O2 SLEEPER --> O3 O1 --> RES[Net Anti Creep Resistance] O2 --> RES O3 --> RES ``` --- ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Creep Measurement Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Survey of Track Section] --> MARK[Step A: Fix two Creep Indicator Posts
concrete pillars at rail ends] MARK --> DART[Step B: Punch two reference darts on rail foot
aligned with posts] DART --> CHORD[Step C: Stretch steel measuring chord
between posts] CHORD --> READ1[Step D: Record initial reading R1
date and temperature noted] READ1 --> WAIT[Wait 7 to 14 days] WAIT --> READ2[Step E: Record new reading R2] READ2 --> CALC[Step F: Calculate creep delta
delta L = R2 minus R1] CALC --> JUDGE[Step G: Compare with limit 150 mm] JUDGE -->|Below Limit| OK[Continue Monitoring] JUDGE -->|Above Limit| ACT[Reset Rail and Install Anti Creepers] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Question A1:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define rail creep. List any four ill-effects of creep on a railway track.** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Rail creep is the *gradual longitudinal movement of rails in the track* in the direction of traffic (or sometimes against it) relative to the sleepers. It is caused by dynamic wheel-rail interaction, percussion at joints, and friction during traction/braking. **Four Ill-effects:** 1. **Sleepers move out of square** — sleeper spacing is disturbed, leading to gauge and alignment irregularities. 2. **Expansion gaps at joints get distorted** — gap may close (causing buckling in summer) or open excessively (causing rough riding and pull-aparts in winter). 3. **Points and crossings are misaligned** — switch rods cannot operate freely; interlocking is disturbed. 4. **Ballast gets disturbed** — lateral resistance reduces, affecting track stability especially on curves. --- ### **Question A2:** `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]` **State the three classical theories of creep and indicate which one is most widely accepted.** **Model Answer:** The three classical theories of creep are: 1. **Wave Motion Theory** (also called Shamanovsky's theory) — explains creep as a result of bending waves set up by moving wheel loads. 2. **Percussion Theory** — explains creep by the horizontal component of impact at rail joints. 3. **Drag Theory** (Theory of Friction) — explains creep as reaction to wheel slip during acceleration and braking. **Most widely accepted:** **Wave Motion Theory**, because it explains creep on continuous welded track where percussion cannot occur, and field observations confirm that creep increases with train speed and axle load, as predicted by wave theory. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Question B1: Choice A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a)** Explain the **wave motion theory** of creep with a neat sketch. Discuss how train speed and axle load affect creep rate. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **method of measurement of creep** using creep indicator posts. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Part (a):** **1. Statement of Theory (2 marks):** According to the wave motion theory (proposed by Shamanovsky and developed by various researchers), the rail acts as a long beam resting on a continuous elastic foundation (sleepers and ballast treated as Winkler foundation). When a moving wheel load passes, it sets up a transverse deflection wave in the rail. The wave has an *asymmetric shape* — the front slope (ahead of wheel) is steeper than the rear slope (behind wheel). Because of this asymmetry, the rail experiences a *net longitudinal forward displacement* per wave cycle, which cumulatively appears as creep. **2. Mathematical Expression (2 marks):** The deflection of the rail under a moving load $P$ is: $$y(x,t) = \frac{P\lambda}{2k} \cdot e^{-\lambda\vert x - vt\vert}\left[\cos\lambda(x-vt) + \sin\lambda(x-vt)\right]$$ where $\lambda = \sqrt[4]{\dfrac{k}{4EI}}$. The net forward displacement per cycle is: $$\Delta_{net} \propto \frac{P^2 \cdot v}{k \cdot EI}$$ **3. Effect of Speed and Load (2 marks):** - **Train speed ($v$):** Higher speed → greater asymmetry in wave → more creep. Creep rate is *directly proportional* to train speed. - **Axle load ($P$):** Higher axle load → deeper deflection → larger asymmetric wave. Creep is proportional to **$P^2$**. **4. Field Implications (1 mark):** This explains why **heavy freight corridors** and **high-speed corridors** exhibit significantly higher creep than light traffic branch lines, and why modern tracks use **higher sleeper density** and **stiffer ballast** to control creep. --- #### **Model Solution — Part (b):** **1. Equipment Required (2 marks):** - Two **creep indicator posts** (concrete pillars with reference marks) — fixed firmly in the ground *outside* the track formation at the two ends of the section being monitored. - Two **rail darts / punch marks** on the rail foot, exactly aligned with the reference marks on the posts. - A **steel measuring tape** (or invar tape) accurate to 1 mm. - **Thermometer** for recording rail temperature. **2. Procedure (3 marks):** | Step | Action | |---|---| | 1 | Fix two creep posts at the ends of the monitoring section, on either side of the track. | | 2 | Mark two reference points (darts/punch marks) on the rail foot aligned with the post references. | | 3 | Take initial reading $R_1$ between the two posts with the steel tape. Record date, time, and rail temperature. | | 4 | After 7–14 days, repeat the reading $R_2$ at the same locations and same temperature band. | | 5 | Compute creep $\Delta L = R_2 - R_1$. The sign indicates direction (positive = forward creep, negative = backward creep). | **3. Calculation (1 mark):** $$C = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0 \cdot t} \text{ in mm/m/day}$$ **4. Limit (1 mark):** Compare with **allowable limit of 150 mm**. If exceeded, the track must be reset and anti-creepers installed. --- ### **Question B1: Choice B** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **(a)** List the **causes and effects of creep**. Explain the **drag theory** in detail. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe with sketches **various types of anti-creepers** used to prevent creep. State the conditions under which the density of anti-creepers is increased. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Part (a):** **1. Causes of Creep (2 marks):** - Wave action of the rail under moving wheel loads. - Impact at rail joints (Percussion). - Friction between wheel and rail during acceleration and braking. - Pumping action of sleepers under heavy loads. - Improper expansion gap at joints. - Inadequate sleeper density or poor ballast packing. - Steep gradients and sharp curves intensify creep. **2. Effects of Creep (2 marks):** - Sleeper skewing and loss of spacing. - Distortion of expansion gaps at joints → buckling or pull-apart. - Misalignment of switches and crossings. - Loss of track geometry (gauge, alignment, cross-level). - Reduction of lateral resistance. **3. Drag Theory (3 marks):** According to the **drag theory** (also called the *friction theory*), creep is generated as the **reaction to wheel slip** during traction and braking events. **Case 1 — Acceleration:** When a locomotive accelerates from rest, the driving wheels rotate and tend to slip **backward** relative to the rail surface. The resulting friction force on the rail acts **forward** (Newton's third law). This forward frictional drag *pushes the rail longitudinally*, causing creep in the direction of traffic. **Case 2 — Braking:** When the train brakes, braking wheels tend to lock and skid. They slip **forward** relative to the rail. The friction force on the rail acts **backward**, causing creep *against* the direction of traffic. **Case 3 — Steady Running:** At constant speed, no significant slip occurs; creep from drag theory is negligible. Wave theory dominates in this regime. **Direction of Creep (summary):** - Acceleration zones (e.g., just after station start) → forward creep. - Braking zones (e.g., approach to signals, down-gradient) → backward creep. - This explains observed backward creep near station throats. --- #### **Model Solution — Part (b):** **1. Need for Anti-Creepers (1 mark):** Anti-creepers are devices that mechanically anchor the rail to the sleeper, increasing the longitudinal restraint and preventing progressive rail movement. **2. Types of Anti-Creepers (5 marks):** **Type 1: Bearing Plate Anchors** - A bearing plate is fixed under the rail foot on wooden sleepers. - The plate has *anchor lugs / claws* on its underside that bite into the sleeper. - Provides both vertical load distribution and longitudinal restraint. **Type 2: Ellis Anti-Creeper** - A patented spring-steel anchor. - Wraps around the rail foot and is wedged against the sleeper side. - Provides firm grip without damaging the rail. - Most commonly used on wooden sleeper tracks. **Type 3: Spring-Type Anti-Creeper (Fair Type)** - A heavy spring-steel component clamped to the rail base. - Uses elastic restoring force to oppose rail movement. **Type 4: Pandrol / Vossloh / Elastic Fastenings (Modern)** - On concrete sleeper tracks, the elastic clip itself (e.g., Pandrol clip) anchors the rail. - Combined with **welded rails**, creep is virtually eliminated. **Type 5: Steel Sleepers** - Steel sleepers have an inherent inverted-bowl shape that grips the rail base. - Built-in anti-creep property; no separate device needed. **3. Conditions Requiring Increased Anti-Creeper Density (1 mark):** - Track on **steep gradients** (more than 1 in 100). - Track on **sharp curves** (radius < 600 m). - Track sections in **tunnel approaches** or near **waterlogged formations**. - **Approaches to stations / signal stops** (heavy braking zones). - **Heavy haul freight corridors**. Generally, on plain tangent track, anti-creepers are placed on **alternate sleepers** (≈22 per 13 m rail). On the above critical sections, they are placed on **every sleeper** (≈44 per 13 m rail). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] **Common Mark-Loss Traps (Read Carefully!)** > 1. **Confusing creep with buckling:** Creep is *longitudinal movement* of rails; buckling is *lateral displacement due to thermal stress*. Both are related but distinct phenomena. > 2. **Mixing up theories' directions:** Wave and Percussion theories predict *forward* creep only. Drag theory predicts *both directions*. Writing "drag theory causes only forward creep" loses marks. > 3. **Forgetting numerical units:** Always quote creep in **mm/m/day** or **mm/day**. Bare numbers without units = 1 mark deducted. > 4. **Wrong expansion gap formula:** Many students write $E = L \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ but forget to mention that the result must be in mm and $L$ in metres. > 5. **Skipping the "field justification" step:** A theory answer without field examples (e.g., "drag theory explains backward creep near station throats") loses half marks. > 6. **Diagrams:** In the ESE exam, drawing the **three theories as labelled flowcharts** (not vague sketches) earns full credit. > 7. **Anti-creeper naming:** Writing "Anchor" without specifying the *type* (Ellis / Fair / Bearing plate) is incomplete. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] **LAST-MINUTE KTU REVISION BULLETS — MEMORISE THESE** - ✅ **Rail creep** = longitudinal rail movement relative to sleepers. - ✅ **Allowable creep** = **150 mm** (BG mainline, IRS). - ✅ **Three theories** = Wave Motion + Percussion + Drag. - ✅ **Wave theory** is most accepted; creep $\propto \dfrac{P^2 \cdot v}{k \cdot EI}$. - ✅ **Percussion theory** applies only at **joints**; eliminated by LWR. - ✅ **Drag theory** explains *backward* creep in braking zones. - ✅ **Measurement** = creep posts + rail darts + steel tape; reading every 7–14 days. - ✅ **Creep rate unit** = **mm/m/day**. - ✅ **Anti-creeper quantity** = one per alternate sleeper = ~22 per 13 m rail (plain track). - ✅ **Anti-creeper quantity** = one per sleeper = ~44 per 13 m rail (curves/gradients). - ✅ **Types of anti-creepers** = Bearing plate anchor, Ellis, Fair (spring), Pandrol clip, Steel sleeper. - ✅ **Effects of creep** = sleeper skew, joint gap distortion, switch misalignment, ballast disturbance, buckling risk. - ✅ **Prevention methods** = anti-creepers + LWR + good ballast + tight fastenings + proper expansion gap. - ✅ **Coefficient of thermal expansion of steel** $\alpha = 11.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ per } °C$. - ✅ **Young's modulus of rail steel** $E = 2.1 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$. - ✅ **Creep formula**: $C = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0 \cdot t}$. - ✅ **Horizontal kick at joint**: $F_h = F_v \tan\alpha$. - ✅ **Anti-creeper always anchors rail to sleeper**, not sleeper to ballast (that is a separate anchor). > **END OF MODULE 1 NOTES — THEORIES, MEASUREMENT & PREVENTION OF CREEP**

topics.title: Rail Fixtures and Fastenings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET754 - RAILWAY, PORT AND HARBOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Rail Fixtures and Fastenings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rail Fixtures and Fastenings** are the essential structural accessories used in a railway track system to **rigidly connect the rails to the sleepers**, **maintain the correct gauge**, **preserve the alignment of the track**, and **transfer the wheel loads efficiently** from the rail to the sleeper and subsequently to the ballast.

In the KTU 2024 framework, the rail-track assembly is treated as a **composite load-bearing system** in which the fastening mechanism acts as the **mechanical interface (load transfer medium)** between the rolling stock (wheels) and the supporting ballast subgrade.

### 1.1.1 Primary Functional Objectives

The fixtures and fastenings must collectively achieve **five critical engineering objectives**:

1. **Gauge Maintenance:** Hold the two rails at a precise distance (for Indian Broad Gauge, BG = **1676 mm**).
2. **Load Transfer:** Transmit vertical, lateral, and longitudinal wheel forces to the sleepers.
3. **Rail Anchoring:** Prevent the rail from creeping (longitudinal movement) due to braking/traction forces.
4. **Thermal Stress Accommodation:** Permit controlled expansion/contraction under temperature variations.
5. **Vibration Dampening:** Absorb dynamic impact loads and reduce fatigue failure of components.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Surgical Stitch" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a railway track as a human spine, and the rails as the long bones. The **fish plates** are like the **surgical stitches** that join two broken bone segments (rail joints), while the **spikes and keys** are like the **ligaments and tendons** that anchor the bone (rail) to the muscle (sleeper). Just as ligaments must be flexible enough to allow movement yet strong enough to hold bones in place, fastenings must be rigid enough to maintain gauge but resilient enough to absorb dynamic wheel impacts.

Imagine you are assembling a **wooden bridge from popsicle sticks**:
- The **rails** are the longitudinal load-bearing members.
- The **sleepers** are the transverse supports.
- The **fastenings** are the **glue, nails, and screws** that keep everything from falling apart when someone walks (train) over it.

Without proper fastenings, the track would behave like a loose, rattling structure — exactly what happens in poorly maintained railway lines where you hear the loud "clickety-clack" at every joint.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Rail Fixtures and Fastenings

The complete family of track fittings can be systematically classified into the following engineering categories:

### 1.3.1 Rail Joint Fixtures (Used at Rail Ends)
| Component | Primary Function |
|---|---|
| **Fish Plate (Splice Bar / Angle Bar)** | Joins two consecutive rail ends |
| **Fish Bolts** | Tighten fish plates against the rail |
| **Fishing Surfaces** | Machined contact zones on rail/web |

### 1.3.2 Rail-to-Sleeper Fastenings
| Component | Primary Function |
|---|---|
| **Spikes (Dog, Screw, Round, Elastic)** | Anchor rail to wooden sleeper |
| **Screw Spikes** | Used with concrete sleepers (elastic fastenings) |
| **Hook Bolts** | Used in conjunction with chairs |

### 1.3.3 Sleepers-to-Rail Interfacing Devices
| Component | Primary Function |
|---|---|
| **Chairs (CI / Steel)** | Provide seating for rail on sleeper |
| **Keys (Wooden, Metal, Spring)** | Lock rail into the chair |
| **Bearing Plates / Tie Plates** | Distribute load over larger sleeper area |
| **Slide Chairs** | Accommodate rail expansion at joints |

### 1.3.4 Anti-Creep Devices
| Component | Primary Function |
|---|---|
| **Rail Anchors (Anti-creepers)** | Prevent longitudinal rail movement |
| **Spring Washers / Lock Washers** | Maintain bolt tension under vibration |

---

## 1.4 Standard Track Gauge Values (India)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU numerical problems, the following gauge values are **standardized and must be memorized**:
> - **Broad Gauge (BG):** **1676 mm** (5 ft 6 in) — Indian Railways standard
> - **Meter Gauge (MG):** **1000 mm** (3 ft 3⅜ in)
> - **Narrow Gauge (NG):** **762 mm** (2 ft 6 in) and **610 mm** (2 ft 0 in)
> - **Standard Gauge (Worldwide):** **1435 mm** (4 ft 8½ in)

These values are critical for any KTU question on track geometry, super-elevation, or gauge widening on curves.

---

## 1.5 Track Structure Hierarchy — Visual Overview

```
            [Wheel Flange + Tread]            ← Wheel-rail contact
                       ↓ contact patch (~150 mm²)
            ═══════════════════════════        ← RAIL (52/60/90 kg/m)
                       ↓
            [Fish Plates at Joints]            ← Rail-to-Rail connection
                       ↓
            [Spikes / Elastic Fastenings]      ← Rail-to-Sleeper connection
                       ↓
            [Sleeper (Wooden/Concrete/Steel)]  ← Load distribution
                       ↓
            [Bearing Plate (optional)]         ← Load spreading
                       ↓
            [Ballast (50 mm crushed stone)]    ← Load to subgrade
                       ↓
            [Subgrade (Compacted Earth)]       ← Foundation
```

This **top-down load path** is the foundational concept upon which all KTU problems on track design and fastening mechanics are based.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fish Plate (Splice Bar) — Detailed Engineering Analysis

### 2.1.1 Definition and Material Composition

> [!NOTE]
> A **Fish Plate** is a flat or shaped metal plate (typically **mild steel** or **medium carbon steel**) bolted to the web and foot of two adjoining rail ends to maintain continuity of the rail line and transfer wheel loads from one rail to the next at the joint.

The fish plate derives its name from the **herring-bone fishing pattern** of early 19th-century joint designs. In KTU parlance, it is also called a **splice bar** or **angle bar**.

### 2.1.2 Geometric Cross-Section

Modern fish plates are **sectioned to match the rail profile**, typically having a **C-shape or hook-and-eye cross-section** that grips the underside of the rail foot and the top of the rail head.

```
   ┌─────────────────┐
   │  RAIL HEAD      │ ← Top bearing surface
   ├─────────────────┤
   │  RAIL WEB       │ ← Vertical web
   ├─────────────────┤
   │  RAIL FOOT      │ ← Bottom flange
   └─────────────────┘
   [FISH PLATE TOP HOOK] — grips head underside
   [FISH PLATE BOTTOM HOOK] — grips foot top
   [FISH PLATE BOLT HOLES] — 4 to 6 holes per pair
```

### 2.1.3 Types of Fish Plates

| Type | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Flat Fish Plate (Common)** | Two flat plates, one on each side of web | Older tracks, light traffic |
| **Angle Fish Plate (Standard)** | Top and bottom hooks to engage rail foot/head | BG Mainline tracks (most common) |
| **Joggled Fish Plate** | Offset section for heavily worn rails | Re-rail works |
| **Compromise Fish Plate** | Different rail sections on each side | Rail section transitions |
| **Insulated Fish Plate** | Nylon/fiber bushings, electrically non-conductive | Track circuit / signaling zones |
| **Bonded Fish Plate** | Welded metallic continuity bond | Electrified sections (avoids resistance) |

### 2.1.4 Fish Plate Design Principles (KTU Analytical Framework)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The fish plate must satisfy **four mechanical design requirements** simultaneously:
> 1. **Stiffness** to resist bending under wheel load
> 2. **Strength** to transfer shear across the joint
> 3. **Toughness** to absorb impact at the rail joint gap
> 4. **Fatigue resistance** under 10⁸ to 10⁹ load cycles per year

### 2.1.5 Load Transfer Mechanism at a Rail Joint

The fish plate works in **flexural bending** (not direct tension/compression). When a wheel approaches the joint:
1. The approaching rail deflects elastically (cantilever action).
2. The wheel load is transferred through the **fish plate in bending and shear** to the trailing rail.
3. The fish plate thus acts as a **suspension bridge** spanning the joint gap.

---

## 2.2 Spikes — Engineering Analysis of Rail-to-Sleeper Fastening

### 2.2.1 The Dog Spike (Most Common on Wooden Sleepers)

> [!NOTE]
> A **Dog Spike** is a large, square-bodied, chisel-pointed spike with an offset head, so named because the head resembles a dog's head when viewed in profile.

**Key Specifications (KTU Standard):**
- **Length:** **127 mm (5 inches)** for standard 52 kg/m rail
- **Cross-section:** Square, **22 mm × 22 mm**
- **Material:** Mild steel (Grade Fe 410 / IS 2062)
- **Head shape:** Asymmetric, "L" or "J" shaped
- **Ultimate tensile strength:** ≥ **410 MPa**
- **Driving resistance:** **3 to 5 kN** to drive, **15 to 25 kN** to extract

**Theoretical Holding Capacity:**
A dog spike derives its holding power from **three mechanisms**:
1. **Friction** between spike shank and wood fibers
