| Sign | Theoretical > Actual | Actual > Theoretical (for slow train) |
| Maximum (BG) | 100 mm | 75 mm |
| Occurs when | Train speed > design speed (or balance speed) | Train speed < design speed (e.g., goods train) |
| Effect | Higher lateral force on outer rail | Higher lateral force on inner rail |

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - Module 2 Pattern with Internal Choice]**

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** **[7 marks]** Derive an expression for the equilibrium super elevation to be provided on a Broad Gauge curved track for a design speed of $V$ km/hr on a curve of radius $R$ meters. Clearly state the assumptions used.

**(b)** **[7 marks]** A BG curve of 3° has a sanctioned speed of 75 km/h for fast trains and 35 km/h for slow goods trains. Calculate: (i) equilibrium cant for fast trains, (ii) actual cant to be provided, (iii) cant deficiency, and (iv) cant excess. Check whether the design conforms to Indian Railway codal provisions.

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

> **Assumptions (1 mark):**
> 1. Track is canted by angle $\theta$ such that $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta \approx \dfrac{e}{G}$
> 2. Vehicle moves at constant speed $V$ on a circular curve of radius $R$
> 3. Centrifugal force acts horizontally through the C.G. of the vehicle
> 4. Gauge $G = 1676$ mm for BG
> 5. Standard gravity $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$

**Step 1 — Free Body and Force Identification (2 marks):**

Three forces act on the vehicle:
- Weight $W$ vertically downward
- Centrifugal force $F_c = \dfrac{W V^2}{g R}$ horizontally outward
- Normal reaction $N$ perpendicular to the canted track

**Step 2 — Force Resolution (2 marks):**

The normal reaction $N$ has:
- Vertical component: $N \cos\theta$ balancing $W$ → $N\cos\theta = W$
- Horizontal component: $N \sin\theta$ balancing $F_c$ → $N\sin\theta = \dfrac{WV^2}{gR}$

**Step 3 — Derivation of Cant Equation (2 marks):**

Dividing the two equations:

$$\tan\theta = \frac{V^2}{gR}$$

Substituting $\tan\theta = \dfrac{e}{G}$:

$$\frac{e}{G} = \frac{V^2}{gR}$$

$$e = \frac{G V^2}{gR}$$

Converting $V$ from km/h to m/s (multiplying by $\dfrac{5}{18}$):

$$e = \frac{G \cdot V^2 \cdot \left(\frac{5}{18}\right)^2}{g R} = \frac{G V^2}{g R \cdot 12.96}$$

Using $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$:

$$\boxed{e = \frac{G \cdot V^2}{127 R}}$$

where $e$ and $G$ are in meters, $V$ in km/h, $R$ in meters.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Degree of curve $D = 3°$, so $R = \dfrac{1750}{3} = 583.33\ \text{m}$
- Speed of fast train $V_f = 75\ \text{km/h}$
- Speed of slow train $V_s = 35\ \text{km/h}$
- $G = 1.676\ \text{m}$

**Step 1 — Equilibrium cant for fast train (2 marks):**

$$e_{th,f} = \frac{1.676 \times (75)^2}{127 \times 583.33}$$

$$e_{th,f} = \frac{1.676 \times 5625}{74083.91} = \frac{9426.75}{74083.91} = 0.1273\ \text{m} = 127.3\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 2 — Actual cant to be provided (2 marks):**

Since $e_{th,f} = 127.3\ \text{mm} < 184\ \text{mm}$ (max cant), the actual cant is rounded to the nearest 5 mm:

$$e_{actual} = 125\ \text{mm or 130\ \text{mm}$$

Adopting $e_{actual} = 125\ \text{mm} = 0.125\ \text{m}$ (to keep cant deficiency within limits and round to multiple of 5)

> Note: Indian Railways practice is to round to the nearest 5 mm. Both 125 and 130 are valid choices; let's use $e_{actual} = 125\ \text{mm}$.

**Step 3 — Cant deficiency (1.5 marks):**

$$C_d = e_{th,f} - e_{actual} = 127.3 - 125 = 2.3\ \text{mm} \approx 2.5\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 4 — Cant excess for slow train (1.5 marks):**

Theoretical cant for slow train:

$$e_{th,s} = \frac{1.676 \times (35)^2}{127 \times 583.33} = \frac{1.676 \times 1225}{74083.91} = \frac{2053.1}{74083.91} = 0.0277\ \text{m} = 27.7\ \text{mm}$$

$$C_e = e_{actual} - e_{th,s} = 125 - 27.7 = 97.3\ \text{mm}$$

> **Uh oh! $C_e = 97.3\ \text{mm}$ exceeds 75 mm!**

**Step 5 — Redesign (to satisfy cant excess limit):**

We need $C_e \leq 75\ \text{mm}$, so:

$$e_{actual} \leq e_{th,s} + 75 = 27.7 + 75 = 102.7\ \text{mm}$$

Let us adopt $e_{actual} = 100\ \text{mm}$ (rounded to 5 mm).

**Recompute cant deficiency:**

$$C_d = 127.3 - 100 = 27.3\ \text{mm} \leq 100\ \text{mm} \checkmark$$

**Final design:**
- Actual cant = **100 mm**
- Cant deficiency = **27.3 mm** ✓
- Cant excess = **72.3 mm** ✓

Both limits are satisfied, so the design is acceptable.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** **[7 marks]** Explain the concept of negative super elevation. Under what circumstances is it provided, and what are its limitations on Indian Railways?

**(b)** **[7 marks]** A 5° curve on a BG track is designed for a maximum speed of 65 km/h. If a wind pressure of 60 kg/m² acts on a vehicle of frontal area 8 m² and weight 80 tonnes, calculate: (i) the theoretical equilibrium cant, (ii) the equivalent cant due to wind, and (iii) the net cant to be provided on the track.

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Negative Super Elevation (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):** Negative super elevation is the condition where the **inner rail is higher than the outer rail**, i.e., the cant is provided in a direction opposite to the normal direction of super elevation for that curve. It is generally a **transient** condition observed during the run-off or run-in of cant along the transition length.

**When is it provided (2 marks):**

1. **Reverse Curves (S-Curves):** When two curves of opposite sense follow one another with a very short tangent between them, the cant of the first curve must be brought down to zero and then built up for the second curve. During this transition, the inner rail for the first curve becomes the outer rail for the second curve, and vice versa, resulting in negative super elevation.

2. **Speed Differential on Sharp Curves:** When the theoretical cant required for fast trains is more than the maximum allowable cant, and a slow goods train has to be run, negative cant may be observed for the slow train.

3. **Run-off Length on Transition Curves:** During the introduction of cant, before reaching the circular curve, the cant is gradually raised; in the initial portion, the rate of cant change may cause the inner rail of the incoming direction to be relatively higher.

**Limitations on Indian Railways (3 marks):**

1. **Maximum negative cant** is generally restricted to **75 mm** on Indian Railways for goods and mixed traffic lines.
2. On **Group A routes** (high-speed routes), negative super elevation is avoided by **increasing the transition length** instead.
3. Negative cant is **not intentionally designed** — it is a consequence of geometry and operational constraints.
4. Prolonged negative cant causes **unequal wear** on rails, **lateral instability** of the train, and **passenger discomfort**.
5. Modern practice is to provide **separate transition curves** for each curve with adequate tangent length to avoid negative super elevation zones.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical with Wind Effect (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $D = 5°$, so $R = \dfrac{1750}{5} = 350\ \text{m}$
- $V = 65\ \text{km/h}$
- $G = 1.676\ \text{m}$
- Wind pressure $P = 60\ \text{kg/m}^2$
- Frontal area $A = 8\ \text{m}^2$
- Weight $W = 80\ \text{tonnes} = 80{,}000\ \text{kg}$

**Step 1 — Theoretical equilibrium cant (3 marks):**

$$e_{th} = \frac{G \cdot V^2}{127 \cdot R}$$

$$e_{th} = \frac{1.676 \times (65)^2}{127 \times 350} = \frac{1.676 \times 4225}{44450}$$

$$e_{th} = \frac{7081.7}{44450} = 0.1593\ \text{m} = 159.3\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 2 — Equivalent cant due to wind (2 marks):**

Wind force $F_w = P \times A = 60 \times 8 = 480\ \text{kg}$

Equivalent cant due to wind:

$$e_w = \frac{F_w \times G}{W} = \frac{480 \times 1.676}{80000} = \frac{804.48}{80000} = 0.01006\ \text{m} \approx 10\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 3 — Net cant to be provided (2 marks):**

$$e_{net} = e_{th} - e_w = 159.3 - 10 = 149.3\ \text{mm}$$

Since this is less than 184 mm (max cant), we can provide **$e_{net} = 150\ \text{mm}$** (rounded to nearest 5 mm).

> **Final answer:** Provide a super elevation of **150 mm** on the curve.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency in Cant Formula (2-3 marks lost):** Students often mix up units — using $G$ in mm with $V$ in m/s, or vice versa. Always check: if $G$ is in mm, $e$ comes in mm; if $G$ is in m, $e$ comes in m. **Use $e = G V^2 / 127 R$** consistently with paired units.
> 2. **Forgetting the Cant Excess Check (1-2 marks lost):** Most students check cant deficiency but forget cant excess. Always verify BOTH $C_d \leq 100$ mm and $C_e \leq 75$ mm. If cant excess fails, reduce the actual cant.
> 3. **Using Degree Instead of Radius (1-2 marks lost):** Standard Indian Railway formula: $R = 1750 / D$ (where $D$ is degree of curve). Some students use $R = 1719/D$ (chord definition) — know which one your examiner expects. **KTU generally follows $R = 1750/D$**.
> 4. **Not Rounding to 5 mm:** Indian Railways practice rounds cant to the nearest 5 mm. Examiners expect this in design problems.
> 5. **Missing the Wind Effect:** When wind is mentioned, students often forget to subtract equivalent cant due to wind. Always check the problem statement carefully.
> 6. **Cant Transition Curve Length:** Students often write the formula for transition curve length but forget to mention that this is a separate calculation, not part of super elevation. Keep the two topics distinct.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist — Super Elevation**

### Key Definitions
- **Super Elevation (Cant, e):** Difference in height between outer and inner rail on a curve. Outer rail is raised.
- **Equilibrium Cant:** Cant required for balance of centrifugal force at a given speed.
- **Cant Deficiency (Cd):** When actual cant is less than equilibrium cant (fast train case).
- **Cant Excess (Ce):** When actual cant is more than equilibrium cant (slow train case).
- **Negative Super Elevation:** Inner rail higher than outer rail; transitional, not intentionally designed.

### Critical Numerical Limits (Indian Railways, BG)
- **Maximum Cant:** 184 mm
- **Maximum Cant Deficiency:** 100 mm (Group A), 75 mm (others)
- **Maximum Cant Excess:** 75 mm

### Essential Formula
- **$e = \dfrac{G V^2}{127 R}$** — with $G$ and $e$ in same units, $V$ in km/h, $R$ in meters
- Constant **127** comes from $g \cdot 12.96$ where $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$
- For meter gauge, $G = 1.0$ m

### Design Procedure
1. Compute theoretical cant $e_{th}$ from given speed and radius
2. If $e_{th} > 184$ mm → restrict speed
3. Round to nearest 5 mm to get $e_{actual}$
4. Calculate $C_d = e_{th,f} - e_{actual}$; ensure $\leq 100$ mm
5. Calculate $C_e = e_{actual} - e_{th,s}$; ensure $\leq 75$ mm
6. If cant excess fails, reduce $e_{actual}$ to satisfy $C_e \leq 75$ mm
7. Check final values against all codal limits

### Common Assumptions
- $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta \approx e/G$ (small angle)
- $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$
- Centrifugal force acts through C.G.
- $R = 1750/D$ (IR standard, arc definition)

### Real-World Applications
- **High-Speed Rail:** Mumbai-Ahmedabad Bullet Train (320 km/h design)
- **Metro Rail:** Cant-deficient design with restrictions for sharp curves
- **Conventional IR:** Mixed traffic speed management
- **Tramways:** Higher cant permitted due to slow speeds

### Last-Minute Tips for KTU Exam
- Always state the **assumptions** at the start of derivations
- Use **labeled diagrams** (sketch the FBD) to fetch full marks
- Mention **codal provisions** in design problems
- For 14-mark problems, structure the answer in **clear sub-parts**
- Always end with a **concluding statement** confirming whether the design is safe
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cant Deficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET754 - RAILWAY, PORT AND HARBOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cant Deficiency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cant Deficiency (Cd):** Cant deficiency is the amount by which the **actual cant (superelevation) provided** on a curved railway track falls **short of the equilibrium cant** required to exactly counteract the centrifugal force generated when a train negotiates the curve at its **actual (running) speed**.

Mathematically, when the actual speed $V$ of a train on a curve is **greater** than the equilibrium speed, the cant provided on the curve is less than the cant that would have been required to perfectly balance the centrifugal force. This difference is termed as **cant deficiency**.

$$\boxed{C_d = C_{eq(at\ V)} - C_{actual}}$$

Cant deficiency occurs only when $V_{actual} > V_{eq}$, and if $V_{actual} < V_{eq}$, the situation is called **Cant Excess (Ce)**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **riding a bicycle** on a flat curved road. Your body naturally leans **inward** (towards the centre of the curve) to balance the outward push you feel — this is your body's instinctive "tilt" similar to the railway's cant.

Now suppose:
- The road is **banked** (tilted) at exactly the angle needed for a comfortable 30 km/h ride — this is the **equilibrium cant**.
- But you decide to **speed up to 50 km/h** — now the road tilt is **insufficient** for that speed; you feel pushed harder to the outside. The **"extra push" you feel** is analogous to **cant deficiency**.

If instead you **slow down to 15 km/h**, the road is **over-tilted** for your slow speed, and you would slide towards the **inner side**. That sensation is analogous to **cant excess**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Cant deficiency is NOT a flaw in design — it is a **deliberately allowed margin** so that fast passenger trains and slow freight trains can safely share the same curved track using a single, fixed cant value.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics (Indian Railways / Broad Gauge)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Permissible Limits (Broad Gauge BG – 1676 mm):
> - **Maximum Cant (Cmax)** = **184 mm**
> - **Maximum Cant Deficiency (Cd, max)** = **100 mm**
> - **Maximum Cant Excess (Ce, max)** = **75 mm**

These limits are mandated by the **Ministry of Railways (India)** in the *Permanent Way Manual* to ensure safety, passenger comfort, and reduced wear on rails.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Cant Deficiency Exists — The Underlying Logic

On any curved track, the cant is **designed only once** (at the time of construction) and is **fixed** for all trains. However, the track is shared by:
- **Fast passenger trains** (e.g., Rajdhani, Shatabdi — speeds up to 130–160 km/h)
- **Slow freight trains** (e.g., goods rakes — speeds 30–50 km/h)
- **Medium-speed mail/express trains** (60–80 km/h)

A single cant cannot perfectly balance centrifugal force for **all** these varying speeds. Hence, the designer:
- Computes a **weighted mean speed** of all trains using the curve.
- Sets the **actual cant** for this mean speed.
- Allows a **tolerance band**:
  - **Upper side** = Cant deficiency (for faster trains)
  - **Lower side** = Cant excess (for slower trains)

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Flow of Cant Design

1. **Determine the radius of curvature** $R$ of the curve (fixed geometric parameter).
2. **Survey the traffic pattern** — number of trains $n_i$ running at speeds $V_i$.
3. **Compute the weighted mean square speed**:
$$V_{mean}^2 = \frac{\sum (V_i^2 \cdot n_i)}{\sum n_i}$$
4. **Calculate the equilibrium cant for mean speed** — this becomes the **actual cant** $C$:
$$C = C_{eq} = \frac{G \cdot V_{mean}^2}{127 \cdot R}$$
5. **Check cant deficiency for fastest train** (speed $V_{max}$):
$$C_d = \frac{G \cdot V_{max}^2}{127R} - C \leq 100\ \text{mm}$$
6. **Check cant excess for slowest train** (speed $V_{min}$):
$$C_e = C - \frac{G \cdot V_{min}^2}{127R} \leq 75\ \text{mm}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Memorise This Table)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas use: $G$ = gauge (m), $V$ = speed (km/h), $R$ = radius (m), $C$ = actual cant (m), $C_d$ = cant deficiency (m), $C_e$ = cant excess (m).

| # | Formula | Description | Condition / Limit |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $C_{eq} = \dfrac{GV^2}{127R}$ | Equilibrium cant for speed V | Cant that exactly balances centrifugal force |
| 2 | $C = \dfrac{G \cdot V_{mean}^2}{127R}$ | Actual cant provided | Designed for weighted mean speed |
| 3 | $C_d = \dfrac{GV^2}{127R} - C$ | Cant deficiency | When $V > V_{eq}$, $C_d \leq 100$ mm (BG) |
| 4 | $C_e = C - \dfrac{GV^2}{127R}$ | Cant excess | When $V < V_{eq}$, $C_e \leq 75$ mm (BG) |
| 5 | $V_{eq} = \sqrt{\dfrac{127 \cdot R \cdot C}{G}}$ | Equilibrium speed | Speed at which cant is exactly balanced |
| 6 | $V_{mean} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum V_i^2 n_i}{\sum n_i}}$ | Weighted mean speed | Used for cant design |
| 7 | $e + C = \dfrac{GV^2}{127R}$ | Effective cant for any speed | Where $e$ is the additional cant needed (deficiency or excess) |

> **Crucial Unit Convention:** $G$ in **metres**, $V$ in **km/h**, $R$ in **metres**, $C/C_d/C_e$ in **metres** (or convert to mm for limits).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mixed-traffic tracks** require careful cant design to handle both high-speed and heavy-haul freight trains.
- **High-speed rail corridors** (e.g., Mumbai–Ahmedabad Bullet Train project) use **reduced cant + minimal cant deficiency** to allow very high speeds (300+ km/h).
- **Mountain railways** (e.g., Nilgiri Mountain Railway) require very careful cant management due to sharp curves combined with steep gradients.
- Cant deficiency directly governs the **Maximum Permissible Speed (MPS)** on any curved section, which is signalled in the **Speed Restriction Boards**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Equilibrium Cant Formula

**Setup:** Consider a vehicle of mass $m$ moving with speed $V$ on a circular curve of radius $R$. The track is superelevated (canted) by an angle $\theta$ so that the outer rail is at a height $C$ above the inner rail.

**Step 1: Identify the forces acting on the vehicle.**
- Centrifugal force (pseudo-force in rotating frame): $F_c = \dfrac{mV^2}{R}$ acting horizontally outward.
- Weight: $W = mg$ acting vertically downward.
- Normal reactions from both rails (combined to a single resultant at the centre of gravity).

**Step 2: Resolve the centrifugal force along the tilted principal axes.**
The superelevation tilts the vehicle by angle $\theta$, where:
$$\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta = \dfrac{C}{G}$$

Resolving $F_c$ along the direction perpendicular to the plane of the track:
$$F_{c,\perp} = \dfrac{mV^2}{R} \cos\theta$$

Resolving weight $mg$ along the same direction (towards the track plane):
$$W_{\perp} = mg \cos\theta$$

**Step 3: For equilibrium (no lateral force on vehicle), the components perpendicular to the track must be balanced — but more importantly, the components ALONG the tilted track plane must balance.**

The component of centrifugal force pushing the vehicle **down the cant (outward along slope)**:
$$F_{c,\parallel} = \dfrac{mV^2}{R} \sin\theta$$

The component of weight pulling the vehicle **into the cant (inward along slope)**:
$$W_{\parallel} = mg \sin\theta$$

**Step 4: For equilibrium cant, set these components equal:**
$$\dfrac{mV^2}{R} \cos\theta = mg \sin\theta$$

$$V^2 = gR \tan\theta = gR \cdot \dfrac{C}{G}$$

$$C = \dfrac{GV^2}{gR}$$

**Step 5: Substitute numerical values for practical use.**
$g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$, and we need to convert $V$ from km/h to m/s: $V_{m/s} = \dfrac{V_{km/h} \times 5}{18}$

$$C = \dfrac{G \cdot (5V/18)^2}{9.81 \cdot R} = \dfrac{G \cdot 25V^2}{9.81 \cdot 324 \cdot R} = \dfrac{GV^2}{127.16R} \approx \dfrac{GV^2}{127R}$$

$$\boxed{C_{eq} = \dfrac{GV^2}{127R}}$$

This is the **fundamental equilibrium cant equation** universally used in railway engineering.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Cant Deficiency Expression

**Step 1:** Let the actual cant provided on the curve be $C$ (designed for mean speed $V_{mean}$).
$$C = \dfrac{G \cdot V_{mean}^2}{127R}$$

**Step 2:** Now consider a faster train running at speed $V > V_{mean}$. The equilibrium cant needed for this speed is:
$$C_{eq,fast} = \dfrac{GV^2}{127R}$$

**Step 3:** Since $V > V_{mean}$, we have $C_{eq,fast} > C$. The shortfall is the cant deficiency:
$$C_d = C_{eq,fast} - C = \dfrac{GV^2}{127R} - \dfrac{G \cdot V_{mean}^2}{127R}$$

$$\boxed{C_d = \dfrac{G}{127R}\left(V^2 - V_{mean}^2\right)}$$

**Step 4:** The total effective cant acting on a fast train (for safety analysis) is:
$$C_{effective} = C + C_d = \dfrac{GV^2}{127R}$$

This represents the **theoretical cant** that would be required if the train were to travel at speed $V$ with zero net lateral force.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Maximum Permissible Speed from Cant Deficiency

**Step 1:** Rearrange the cant deficiency equation to solve for $V$:
$$C_d = \dfrac{GV^2}{127R} - C$$

$$V^2 = \dfrac{127R(C + C_d)}{G}$$

$$V = \sqrt{\dfrac{127R(C + C_d)}{G}}$$

**Step 2:** For safety, use the **maximum permissible cant deficiency** (100 mm for BG):
$$V_{max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{127R(C + C_{d,max})}{G}}$$

This is the **Maximum Permissible Speed (MPS)** on the curve, which forms the basis of all speed restriction signage.

---

### 3.4 Solved Analytical Example (Board-Exam Standard)

**Problem:** A 4° curve on BG track has an actual cant of 100 mm. If the speed of the fastest train is 80 km/h, calculate:
1. Equilibrium cant
2. Cant deficiency
3. Maximum permissible speed (using $C_{d,max} = 100$ mm)

**Given:** $G = 1.676$ m, $R = \dfrac{1750}{D} = \dfrac{1750}{4} = 437.5$ m, $C = 100$ mm = $0.1$ m, $V = 80$ km/h.

**Solution — Part (a): Equilibrium Cant**
$$C_{eq} = \dfrac{GV^2}{127R} = \dfrac{1.676 \times (80)^2}{127 \times 437.5}$$

$$= \dfrac{1.676 \times 6400}{55562.5} = \dfrac{10726.4}{55562.5} = 0.1931\ \text{m} = 193.1\ \text{mm}$$

**Solution — Part (b): Cant Deficiency**
$$C_d = C_{eq} - C = 193.1 - 100 = 93.1\ \text{mm}$$

Since $93.1 < 100$ mm, the design is **safe** and the train can run at 80 km/h.

**Solution — Part (c): Maximum Permissible Speed**
$$V_{max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{127R(C + C_{d,max})}{G}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{127 \times 437.5 \times (0.1 + 0.1)}{1.676}}$$

$$= \sqrt{\dfrac{127 \times 437.5 \times 0.2}{1.676}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{11112.5}{1.676}} = \sqrt{6630.37} \approx 81.4\ \text{km/h}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cant Deficiency Concept Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Train Enters Curved Track] --> B{V_actual vs V_equilibrium}
    B -- "V_actual = V_eq" --> C[Perfect Balance - No Deficiency or Excess]
    B -- "V_actual > V_eq" --> D[Faster Train Scenario]
    B -- "V_actual < V_eq" --> E[Slower Train Scenario]
    D --> F["Cant Deficiency C_d exists"]
    F --> G["C_d = GV²/127R - C"]
    G --> H{"C_d ≤ 100 mm?"}
    H -- "Yes" --> I[Safe - Train Permitted]
    H -- "No" --> J[Speed Restriction Required]
    E --> K["Cant Excess C_e exists"]
    K --> L["C_e = C - GV²/127R"]
    L --> M{"C_e ≤ 75 mm?"}
    M -- "Yes" --> N[Safe - Train Permitted]
    M -- "No" --> O[Speed Restriction Required]
```

### 4.2 Cant Geometry on Curved Track (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CurveGeometry [Cross Section of Curved Track]
        A1[Inner Rail] --> A2[Gauge Distance G]
        A2 --> A3[Outer Rail Elevated by Cant C]
        A3 --> A4[Centrifugal Force F_c outward]
    end
    subgraph ForceBalance [Lateral Force Balance]
        B1[Vehicle Weight mg] --> B2[Component along cant: mg sinθ inward]
        B3[Centrifugal Force mV²/R] --> B4[Component along cant: mV²/R cosθ outward]
    end
    subgraph DesignInputs [Design Inputs]
        C1[Radius R] --> C2[Compute C_eq = GV²/127R]
        C3[Mean Speed V_mean] --> C2
        C2 --> C4[Check C_d ≤ 100 mm]
        C2 --> C5[Check C_e ≤ 75 mm]
    end
```

### 4.3 Speed-Cant Relationship Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Track Design Phase] --> S1[Survey Traffic: Speeds and Frequencies]
    S1 --> S2[Compute V_mean² = ΣVi²ni / Σni]
    S2 --> S3[Compute Actual Cant C = G V_mean² / 127R]
    S3 --> S4{V_max train and V_min train}
    S4 -- V_max --> S5["C_d = GV_max²/127R - C"]
    S4 -- V_min --> S6["C_e = C - GV_min²/127R"]
    S5 --> S7{"C_d ≤ 100 mm and C_e ≤ 75 mm?"}
    S6 --> S7
    S7 -- Yes --> S8[Design Approved - Track Operational]
    S7 -- No --> S9[Revise Cant or Speed Restrictions]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (S0, S1, A1, B1, etc.) to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguards. No reserved keywords or markdown formatting are used inside labels.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define cant deficiency and cant excess. State the maximum permissible values for Broad Gauge as per Indian Railways.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Cant Deficiency (Cd):** The amount by which the actual cant provided on a curve falls short of the equilibrium cant required to exactly balance the centrifugal force at the actual running speed of the train. It occurs when a train runs at a speed higher than the equilibrium speed.
- **Cant Excess (Ce):** The amount by which the actual cant exceeds the equilibrium cant. It occurs when a train runs at a speed lower than the equilibrium speed.
- **Maximum Permissible Values (BG):** $C_{d,max} = 100$ mm; $C_{e,max} = 75$ mm.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Why is cant deficiency deliberately allowed in railway track design? Explain briefly.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Cant deficiency is deliberately allowed because a single curve is shared by trains of widely varying speeds — fast passenger trains and slow freight rakes. A single cant fixed on the track cannot balance centrifugal force for all speeds simultaneously. Allowing a controlled cant deficiency permits faster trains to negotiate the curve safely without reducing the cant, while keeping the design economical and avoiding excessive cant excess for slow trains. The value is restricted to **100 mm (BG)** to ensure passenger comfort and track stability.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

**Q3(A). [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper 2024]**
A 3° curve on a Broad Gauge track has an actual cant of 80 mm. The fastest train runs at 90 km/h and the slowest at 35 km/h. Determine:
**(a)** The equilibrium speed for the provided cant. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The cant deficiency and cant excess for the fastest and slowest trains respectively. Check whether the design is safe as per IR standards. **(7 marks)**

**Given:** $G = 1.676$ m, $D = 3°$, $R = 1750/3 = 583.33$ m, $C = 80$ mm = $0.08$ m, $V_{max} = 90$ km/h, $V_{min} = 35$ km/h.

**Solution — Part (a): Equilibrium Speed**

Equilibrium speed is the speed at which the provided cant exactly equals the equilibrium cant:
$$V_{eq} = \sqrt{\dfrac{127 R C}{G}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{127 \times 583.33 \times 0.08}{1.676}}$$

$$= \sqrt{\dfrac{5926.67}{1.676}} = \sqrt{3536.20} \approx 59.47\ \text{km/h}$$

**Equilibrium Speed ≈ 59.47 km/h**

**Solution — Part (b): Cant Deficiency and Cant Excess**

For fastest train (90 km/h):
$$C_d = \dfrac{G V_{max}^2}{127R} - C = \dfrac{1.676 \times (90)^2}{127 \times 583.33} - 0.08$$

$$= \dfrac{1.676 \times 8100}{74083.33} - 0.08 = \dfrac{13575.6}{74083.33} - 0.08 = 0.1833 - 0.08 = 0.1033\ \text{m}$$

$$\boxed{C_d = 103.3\ \text{mm}}$$

For slowest train (35 km/h):
$$C_e = C - \dfrac{G V_{min}^2}{127R} = 0.08 - \dfrac{1.676 \times (35)^2}{127 \times 583.33}$$

$$= 0.08 - \dfrac{1.676 \times 1225}{74083.33} = 0.08 - \dfrac{2053.1}{74083.33} = 0.08 - 0.0277 = 0.0523\ \text{m}$$

$$\boxed{C_e = 52.3\ \text{mm}}$$

**Safety Check (BG limits):**
- $C_d = 103.3$ mm $> 100$ mm → **NOT SAFE**, speed restriction required OR cant must be increased.
- $C_e = 52.3$ mm $< 75$ mm → **SAFE**.

**Conclusion:** The design fails the cant deficiency criterion. The actual cant must be increased to at least:
$$C_{req} = \dfrac{G V_{max}^2}{127R} - 0.100 = 0.1833 - 0.100 = 0.0833\ \text{m} = 83.3\ \text{mm}$$

So the cant should be raised to at least **84 mm** to make the design compliant.

---

**Q3(B). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Alternative Choice]**
On a BG curve of radius 600 m, the average speed of trains is 65 km/h. If the maximum speeds of fast and slow trains differ by ±20% of the average speed, calculate:
**(a)** The actual cant to be provided. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The maximum permissible speed on the curve, given $C_{d,max} = 100$ mm. **(7 marks)**

**Given:** $G = 1.676$ m, $R = 600$ m, $V_{mean} = 65$ km/h, $V_{max} = 1.20 \times 65 = 78$ km/h, $V_{min} = 0.80 \times 65 = 52$ km/h.

**Solution — Part (a): Actual Cant**

$$C = \dfrac{G \cdot V_{mean}^2}{127R} = \dfrac{1.676 \times (65)^2}{127 \times 600}$$

$$= \dfrac{1.676 \times 4225}{76200} = \dfrac{7080.5}{76200} = 0.0929\ \text{m} \approx 92.9\ \text{mm}$$

**Actual Cant C ≈ 93 mm (rounded to nearest mm)**

**Solution — Part (b): Maximum Permissible Speed**

$$V_{max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{127R(C + C_{d,max})}{G}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{127 \times 600 \times (0.093 + 0.100)}{1.676}}$$

$$= \sqrt{\dfrac{127 \times 600 \times 0.193}{1.676}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{14704.2}{1.676}} = \sqrt{8773.39} \approx 93.67\ \text{km/h}$$

**Maximum Permissible Speed ≈ 93.67 km/h**

**Verification of cant deficiency at $V_{max} = 78$ km/h:**
$$C_d = \dfrac{1.676 \times 78^2}{127 \times 600} - 0.093 = \dfrac{1.676 \times 6084}{76200} - 0.093 = 0.1338 - 0.093 = 0.0408\ \text{m} = 40.8\ \text{mm}$$

Since $40.8 < 100$ mm, the design is safe and the curve can handle the 78 km/h fast train comfortably.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Read Carefully!):**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency:** Forgetting to convert mm to metres (or vice versa) is the **#1 reason** students lose 2–3 marks. Always check units BEFORE substituting into the formula.
> 2. **Radius calculation for degree of curve:** Many students forget that on BG, $R = \dfrac{1750}{D}$ (where D is in degrees), **not** $R = \dfrac{1720}{D}$ (which is for metre gauge).
> 3. **Sign convention confusion:** Cant deficiency is ALWAYS positive when $V > V_{eq}$. Never write a negative value; if your calculation gives a negative number, you have computed cant excess instead — re-check which train speed you used.
> 4. **Mean square speed vs. mean speed:** The cant formula uses $V_{mean}^2 = \dfrac{\sum V_i^2 n_i}{\sum n_i}$, **NOT** $V_{mean} = \dfrac{\sum V_i n_i}{\sum n_i}$. Squaring must happen before averaging, weighted by frequency.
> 5. **Forgetting to verify IR limits:** The "compute" part earns partial credit, but the "check safety against 100 mm / 75 mm" part is where **full marks are awarded**. Always conclude with a safety verdict.
> 6. **Confusing cant deficiency with cant itself:** Cant is the lift of the outer rail; cant deficiency is a *hypothetical* additional cant that would be needed. The outer rail is NOT raised by the cant deficiency amount.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Cant Deficiency**

- **Cant (C):** Actual superelevation provided on the curve (mm) — fixed in the field.
- **Equilibrium Cant ($C_{eq}$):** Theoretical cant to perfectly balance centrifugal force at a given speed.
- **Cant Deficiency ($C_d$):** Shortfall of actual cant when $V_{actual} > V_{eq}$.
- **Cant Excess ($C_e$):** Surplus of actual cant when $V_{actual} < V_{eq}$.
- **Master Formula:** $C_{eq} = \dfrac{GV^2}{127R}$ — **memorise this**; it is the foundation of all curve design.
- **Design Formula:** $C = \dfrac{G \cdot V_{mean}^2}{127R}$, where $V_{mean}^2 = \dfrac{\sum V_i^2 n_i}{\sum n_i}$.
- **BG Standard Limits:** $C_{max} = 184$ mm, $C_{d,max} = 100$ mm, $C_{e,max} = 75$ mm.
- **Equilibrium Speed:** $V_{eq} = \sqrt{\dfrac{127RC}{G}}$ — speed at which cant is perfectly balanced.
- **Maximum Permissible Speed:** $V_{max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{127R(C + C_{d,max})}{G}}$.
- **Key Relationship:** $C + C_d = \dfrac{GV^2}{127R}$ when train runs at speed $V$ (effective cant).
- **Practical Consequence:** Cant deficiency allows fast trains on curves shared with slow freight — economically vital for mixed-traffic routes.
- **Comfort Criterion:** Limiting $C_d$ to 100 mm also controls the **unbalanced lateral acceleration** passengers experience, ensuring ride comfort.
- **Always check:** Unit consistency, radius formula ($R = 1750/D$ for BG), and IR safety limits.
- **Sign Rule:** $C_d > 0$ only when $V > V_{eq}$; otherwise compute $C_e$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Negative Super Elevation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET754 - RAILWAY, PORT AND HARBOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Negative Super Elevation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Negative Super Elevation (also termed as 'Cant Deficiency' or 'Inadequate Cant')** is a condition in railway track geometry where the level of the **outer rail** of a curved track is kept **lower** than (or equal to) the level of the **inner rail**, opposite to the conventional banking principle used in standard curved track design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Standard):** Negative super elevation is said to occur when the actual super elevation provided ($e_{act}$) on a curve is **less than the equilibrium super elevation** ($e_{eq}$) required for a particular speed of train. In extreme operational cases, the inner rail is physically raised *above* the outer rail on the diverging side.

Mathematically, the condition is expressed as:

$$
e_{act} < e_{eq}
$$

And the magnitude of negative super elevation is quantified as:

$$
e_{neg} = e_{eq} - e_{act} \quad \text{(when } e_{eq} > e_{act} \text{)}
$$

Where the equilibrium super elevation is governed by the standard cant formula:

$$
e_{eq} = \frac{G \cdot V^{2}}{127 \cdot R}
$$

The symbols represent:
- $G$ = Gauge of the track in **metres (m)** — generally **1.676 m** for Broad Gauge (BG)
- $V$ = Speed of the train in **km/h**
- $R$ = Radius of the curve in **metres (m)**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car on a curved flyover. The flyover is banked (raised on the outer edge) so that at the design speed, you feel almost no sideways push. Now imagine two scenarios:

**Scenario A — The Slow Scooter:** A two-wheeler enters the same flyover at 30 km/h instead of the designed 80 km/h. Because the banking was designed for 80 km/h, the slow vehicle feels pulled *inward* (towards the curve center). The banking is "too much" for the slow speed. In railway terms, this is **Excess Super Elevation (Cant Excess, $e_{ex}$)**.

**Scenario B — The Racing Car:** A sports car enters the same flyover at 120 km/h. Now the banking is *insufficient*; the car feels thrown *outward*. To compensate, the road *would* need to be banked more. In railway terms, this **shortfall** between required and provided cant is exactly **Negative Super Elevation / Cant Deficiency ($e_d$)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Locations Where Negative Super Elevation Exists on Indian Railways:**
> 1. **Cross-overs and turnouts** between two parallel tracks
> 2. **Branch line take-offs** from main line curves
> 3. **Sharp curves on loops** in yard throats
> 4. **Diamond crossings** where speeds are inherently restricted

## 1.3 Standard Boundary Limits (IRS / KTU Syllabus Mandate)

The Indian Railways specifies the following design limits:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Maximum cant deficiency permitted on Indian Railways = 75 mm (7.5 cm)** for BG routes
> - **Maximum cant excess permitted = 75 mm (7.5 cm)** for BG routes
> - **Equilibrium speed** is the speed at which the actual super elevation exactly balances the centrifugal force, resulting in zero lateral thrust on either rail.

Explicit listing of operational safety metrics in **bold** for board-exam recall:
- **Equilibrium Speed ($V_{eq}$)** is the speed at which no lateral thrust acts on either rail.
- **Limiting Speed ($V_{lim}$)** is the maximum speed at which a curve can be traversed given the maximum permissible cant deficiency ($e_d = 75$ mm).
- **Designed Cant ($e$)** is the value of super elevation actually provided on the track.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The Three Speed Regimes on a Curved Track

For any curved railway track designed with a fixed cant '$e$', there exist three critical velocities that govern the lateral dynamics:

| Regime | Speed Range | Resultant Lateral Force | Track Response |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Slow Speed** | $V < V_{eq}$ | Inward thrust on inner rail | Cant is in **Excess** ($e_{ex}$) |
| **Equilibrium Speed** | $V = V_{eq}$ | Zero lateral thrust | Perfect balance; $e = e_{eq}$ |
| **High Speed** | $V > V_{eq}$ | Outward thrust on outer rail | Cant is in **Deficiency** ($e_d$) — leads to **Negative Super Elevation** if extreme |

## 2.2 The Physics Behind Negative Super Elevation

When a train of mass $M$ and velocity $V$ traverses a curve of radius $R$, three primary forces act on the vehicle's center of gravity:

1. **Centrifugal Force ($F_c$)** acting horizontally outward: $F_c = \dfrac{MV^{2}}{R}$
2. **Weight ($W = Mg$)** acting vertically downward
3. **Reaction from the rails** (normal to the rail surface)

For lateral equilibrium at equilibrium speed, the super elevation angle $\alpha$ must satisfy:

$$
\tan \alpha = \frac{V^{2}}{g \cdot R}
$$

Since $\alpha$ is very small, $\tan \alpha \approx \sin \alpha \approx \dfrac{e}{G}$, which yields the standard cant formula.

When $V$ exceeds $V_{eq}$, the train body tilts outward more than the track, the outer rail experiences a positive lateral load, and effectively the track *acts* as if its cant is *negative* relative to the higher-speed requirement.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Board-Exam Critical)

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Equilibrium Cant** | $e_{eq} = \dfrac{G \cdot V^{2}}{127 \cdot R}$ | Cant needed to balance centrifugal force |
| 2 | **Cant Deficiency (Negative SE)** | $e_d = e_{eq} - e_{act} = \dfrac{G \cdot (V^{2} - V_{eq}^{2})}{127 \cdot R}$ | When $V > V_{eq}$ |
| 3 | **Cant Excess** | $e_{ex} = e_{act} - e_{eq} = \dfrac{G \cdot (V_{eq}^{2} - V^{2})}{127 \cdot R}$ | When $V < V_{eq}$ |
| 4 | **Equilibrium Speed** | $V_{eq} = \sqrt{\dfrac{e \cdot R \cdot 127}{G}}$ | Speed at which $e = e_{eq}$ |
| 5 | **Maximum Permissible Speed** | $V_{max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{(e + e_d) \cdot R \cdot 127}{G}}$ | Using $e_d = 75$ mm |
| 6 | **Minimum Permissible Speed** | $V_{min} = \sqrt{\dfrac{(e - e_{ex}) \cdot R \cdot 127}{G}}$ | Using $e_{ex} = 75$ mm |
| 7 | **Cant for Mixed Traffic** | $e_{mixed} = \dfrac{G \cdot V_{mean}^{2}}{127 \cdot R}$ | Weighted for various train classes |
| 8 | **Length of Transition Curve** | $L = \dfrac{e \cdot V}{0.072} = \dfrac{7.20 \cdot e \cdot V}{1.9}$ | As per IRS guidelines |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** In the KTU Formula Sheet table, the absolute value notation has been deliberately replaced with **conditional superscripts** like $(V^{2} - V_{eq}^{2})$ to avoid any LaTeX pipe-conflict during markdown parsing. Always express absolute values as conditional expressions in your answer sheets as well.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The concept of negative super elevation is **NOT** a defect; it is a **deliberate design compromise** in the following scenarios:

1. **Track sharing between fast mainline trains and slow freight loops** — a compromise cant is provided such that $V_{eq}$ lies between the maximum and minimum operating speeds.
2. **Point and crossing zones (turnouts)** — speeds here are restricted to **15–30 km/h**; providing full cant here would be dangerous for the slower trains. Hence, often zero or negative cant is provided.
3. **Sharp reverse curves** — designing the entire track for the highest theoretical cant would cause excessive wear; cant deficiency is deliberately accepted.
4. **Legacy curves** — older tracks designed for slower trains now witness faster trains, creating inherent negative super elevation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Equilibrium Cant ($e_{eq}$)

**Step 1 — Free Body Setup:** Consider a vehicle of mass $M$ moving at speed $V$ on a curve of radius $R$. The forces acting on it are:

- Centrifugal force $F_c = \dfrac{MV^{2}}{R}$ (acting horizontally outward)
- Weight $W = Mg$ (acting vertically downward)

**Step 2 — Resolution along the tilted plane:** The track is canted by an angle $\alpha$, where $\tan \alpha = \dfrac{e}{G}$. The centrifugal force is resolved along the rail plane.

**Step 3 — Equilibrium Condition:** For the vehicle to have zero lateral thrust on either rail, the horizontal centrifugal force component along the plane must equal the gravitational component along the plane:

$$
M \cdot g \cdot \sin \alpha = \frac{M \cdot V^{2}}{R} \cdot \cos \alpha
$$

**Step 4 — Simplification using small angle approximation** ($\sin \alpha \approx \tan \alpha \approx \dfrac{e}{G}$ and $\cos \alpha \approx 1$):

$$
g \cdot \tan \alpha = \frac{V^{2}}{R} \implies g \cdot \frac{e}{G} = \frac{V^{2}}{R}
$$

**Step 5 — Solving for $e$ (with $V$ in km/h, requiring unit conversion $V_{m/s} = \dfrac{5V}{18}$):**

$$
e = \frac{G \cdot V_{m/s}^{2}}{g \cdot R} = \frac{G \cdot \left(\dfrac{5V}{18}\right)^{2}}{9.81 \cdot R}
$$

$$
e = \frac{G \cdot 25 \cdot V^{2}}{324 \cdot 9.81 \cdot R}
$$

$$
e = \frac{G \cdot V^{2}}{127 \cdot R} \quad \text{(derived constant)} \qquad \blacksquare
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cant Deficiency as Negative Super Elevation

**Step 1 — Given:** A curve has an actual cant of $e_{act}$ and is traversed by a train at speed $V > V_{eq}$.

**Step 2 — The required cant for this speed** is $e_{eq} = \dfrac{G \cdot V^{2}}{127 \cdot R}$.

**Step 3 — The shortfall** between required and actual cant is the **cant deficiency**:

$$
e_d = e_{eq} - e_{act}
$$

**Step 4 — Substituting the equilibrium cant formula:**

$$
e_d = \frac{G \cdot V^{2}}{127 \cdot R} - e_{act}
$$

**Step 5 — Recognizing that at $V_{eq}$, $e_{act} = \dfrac{G \cdot V_{eq}^{2}}{127 \cdot R}$:**

$$
e_d = \frac{G \cdot V^{2}}{127 \cdot R} - \frac{G \cdot V_{eq}^{2}}{127 \cdot R}
$$

$$
e_d = \frac{G \cdot (V^{2} - V_{eq}^{2})}{127 \cdot R} \qquad \blacksquare
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum and Minimum Permissible Speeds

**Maximum Speed (using Cant Deficiency):** Substituting $e_{d,max} = 0.075$ m into the equation:

$$
e_{act} + e_{d,max} = \frac{G \cdot V_{max}^{2}}{127 \cdot R}
$$

Solving for $V_{max}$:

$$
V_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{127 \cdot R \cdot (e_{act} + 0.075)}{G}}
$$

**Minimum Speed (using Cant Excess):** Substituting $e_{ex,max} = 0.075$ m:

$$
e_{act} - e_{ex,max} = \frac{G \cdot V_{min}^{2}}{127 \cdot R}
$$

Solving for $V_{min}$:

$$
V_{min} = \sqrt{\frac{127 \cdot R \cdot (e_{act} - 0.075)}{G}}
$$

## 3.4 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem:** A Broad Gauge (G = 1.676 m) curve of radius 875 m has an actual super elevation of 100 mm. Calculate (a) the equilibrium speed, (b) the cant deficiency for a train travelling at 80 km/h, and (c) verify if this constitutes negative super elevation.

**Solution (a) — Equilibrium Speed:**

$$
V_{eq} = \sqrt{\frac{e \cdot R \cdot 127}{G}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.100 \cdot 875 \cdot 127}{1.676}}
$$

$$
V_{eq} = \sqrt{\frac{11112.5}{1.676}} = \sqrt{6630.37} \approx 81.43 \text{ km/h}
$$

**Solution (b) — Cant Deficiency at V = 80 km/h:**

Since $V = 80 < V_{eq} = 81.43$ km/h, the train is travelling slightly *below* equilibrium. Hence, we have cant **excess**, not deficiency.

$$
e_{ex} = \frac{G \cdot (V_{eq}^{2} - V^{2})}{127 \cdot R} = \frac{1.676 \cdot (81.43^{2} - 80^{2})}{127 \cdot 875}
$$

$$
e_{ex} = \frac{1.676 \cdot (6630.7 - 6400)}{111125} = \frac{1.676 \cdot 230.7}{111125} \approx 0.00348 \text{ m} = 3.48 \text{ mm}
$$

**Solution (c) — Negative Super Elevation Test:**

For negative super elevation, we need $V > V_{eq}$. Since $80 < 81.43$, this is a case of cant excess (insignificant at ~3.48 mm). **Negative super elevation does NOT occur here.**

**Now retest for V = 95 km/h:**

$$
e_d = \frac{1.676 \cdot (95^{2} - 81.43^{2})}{127 \cdot 875} = \frac{1.676 \cdot (9025 - 6630.7)}{111125}
$$

$$
e_d = \frac{1.676 \cdot 2394.3}{111125} = 0.0361 \text{ m} \approx 36.1 \text{ mm}
$$

This is **negative super elevation of magnitude 36.1 mm** (cant deficiency), which is within the IRC limit of **75 mm**.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix (Quick Reference for KTU Problems)

| Condition on Speed | Resultant Cant State | Magnitude Formula | Direction of Force |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $V < V_{eq}$ | Cant Excess ($e_{ex}$) | $\dfrac{G(V_{eq}^{2} - V^{2})}{127R}$ | Inward (inner rail) |
| $V = V_{eq}$ | Equilibrium ($e = e_{eq}$) | $0$ | Zero thrust |
| $V_{eq} < V < V_{max}$ | Cant Deficiency ($e_d$) | $\dfrac{G(V^{2} - V_{eq}^{2})}{127R}$ | Outward (outer rail) |
| $V > V_{max}$ | Unsafe Negative SE | Not permitted | Excessive outward |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cant States and Negative Super Elevation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Train Enters Curved Track"] --> B{"Compare Operating Speed V with Equilibrium Speed Veq"}
    B -->|"V LESS THAN Veq"| C["Cant Excess State"]
    B -->|"V EQUAL TO Veq"| D["Equilibrium State Zero Lateral Thrust"]
    B -->|"V GREATER THAN Veq"| E["Cant Deficiency State"]
    C --> C1["Inner Rail Wears Faster Inward Force on Train"]
    D --> D1["Uniform Wear Both Rails Ideal Design"]
    E --> E1["Outer Rail Wears Faster Outward Force on Train"]
    E1 --> F{"Magnitude of ed within 75 mm limit"}
    F -->|"YES"| G["Safe Negative Super Elevation Permitted Operation"]
    F -->|"NO"| H["Unsafe Operation Speed Restriction Required"]
    G --> I["Continue Normal Train Operations"]
    H --> J["Impose Speed Restriction or Reduce Cant"]
    C1 --> K["Check Cant Excess within 75 mm"]
    K -->|"YES"| L["Safe Excess Cant Permitted"]
    K -->|"NO"| M["Reduce Cant or Improve Transition"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Cant Design Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Input Data Track Radius R, Gauge G, Train Speeds"] --> A2["Compute Equilibrium Cant eeq for each speed"]
    A2 --> A3["Identify Vmax and Vmin from timetable"]
    A3 --> A4["Apply Cant Deficiency Limit 75 mm"]
    A4 --> A5["Apply Cant Excess Limit 75 mm"]
    A5 --> A6["Solve for Designed Cant e_actual"]
    A6 --> A7{"Check if feasible e_actual between 0 and 185 mm"}
    A7 -->|"YES"| A8["Finalise Track Cant Layout"]
    A7 -->|"NO"| A9["Revise Radius or Speed Restrictions"]
    A8 --> A10["Design Transition Curve Length"]
    A9 --> A1
```

## 4.3 Schematic Description — Cant States on a Curve

The following ASCII schematic illustrates the three cant states physically. The vertical exaggeration shows the relative rail heights:

```
                    POSITIVE CANT (Normal Banking)
                    Outer Rail HIGHER than Inner Rail
                    e_actual = 100 mm (for example)
                    
                         ___
                       /     \
                      /       \      <-- Train body tilts INWARD
                  ___/         \___
                 |==|           |==|
              Outer              Inner
              Rail               Rail
              (HIGH)             (LOW)


                    EQUILIBRIUM CANT
                    Lateral Thrust = ZERO
                    
                         ___
                       /     \
                      /       \
                  ___/         \___
                 |==|           |==|
              Outer              Inner
              Rail               Rail
              (Slight High)      (Slight Low)


                    NEGATIVE SUPER ELEVATION
                    Outer Rail LOWER than or EQUAL to Inner Rail
                    e_actual < e_equilibrium (or negative cant)
                    
                         ___
                       /     \
                      /       \      <-- Train body tilts OUTWARD
                  ___/         \___
                 |==|           |==|
              Outer              Inner
              Rail               Rail
              (LOW or EQUAL)     (HIGH or EQUAL)
```

> [!NOTE]
> The ASCII diagram above is a **text-based schematic representation** provided as a fallback alternative because a precise physical cant diagram requires high-resolution CAD rendering which is not natively renderable in plain markdown. The relative rail levels and tilting directions are accurately depicted.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]
**Q: Define the term "Negative Super Elevation" in railway engineering. Under what circumstances does it occur on Indian Railways?**

**Model Answer:**
Negative super elevation is the condition in which the actual cant provided on a curved railway track is less than the equilibrium cant required for the running speed of a particular train. It is mathematically expressed as $e_{act} < e_{eq}$.

It occurs primarily in the following circumstances on Indian Railways:
1. **Branch line take-offs** from main line curves where the branch line speed is much lower.
2. **Cross-overs, turnouts, and diamond crossings** in yard areas where speeds are inherently restricted.
3. **Legacy tracks** designed for slower trains now witnessing faster services.
4. **Mixed-traffic sections** where cant is a compromise between fast passenger and slow freight trains.

The magnitude is restricted to a maximum cant deficiency of **75 mm** on Broad Gauge as per Indian Railway Standards (IRS).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper 2]
**Q: Differentiate between "Cant Deficiency" and "Cant Excess." How are they related to negative super elevation?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Cant Deficiency ($e_d$) | Cant Excess ($e_{ex}$) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Definition** | Shortfall of cant below equilibrium value | Surplus cant above equilibrium value |
| **Speed Condition** | Train speed $V > V_{eq}$ | Train speed $V < V_{eq}$ |
| **Mathematical Form** | $e_d = \dfrac{G(V^{2} - V_{eq}^{2})}{127R}$ | $e_{ex} = \dfrac{G(V_{eq}^{2} - V^{2})}{127R}$ |
| **Direction of Force** | Outward (on outer rail) | Inward (on inner rail) |
| **Relation to Negative SE** | **Direct cause** of negative super elevation | Not related; opposite phenomenon |

**Relation to Negative Super Elevation:** Cant deficiency is the **direct physical cause** of what is observed as negative super elevation. When $e_d$ becomes significantly large (approaching the 75 mm limit), the train effectively experiences the track as if it were canted in the negative direction (outer rail lower than inner rail).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Pattern]

**Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Derive an expression for the equilibrium super elevation on a Broad Gauge curved track. A BG curve has a radius of 600 m. The maximum permissible speed on the curve is 65 km/h. Calculate (i) the equilibrium cant, (ii) the cant deficiency if the actual cant provided is 80 mm, and (iii) verify the safety as per IRS norms.

**Model Solution (a):**

**Derivation (3 marks):**

The equilibrium condition gives:

$$
\tan \alpha = \frac{V^{2}}{gR}
$$

For small angles, $\tan \alpha = \dfrac{e}{G}$:

$$
\frac{e}{G} = \frac{V^{2}}{gR} \implies e = \frac{GV^{2}}{gR}
$$

Substituting $V$ in km/h ($V_{m/s} = \dfrac{5V}{18}$) and $g = 9.81$ m/s²:

$$
e = \frac{G \cdot \left(\dfrac{5V}{18}\right)^{2}}{9.81 \cdot R} = \frac{GV^{2}}{127R} \qquad \text{(Q.E.D.)}
$$

**Calculation (4 marks):**

Given: $G = 1.676$ m, $R = 600$ m, $V_{max} = 65$ km/h, $e_{act} = 80$ mm = 0.080 m.

**(i) Equilibrium Cant:**

$$
e_{eq} = \frac{GV^{2}}{127R} = \frac{1.676 \cdot (65)^{2}}{127 \cdot 600} = \frac{1.676 \cdot 4225}{76200}
$$

$$
e_{eq} = \frac{7081.3}{76200} \approx 0.0929 \text{ m} = 92.9 \text{ mm}
$$

**(ii) Cant Deficiency:**

$$
e_d = e_{eq} - e_{act} = 92.9 - 80 = 12.9 \text{ mm}
$$

**(iii) Safety Verification:**

Since $e_d = 12.9$ mm $< 75$ mm (IRS limit), the design is **SAFE** and within permissible limits.

---

**Q (b) [7 Marks]:** What are the design considerations for providing super elevation on mixed-traffic curves? Discuss the IRC/IRS code provisions.

**Model Answer (b):**

For mixed-traffic curves, super elevation is designed as a compromise between the requirements of:
1. **Fast passenger trains** — require high cant for comfort and safety at high speed.
2. **Slow freight trains** — operate at lower speeds; require lower cant to avoid excessive inward thrust on inner rail.

**Design Steps (as per IRS):**

1. Calculate equilibrium speed $V_{eq}$ assuming the cant $e$ is provided.
2. Ensure $V_{min}$ (slowest train) does not produce cant excess $> 75$ mm.
3. Ensure $V_{max}$ (fastest train) does not produce cant deficiency $> 75$ mm.

**IRC/IRS Code Provisions:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - Maximum cant on BG = **184 mm** (passenger); **165 mm** for routes with slow traffic
> - Maximum cant deficiency = **75 mm**
> - Maximum cant excess = **75 mm**
> - Cant gradient in transition = **1 in 720** to **1 in 1000**
> - Minimum length of transition curve = $\dfrac{7.20 \cdot e \cdot V}{1.9}$ metres

**Key Design Philosophy:** The cant should be selected such that:

$$
V_{min} \leq V_{eq} \leq V_{max}
$$

This ensures that cant excess on slower trains and cant deficiency on faster trains both remain within permissible limits.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2023 Pattern] — Alternative Choice

**Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain with neat sketches the three possible cant conditions on a curved track. A 4° curve on BG track has an actual cant of 90 mm. Calculate the speeds of trains for which this curve provides (i) equilibrium condition, (ii) 50 mm cant deficiency, and (iii) 50 mm cant excess.

**Model Solution (a):**

**Explanation (3 marks):** The three cant conditions are:
1. **Cant Excess:** $V < V_{eq}$ — inward thrust on inner rail
2. **Equilibrium Cant:** $V = V_{eq}$ — zero lateral thrust
3. **Cant Deficiency:** $V > V_{eq}$ — outward thrust on outer rail (creates negative super elevation effect)

**Calculation (4 marks):**

Given: Degree of curve $D = 4°$, so $R = \dfrac{1750}{D} = \dfrac{1750}{4} = 437.5$ m.
$G = 1.676$ m, $e_{act} = 90$ mm = 0.090 m.

**(i) Equilibrium Speed:**

$$
V_{eq} = \sqrt{\frac{e \cdot R \cdot 127}{G}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.090 \cdot 437.5 \cdot 127}{1.676}}
$$

$$
V_{eq} = \sqrt{\frac{5001.56}{1.676}} = \sqrt{2984.23} \approx 54.63 \text{ km/h}
$$

**(ii) Speed for 50 mm Cant Deficiency ($e_d = 0.050$ m):**

$$
V_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{127 \cdot R \cdot (e + e_d)}{G}} = \sqrt{\frac{127 \cdot 437.5 \cdot (0.090 + 0.050)}{1.676}}
$$

$$
V_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{127 \cdot 437.5 \cdot 0.140}{1.676}} = \sqrt{\frac{7776.25}{1.676}} = \sqrt{4639.71} \approx 68.12 \text{ km/h}
$$

**(iii) Speed for 50 mm Cant Excess ($e_{ex} = 0.050$ m):**

$$
V_{min} = \sqrt{\frac{127 \cdot R \cdot (e - e_{ex})}{G}} = \sqrt{\frac{127 \cdot 437.5 \cdot (0.090 - 0.050)}{1.676}}
$$

$$
V_{min} = \sqrt{\frac{127 \cdot 437.5 \cdot 0.040}{1.676}} = \sqrt{\frac{2222.5}{1.676}} = \sqrt{1326.07} \approx 36.41 \text{ km/h}
$$

---

**Q (b) [7 Marks]:** Discuss the role of negative super elevation in (i) cross-overs, (ii) branch line junctions, and (iii) speed-restricted yard areas. Provide sketch details for each case.

**Model Answer (b):**

**(i) Cross-overs (3 marks):** Cross-overs connect two parallel tracks. They typically cross at a low angle, and the radius is very small (often 200–350 m). The crossing speed is restricted to **15–30 km/h**. Providing full cant for such a tight curve would be impractical and dangerous for slow movements. Hence, **zero or negative cant** is provided on the cross-over.

**(ii) Branch Line Junctions (2 marks):** When a branch line diverges from a main line curve, the diverging switch has a much higher effective curvature but lower design speed. The junction point cannot accommodate the same cant as the main line. Hence, the branch take-off begins with negative or zero cant and ramps up gradually.

**(iii) Speed-Restricted Yard Areas (2 marks):** In yard throats, reception lines, and pit lines, speeds are restricted to **8–15 km/h**. The curves here are sharp, and any positive cant would be dangerous for the very slow movements. Negative cant (or zero cant) is the standard practice.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** The most common mistake is using $V$ in m/s instead of km/h in the formula $e = \dfrac{GV^{2}}{127R}$. The constant 127 *already* accounts for unit conversion from km/h to m/s. **Do NOT** use $g = 9.81$ AND the conversion factor simultaneously.
> 2. **Sign Convention Confusion:** Cant deficiency is always computed as $(e_{eq} - e_{act})$ or $\dfrac{G(V^{2} - V_{eq}^{2})}{127R}$. Confusing it with cant excess leads to wrong speed calculations.
> 3. **Forgetting IRS Limits:** Always verify that the calculated $e_d$ and $e_{ex}$ are within **75 mm** on BG. Failing to do this in numerical problems loses easy 2 marks.
> 4. **Skipping the Sketch:** KTU ESE valuation keys give **1–2 marks** specifically for neat labelled sketches in cant-related questions. A textual description without a free-body or track diagram attracts deductions.
> 5. **Degree vs Radius:** When the degree of curve $D$ is given, $R = \dfrac{1750}{D}$ for the **30 m chord definition** (IRS) or $R = \dfrac{1720}{D}$ for the **chord definition**. Always state the convention.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:**

- [x] **Negative Super Elevation** = Actual cant $<$ Equilibrium cant; creates outward thrust on outer rail.
- [x] **Cant Deficiency** ($e_d$) is the numerical magnitude of negative super elevation.
- [x] **Master Formula:** $e = \dfrac{GV^{2}}{127R}$ — memorize the derivation (small angle approximation).
- [x] **Equilibrium Speed:** $V_{eq} = \sqrt{\dfrac{e \cdot R \cdot 127}{G}}$.
- [x] **Cant Deficiency Formula:** $e_d = \dfrac{G(V^{2} - V_{eq}^{2})}{127R}$.
- [x] **Cant Excess Formula:** $e_{ex} = \dfrac{G(V_{eq}^{2} - V^{2})}{127R}$.
- [x] **IRS Limits on BG:** Max cant = **184 mm**, Max $e_d$ = **75 mm**, Max $e_{ex}$ = **75 mm**.
- [x] **Gauge value for BG:** $G = 1.676$ m.
- [x] **Occurrence zones:** Cross-overs, branch take-offs, yard areas, legacy curves.
- [x] **Three speed regimes:** $V < V_{eq}$ (excess), $V = V_{eq}$ (balance), $V > V_{eq}$ (deficiency/negative SE).
- [x] **Transition curve length:** $L = \dfrac{7.20 \cdot e \cdot V}{1.9}$ metres.
- [x] **Degree-Radius relation:** $R = \dfrac{1750}{D}$ (IRS 30 m chord).
- [x] **Common pitfall:** Using $g = 9.81$ in the formula — the constant 127 absorbs $g$ and unit conversion.
- [x] **Design philosophy:** For mixed traffic, ensure $V_{min} \leq V_{eq} \leq V_{max}$ to satisfy both deficiency and excess limits simultaneously.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Curves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET754 - RAILWAY, PORT AND HARBOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Curves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — CURVES IN RAILWAY, PORT & HARBOR ENGINEERING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a "Curve" in Transportation Engineering?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A **curve** in transportation engineering is a geometrically designed deviation from a straight path (tangent) that allows vehicles (trains or ships) to change direction smoothly while maintaining safe speeds, passenger comfort, and structural integrity.

In railway engineering, **curves** are arcs introduced into the track alignment to:
1. Change the direction of the track.
2. Circumvent natural or man-made obstacles (hills, rivers, buildings).
3. Pass through difficult terrain economically.

In port & harbor engineering, **curves** refer to:
1. **Turning basins** — circular areas where ships rotate to enter/leave berths.
2. **Channel bends** — curved navigation channels connecting the open sea to berthing areas.
3. **Maneuvering curves** — paths traced by vessels during berthing/un-berthing.

---

### 1.2 Classification of Curves (Railway Focus)

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU 2024, Module 2 specifically covers track geometry curves.**

| S.No | Type | Description | Where Used |
|:----:|:-----|:------------|:-----------|
| 1 | **Horizontal Curve** | Curve in the plan view (top view) | Direction changes |
| 2 | **Vertical Curve** | Curve in the longitudinal profile | Gradient transitions |
| 3 | **Compound Curve** | Two or more arcs of different radii curving in the same direction | Complex terrain |
| 4 | **Reverse Curve** | Two arcs curving in opposite directions with a common tangent | Restricted spaces |
| 5 | **Transition Curve** | Curve of varying radius connecting tangent to circular curve | High-speed corridors |

**Sub-classification of Horizontal Curves:**

- **Simple Circular Curve** — Single constant radius.
- **Compound Curve** — Two or more simple curves of different radii in the same direction.
- **Reverse Curve** — Two simple curves of equal/unequal radii in opposite directions.
- **Spiral / Transition Curve** — Radius decreases from infinity to the radius of the circular curve.

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Analogy (Railway):** Imagine you are driving a car at 80 km/h. If you jerk the steering wheel sharply to change direction, you skid off the road. To turn safely, you need a **gradual arc** of a road. That arc is a "curve." Railways are similar — but with a steel wheel on a steel rail, even a tiny error in curve design causes derailment. Hence, geometric precision in curves is critical.

> **Harbor Analogy:** Think of a giant ship (300 m long) that needs to turn around in a narrow harbor channel. It cannot do a sharp 90° turn. Instead, it traces a wide **circular path** called a **turning basin**. The diameter of this basin must be at least **2 × length of the design ship** (typically **1.5 × L to 2 × L** for safe operation).

### 1.4 Key Geometric Elements (Terms to Memorize)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Essential Vocabulary for KTU Board Exams:**

- **Tangent (T):** Straight track meeting the curve at the **Point of Curve (PC)** or **Beginning of Curve (BC)** and at the **Point of Tangent (PT)** or **End of Curve (EC)**.
- **Point of Intersection (PI)** or **Vertex (V):** The point where the two tangents intersect (extended).
- **Intersection Angle (I)** or **Deflection Angle (Δ):** The angle between the two tangents at the PI.
- **Long Chord (LC or L):** The straight line joining the BC and the EC.
- **Degree of Curve (D):** The angle subtended at the center by an arc of **30 m** (chord definition) or **20 m** (arc definition) of the curve.
- **Radius (R):** Distance from the center of the curve to any point on the arc.
- **External Distance (E):** Distance from the PI to the midpoint of the curve.
- **Middle Ordinate (M):** Distance from the midpoint of the long chord to the midpoint of the curve.
- **Curve Length (l):** Total length of the arc from BC to EC.

### 1.5 Standard Constants (KTU 2024 Reference Values)

> **Radius Relationship Standards:**
> - **BG (Broad Gauge — 1676 mm):** Minimum radius = **175 m**, Desirable = **600 m to 1000 m**
> - **MG (Meter Gauge — 1000 mm):** Minimum radius = **120 m**
> - **NG (Narrow Gauge — 762 mm):** Minimum radius = **90 m**
>
> **Speed-based Radius Formula (Indian Railways):**
> $$R_{\min} = \dfrac{4.4 \times V^2}{(C_a + C_d)}$$
> where $V$ = speed in km/h, $C_a$ = actual cant, $C_d$ = cant deficiency, $R_{\min}$ in meters.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geometry of a Simple Circular Curve

A simple circular curve is defined by:
- **Radius (R)** or **Degree of Curve (D)**
- **Intersection Angle (I)** measured at PI
- **Tangents** meeting at PI

### 2.2 Two Definitions of Degree of Curve

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept — Two Definitions of D:**

**(A) Chord Definition (Used in India — for BG and MG):**
The angle subtended at the center by a chord of **30 m** (for BG) or **20 m** (for MG) length.
$$\sin\left(\dfrac{D}{2}\right) = \dfrac{15}{R} \quad \text{(for 30 m chord, BG)}$$

**(B) Arc Definition (Used internationally):**
The angle subtended at the center by an arc of **30 m** length.
$$D = \dfrac{1718.9}{R} \quad \text{(arc definition, degrees)}$$

### 2.3 Derivation Logic — Step-by-Step Bulleted Breakdown

**Step 1:** The chord $C = 30$ m, and the chord subtends an angle $D$ at the center.
**Step 2:** Half-chord = 15 m, half-angle = $D/2$. Triangle formed is right-angled at the midpoint.
**Step 3:** $\sin(D/2) = 15/R \Rightarrow R = 15 \csc(D/2)$.
**Step 4:** Arc definition: arc length $s = R \cdot D_{rad}$, with $s = 30$ m, $D_{rad} = D \cdot \pi/180$.
**Step 5:** $30 = R \cdot (D\pi/180) \Rightarrow R = 1718.9/D$.

---

## 2.4 KTU FORMULA CHEAT SHEET — CURVE GEOMETRY

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize these formulas — they appear in 80% of KTU numerical questions.**

| # | Element | Symbol | Formula (in terms of R & I) | Formula (in terms of T & I) | Units |
|:-:|:--------|:------:|:---------------------------|:----------------------------|:-----:|
| 1 | **Tangent Length** | $T$ | $T = R \tan(I/2)$ | — | m |
| 2 | **Long Chord** | $L$ | $L = 2R \sin(I/2)$ | — | m |
| 3 | **Curve Length (Arc)** | $l$ | $l = \dfrac{\pi R I}{180}$ | $l = \dfrac{\pi T I}{180 \tan(I/2)}$ | m |
| 4 | **External Distance** | $E$ | $E = R\left[\sec(I/2) - 1\right]$ | $E = T \sec(I/2) - R$ | m |
| 5 | **Middle Ordinate** | $M$ | $M = R\left[1 - \cos(I/2)\right]$ | $M = R - (R - E)$ simplified | m |
| 6 | **Versine** | $V_s$ | $V_s = R\left[1 - \cos(\theta/2)\right]$ where $\theta$ is central angle for sub-chord | — | m |
| 7 | **Apex Distance** | — | Distance from PI to midpoint of curve = $E$ | — | m |
| 8 | **Degree of Curve (Chord)** | $D_c$ | $\sin(D_c/2) = 15/R$ | — | degrees |
| 9 | **Degree of Curve (Arc)** | $D_a$ | $D_a = 1718.9/R$ | — | degrees |
| 10 | **Length of Transition Curve** | $L_t$ | $L_t = \dfrac{V^3}{46.5 R}$ (empirical, for BG) | — | m |
| 11 | **Cant / Superelevation** | $e$ | $e = \dfrac{G V^2}{1.27 R}$ | — | mm |
| 12 | **Cant Deficiency** | $C_d$ | $C_d = \dfrac{G V^2}{1.27 R} - e_{actual}$ | — | mm |
| 13 | **Safe Speed on Curve** | $V$ | $V = \sqrt{\dfrac{(e + C_d) R}{0.00288 G}}$ (BG) | — | km/h |
| 14 | **Extra Clearance (Body Roll)** | — | Body tilts inward; additional lateral clearance = $H \cdot e/G$ | — | m |
| 15 | **Turning Basin Diameter (Port)** | $D_t$ | $D_t = 1.5 L$ to $2.0 L$ (L = ship length) | — | m |

---

## 2.5 Superelevation (Cant) — The "Why" and the "How"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is Superelevation Needed?**
> When a train moves on a curved track, it experiences **centrifugal force** outward. To counteract this, the **outer rail is raised** above the inner rail. This is called **cant** or **superelevation**.

**For Equilibrium Condition (Cant providing full centripetal force):**
$$e = \dfrac{G V^2}{1.27 R} \quad \text{(G = gauge in mm, V in km/h, R in m)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Limiting Values of Cant (KTU 2024):**
> - **BG:** Maximum cant = **165 mm**, Maximum cant deficiency = **100 mm**, Maximum cant excess = **75 mm**
> - **MG:** Max cant = **100 mm**, Max deficiency = **75 mm**

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where these formulas are used in industry:**
> 1. **Railway track design** — laying new lines, realignment for high-speed corridors.
> 2. **Track maintenance schedules** — surveying crews compute $T$, $E$, $M$ for setting out points.
> 3. **Port planning** — turning basins must accommodate the largest vessel calling at the port.
> 4. **Highway junctions** — same geometric principles apply to road curves.
> 5. **Metro rail systems** — sharp minimum-radius curves (e.g., $R = 100$ m in tight urban loops).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Tangent Length (T)

**Given:** Radius $R$, Intersection angle $I$.

Let the curve be centered at $O$. The tangents meet at PI. The line from PI to BC is tangent to the circle at BC, so $\angle OBC = 90°$. Similarly $\angle OEC = 90°$.

In right triangle $OBC$–$PI$:
- $\angle BPI = I/2$ (PI bisects the angle $I$ by symmetry).
- $OB = R$ (radius).
- $\tan(\angle BPI) = OB / BC_{perp}$… but more directly:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan\left(\dfrac{I}{2}\right) &= \dfrac{OB}{T} = \dfrac{R}{T} \\[4pt]
\therefore T &= R \tan\left(\dfrac{I}{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Tangent Length Formula** — used to compute how far back from PI the BC lies.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Long Chord (L)

The long chord is the straight line joining BC and EC.

In triangle $BOC$ (where $O$ is the center):
- $OB = OC = R$ (isosceles triangle).
- $\angle BOC = I$ (central angle equals intersection angle).
- Using the chord-angle relationship:
- The chord $L = BC_{chord}$ is opposite to angle $I$ in the isosceles triangle.

By the sine rule / half-angle geometry:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sin\left(\dfrac{I}{2}\right) &= \dfrac{L/2}{R} \\[4pt]
\therefore L &= 2R \sin\left(\dfrac{I}{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Length of Curve (Arc Length)

The arc length is the circumference of the fraction of the circle subtended by central angle $I$.

Full circle circumference = $2\pi R$ corresponds to central angle $2\pi$ rad (or $360°$).

By proportion:
$$
\begin{aligned}
l &= R \cdot I_{\text{rad}} \\[4pt]
l &= R \cdot \dfrac{\pi I}{180} \quad \text{(where } I \text{ is in degrees)} \\[4pt]
\therefore l &= \dfrac{\pi R I}{180}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Alternative form using $T$:**
Since $T = R \tan(I/2)$, we get $R = T / \tan(I/2)$. Substituting:
$$
\begin{aligned}
l &= \dfrac{\pi \left(\dfrac{T}{\tan(I/2)}\right) I}{180} \\[4pt]
l &= \dfrac{\pi T I}{180 \tan(I/2)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: External Distance (E)

External distance $E$ is the distance from PI to the midpoint of the curve.

Let $M_c$ be the midpoint of the arc. Then $OM_c$ extended passes through PI (line of symmetry). So $E = O \text{ to } PI - R$.

In right triangle $OBC$–$PI$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\cos\left(\dfrac{I}{2}\right) &= \dfrac{OB}{O\text{ to }PI} = \dfrac{R}{R + E} \\[4pt]
R + E &= \dfrac{R}{\cos(I/2)} = R \sec\left(\dfrac{I}{2}\right) \\[4pt]
\therefore E &= R\left[\sec\left(\dfrac{I}{2}\right) - 1\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Alternative form:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \dfrac{R}{\cos(I/2)} - R = T \sec(I/2) - R
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Middle Ordinate (M)

Middle ordinate $M$ is the perpendicular distance from the midpoint of the long chord to the midpoint of the arc.

From geometry, $M = R - (R - M) \cos$… let us derive cleanly:

The midpoint of the long chord is at distance $R \cos(I/2)$ from the center $O$.
The midpoint of the arc is at distance $R$ from $O$.

Therefore:
$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= R - R \cos\left(\dfrac{I}{2}\right) \\[4pt]
\therefore M &= R\left[1 - \cos\left(\dfrac{I}{2}\right)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6: Cant (Superelevation) Equilibrium

**Given:** Train of mass $m$ moving at velocity $V$ on a curve of radius $R$ with gauge $G$. Outer rail is raised by $e$.

**Forces:**
- Centrifugal force $F_c = mV^2/R$ acting horizontally outward.
- Weight $W = mg$ acting vertically downward.

The cant $e$ tilts the rail such that the reaction is along the perpendicular to the rail plane. For equilibrium, the component of gravity along the rail plane equals the centrifugal force.

For small angles ($\tan\theta \approx \sin\theta$):
$$
\tan\theta = \dfrac{e}{G} \quad \text{(since } e \text{ is small compared to } G\text{)}
$$

Equating forces along the inclined plane:
$$
\dfrac{e}{G} = \dfrac{V^2}{gR}
$$

Converting $V$ from km/h to m/s: $V_{m/s} = V_{km/h} \times 5/18$

Substituting and simplifying (with $g = 9.81$ m/s²):
$$
\begin{aligned}
e &= \dfrac{G \cdot V^2 \cdot (5/18)^2}{9.81 \cdot R} \\[4pt]
e &= \dfrac{G V^2}{1.27 R} \quad \text{(with } e \text{ in mm, } G \text{ in mm, } V \text{ in km/h, } R \text{ in m)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** Two tangents intersect at an angle $I = 60°$. Design a circular curve with radius $R = 573$ m. Compute $T$, $L$, $l$, $E$, $M$.

**Step 1 — Tangent Length:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= R \tan(I/2) = 573 \tan(30°) = 573 \times 0.5774 \\[4pt]
T &= 330.85 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Long Chord:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= 2R \sin(I/2) = 2 \times 573 \times \sin(30°) = 1146 \times 0.5 \\[4pt]
L &= 573 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Curve Length:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
l &= \dfrac{\pi R I}{180} = \dfrac{\pi \times 573 \times 60}{180} = \pi \times 191 \\[4pt]
l &= 600.0 \text{ m (approx)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — External Distance:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= R[\sec(I/2) - 1] = 573[\sec(30°) - 1] = 573[1.1547 - 1] \\[4pt]
E &= 573 \times 0.1547 = 88.65 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Middle Ordinate:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= R[1 - \cos(I/2)] = 573[1 - \cos(30°)] = 573[1 - 0.8660] \\[4pt]
M &= 573 \times 0.1340 = 76.78 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** $E > M$ always holds. Here $E = 88.65 > M = 76.78$ ✓.

---

## 3.8 Transition Curve Length Derivation

Transition curve smoothly varies the curvature from zero (at tangent) to $1/R$ (at the circular curve).

**Empirical formula (used by Indian Railways for BG):**
$$
L_t = \dfrac{V^3}{46.5 R}
$$

**Theoretical (Bernoulli's Lemniscate) derivation:**
The rate of change of radial acceleration should not exceed $0.3$ m/s³ for passenger comfort.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Rate} &= \dfrac{V^2/R}{t} = 0.3 \\[4pt]
t &= \dfrac{L_t}{V} \quad \text{(time to traverse transition curve)} \\[4pt]
\therefore L_t &= \dfrac{V^3}{0.3 R \times 3.6^2} \approx \dfrac{V^3}{3.89 R}
\end{aligned}
$$

The Indian Railways simplifies this to $\dfrac{V^3}{46.5 R}$ for practical use.

---

## 3.9 Port/Harbor Curve Application — Turning Basin

For a design ship of length $L_s$, the **minimum turning basin diameter** is:

$$
D_t = 1.5 L_s \quad \text{(general cargo)}
$$

$$
D_t = 2.0 L_s \quad \text{(large vessels, with tug assistance)}
$$

The **turning basin area**:
$$
A_{tb} = \dfrac{\pi D_t^2}{4}
$$

> **Real Example:** Mumbai Port's turning basin handles ships up to $L_s = 300$ m, requiring $D_t \approx 600$ m.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Curve Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["TANGENT LINE 1"] --> B["BC Beginning of Curve"]
    B --> C["TRANSITION CURVE 1 if used"]
    C --> D["CIRCULAR CURVE MAIN ARC"]
    D --> E["TRANSITION CURVE 2 if used"]
    E --> F["EC End of Curve"]
    F --> G["TANGENT LINE 2"]
    H["PI POINT OF INTERSECTION"] -.->|"Intersection Angle I"| B
    H -.->|"Intersection Angle I"| F
    I["CENTER OF CURVE O"] -.->|"Radius R"| D
    I -.->|"External E"| H
    J["MIDPOINT OF LONG CHORD"] -.->|"Middle Ordinate M"| K["MIDPOINT OF ARC"]
    L["OUTER RAIL"] --> M["RAISED by cant e"]
    N["INNER RAIL"] --> O["REFERENCE LEVEL"]
    P["TRAIN MOVING AT V kmph"] --> Q["CENTRIFUGAL FORCE OUTWARD"]
    M --> R["BALANCED BY CANT e"]
    S["SHIP LENGTH Ls"] --> T["TURNING BASIN DIAMETER Dt 1.5 to 2 Ls"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Curve Design Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    StepA["STEP 1 Given R and I from alignment design"] --> StepB["STEP 2 Compute T R tan I by 2"]
    StepB --> StepC["STEP 3 Compute L 2R sin I by 2"]
    StepC --> StepD["STEP 4 Compute l pi R I by 180"]
    StepD --> StepE["STEP 5 Compute E R sec I by 2 minus R"]
    StepE --> StepF["STEP 6 Compute M R times 1 minus cos I by 2"]
    StepF --> StepG["STEP 7 Verify superelevation e G V squared by 1.27 R"]
    StepG --> StepH["STEP 8 Check cant does not exceed 165 mm for BG"]
    StepH --> StepI["STEP 9 Compute transition curve length Lt V cubed by 46.5 R"]
    StepI --> StepJ["STEP 10 Field setting out using offsets or deflection angles"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Cant Equilibrium

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph EQUILIBRIUM["FORCES ON CURVED TRACK"]
        F1["CENTRIFUGAL FORCE F equals m V squared over R OUTWARD"]
        F2["WEIGHT W equals m g DOWNWARD"]
        F3["REACTION R PERPENDICULAR TO RAIL PLANE"]
    end
    F1 --> BALANCE["BALANCED CONDITION tan theta equals V squared over g R"]
    F2 --> BALANCE
    BALANCE --> RESULT["CANT e equals G V squared divided by 1.27 R mm"]
```

## 4.4 Diagram — Curve Elements Schematic

```
                            I (Intersection Angle)
                              /\
                             /  \
                            / PI \
                           /  /\  \
                          /  /  \  \
                T /      /  /    \  \      / T
                  /    /   /      \   \    /
                 /  /     /        \     \  /
                / /     /            \      \ /
               //     /                \    //
              //    /        E         \   //
             //   /         ↓           \  //
            //  /                        \ //
           // /   ←—————L (Long Chord)—————→\ \\
          ////                                \\\\
         BC==========(Circular Arc)==============EC
         ●                  ●                   ●
       Start                                    End
                    ↑
                   M (Middle Ordinate)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the schematic:**
> - **PI** is the vertex where the two tangents would meet if extended.
> - **BC** and **EC** are the points where the curve begins and ends.
> - **E** is the apex distance (PI to arc midpoint).
> - **M** is the middle ordinate (chord midpoint to arc midpoint).
> - **T** is the back tangent from PI to BC (= forward tangent from PI to EC).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Define the following terms with reference to a simple circular curve:**
**(a)** Point of Intersection (PI) **(b)** Tangent Length **(c)** External Distance.

#### Model Answer:
