# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part A questions are 2-mark or 3-mark short-answer questions requiring concise, definition-based responses. Each answer should be **3–5 sentences** with a crisp definition and example.

---

### Question 1 (3 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**
**Define a natural harbor. State any two of its advantages and one disadvantage.**

**Model Answer:**

A natural harbor is a sheltered water body where protection, depth, and shape are provided by pre-existing geographical features such as bays, inlets, river mouths, islands, or headlands, without the need for major engineered structures.

**Advantages:**
1. **Low capital cost** — Since natural protection already exists, the investment required is limited to dredging, berthing, and storage facilities. Examples: Mumbai, Vishakhapatnam.
2. **Reduced construction time** — The harbor can be commissioned in 2–5 years as the basin is already sheltered.

**Disadvantage:**
1. **Limited scope for expansion** — The fixed geographical boundaries restrict the addition of new berths, deeper basins, or larger facilities to accommodate mega-vessels. Natural harbors are also vulnerable to siltation at river mouths and storm surges.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - June 2023]**
**List any three advantages and two disadvantages of water transportation.**

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages:**
1. **Lowest cost per tonne-kilometer** — Ocean shipping costs are typically 1/4 of road and 1/40 of air transport, making it ideal for bulk cargo.
2. **Highest carrying capacity** — A single Capesize vessel can carry 1,50,000–2,00,000 DWT, equivalent to ~7,200 trucks.
3. **Energy efficient and low carbon footprint** — Shipping emits only ~10–15 g CO₂ per tonne-km, the lowest among all major transport modes.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Slow speed** — Cargo ships travel at 15–25 knots (~28–46 km/h), making water transport unsuitable for time-sensitive cargo.
2. **Limited accessibility and high port turnaround time** — Restricted to coastal/river routes and vessels may wait 2–7 days for berthing at congested ports.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks with internal choice. Each question has two sub-parts, typically (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. The model solutions must be structured, complete, and match KTU valuation key expectations.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — **OPTION A**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the advantages and disadvantages of **natural harbors** with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Compare the advantages and disadvantages of **water transportation with road and rail transport** as competing modes for cargo movement. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

A natural harbor is a body of water along a coast where ships can anchor safely, protected by **pre-existing geographical features** such as bays, headlands, river mouths, or offshore islands. The protection is offered by nature, and only minor engineering interventions (dredging, berth construction, storage facilities) are required.

**Advantages of Natural Harbors:**

1. **Low Capital Cost:** Since natural protection is already available, the need for massive engineered breakwaters is eliminated. Examples: **Mumbai Port** (protected by the mainland and Elephanta Caves headland), **Sydney Harbour** (protected by the harbor headlands). CAPEX is restricted to dredging and berthing facilities.

2. **Reduced Construction Time:** Natural harbors can be operational in 2–5 years, compared to 8–15 years for fully artificial harbors, because the basin is already sheltered.

3. **Natural Wave Attenuation:** Geographical features absorb 70–90% of incoming wave energy, providing calmer waters and reducing operational downtime during rough weather.

4. **Lower Maintenance Cost:** The natural protection system requires minimal structural upkeep, lowering the long-term operating expenditure (OPEX).

5. **Strategic and Historical Importance:** Many world-class ports developed around natural harbors with centuries of maritime tradition, including **San Francisco Bay**, **Vishakhapatnam**, and **Halifax**.

6. **Better Co-existence with Marine Ecology:** Established ecosystems (subject to environmental regulations) often exist alongside natural harbors.

**Disadvantages of Natural Harbors:**

1. **Geographical Constraint on Site Selection:** The port location is fixed by nature, which may not align with trade routes, hinterland connectivity, or strategic needs.

2. **Limited Expansion Capability:** Fixed geographical boundaries restrict the addition of new berths, deeper basins, or handling facilities for modern mega-vessels.

3. **Susceptibility to Natural Disasters:** Located in bays and inlets, these harbors are vulnerable to cyclones, tsunamis, and storm surges.

4. **Siltation Problem:** River-mouth natural harbors suffer from continuous silt deposition, requiring regular dredging. Siltation rates can be 0.3 to 1.5 m/year, costing 5–15% of annual OPEX.

5. **Inadequate Depth for Modern Vessels:** Modern Capesize vessels require drafts of 18–25 m, which may not be available naturally.

**Examples (India):** Mumbai Port, Vishakhapatnam, Mormugao (Goa), Paradip.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

Water transportation is the movement of cargo and passengers over oceans, seas, rivers, and canals using ships, barges, and boats. It is one of the four primary modes of transport, the others being road, rail, and air.

**Comparison of Water Transport with Road and Rail:**

| Criterion | Water | Rail | Road |
|-----------|-------|------|------|
| **Cost per tonne-km** | **Lowest (₹1)** | Low (₹2) | Moderate (₹4) |
| **Speed** | 28–46 km/h | 60–80 km/h | 40–60 km/h |
| **Capacity per trip** | **Highest (up to 2,00,000 DWT)** | High (rake of 50–60 wagons, ~3000–5000 t) | Moderate (truck, ~25 t) |
| **Flexibility** | Low (fixed sea/river routes) | Moderate (fixed tracks) | **Highest (door-to-door)** |
| **Initial Infrastructure** | **Very High (port, breakwater)** | High (railway tracks, terminals) | High (roads, bridges) |
| **Reliability in Weather** | Low (monsoon, cyclones) | Moderate | **High** |
| **Environmental Impact (per tonne-km)** | **Lowest (~10–15 g CO₂)** | Low (~20–30 g CO₂) | High (~60–100 g CO₂) |
| **Best Cargo Type** | Bulk (coal, ore, oil) | Bulk + containers | All types |

**Advantages of Water over Road and Rail:**
1. **Lowest freight cost** for bulk goods.
2. **Massive carrying capacity** in a single trip.
3. **Lowest energy consumption and CO₂ footprint per tonne-km**.
4. **Suitable for non-perishable, heavy, bulky cargo** like coal, ore, crude oil, and LNG.

**Disadvantages of Water compared to Road and Rail:**
1. **Slowest speed** among the three.
2. **Limited accessibility** — cannot provide door-to-door service.
3. **Vulnerable to weather** — monsoon and cyclones disrupt operations.
4. **Long port turnaround time** — 2–7 days waiting for berthing.

**Conclusion:** Water transportation is the most economical and environmentally sustainable mode for bulk cargo over long distances, but it must be supplemented by road and rail for **last-mile connectivity** (intermodal transport).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **OPTION B** (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 3]**
**(a)** Discuss in detail the advantages and disadvantages of **artificial harbors**. Why are they constructed despite their high cost? **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** With neat sketches, describe the **classification of harbors** based on their location, usage, and protection. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

An artificial harbor is a sheltered water body created by engineering structures such as **breakwaters, jetties, sea walls, and dredging operations** at locations lacking natural protection. Examples: **Chennai Port**, **Ennore Port**, **Tuticorin (V.O. Chidambaranar) Port**, and **Kochi Port** (partially artificial with the famous Willingdon Island).

**Why Artificial Harbors are Constructed Despite High Cost:**

1. **Strategic Location Demand:** Sometimes the most economically viable location for a port (near industrial hubs, mining belts, or population centers) does not have a natural harbor. Example: Ennore Port was built to serve the industrial corridor of north Chennai.
2. **Customizable Design:** Engineers can design basin depth, layout, and breakwater orientation to suit present and future vessel sizes.
3. **Scalability:** Designed for phased expansion as trade volume grows.
4. **Accommodates Mega-Vessels:** Basins and channels can be dredged to 18–22 m for Capesize and VLCC vessels.

**Advantages of Artificial Harbors:**

1. **Freedom of Site Selection** — Can be built at the most economically and strategically optimal location.
2. **Customizable Layout** — Depth, basin area, and breakwater orientation can be designed.
3. **Scalability** — Phased expansion possible.
4. **Suitability for Modern Mega-Vessels** — Dredged to 18–25 m draft.
5. **Better Hinterland Connectivity** — Can be sited near industrial corridors and rail/road networks.

**Disadvantages of Artificial Harbors:**

1. **Very High Capital Cost** — Breakwater construction costs ₹50,000–₹2,00,000 per meter; total project cost often exceeds ₹10,000 crore.
2. **Long Construction Duration** — 8–15 years for major breakwater and dredging projects.
3. **High Maintenance Dredging** — Continuous dredging needed to counter siltation, consuming 5–15% of annual OPEX.
4. **Environmental Impact** — Dredging disturbs marine ecosystems, displaces fisheries, and causes coastal erosion.
5. **Complex Engineering** — Requires wave agitation studies, sediment transport modeling, and geotechnical investigations.
6. **Operational Downtime** — May close during cyclones if breakwater is insufficient.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Classification of Harbors** can be presented in a tabular form (sketch-style block representation):

**1. Based on Location:**
- **Natural Harbor** — Sheltered by geographical features (Mumbai).
- **Artificial Harbor** — Sheltered by engineered structures (Chennai).
- **Semi-Natural Harbor** — Combined protection (Kochi).
- **Inland Harbor** — Located on rivers or canals (Kolkata, Allahabad).
- **Open Roadstead** — No protection; vessels anchor offshore and cargo is lightered to shore (sometimes used in India for tanker operations).

**2. Based on Usage:**
- **Commercial Harbor / Port** — Handles general cargo, containers, bulk (Mumbai, Chennai).
- **Fishing Harbor** — Dedicated to fishing fleet (Cochin fishing harbor).
- **Refuge Harbor** — Provides shelter during storms.
- **Military / Naval Harbor** — Restricted to defense vessels (Karwar Naval Base).
- **Recreational Harbor / Marina** — For yachts and pleasure craft (Goa marinas).
- **Specialized Harbor** — For oil, LNG, or ore (Kandla, Hazira LNG terminal).

**3. Based on Protection:**
- **Fully Protected Harbor** — Surrounded by breakwaters on all sides.
- **Partially Protected Harbor** — Open to sea on one side with breakwaters on others.
- **Unprotected Harbor / Open Roadstead** — No breakwater; relies on favorable weather.

**Block Diagram (Schematic Sketch Representation):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Classification of Harbors] --> B[By Location]
    A --> C[By Usage]
    A --> D[By Protection]
    B --> B1[Natural]
    B --> B2[Artificial]
    B --> B3[Semi-Natural]
    B --> B4[Inland]
    C --> C1[Commercial Port]
    C --> C2[Fishing Harbor]
    C --> C3[Naval Harbor]
    C --> C4[Recreational Marina]
    D --> D1[Fully Protected]
    D --> D2[Partially Protected]
    D --> D3[Open Roadstead]
```

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Scheme (Module 3 — Advantages and Disadvantages):**
>
> 1. **Vague, non-quantified answers:** Students often write "low cost" or "slow speed" without any **numerical backing**. Always cite a typical value: e.g., "₹1 per tonne-km for ocean shipping" or "15–25 knots ship speed." This signals engineering depth to the examiner.
>
> 2. **Forgetting examples:** KTU examiners award marks for **specific named examples** — Mumbai, Chennai, Ennore for Indian harbors; Capesize, Panamax for vessel types. Always include at least 2 examples.
>
> 3. **Confusing natural and artificial harbor definitions:** Some students write "natural harbor = no human intervention" and miss that **dredging and berth construction** are still done. The correct interpretation is that **protection is natural**, even if berthing facilities are engineered.
>
> 4. **Omitting units:** Always write **"₹ per tonne-km"**, **"tonnes"**, **"m/year"** etc. Marks are deducted for missing units in formula-based answers.
>
> 5. **Ignoring the intermodal context:** In water transport comparison, students often forget that water transport **requires intermodal connectivity** (road/rail last-mile). This is a high-value point worth 1–2 marks.
>
> 6. **Not mentioning environmental angle:** KTU 2024 scheme places emphasis on **sustainable engineering**. Mentioning CO₂ footprint (~10–15 g/t-km for shipping) and ecosystem impact earns extra marks.
>
> 7. **Mermaid/Block diagrams not included:** While not compulsory, examiners appreciate a **block diagram or table** in 14-mark answers. It demonstrates structured thinking and earns "presentation marks."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 3: Advantages and Disadvantages**

**A. Core Definitions (must memorize verbatim):**
- Harbor, Natural Harbor, Artificial Harbor, Port, Water Transportation, Breakwater, DWT, Draft, UKC, Hinterland.

**B. Numerical Facts to Remember:**
- Ship speed: **15–25 knots** (~28–46 km/h).
- Ship cost: **~₹1 per tonne-km**; road: **~₹4 per tonne-km**; air: **~₹40 per tonne-km**.
- CO₂ per tonne-km: ship **~10–15 g**, road **~60–100 g**.
- Capesize draft: **18–25 m**; Panamax draft: **~12 m**.
- One Capesize vessel ≈ **7,200 trucks** (~86 km convoy).
- Breakwater cost: **₹50,000–₹2,00,000 per meter run**.
- Natural harbor wave attenuation: **70–90%**.
- Construction time: natural = **2–5 years**; artificial = **8–15 years**.
- Siltation rate: **0.3–1.5 m/year** in estuaries.

**C. Indian Examples to Remember:**
- **Natural Harbors:** Mumbai, Vishakhapatnam, Mormugao, Paradip.
- **Artificial Harbors:** Chennai, Ennore, Tuticorin, Mundra (Gujarat).
- **Semi-Natural:** Kochi (Willingdon Island).
- **Fishing Harbors:** Cochin, Sassoon Dock (Mumbai).
- **Naval:** Karwar, Mumbai (INS Angre).
- **Inland:** Kolkata, Allahabad.

**D. Key Decision Rules:**
- **Always prefer natural harbor** if available at a strategic location (low CAPEX, fast commissioning).
- **Choose artificial harbor** when trade demand justifies the investment and no natural harbor exists.
- **Use water transport for bulk cargo** (>1000 tonnes) over long distance (>200 km) when coast/river is accessible.
- **Channel depth** = Draft × (1 + UKC), with UKC = 0.10 to 0.15 for open channels.

**E. Environmental & Sustainability Hooks:**
- Water transport has **lowest CO₂ per tonne-km** → aligns with KTU 2024 NEP 2020 sustainability focus.
- Coastal shipping can reduce road congestion and emissions.
- Dredging has environmental costs; siltation management is a recurring OPEX.

**F. Top 3 Examination Pointers:**
1. Always **quantify** advantages/disadvantages (₹, m, %, km/h).
2. Always cite **2 Indian examples** for any harbor-related question.
3. Always present a **comparison table** for mode-vs-mode questions.

**G. Common 2-Mark Quick Questions:**
- Define natural harbor.
- List two disadvantages of artificial harbor.
- State the cost ratio of water vs. road transport.
- Name two natural harbors in India.
- What is the typical draft of a Capesize vessel?

**H. Common 14-Mark Long Question Patterns:**
- "Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of natural and artificial harbors with examples." (Often asked.)
- "Compare water, rail, and road transportation as competing modes for cargo movement."
- "Explain the factors influencing the choice between a natural and artificial harbor site."

---

> **END OF NOTES — Module 3: Advantages and Disadvantages | PECET754 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Harbours - Classification
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# HARBOURS – CLASSIFICATION

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET754 - RAILWAY, PORT AND HARBOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Harbours - Classification

A **Harbour** (also spelled *Harbor*) is a partially enclosed body of water, whether natural or artificially constructed, which is sufficiently deep and sheltered to provide safe anchorage for ships and to allow the transfer of cargo and passengers between ships and the shore. According to the **Indian Ports Association (IPA)** and **Permanent International Association of Navigation Congresses (PIANC)** standards followed in KTU syllabi, a harbour is a facility that provides **refuge, loading-unloading berths, repair, and supply services** to vessels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A harbour is essentially a sheltered zone of water where ships can be berthed or anchored safely for various marine operations, irrespective of whether it is a natural inlet or a fully engineered, man-made basin.

The term **"Port"** is a superset — a port is a harbour equipped with **terminal facilities, cargo handling equipment, transit sheds, and hinterland connectivity** (rail/road). Not all harbours are ports (e.g., a refuge harbour), but all major ports are harbours.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Many KTU students use *harbour* and *port* interchangeably. In board examinations, examiners expect the distinction — **Port ⊃ Harbour**, and a port must have commercial traffic-handling infrastructure.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **parking lot for ships**. Just as a multi-storey parking structure protects cars from rain, theft, and congestion, a **harbour protects ships** from waves, currents, storms, and collisions. Some parking lots are naturally formed (a clearing in a forest), some are semi-natural (a clearing partly fenced), and some are entirely constructed (a concrete multi-storey garage). Similarly:

- A **natural harbour** is like a pre-existing natural clearing that needs almost no work.
- A **semi-natural harbour** is one that nature mostly built but humans reinforced.
- An **artificial harbour** is a fully engineered concrete basin — like building a parking garage from scratch in open land.

The **classification of harbours** is essentially the civil engineer's way of **cataloging these "ship garages"** based on *where* they are, *what* they are used for, *how* they are built, and *what physical conditions* they operate in. This systematic classification is the foundation of port planning, design, and operational management.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard maritime engineering parameters are used universally in KTU-evaluated problems:

| Parameter | Standard Metric | Notation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Standard ship size classification | Up to **DWT (Deadweight Tonnage)** | DWT |
| Wave protection limit | Fetch length up to **~100 km** | $F$ |
| Tidal range categories | Micro (< 2 m), Meso (2–4 m), Macro (> 4 m) | $TR$ |
| Harbour depth (typical, Panamax) | **12 m to 14.5 m** | $d$ |
| Harbour depth (typical, Capesize) | **18 m to 22 m** | $d$ |
| Berth length per ship | **L = 1.1 × LOA** | $L_{berth}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **LOA (Length Overall)** of a vessel is the maximum length measured from the tip of the bow to the stern. KTU board questions on harbour planning routinely expect you to compute berth length using $L_{berth} = 1.1 \times LOA$.

## 1.4 Why Classification is Important in KTU Context

KTU 2024 Scheme specifically asks this topic because the choice of **harbour type directly drives**:
1. **Capital Expenditure (CAPEX)** — natural harbours cost less than artificial ones.
2. **Maintenance dredging cycles** — determines annual operational cost.
3. **Vessel class acceptance** — Capesize ships cannot enter shallow harbours.
4. **Breakwater length and orientation** — dictates wave protection design.
5. **Environmental impact assessment** — open roadsteads have lower impact than enclosed basins.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & CLASSIFICATION FRAMEWORK

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Why Harbours Are Classified

The classification of harbours is a **multi-criteria taxonomic exercise** used by port engineers to:
- Identify **site suitability** for a new maritime facility.
- Determine the **structural design** approach (breakwaters, dredging, basin shape).
- Allocate **navigation channels, turning basins, and berthing jetties** correctly.
- Define the **operational regime** (tidal vs. non-tidal locking).

The KTU 2024 syllabus recognizes **five primary classification bases**. Each is discussed below with structured logic.

## 2.2 Classification Based on Location / Position Relative to the Coast

This is the most fundamental and frequently asked classification in KTU board papers.

### 2.2.1 Natural Harbour
A harbour formed entirely by **geological processes** — coastal indentations, bays, river estuaries, or fjords — that provide natural shelter. Ships can anchor and berth with **zero to minimal** man-made intervention.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examples (KTU-frequently cited):** Mumbai (protected by Elephanta Caves headland), Visakhapatnam (dual bay), Durban Bay, Sydney Harbour. The natural breakwater effect is provided by **headlands, promontories, or reefs**.

### 2.2.2 Semi-Natural Harbour
A natural harbour that has been **partially improved** by adding breakwaters, jetties, or dredging to overcome the limitations of its natural configuration. The coastline provides partial shelter, but engineered structures enhance the calmness of the basin.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** Chennai Port (Madras) — naturally a moderately protected coast, supplemented by detached breakwaters to ensure year-round operability.

### 2.2.3 Artificial Harbour (Fully Engineered)
A harbour **entirely constructed by man** on an otherwise open and exposed coast. It requires:
- Two converging **breakwaters** (convergent or parallel type).
- Extensive **dredging** to maintain chart depth.
- **Revetments** along the inner shorelines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examples:** Port of Tangier (Morocco), Port Said (Egypt), and the **deep-water port of Vizhinjam (Kerala)** — currently India's first deep-water transshipment port, a flagship KTU-relevant project for Kerala students.

### 2.2.4 Open Roadstead Harbour
A harbour without any protective breakwater, where ships are **anchored offshore** in relatively open sea and are loaded/unloaded via **lighters (barges) or floating cranes**. Used when:
- Seabed is suitable for anchoring.
- Cargo volume is low to moderate.
- Capital investment for full breakwaters is uneconomical.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Example:** Bedi Bunder in the Gulf of Kutch, Gujarat — an open roadstead with lighterage operations.

## 2.3 Classification Based on Utility / Purpose

| Utility Type | Primary Function | Vessel Type | KTU Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Commercial Harbour** | Cargo + passenger trade | Bulk, container, tankers | Cochin Port |
| **Fishery Harbour** | Fishing fleet operations | Trawlers, purse seiners | Sassoon Docks (Mumbai) |
| **Military / Naval Harbour** | Defence operations | Warships, submarines | INS Kadamba (Karwar) |
| **Refuge / Shelter Harbour** | Storm protection, emergency anchorage | All vessel classes | Porbandar (Gujarat) |
| **Marina Harbour** | Recreational & pleasure craft | Yachts, sailboats | Goa Marina |
| **Ship-breaking Harbour** | Vessel dismantling | End-of-life ships | Alang (Gujarat) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Harbour of Refuge:** A special category, often emphasized in KTU Part A questions. Its sole purpose is to provide **storm shelter** — it may not even have berthing facilities. The site is selected based on a **convenient geographical location** along a busy shipping lane and a **sheltered, deep approach**.

## 2.4 Classification Based on Tidal Influence (Construction Type)

This classification is **critical for structural design** and frequently appears in KTU numerical problems.

### 2.4.1 Tidal Harbour
The water level inside the harbour **fluctuates with the tide** at sea. There is **no lock gate**. The depth at the entrance is critical to ensure vessels can enter at **Lowest Low Water Level (LLWL)** or **Mean Low Water Springs (MLWS)** depending on the design standard.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Design Datum:** Indian ports are typically designed at **CD (Chart Datum)** which corresponds to the **lowest tidal level** predicted. Depths in KTU problems are quoted "below CD."

### 2.4.2 Non-Tidal Harbour (Impounded / Locked Basin)
Used in areas of **high tidal range** (typically > 4 m). A **lock gate** at the entrance maintains a **constant water level** inside the harbour, allowing round-the-clock berthing and cargo handling independent of the tide.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examples:** London Tilbury, most European ports on the Atlantic (e.g., Antwerp, Le Havre). India's Cochin Port, with its outer and inner harbour separated by the **Cochin Gut**, has impounded basins controlled by the **Mattancherry Lock Gate**.

### 2.4.3 River / Canal / Lake Harbours
- **River Harbour** — Constructed along a navigable river (e.g., Kolkata Port on the Hooghly).
- **Canal Harbour** — A small basin connected to a navigable canal.
- **Lake Harbour** — Built on a natural or artificial lake (e.g., Chennai's Ennore backwaters basin).

## 2.5 Classification Based on Special Operational Features

| Feature | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Stranded Harbour** | Becomes dry at low tide; vessels rest on the seabed. Common in macro-tidal regions. |
| **Wet Harbour** | Always retains water, even at low tide (achieved by dredging or impounding). |
| **Half-Tide Basin** | A basin closed by a gate that opens only at high tide to allow vessel entry. |
| **Dock Harbour** | An enclosed dock, often impounded, with quays and lock gates. |

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following formulas are routinely tested in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE and CE papers. **Memorize these.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $L_{berth} = 1.1 \times LOA$ | Berth length from vessel Length Overall | m |
| 2 | $L_{berth,total} = n \times L_{berth} + (n-1) \times L_{gap}$ | Total quay length for n ships | m |
| 3 | $V_{dredge} = L_{ch} \times B_{ch} \times d_{design}$ | Volume of dredging in approach channel | m³ |
| 4 | $R_{wave} = \dfrac{H_{s}}{H_{i}}$ | Wave transmission ratio for breakwater | — |
| 5 | $D_{design} = D_{max,loaded} + UKC$ | Channel design depth (UKC = Under-Keel Clearance, ~10–15% of draft) | m |
| 6 | $F = \dfrac{gT^2}{2\pi}$ | Wave fetch relation (deep water) | m |
| 7 | $T_{avg} = \dfrac{\sum T_i \cdot f_i}{\sum f_i}$ | Weighted average tidal cycle for harbour design | hr |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When a problem says "depth required at the entrance," compute $D_{design} = D_{max,loaded} + UKC$ and **explicitly state the datum** (CD or MSL). Most Indian KTU papers use **CD (Chart Datum)**. Missing this loses 2 marks.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The classification framework drives:
- **Tender document specifications** (e.g., for Vizhinjam International Seaport Thiruvananthapuram).
- **Dredging contracts** (quantities differ vastly between open roadstead and enclosed basins).
- **Environmental clearances** (tidal flushing is better in open harbours; locked harbours need pumping).
- **Selection of breakwater cross-section** (rubble mound for deep water, caisson for shallow tidal).
- **Insurance premiums** — natural harbours attract lower insurance than artificial ones.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP ANALYTICAL FRAMEWORK & DERIVATIONS

Since "Harbours – Classification" is primarily a **conceptual taxonomy topic** rather than a heavy numerical derivation, this section provides the **exhaustive analytical framework** — a full classification matrix with detailed logical flow, decision rules, and the underlying engineering derivations (berth length, channel depth, breakwater length) that govern the *placement* of a harbour in any category.

## 3.1 Analytical Decision Tree: How to Classify Any Given Harbour

When the KTU board gives a description, follow this **four-step logical chain** to classify it:

**Step 1 — Identify the natural protection level.**
- Fully enclosed by land/capes? → **Natural Harbour**.
- Partially protected, supplemented by structures? → **Semi-Natural Harbour**.
- No natural protection, fully engineered? → **Artificial Harbour**.
- No breakwaters at all, offshore lighterage only? → **Open Roadstead Harbour**.

**Step 2 — Identify the primary utility.**
- If warships/submarines dominate → **Naval Harbour**.
- If fishing trawlers → **Fishery Harbour**.
- If yachts/pleasure craft → **Marina**.
- If no commercial operations, only storm shelter → **Harbour of Refuge**.

**Step 3 — Identify the tidal regime.**
- Tidal range > 4 m + lock gate present → **Non-Tidal (Impounded) Harbour**.
- Tidal range < 2 m, no gate → **Tidal Harbour**.
- Becomes dry at LLW → **Stranded Harbour**.

**Step 4 — Identify the receiving water body.**
- Sea coast → Coastal harbour.
- River mouth → **River/Estuarine Harbour**.
- Canal → **Canal Harbour**.
- Lake → **Lake Harbour**.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Berth Length for a Multi-Ship Quay

This is one of the most-tested KTU derivations. We derive the **total quay length** required to berth *n* vessels of varying LOAs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Weight:** 14-mark Part B questions frequently ask: *"A port has to berth 4 Panamax vessels of LOA 250 m each, with an inter-vessel gap of 15 m. Compute the total quay length."*

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Let:
- $L_{berth,i}$ = berth length required for ship $i$ (m).
- $LOA_i$ = Length Overall of ship $i$ (m).
- $L_{gap}$ = safety gap between adjacent berthed ships (m).
- $n$ = number of ships.

By the KTU board-approved empirical rule (PIANC standard):

$$L_{berth,i} = 1.1 \times LOA_i$$

The factor **1.1** accounts for:
- **5%** clearance forward (bow fender clearance).
- **5%** clearance aft (stern clearance for mooring lines).

For *n* berthed vessels in a single continuous quay, the **total quay length** $L_{quay}$ is:

$$L_{quay} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_{berth,i} + (n - 1) \cdot L_{gap}$$

If all ships are identical (the typical KTU case, $LOA_i = LOA$ for all $i$):

$$L_{quay} = n \cdot (1.1 \cdot LOA) + (n - 1) \cdot L_{gap}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style 14-mark problem framework):**

> *"A port is to berth 4 Panamax vessels, each of LOA = 250 m. The standard inter-vessel gap is 15 m. Find the total quay length."*

Applying the formula:

$$L_{quay} = 4 \times (1.1 \times 250) + (4 - 1) \times 15$$

Computing the berth length term:

$$1.1 \times 250 = 275 \text{ m}$$

Therefore:

$$L_{quay} = 4 \times 275 + 3 \times 15$$

$$L_{quay} = 1100 + 45 = 1145 \text{ m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Answer:** $L_{quay} = 1145$ m. KTU board awards **7 marks** for the formula derivation, **4 marks** for substitution, and **3 marks** for the final answer with units.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Channel Design Depth

Channel design depth is **the single most important parameter** distinguishing a harbour's category (e.g., a draught-limited harbour vs. a deep-water harbour).

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

The **design depth of the approach channel** must accommodate the **maximum loaded draft** of the design vessel, plus a **safety under-keel clearance (UKC)** to prevent:
- Squat effect (dynamic sinkage in shallow water).
- Wave-induced vertical motion.
- Bottom-touch margin.

Therefore:

$$D_{design} = D_{max,loaded} + UKC$$

Where $UKC$ is empirically (PIANC) given as **10% to 15% of the maximum loaded draft** for soft bottoms and **5% to 10%** for hard bottoms.

$$UKC = k \cdot D_{max,loaded}, \quad k \in [0.10, 0.15]$$

Substituting:

$$D_{design} = D_{max,loaded} + k \cdot D_{max,loaded} = (1 + k) \cdot D_{max,loaded}$$

For the standard KTU case (soft bottom, $k = 0.15$):

$$D_{design} = 1.15 \cdot D_{max,loaded}$$

**Numerical Example:**

> *"A Capesize bulk carrier has a maximum loaded draft of 18 m. Compute the design depth of the approach channel for a soft-bottom seabed."*

$$D_{design} = 1.15 \times 18 = 20.7 \text{ m}$$

In practice, an additional **net under-keel clearance for squat** of about 0.5 m to 1.0 m is added for channels with high vessel speed (> 8 knots), but the KTU board limits the scope to the basic formula above.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Channel depth in natural harbours** (e.g., Mumbai) is maintained largely by **tidal flushing**, requiring minimal dredging. **Channel depth in artificial harbours** (e.g., Tangier) requires **continuous maintenance dredging**, often at a cost of ₹100 crore/year for major Indian ports. This cost differential is one of the most-cited economic justifications for the natural-vs-artificial classification in KTU viva voce.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Decision Criteria for Harbour Classification

| Classification Basis | Decision Criterion | Decision Rule | Resulting Category |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Natural Protection | Does the coastline provide natural shelter? | Yes — fully | Natural Harbour |
| Natural Protection | Natural + man-made structures? | Yes — partially | Semi-Natural Harbour |
| Natural Protection | No natural shelter, full breakwaters? | Yes | Artificial Harbour |
| Natural Protection | No structures, offshore lighterage? | Yes | Open Roadstead |
| Utility | Warships dominant? | Yes | Naval Harbour |
| Utility | Fishing trawlers dominant? | Yes | Fishery Harbour |
| Utility | Only storm shelter, no cargo? | Yes | Harbour of Refuge |
| Tidal Regime | Tidal range > 4 m + lock gate? | Yes | Non-Tidal (Impounded) |
| Tidal Regime | Tidal range < 2 m, no gate? | Yes | Tidal Harbour |
| Receiving Body | River mouth? | Yes | River/Estuarine Harbour |
| Receiving Body | Inland canal? | Yes | Canal Harbour |
| Receiving Body | Lake/coastal lagoon? | Yes | Lake Harbour |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** A single real-world harbour can belong to **multiple categories simultaneously**. For example, **Cochin Port** is simultaneously a *natural* (backwater bay) *tidal* (lock-gated impounded basin) *commercial* (multi-cargo) harbour. Always state **all applicable categories** in board answers.

## 3.5 Worked Example: Classifying an Unknown Harbour (KTU 14-Mark Pattern)

> *"Describe and classify a harbour located on a rocky coastline with two natural headlands forming a 4 km wide bay. The bay receives a 0.8 m tidal range and currently handles bulk cargo and fishing trawlers. The port authorities have proposed adding a 1.2 km offshore breakwater to reduce wave action during the monsoon."*

**Solution Logic (exhaustive):**

1. **Location analysis** — Rocky coastline with two natural headlands → **Natural protection present** → partial natural harbour.
2. **Proposed intervention** — Addition of a 1.2 km breakwater → Engineering augmentation → **Semi-Natural Harbour**.
3. **Tidal range = 0.8 m** → **Micro-tidal regime** → **Tidal Harbour** (no lock gate needed).
4. **Utility** — Bulk cargo + fishing trawlers → Multi-utility → primarily a **Commercial Harbour with a subsidiary Fishery Harbour** function.
5. **Receiving water body** — Sea coast → **Coastal Harbour**.

**Final classification:** *Semi-Natural, Micro-Tidal, Commercial-and-Fishery Coastal Harbour.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Marking Key:** A 14-mark KTU question on harbour classification typically awards **6 marks** for correctly identifying *all applicable categories*, **4 marks** for justifying each with a one-line reason, and **4 marks** for citing **two real-world example ports** that fit the same classification (e.g., Visakhapatnam for semi-natural, Bedi Bunder for open roadstead).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

The following Mermaid diagrams visualize the classification taxonomy and the functional architecture of a typical harbour. They are designed to be **failure-resistant** (no reserved keyword node IDs, no markdown inside labels, all IDs prefixed with letters).

## 4.1 Master Classification Flow — Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[HARBOUR CLASSIFICATION SYSTEM]
    nodeB[CLASSIFICATION BASIS 1 LOCATION]
    nodeC[CLASSIFICATION BASIS 2 UTILITY]
    nodeD[CLASSIFICATION BASIS 3 TIDAL REGIME]
    nodeE[CLASSIFICATION BASIS 4 RECEIVING WATER BODY]
    nodeF[CLASSIFICATION BASIS 5 CONSTRUCTION]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeA --> nodeE
    nodeA --> nodeF

    nodeB --> nodeB1[NATURAL HARBOUR]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[SEMI NATURAL HARBOUR]
    nodeB --> nodeB3[ARTIFICIAL HARBOUR]
    nodeB --> nodeB4[OPEN ROADSTEAD]

    nodeC --> nodeC1[COMMERCIAL HARBOUR]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[FISHERY HARBOUR]
    nodeC --> nodeC3[NAVAL HARBOUR]
    nodeC --> nodeC4[HARBOUR OF REFUGE]
    nodeC --> nodeC5[MARINA HARBOUR]

    nodeD --> nodeD1[TIDAL HARBOUR]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[NON TIDAL IMPOUNDED HARBOUR]
    nodeD --> nodeD3[STRANDED HARBOUR]

    nodeE --> nodeE1[COASTAL HARBOUR]
    nodeE --> nodeE2[RIVER ESTUARINE HARBOUR]
    nodeE --> nodeE3[CANAL HARBOUR]
    nodeE --> nodeE4[LAKE HARBOUR]

    nodeF --> nodeF1[BREAKWATER PROTECTED]
    nodeF --> nodeF2[PIER AND WHARF TYPE]
    nodeF --> nodeF3[DOCK TYPE LOCKED]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Harbour (Block-Level)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeSea[OPEN SEA SIDE] --> nodeChan[APPROACH CHANNEL]
    nodeChan --> nodeEntr[ENTRANCE]
    nodeEntr --> nodeTurn[TURNING BASIN]
    nodeTurn --> nodeBerth[BERTHING JETTIES]
    nodeBerth --> nodeQuay[QUAY APRON]
    nodeQuay --> nodeShed[TRANSIT SHED]
    nodeShed --> nodeStock[YARD STORAGE]
    nodeStock --> nodeHinter[HINTERLAND RAIL ROAD]
    nodeHinter --> nodeCustom[CUSTOMS GATE]

    nodeBreak1[MAIN BREAKWATER] --- nodeSea
    nodeBreak2[SUB BREAKWATER] --- nodeSea
    nodeMole[MOLE] --- nodeBerth
    nodePier[PIER] --- nodeBerth
    nodeDock[DOCK LOCK GATE] --- nodeEntr
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** A typical KTU-illustrated harbour has a layered architecture — the **approach channel** brings vessels from open sea, the **entrance** (often lock-gated in tidal harbours) admits them to a calmer **turning basin**, from which they berth at **jetties/piers/quays**. The **breakwaters** (rubble mound or caisson) provide wave protection. Cargo then moves **transit shed → storage yard → hinterland rail/road → customs clearance**.

## 4.3 Schematic: Natural vs Artificial Harbour Layout

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeN[NATURAL HARBOUR ELEV MUMBAI]
    nodeN --> nodeN1[HEADLAND 1]
    nodeN --> nodeN2[BAY BODY]
    nodeN --> nodeN3[HEADLAND 2]
    nodeN --> nodeN4[BERTHS INSIDE BAY]
    nodeN --> nodeN5[NO MAJOR BREAKWATER]

    nodeAR[ARTIFICIAL HARBOUR ELEV TANGIER]
    nodeAR --> nodeAR1[OPEN COAST NO NATURAL SHELTER]
    nodeAR --> nodeAR2[MAIN BREAKWATER]
    nodeAR --> nodeAR3[SUB BREAKWATER]
    nodeAR --> nodeAR4[ENTRANCE CHANNEL]
    nodeAR --> nodeAR5[ARTIFICIAL BASIN]
    nodeAR --> nodeAR6[QUAY WALLS]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparative Insight:** A natural harbour has its **breakwater effect provided by the headlands** (e.g., Mumbai — *Hornby Vellard* point and *Elephanta* headland), whereas an artificial harbour has its breakwaters **engineered from scratch** as concrete/rubble structures. This single difference justifies the entire cost differential in KTU economic analysis problems.

## 4.4 Decision Logic Flow — Classifying a Given Harbour

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[START IDENTIFY HARBOUR] --> q1{NATURAL SHELTER BY COAST}
    q1 -- YES FULL --> a1[NATURAL HARBOUR]
    q1 -- PARTIAL --> a2[SEMI NATURAL HARBOUR]
    q1 -- NO --> a3{STRUCTURES PRESENT}
    a3 -- YES BREAKWATER --> a4[ARTIFICIAL HARBOUR]
    a3 -- NO --> a5[OPEN ROADSTEAD]

    a1 --> q2{PRIMARY PURPOSE}
    a2 --> q2
    a4 --> q2
    a5 --> q2
    q2 --> c1[COMMERCIAL]
    q2 --> c2[FISHERY]
    q2 --> c3[NAVAL]
    q2 --> c4[REFUGE]
    q2 --> c5[MARINA]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define a harbour and distinguish between a harbour and a port.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **harbour** is a sheltered body of water, natural or artificial, that provides safe anchorage, berthing, and shelter for ships during loading, unloading, and storm conditions. The primary function is **protection of vessels**.

A **port** is a harbour that has been **fully equipped with terminal facilities** — cargo-handling equipment (cranes, conveyors), transit sheds, warehouses, customs offices, and hinterland transport connectivity (rail/road links) — to enable commercial trade.

**Key Distinction:**

- **Harbour:** Protection + anchorage → may have no commercial traffic.
- **Port:** Harbour + commercial infrastructure → always handles cargo/passengers.

> All ports are harbours, but not all harbours are ports. **Harbour of Refuge** is an example of a harbour that is not a port.

> **Mark Split:** 1 mark for harbour definition, 1 mark for port definition, 1 mark for the distinction + example.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**What is a harbour of refuge? Give two examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **harbour of refuge** (also called a *shelter harbour* or *haven*) is a harbour that exists **exclusively to provide storm shelter and emergency anchorage** to vessels in distress. It is **not** primarily designed for cargo or passenger handling, though it may have auxiliary berthing facilities.

The selection criteria for a harbour of refuge are:
1. **Convenient location** along a frequently used shipping route.
2. **Natural deep-water approach** that does not require extensive dredging.
3. **Naturally sheltered** from prevailing winds and waves.
4. **Easy access and exit** during storm conditions.

**Examples:**
- **Porbandar**, Gujarat — naturally sheltered bay on the western Indian coast.
- **Port Shelter**, Hong Kong — a designated typhoon refuge for the South China Sea fleet.

> **Mark Split:** 2 marks for definition + criteria, 1 mark for examples.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
*(a)* Classify harbours based on their **location relative to the coast**, with suitable sketches and examples. (7 marks)
*(b)* A port is to berth **5 Panamax vessels**, each of LOA = 245 m, with a standard inter-vessel gap of 12 m. Compute the **total quay length required**. State any assumptions made. (7 marks)

---

**Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks):**

Harbours are classified into **four types** based on location relative to the coast:

1. **Natural Harbour** — A harbour formed entirely by geological processes. The coastline's natural features (headlands, bays, promontories, fjords) provide inherent wave protection. **Examples:** Mumbai Harbour, Sydney Harbour, Durban Bay. *(1.5 marks)*

2. **Semi-Natural Harbour** — A natural harbour that has been enhanced with **man-made breakwaters, jetties, or dredging** to overcome limitations of the natural configuration. The site offered some natural protection, but full all-weather operability required structural augmentation. **Examples:** Chennai Port, Visakhapatnam Port. *(1.5 marks)*

3. **Artificial Harbour** — A harbour **entirely constructed by engineers** on an otherwise open, exposed coast. It requires:
   - Two converging breakwaters (rubble mound or caisson type).
   - Dredging to maintain design depth.
   - Revetments along inner shorelines.
   
   **Examples:** Port Said (Egypt), Tangier (Morocco), Vizhinjam International Seaport (Kerala). *(2 marks)*

4. **Open Roadstead Harbour** — A harbour with **no breakwaters** at all. Vessels are anchored offshore in relatively open water; cargo is transferred via **lighters, barges, or floating cranes**. Suitable for moderate traffic and naturally suitable seabed anchoring. **Examples:** Bedi Bunder (Gujarat), some older Gulf ports. *(2 marks)*

> Refer to **Section 4.3 schematic** above for the visual comparison of natural vs artificial.

> **Mark Split:** 1 mark for each type's definition, 0.5 mark for each example, 0.5 mark for sub-criteria. Total 7.

---

**Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Number of vessels: $n = 5$
- LOA per vessel: $LOA = 245$ m
- Inter-vessel gap: $L_{gap} = 12$ m

**Formula (PIANC / KTU standard):**

$$L_{quay} = n \cdot (1.1 \cdot LOA) + (n - 1) \cdot L_{gap}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. All vessels are identical in LOA.
2. The 1.1 factor accounts for 5% bow clearance + 5% stern clearance (PIANC standard).
3. Inter-vessel gap of 12 m is constant and accounts for fender compression and mooring line slack.
4. Berths are continuous (no piers separating groups).

**Step 1: Berth length per vessel.**

$$L_{berth} = 1.1 \times 245 = 269.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Total berth length for 5 vessels.**

$$L_{berths,total} = 5 \times 269.5 = 1347.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Total inter-vessel gaps.**

$$(n - 1) \cdot L_{gap} = 4 \times 12 = 48 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Total quay length.**

$$L_{quay} = 1347.5 + 48 = 1395.5 \text{ m}$$

> **Final Answer:** $L_{quay} = 1395.5$ m $\approx 1396$ m.

> **Mark Split:** 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for assumptions, 2 marks for substitution, 2 marks for final answer with units, 1 mark for cross-check or discussion.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] — *Alternative Choice*
*(a)* Discuss the **classification of harbours based on tidal influence**, with neat sketches. Differentiate between tidal, non-tidal, and stranded harbours. (7 marks)
*(b)* A harbour channel is to be designed for a **Capesize bulk carrier** with a maximum loaded draft of **17.5 m**. The seabed is **soft silt**. Calculate the **design depth of the approach channel** using the KTU standard formula. (7 marks)

---

**Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks):**

Classification of harbours based on tidal influence:

1. **Tidal Harbour (Open Basin)** — The water level inside the harbour **rises and falls with the tide**. There is **no lock gate** at the entrance. Vessels enter and leave freely at any tide stage above LLWL. **Design depth is referenced to Chart Datum (CD)**, the lowest predictable tide. Common in **micro-tidal (TR < 2 m)** and **meso-tidal (TR 2–4 m)** regions.
   
   **Examples:** Mumbai, Cochin (Outer Harbour), most Indian ports. *(2 marks)*

2. **Non-Tidal Harbour (Impounded / Locked Basin)** — In **macro-tidal (TR > 4 m)** regions, a **lock gate** is provided at the entrance. The water level inside is **maintained at a constant level** (typically the level of Mean High Water Springs, MHWS). This allows round-the-clock berthing, regardless of the outside tide. Vessels enter only when the **lock is filled** to match the sea level.
   
   **Examples:** London Tilbury, Antwerp, Le Havre, and India's **Cochin Inner Harbour** (controlled by the Mattancherry Lock). *(2.5 marks)*

3. **Stranded Harbour** — A harbour in a **macro-tidal region where the basin is allowed to become dry at low tide**. Vessels **rest on the seabed** (often on prepared soft moorings) during the low-water period. Loading/unloading is done at high tide. This is a **low-capital solution** for small fishing harbours. *(1.5 marks)*

4. **Half-Tide Basin** — A small basin **closed by a gate that opens only near high tide**, allowing vessels to enter and then trapping the water as the tide recedes. The retained water provides a few hours of floating depth. *(1 mark)*

> **Mark Split:** 1.5 marks for each of the first three types, 1 mark for half-tide basin, 0.5 mark for distinguishing feature.

---

**Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Maximum loaded draft: $D_{max,loaded} = 17.5$ m
- Seabed: soft silt → use $k = 0.15$ (PIANC UKC for soft bottom)

**Step 1: Recall the KTU formula.**

$$D_{design} = (1 + k) \cdot D_{max,loaded}$$

Where $k$ = UKC factor as a fraction of the maximum loaded draft.

**Step 2: Identify $k$.**

For **soft silt seabed**, PIANC and KTU board standard: $k = 0.15$ (i.e., 15% UKC).

**Step 3: Substitute.**

$$D_{design} = (1 + 0.15) \times 17.5$$

$$D_{design} = 1.15 \times 17.5$$

**Step 4: Compute.**

$$D_{design} = 20.125 \text{ m}$$

> **Final Answer:** The design depth of the approach channel is **20.125 m**, referenced to **Chart Datum (CD)**. In practice, an additional allowance of **0.5 m to 1.0 m** is added for vessel squat in high-speed channels, taking the **operational dredging depth to ~21 m**.

> **Mark Split:** 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for $k$ identification, 2 marks for substitution, 2 marks for answer, 1 mark for the squat/CD discussion.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Datum Error:** Stating the channel depth "20.125 m" without specifying it is *below Chart Datum* loses 1 mark. Always write "**20.125 m below CD**."
> 2. **UKC Factor Error:** Using $k = 0.10$ (hard rock) for soft silt loses 1 mark. Memorize: *soft seabed → 15% UKC; hard seabed → 10% UKC.*
> 3. **Single-Category Classification:** A KTU question like "Classify the harbour" expects **all applicable categories**, not just one. Cochin is *natural + tidal + commercial + coastal*. Listing only "natural" loses 3 marks.
> 4. **Confusing Harbour and Port:** The first sentence of any answer should clarify the distinction. Examiners do deduct 1 mark for interchange.
> 5. **Unit Omission:** Quoting $L_{quay} = 1395.5$ without "**m**" loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **harbour** is a *sheltered water body*; a **port** is a *harbour + commercial infrastructure*. *(Always clarify in opening line.)*
- **Five classification bases:** Location, Utility, Tidal regime, Receiving water body, Construction. *(List at least 3 in any 14-mark answer.)*
- **Four location-based types:** Natural, Semi-Natural, Artificial, Open Roadstead.
- **Harbour of Refuge** = storm shelter only; not a port. Examples: Porbandar, Port Shelter (Hong Kong).
- **Berth length formula:** $L_{berth} = 1.1 \times LOA$ — the 1.1 factor = 5% bow + 5% stern clearance.
- **Total quay for *n* ships:** $L_{quay} = n \cdot (1.1 \cdot LOA) + (n - 1) \cdot L_{gap}$.
- **Channel design depth:** $D_{design} = 1.15 \cdot D_{max,loaded}$ (soft seabed) — reference datum is **Chart Datum (CD)**.
- **Tidal categories:** Micro (< 2 m), Meso (2–4 m), Macro (> 4 m). Lock gates are economical only for **macro-tidal** regions.
- **Standard vessel classes:** Panamax (LOA ~ 245–295 m, draft ~ 12–14.5 m), Capesize (LOA ~ 290 m, draft ~ 18 m).
- **Famous natural harbours in KTU syllabus:** Mumbai, Visakhapatnam, Sydney.
- **Famous artificial harbours in KTU syllabus:** Vizhinjam (Kerala — under construction, **important for KTU context**), Tangier, Port Said.
- **Open Roadstead example:** Bedi Bunder (Gujarat).
- **Kerala-specific KTU relevance:** Vizhinjam International Seaport Thiruvananthapuram is India's first **deep-water transshipment port** with natural depth of **20+ m** close to shore — a flagship example of a **natural deep-water harbour** on Kerala's coast.
- **Always state assumptions** before numerical substitution — KTU examiners award 1 mark for the assumptions block.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Requirements and Characteristics of Good Harbour
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET754 - RAILWAY, PORT AND HARBOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Requirements and Characteristics of Good Harbour

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Harbour** is a sheltered body of water, either natural or artificially created, where ships, boats, and other waterborne vessels can find refuge, anchor safely, and conduct loading/unloading operations protected from the destructive forces of waves, winds, currents, and storms. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (PECET754) and standard port engineering literature, a harbour is a comprehensive maritime infrastructure that combines **breakwaters, approach channels, anchorages, berthing facilities, turning basins, transit sheds, and hinterland connectivity** to provide a complete operating environment for vessels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinction between Harbour, Port, and Dock — KTU Frequently Tested Concept**
> - **Harbour**: The physical, sheltered water body (protected zone) where vessels anchor and take shelter.
> - **Port**: The entire commercial complex, including the harbour plus its cargo-handling terminals, storage yards, transit sheds, customs, and transport links.
> - **Dock**: An enclosed water space within a harbour used for loading/unloading, with a system of locks that allow water level to be maintained independent of tides (e.g., **Wet Dock**, **Dry Dock**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** A *good harbour* is not merely a sheltered water body — it must satisfy the **functional requirements** (depth, area, approach, anchorage) and possess the **physical characteristics** (tidal range, holding ground, weather protection) that ensure safe, efficient, and economical operation of vessels throughout their service life.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a large open-air swimming pool versus a sheltered indoor swimming pool during a storm. The outdoor pool is useless when waves crash in and winds whip the water — you cannot safely dock boats. The indoor pool, however, is calm, protected, and always usable.

**A harbour is exactly this "indoor pool" for ships** — a man-made (or naturally-sheltered) calm water zone where massive ships — sometimes 300+ metres long — can safely:

1. **Stop and rest** (anchorage).
2. **Turn around** (turning basin).
3. **Park alongside a wall** (berthing/quay).
4. **Load and unload cargo** (cargo handling).

Just like an indoor pool needs strong walls, a roof, controlled entry/exit, and enough depth, a harbour needs **breakwaters, dredged approach channels, deep berths, and good hinterland connections**.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** Think **H.A.L.T.** for the four pillars of a good harbour:
> **H**olding ground good, **A**dequate depth & area, **L**ittle tidal range, **T**rouble-free approach.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

The following **standard KTU-recognised engineering metrics** govern harbour design:

- **Minimum water depth in approach channel:** typically **1.1 to 1.5 times** the maximum draft of the design vessel, accounting for **squat, trim, wave-induced motion, and under-keel clearance (UKC)**.
- **Minimum depth at berth:** generally **1.1 × LOA draft + 1.2 m** additional clearance.
- **Tidal range tolerated (ideal):** less than **1.0 m** for an ideal harbour; up to **3.0 m** is acceptable with tidal basins.
- **Turning basin diameter:** typically **2.0 to 2.5 times the LOA** (Length Overall) of the largest design ship.
- **Wave height allowed inside harbour:** generally less than **0.5 m** during normal operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **What makes a harbour "good"?** A good harbour must be **technically efficient, economically viable, environmentally sustainable, and operationally safe** — failure on any of these counts renders the design unacceptable in KTU board examinations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Requirements of a Good Harbour

The **operational logic** of harbour design is based on a hierarchical set of requirements. Each requirement addresses a specific failure mode that could compromise vessel safety or port productivity.

#### 2.1.1 Adequate Depth of Water

**Why needed:** A ship floats when the buoyant force equals its weight. The vessel sits at a particular **draft** (vertical distance from waterline to keel). If water depth is insufficient, the ship will **ground** — causing hull damage, propeller strike, or total loss.

**How it is achieved:**
- Natural deep water (e.g., Mumbai harbour with depths of **10–12 m**).
- Dredging of approach channels and basins.
- Use of **tidal advantage** — entering at high tide.

**Depth equation:**

$$D_{\text{required}} = D_{\text{ship}} + S_{\text{squat}} + U_{\text{UKC}} + W_{\text{wave}}$$

Where:
- $D_{\text{ship}}$ = static draft of loaded vessel (m).
- $S_{\text{squat}}$ = sinkage due to forward motion, typically **0.3 to 0.8 m**.
- $U_{\text{UKC}}$ = under-keel clearance, minimum **10% of draft** or **0.6 m**, whichever is greater.
- $W_{\text{wave}}$ = allowance for wave-induced vertical motion, **0.3 to 0.5 m** for sheltered harbours.

#### 2.1.2 Adequate Area of Water

**Why needed:** Multiple ships must simultaneously anchor, maneuver, and berth. Congestion causes collisions, delays, and economic loss.

**How it is achieved:**
- Natural bays and estuaries providing large surface area.
- Artificial breakwaters enclosing a basin.
- Segregation into **inner harbour**, **outer harbour**, **anchorage zones**, and **turning basins**.

**Area equation (anchorage density):**

$$A_{\text{anchorage}} = N \times \pi r^2$$

Where $r$ = anchor swing radius (typically **6 × ship length** for single anchor, **4 × ship length** for two anchors), and $N$ = number of vessels accommodated.

#### 2.1.3 Good Holding Ground

**Why needed:** The seabed must provide adequate **anchor grip** so that a vessel does not drag during storms or strong currents. Soft mud, loose sand, and rocky bottoms all fail differently.

**How it is ensured:**
- **Preferred seabed:** stiff clay, compacted sand, or mud with clay content.
- **Avoided seabed:** loose silt, rock, coral, or shifting sand.

> [!WARNING]
> A harbour with deep water but poor holding ground (e.g., smooth rock) is **dangerous** — a single storm can cause multiple vessels to drag anchor and collide.

#### 2.1.4 Safe and Easy Approach

**Why needed:** Ships must enter and exit the harbour under all weather conditions. A tortuous, narrow, or current-swept approach is a recipe for groundings.

**How it is ensured:**
- A **straight approach channel** of adequate width.
- **Navigational aids**: lighthouses, buoys, leading lights, radar beacons, GPS markers.
- **Adequate turning circle** before the entrance.

**Channel width equation (PIANC guidelines):**

$$W_{\text{channel}} = 1.5 \times B_{\text{beam}} + 2 \times \text{lateral clearance}$$

For a Panamax vessel of beam $B = 32.2$ m: $W_{\text{channel}} \approx 1.5(32.2) + 2(0.5B) = 48.3 + 32.2 \approx 80.5$ m (one-way), about **160–200 m** for two-way traffic.

#### 2.1.5 Protection from Waves, Winds, and Storms

**Why needed:** Storm waves can be **6–10 m high** in open seas and would destroy any moored vessel.

**How it is ensured:**
- **Natural protection:** bays, headlands, islands, reefs.
- **Artificial protection:** breakwaters (rubble mound, caisson, composite).
- **Strategic orientation** of harbour entrance to face away from prevailing storm direction.

#### 2.1.6 Small Tidal Range

**Why needed:** Large tides (e.g., Gulf of Cambay, India, with **>10 m range**) require continuous dredging, expensive lock systems, and complex cargo handling.

**How it is managed:**
- **Closed docks** with locks (e.g., London, Antwerp).
- **Tidal basins** with supplementary impounded docks.
- Acceptable range: **< 2 m** ideally.

#### 2.1.7 Economical Construction and Maintenance

**Why needed:** A harbour that is technically perfect but financially unviable is a commercial failure.

**How it is ensured:**
- **Initial capital cost** of breakwaters, dredging, and quay walls.
- **Annual maintenance cost** of dredging, repairs, and equipment.
- **Cost-benefit analysis** based on cargo throughput projections.

---

### 2.2 Characteristics of a Good Harbour

A good harbour exhibits the following **physical and operational characteristics** that distinguish it from a mere sheltered water body:

| # | Characteristic | Description | KTU Relevance |
|---|----------------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Sufficient Size** | Adequate water area for present and future traffic | Frequently asked in Part A |
| 2 | **Adequate Depth** | Deeper than the maximum draft of design vessels | Direct question every year |
| 3 | **Suitable Shape** | Compact, easy to defend, with wide entrance | High-weightage in design questions |
| 4 | **Small Tidal Range** | Preferably < 1 m | Common in comparison questions |
| 5 | **Good Holding Ground** | Seabed that grips anchors | Frequently tested |
| 6 | **Easy Approach** | Straight, wide, well-marked channel | Tested in layout questions |
| 7 | **Protection from Forces** | Breakwaters, headlands, orientation | Module 3 core topic |
| 8 | **Adequate Berthing** | Sufficient quay length for expected fleet | Tested in Part B problems |
| 9 | **Good Hinterland** | Rail/road connectivity | Strategic and economic question |
| 10 | **Silt-Free Basin** | Minimal sedimentation, low dredging | Frequently tested |
| 11 | **Cheap Construction** | Economically viable | Common Part A question |

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates all high-yield formulas and design metrics tested in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations for Module 3.

| S.No. | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Typical Value / Unit | Engineering Use |
|-------|---------------------|------------|----------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Required water depth | $D = d + S + U + W$ | metres (m) | Channel/berth design |
| 2 | Under-keel clearance (UKC) | $U = \max(0.1 d, 0.6)$ m | **0.6 m to 1.5 m** | Safety margin |
| 3 | Ship squat | $S = \dfrac{C_b \cdot V^2}{100}$ (approx.) | **0.3 to 0.8 m** | Dynamic sinkage |
| 4 | Turning basin diameter | $D_t = 2.0$ to $2.5 \times L_{LOA}$ | metres (m) | Maneuvering zone |
| 5 | Anchorage area (single ship) | $A = \pi r^2$, $r = 6 L$ | square metres (m²) | Mooring zone |
| 6 | Channel width (one-way) | $W = 1.5 B + 2 \times 0.5 B$ | **80–120 m** | Approach design |
| 7 | Channel width (two-way) | $W_{2} = 2 \times W_1 + \text{clearance}$ | **160–250 m** | Two-way traffic |
| 8 | Berth length | $L_b = L_{LOA} + (1.0 \text{ to } 1.5)$ m | metres (m) | Quay design |
| 9 | Wave height (acceptable) | $H_s \leq 0.5$ m inside harbour | metres (m) | Operational limit |
| 10 | Tidal range (ideal) | $\Delta T < 1.0$ m | metres (m) | Site selection |
| 11 | Approach channel length | $L_c \geq 5$ to $10 \times L_{LOA}$ | metres (m) | Maneuvering safety |
| 12 | Holding ground pull-out force | $F = W_{anchor} \cdot \mu$ | kilonewtons (kN) | Anchor design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All metric units must be **consistent (SI)**. KTU examiners award zero credit for mixed-unit answers (e.g., depth in feet, length in metres).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These requirements and characteristics are not academic — they directly govern the success of major world ports:

- **Mumbai Port Trust (MbPT):** Natural deep harbour with depths of **10–11 m**; minor tidal range (~1.5 m).
- **Cochin Port (Kerala):** All-weather natural harbour with **>15 m depth** at outer channel.
- **Kandla Port (Gujarat):** Located on Kandla Creek, has **large tidal range (~6 m)**, requiring lock-gate impounded docks.
- **Visakhapatnam Port:** Twin breakwaters (one of the **longest in India**, ~3.6 km) protecting an artificial harbour on the east coast.
- **Singapore Port:** World-class depth of **>16 m**, minimal tidal range, deep natural approach — making it one of the busiest container ports globally.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When comparing two harbours in a 14-mark question, always structure your answer as a **table** comparing 5–6 characteristics — this earns full marks for systematic presentation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Natural vs Artificial Harbours

This **comparative matrix** is one of the most frequently tested 14-mark question patterns in KTU 2024 examinations:

| Parameter | Natural Harbour | Artificial Harbour |
|-----------|-----------------|---------------------|
| **Definition** | Naturally protected by headlands, bays, or islands | Constructed using breakwaters and dredging |
| **Depth** | Often very deep (e.g., Singapore, Cochin) | Requires continuous dredging |
| **Construction Cost** | Low initial cost | Very high (breakwaters cost ₹50,000–₹2,00,000 per metre run) |
| **Siltation** | Generally less | More, due to breakwater-induced current changes |
| **Examples** | Mumbai, Cochin, Sydney | Visakhapatnam, Chennai, Chennai Port |
| **Maintenance** | Moderate | High (dredging + structural) |
| **Time to Develop** | Existing or minor modification | 5–15 years |
| **Flexibility** | Limited by natural geometry | Designed to exact requirements |
| **Hinterland** | Often restricted by terrain | Can be planned |
| **Best Suited For** | Long-established, deep-draft traffic | Modern, large-scale container traffic |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Required Water Depth in Approach Channel

This is the **most frequently tested derivation** in KTU Module 3.

**Given:** A Panamax bulk carrier with:
- Static loaded draft $d = 14.5$ m.
- Service speed $V = 12$ knots ($\approx 6.17$ m/s).
- Block coefficient $C_b = 0.72$.
- Design wave height in channel $H_s = 0.6$ m.
- Soft clay seabed (allowable UKC = 10% of draft).

**Step 1: Establish static draft.**

$$d = 14.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Compute ship squat using simplified Barrass formula.**

$$\begin{aligned}
S &= \dfrac{C_b \cdot V^2}{100} \\
S &= \dfrac{0.72 \times (6.17)^2}{100} \\
S &= \dfrac{0.72 \times 38.07}{100} \\
S &= \dfrac{27.41}{100} \\
S &= 0.274 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

> *Logic:* Squat is the additional sinkage caused by Bernoulli-effect water flow beneath the moving hull. It increases with the square of speed.

**Step 3: Compute under-keel clearance (UKC).**

$$\begin{aligned}
U &= \max(0.1 \times d, 0.6) \\
U &= \max(0.1 \times 14.5, 0.6) \\
U &= \max(1.45, 0.6) \\
U &= 1.45 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

> *Logic:* UKC is a safety buffer that accounts for trim, list, squat variation, and instrument error. PIANC prescribes a minimum of 10% of draft for soft seabeds.

**Step 4: Compute wave-induced vertical motion allowance.**

$$W = 0.5 \times H_s = 0.5 \times 0.6 = 0.3 \text{ m}$$

> *Logic:* In a sheltered channel, the ship rides the wave at roughly half the wave height — accounting for the worst-case vertical excursion of the keel.

**Step 5: Sum all components for required depth.**

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{required}} &= d + S + U + W \\
D_{\text{required}} &= 14.5 + 0.274 + 1.45 + 0.3 \\
D_{\text{required}} &= 16.524 \text{ m} \\
D_{\text{required}} &\approx 16.5 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

> *Logic:* This is the minimum water depth from seabed to mean low water springs (MLWS) needed for safe transit. The harbour authority must dredge to this depth and maintain it.

**Step 6: Add tidal and safety margin (optional, for design verification).**

$$D_{\text{design}} = D_{\text{required}} + \text{Margin} = 16.5 + 0.5 = 17.0 \text{ m (final design depth)}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common Site Selection Criteria

KTU frequently tests this as a 7-mark sub-part. The matrix is the answer skeleton:

| Criterion | Preferred Value / Condition | Engineering Rationale |
|-----------|----------------------------|----------------------|
| **Depth at approach** | ≥ 1.2 × max vessel draft | Avoids grounding |
| **Tidal range** | < 2.0 m (ideal < 1.0 m) | Reduces lock cost |
| **Wave height (open sea)** | < 6 m for 95% of year | Lower breakwater cost |
| **Currents at entrance** | < 1.5 m/s | Safe navigation |
| **Seabed material** | Stiff clay, compacted sand | Good holding ground |
| **Geology of coast** | Stable rock or stiff soil | Supports breakwater loads |
| **Hinterland gradient** | Gentle, below 2% | Easy rail/road connection |
| **Tidal basin area** | Adequate for projected traffic | Prevents congestion |
| **Distance from city** | 5–25 km | Pollution, congestion control |
| **Seismic zone** | Low-to-moderate | Structural safety |

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Logical Walkthrough — "Is the Site a Good Harbour?"

This **decision-tree logic** is what KTU expects in 14-mark design questions:

**Step 1 — Bathymetry Check:**
Verify natural depth $D_n \geq D_{\text{required}}$. If yes, proceed. If no, dredging cost must be evaluated.

**Step 2 — Holding Ground Verification:**
Sample seabed. If $C_u > 5$ kPa (undrained shear strength), acceptable. Otherwise, reject site or use suction piles.

**Step 3 — Tidal Data Analysis:**
Compute $\Delta T$ over 19-year cycle. If $\Delta T < 1$ m → ideal. If $1 \leq \Delta T \leq 3$ m → acceptable with tidal basin. If $\Delta T > 3$ m → require impounded docks with locks.

**Step 4 — Wave Climate Study:**
Use hindcast data (typically 30 years) for design wave. If $H_{\text{design}} < 4$ m → rubble mound breakwater feasible. If $H_{\text{design}} > 6$ m → composite/caisson breakwater required.

**Step 5 — Approach Geometry:**
Plot vessel track. If $R_{\text{turn}} \leq 3 \times L_{LOA}$, single turning basin works. If $R_{\text{turn}} > 3 L_{LOA}$, multiple basins or pilot-assisted turning required.

**Step 6 — Hinterland Connectivity:**
Verify road/rail network within 5 km. Otherwise, factor in additional hinterland cost (typically 15–25% of project cost).

**Step 7 — Economic Feasibility:**
Compute Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR). Accept site if $BCR \geq 1.5$. Otherwise, downgrade port capacity or reject.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU answer sheets, this **step-by-step evaluation tree** style is worth full 14 marks when supported by sketches and tables.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Layout of a Typical Harbour — Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture flow** of a typical harbour, showing all critical components and their interactions (rendered in lieu of a physical cross-section to comply with diagram safeguards).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[OPEN SEA APPROACH ZONE] --> A2[NAVIGATION BUOYS AND LEADING LIGHTS]
    A2 --> A3[APPROACH CHANNEL DREDGED DEPTH 16 TO 20 M]
    A3 --> A4[HARBOUR ENTRANCE CONTROLLED BY BREAKWATERS]
    A4 --> A5[TURNING BASIN DIAMETER 2 TO 2.5 LOA]
    A5 --> A6[ANCHORAGE ZONE HOLDING GROUND CLAY]
    A5 --> A7[BERTHING QUAY LENGTH = LOA PLUS 1.5 M]
    A7 --> A8[CARGO HANDLING ZONE SHEDS CRANES]
    A8 --> A9[STORAGE YARD CONTAINERS BULK]
    A9 --> A10[HINTERLAND ROAD AND RAIL CONNECTIVITY]
    A4 --> A11[BREAKWATER MAIN LENGTH UP TO 4 KM]
    A4 --> A12[BREAKWATER SECONDARY LENGTH UP TO 2 KM]
    A11 --> A13[WAVE ATTENUATION ZONE H S LESS THAN 0.5 M]
    A12 --> A13
    A13 --> A5
    A5 --> A14[TIDAL BASIN IF RANGE EXCEEDS 1 M]
    A14 --> A15[LOCK GATE STRUCTURE FOR IMPOUNDED DOCKS]
    A15 --> A16[DRY DOCK FOR SHIP REPAIR]
```

### 4.2 Hierarchy of Harbour Requirements — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R0[REQUIREMENTS OF A GOOD HARBOUR] --> R1[FUNCTIONAL REQUIREMENTS]
    R0 --> R2[PHYSICAL CHARACTERISTICS]
    R0 --> R3[OPERATIONAL REQUIREMENTS]
    R1 --> R1A[ADEQUATE DEPTH]
    R1 --> R1B[ADEQUATE AREA]
    R1 --> R1C[SAFE APPROACH CHANNEL]
    R1 --> R1D[PROPER BERTHING FACILITIES]
    R2 --> R2A[GOOD HOLDING GROUND]
    R2 --> R2B[SMALL TIDAL RANGE]
    R2 --> R2C[PROTECTION FROM WAVES AND WINDS]
    R2 --> R2D[FREE FROM SILTATION]
    R3 --> R3A[ECONOMICAL CONSTRUCTION]
    R3 --> R3B[LOW MAINTENANCE COST]
    R3 --> R3C[STRATEGIC LOCATION]
    R3 --> R3D[GOOD HINTERLAND CONNECTIVITY]
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Harbour Design Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[SITE SELECTION] --> S2[BATHYMETRIC SURVEY]
    S2 --> S3[DEPTH ANALYSIS]
    S2 --> S4[SEDIMENT STUDY]
    S2 --> S5[WAVE CLIMATE STUDY]
    S2 --> S6[TIDAL DATA COLLECTION]
    S3 --> S7{DEPTH SUFFICIENT}
    S4 --> S8{SILTATION RATE}
    S5 --> S9{BREAKWATER FEASIBLE}
    S6 --> S10{TIDAL RANGE}
    S7 -->|NO| S11[DREDGING REQUIRED]
    S7 -->|YES| S12[PROCEED]
    S8 -->|HIGH| S13[REJECT SITE]
    S8 -->|LOW| S12
    S9 -->|NO| S14[REJECT SITE]
    S9 -->|YES| S12
    S10 -->|HIGH| S15[LOCK DOCK REQUIRED]
    S10 -->|LOW| S12
    S11 --> S12
    S12 --> S16[LAYOUT DESIGN]
    S15 --> S16
    S16 --> S17[COST ESTIMATION]
    S17 --> S18{BCR GREATER 1.5}
    S18 -->|NO| S19[REVISE]
    S18 -->|YES| S20[HARBOUR APPROVED]
    S19 --> S16
```

### 4.4 Schematic Block Diagram — Harbour Zone Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Z0[HARBOUR ZONE CLASSIFICATION] --> Z1[ZONE 1 SEA APPROACH]
    Z0 --> Z2[ZONE 2 ENTRANCE CHANNEL]

    Z0 --> Z4[ZONE 4 INNER HARBOUR]

```

> [!TIP]
> In KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination) answer sheets, **schematic block diagrams** of this kind score high — examiners award up to **2–3 bonus marks** for clear visual representation even if the textual answer is partial.

---
