> **Final Answer:** Net approach channel depth below LLWL = **13.85 m**; Gross dredging depth = **14.65 m**. This single number, once established, dictates the dredging contractor's mobilisation, the volume of spoil to be removed, and the dredger fleet selection.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Harbour Depth Using Fresh-Water Correction

**Problem Statement:**

> A bulk carrier with a seawater draft of 11.5 m is to berth at a riverine port where the water density is 1.005 t/m³. The design wind speed is 18 m/s and the effective fetch is 20 km. Compute the **wave height using King's formula** and the **corrected fresh-water draft**. Take $C_b = 0.65$, $V = 6$ knots, and assume sandy bottom.

**Step (i) — Fresh-water draft correction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{fw} &= T_{sw} \cdot \left( \frac{\rho_{sw}}{\rho_{fw}} \right) \\
&= 11.5 \times \left( \frac{1.025}{1.005} \right) \\
&= 11.5 \times 1.01990 \\
&= 11.729 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Draft increase = $11.729 - 11.500 = 0.229 \text{ m} \approx 0.23 \text{ m}$.

**Step (ii) — Wave height by King's formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= 0.34 \cdot \sqrt{F \cdot U} \\
&= 0.34 \cdot \sqrt{20 \times 18} \\
&= 0.34 \cdot \sqrt{360} \\
&= 0.34 \times 18.9737 \\
&= 6.451 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Note: $H = 6.45$ m is unrealistically high for $F = 20$ km and $U = 18$ m/s under King's formula, indicating the ship cannot safely berth at this port without breakwater protection (King's formula is for open fetch — actual harbour wave heights will be lower due to diffraction and bottom friction).

**Step (iii) — Squat allowance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_q &= \frac{C_b \cdot V^2}{100} \\
&= \frac{0.65 \times 36}{100} \\
&= 0.234 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (iv) — Wave allowance (sheltered basin, factor 0.3):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_w &= 0.3 \times H_s \\
&= 0.3 \times 6.451 \\
&= 1.935 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> This confirms the berth cannot accept this ship without wave protection reducing $H_s$ to about **1.0–1.5 m**. The designer would either (a) build a breakwater to reduce $H_s$, (b) restrict operations to low-wave seasons, or (c) accept a smaller design vessel.

**Step (v) — Net depth at alongside berth (sandy bottom, $S_b = 0.45$ m, $S_t = 0.15$ m):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{net}} &= T_{fw} + S_q + S_w + S_t + S_b \\
&= 11.729 + 0.234 + 1.935 + 0.15 + 0.45 \\
&= 14.498 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer:** Required berth depth below LLWL ≈ **14.50 m**. The fresh-water correction alone added **0.23 m**, and the wave environment added nearly **2 m** — both dominant design drivers.

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Comparative Depth Zones (Synthesis Problem)

**Problem Statement:**

> Using the design vessel from Example 1 ($T = 12.0$ m, $C_b = 0.70$), compute and **tabulate** the net depth required at four harbour zones, given:
> - Approach channel: $V = 10$ knots, $H_s = 2.0$ m
> - Harbour entrance: $V = 6$ knots, $H_s = 1.5$ m
> - Turning basin: $V = 4$ knots, $H_s = 0.5$ m
> - Alongside berth: $V = 0$ (stationary), $H_s = 0.3$ m
>
> Assume $S_t = 0.20$ m and $S_b = 0.45$ m throughout.

**Solution Table:**

| Zone | $V$ (knots) | $S_q$ (m) | $H_s$ (m) | $S_w$ factor | $S_w$ (m) | $D_{\text{net}}$ (m) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Approach Channel | 10 | 0.70 | 2.0 | 0.5 | 1.00 | **14.35** |
| Harbour Entrance | 6 | 0.252 | 1.5 | 0.5 | 0.75 | **13.652** |
| Turning Basin | 4 | 0.112 | 0.5 | 0.3 | 0.15 | **12.912** |
| Alongside Berth | 0 | 0.000 | 0.3 | 0.3 | 0.09 | **12.74** |

Calculation check for the **alongside berth**:

$$
D_{\text{net,berth}} = 12.0 + 0.0 + 0.09 + 0.20 + 0.45 = 12.74 \text{ m}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Insight:** The approach channel requires the maximum depth (14.35 m) — about **1.6 m deeper** than the alongside berth. This is why dredging is concentrated in the approach zone and explains why the design vessel's draft alone (12.0 m) is **never** the actual design depth. Board answers must always demonstrate this 15–20% surcharge.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity Analysis

This matrix shows how each parameter affects the net depth. Values are sensitivities $\partial D_{\text{net}} / \partial X$ where $X$ is the parameter varied.

| Parameter | Symbol | Sensitivity | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ship speed (+1 knot) | $V$ | $+2 \cdot C_b \cdot V / 100$ | High leverage — speed restrictions in channels save dredging cost |
| Wave height (+0.5 m) | $H_s$ | $+0.25$ to $+0.30$ m | Breakwaters give excellent ROI on dredging savings |
| Ship draft (+0.5 m) | $T$ | $+0.50$ m (linear) | Vessel size selection directly drives dredging cost |
| Bottom material change (mud → rock) | $S_b$ | $+0.30$ m | Geological survey is essential during feasibility |
| Datum change (LLWL → MSL) | — | $+\frac{1}{2}\text{tidal range}$ | Datum choice alone can shift depth by metres |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Using the same wave allowance factor (0.5) for all zones. The sheltered basin and berth require a **reduced factor (0.3)** because the ship is stationary and partially shielded. Applying 0.5 uniformly causes over-dredging and wastage of crores in capital cost.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Harbour Depth — Zone-Wise Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **logical flow of depth determination** across harbour zones, showing how each zone's depth depends on a different combination of input parameters.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Design Vessel Selection] --> B[Static Draft T]
    A --> C[Block Coefficient Cb]
    A --> D[Length Overall LOA]

    B --> E[Net Depth Computation Hub]
    C --> F[Squat Allowance Sq]
    D --> F
    F --> E

    G[Fetch F and Wind U] --> H[Wave Height H Kings Formula]
    H --> I[Wave Allowance Sw]
    I --> E

    J[Tide Data] --> K[Datum Selection LLWL MSL]
    K --> E

    L[Bottom Soil Investigation] --> M[Bottom Clearance Sb]
    M --> E

    N[Operational Restrictions] --> O[Trim Allowance St]
    O --> E

    E --> P[Approach Channel Depth]
    E --> Q[Harbour Entrance Depth]
    E --> R[Turning Basin Depth]
    E --> S[Alongside Berth Depth]

    P --> T[Gross Dredging Depth Dgross]
    Q --> T
    R --> T
    S --> T

    T --> U[Siltation Allowance hsilt]
    U --> V[Dredging Tolerance htol]
    V --> W[Final Design Depth for Construction]
```

## 4.2 Decision Tree for Depth Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Determine Harbour Depth] --> Q1{Which zone?}
    Q1 -->|Approach Channel| AC[Use factor 1.15 to 1.20 times T]
    Q1 -->|Entrance| EN[Use factor 1.15 to 1.20 times T]
    Q1 -->|Turning Basin| TB[Use factor 1.20 to 1.30 times T]
    Q1 -->|Alongside Berth| AL[Use factor 1.10 to 1.15 times T]
    Q1 -->|Inner Basin| IB[Use factor 1.10 to 1.15 times T]

    AC --> Calc[Component-wise Calculation]
    EN --> Calc
    TB --> Calc
    AL --> Calc
    IB --> Calc

    Calc --> Datum{Datum Selected?}
    Datum -->|LLWL| LLWL[No tidal addition needed]
    Datum -->|MSL| MSL[Add half tidal range]
    Datum -->|HHWL| HHWL[Subtract tidal range]

    LLWL --> Out[Report Dnet and Dgross]
    MSL --> Out
    HHWL --> Out
```

## 4.3 Cross-Sectional Schematic of a Typical Harbour

The following block diagram (used in lieu of a physical section drawing, since Mermaid cannot render complex civil cross-sections) shows the **functional relationship** between the various zones and their respective design depths.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SEA[Open Sea]
        S0[Design Wave Height Hs open water]
    end

    subgraph AP[Approach Channel Zone]
        A1[Dnet 1.15 to 1.20 times T]
        A2[Highest speed and wave exposure]
    end

    subgraph EN[Harbour Entrance Zone]
        E1[Dnet 1.15 to 1.20 times T]
        E2[Wave refraction and diffraction peak]
    end

    subgraph TB[Turning Basin Zone]
        T1[Dnet 1.20 to 1.30 times T]
        T2[Maximum squat at low manoeuvring speed]
    end

    subgraph BR[Alongside Berth Zone]
        B1[Dnet 1.10 to 1.15 times T]
        B2[Ship stationary, wave attenuated]
    end

    SEA --> AP --> EN --> TB --> BR
```

## 4.4 Visual Summary — Depth Composition Bar Chart (Conceptual)

The chart below illustrates the **typical proportional contribution** of each component to the net depth in an approach channel (numerical values from Example 1).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Stack[Net Depth Components m for Approach Channel Example 1]
        D1[Static Draft T 12.00 m 87 percent]
        D2[Squat Sq 0.448 m 3 percent]
        D3[Wave Sw 0.75 m 5 percent]
        D4[Trim St 0.20 m 1 percent]
        D5[Bottom Sb 0.45 m 3 percent]
    end

    D1 --> D2 --> D3 --> D4 --> D5 --> Final[Net Depth 13.85 m]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The static draft dominates the design (~87%), but the small dynamic allowances cumulatively add **~13% extra** depth — a non-trivial dredging cost multiplier. KTU examiners often test this proportional understanding.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]

**Define harbour depth. List the various components that contribute to the required depth of water in a harbour.**

**Model Answer (8–10 lines, ~3-mark depth):**

Harbour depth is the vertical distance measured below a specified reference datum (usually Lowest Low Water Level) that must be maintained at various navigational zones of a harbour to allow safe movement and berthing of design vessels under all operating conditions.

The major components are:

1. **Static loaded draft** ($T$) of the largest design ship
2. **Squat allowance** ($S_q$) — vertical sinkage due to hydrodynamic effects in shallow water
3. **Wave-induced allowance** ($S_w$) — vertical heave motion in waves
4. **Trim allowance** ($S_t$) — bow or stern sinkage due to loading
5. **Bottom material clearance** ($S_b$) — safety margin based on seabed soil
6. **Siltation and dredging tolerance** — for gross dredging depth

The **net depth** is the sum of the first five, while the **gross depth** additionally includes siltation and tolerance.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]

**Explain why the depth required at the turning basin of a harbour is greater than the depth required at the alongside berth.**

**Model Answer (6–8 lines, ~3-mark depth):**

Although the turning basin is sheltered and has minimal wave action, it requires the **greatest depth** in the harbour for three reasons:

1. **Maximum squat occurs at low manoeuvring speed** — when a ship moves slowly through shallow water with high blockage ratio, the Barrass II formula gives large squat values, often exceeding those at full approach speed.
2. **Manoeuvring-induced dynamic sinkage** — turning creates cross-flows and pressure asymmetries that increase effective draft.
3. **Safety against grounding during rotation** — a ship rotating in a confined area must have adequate keel clearance throughout the turn.

In contrast, an alongside berthed ship is **stationary** ($S_q = 0$) and is shielded by fenders, dolphins, and mooring lines. Hence the berth depth is typically 10–15% above static draft, while the turning basin depth is 20–30% above static draft.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks (Modeled on KTU 2024 Pattern)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 3 Pattern]**

> A harbour is to be designed to receive Handymax-class bulk carriers. The following data is given:
>
> - Loaded draft of design vessel: $T = 10.5$ m (in seawater of density 1.025 t/m³)
> - Block coefficient: $C_b = 0.68$
> - Ship speed in the approach channel: $V = 9$ knots
> - Ship speed at harbour entrance: $V = 6$ knots
> - Ship speed in turning basin: $V = 4$ knots
> - Significant wave height in approach channel: $H_s = 1.8$ m
> - Significant wave height at harbour entrance: $H_s = 1.4$ m
> - Significant wave height in turning basin: $H_s = 0.5$ m
> - Significant wave height at alongside berth: $H_s = 0.3$ m
> - Trim allowance: $S_t = 0.15$ m throughout
> - Bottom material: medium sand, $S_b = 0.45$ m
> - Siltation allowance: $h_{\text{silt}} = 0.40$ m/year
> - Dredging tolerance: $h_{\text{tol}} = 0.30$ m
> - Datum: Lowest Low Water Level (LLWL)
>
> **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Compute the net depth of water required in the approach channel and at the harbour entrance. Show all calculations.
>
> **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Compute the net depth of water required in the turning basin and alongside berth, and determine the gross dredging depth for the alongside berth including siltation and dredging tolerance.

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Approach Channel and Harbour Entrance**

**Step 1: Squat in Approach Channel (Barrass II):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_q(\text{approach}) &= \frac{C_b \cdot V^2}{100} \\
&= \frac{0.68 \times 9^2}{100} \\
&= \frac{0.68 \times 81}{100} \\
&= \frac{55.08}{100} \\
&= 0.5508 \text{ m} \approx 0.55 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Wave allowance in approach channel (factor = 0.5):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_w(\text{approach}) &= 0.5 \times 1.8 = 0.90 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Net depth at approach channel:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{net,approach}} &= T + S_q + S_w + S_t + S_b \\
&= 10.5 + 0.55 + 0.90 + 0.15 + 0.45 \\
&= 12.55 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Squat at Harbour Entrance (Barrass II):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_q(\text{entrance}) &= \frac{0.68 \times 6^2}{100} \\
&= \frac{0.68 \times 36}{100} \\
&= \frac{24.48}{100} \\
&= 0.2448 \text{ m} \approx 0.24 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Wave allowance at harbour entrance (factor = 0.5):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_w(\text{entrance}) &= 0.5 \times 1.4 = 0.70 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Net depth at harbour entrance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{net,entrance}} &= 10.5 + 0.24 + 0.70 + 0.15 + 0.45 \\
&= 12.04 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Part (a) Final Answers:** Approach channel net depth = **12.55 m**; Harbour entrance net depth = **12.04 m**.

**Part (b) — Turning Basin, Alongside Berth, and Gross Dredging Depth**

**Step 7: Squat in Turning Basin:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_q(\text{turning}) &= \frac{0.68 \times 4^2}{100} \\
&= \frac{0.68 \times 16}{100} \\
&= \frac{10.88}{100} \\
&= 0.1088 \text{ m} \approx 0.11 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8: Wave allowance in turning basin (sheltered, factor = 0.3):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_w(\text{turning}) &= 0.3 \times 0.5 = 0.15 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 9: Net depth at turning basin:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{net,turning}} &= 10.5 + 0.11 + 0.15 + 0.15 + 0.45 \\
&= 11.36 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 10: Squat at alongside berth (stationary):**

$$
S_q(\text{berth}) = 0 \text{ m} \quad \text{(ship is moored, V = 0)}
$$

**Step 11: Wave allowance at alongside berth (factor = 0.3):**

$$
S_w(\text{berth}) = 0.3 \times 0.3 = 0.09 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 12: Net depth at alongside berth:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{net,berth}} &= 10.5 + 0 + 0.09 + 0.15 + 0.45 \\
&= 11.19 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 13: Gross dredging depth at alongside berth:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{gross,berth}} &= D_{\text{net,berth}} + h_{\text{silt}} + h_{\text{tol}} \\
&= 11.19 + 0.40 + 0.30 \\
&= 11.89 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Part (b) Final Answers:** Turning basin net depth = **11.36 m**; Alongside berth net depth = **11.19 m**; Gross dredging depth at berth = **11.89 m**.

**Summary Table for Question A:**

| Zone | Net Depth (m) |
|---|---|
| Approach Channel | 12.55 |
| Harbour Entrance | 12.04 |
| Turning Basin | 11.36 |
| Alongside Berth | 11.19 |

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3 Pattern]**

> A harbour site experiences wind of speed 22 m/s blowing over a fetch of 30 km. The design ship has a seawater draft of 11.0 m, block coefficient 0.72, and trim allowance 0.20 m. The harbour bed consists of soft silt.
>
> **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Using King's formula, determine the design wave height. Compute the squat allowance for a ship speed of 7 knots. Hence determine the **net depth required in the approach channel**.
>
> **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** The same ship is to berth at a fresh-water port (water density 1.005 t/m³). Compute the **corrected fresh-water draft** and the **alongside berth depth**, assuming the wave height in the sheltered berth is attenuated to 30% of the value obtained in part (a). The berth bed is mud with $S_b = 0.30$ m.

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Approach Channel Depth**

**Step 1: Wave height using King's formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= 0.34 \cdot \sqrt{F \cdot U} \\
&= 0.34 \cdot \sqrt{30 \times 22} \\
&= 0.34 \cdot \sqrt{660} \\
&= 0.34 \times 25.690 \\
&= 8.735 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Squat allowance (Barrass II, $V = 7$ knots):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_q &= \frac{C_b \cdot V^2}{100} \\
&= \frac{0.72 \times 49}{100} \\
&= \frac{35.28}{100} \\
&= 0.3528 \text{ m} \approx 0.35 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Wave allowance (exposed approach, factor = 0.5):**

$$
S_w = 0.5 \times 8.735 = 4.3675 \text{ m} \approx 4.37 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4: Bottom clearance for soft silt:**

$$
S_b = 0.30 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5: Net depth at approach channel:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{net,approach}} &= T + S_q + S_w + S_t + S_b \\
&= 11.0 + 0.35 + 4.37 + 0.20 + 0.30 \\
&= 16.22 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Part (a) Final Answer:** Design wave height = **8.74 m**; Approach channel net depth = **16.22 m**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Interpretation Note:** King's formula gives a wave height of 8.74 m for the open-sea fetch. This is a **deep-water open-sea wave**, not the wave inside the harbour. In practice, the breakwater and harbour geometry reduce the wave height at the entrance and inside the harbour by 50–80% (diffraction, reflection, bottom friction). For Board exam purposes, the question states the attenuation factor explicitly, so use it as given.

**Part (b) — Fresh-Water Port Berth Depth**

**Step 6: Corrected fresh-water draft:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{fw} &= T_{sw} \cdot \left( \frac{\rho_{sw}}{\rho_{fw}} \right) \\
&= 11.0 \times \left( \frac{1.025}{1.005} \right) \\
&= 11.0 \times 1.01990 \\
&= 11.219 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Attenuated wave height in sheltered berth:**

$$
H_{\text{berth}} = 0.30 \times 8.735 = 2.6205 \text{ m} \approx 2.62 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 8: Wave allowance in sheltered berth (factor = 0.3):**

$$
S_w(\text{berth}) = 0.3 \times 2.62 = 0.786 \text{ m} \approx 0.79 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 9: Squat at berth (ship stationary):**

$$
S_q = 0 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 10: Net depth at alongside berth (mud bottom, $S_b = 0.30$ m):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{net,berth}} &= T_{fw} + S_q + S_w + S_t + S_b \\
&= 11.219 + 0 + 0.79 + 0.20 + 0.30 \\
&= 12.509 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Part (b) Final Answer:** Corrected fresh-water draft = **11.22 m**; Alongside berth net depth = **12.51 m**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Forgetting the fresh-water correction** is the most common error in Question B part (b). Students often write $T_{fw} = 11.0$ m, which underestimates the depth by 0.22 m. A 0.22 m under-dredge can ground a fully laden ship.
> 2. **Confusing King's formula constants** — the constant 0.34 is dimensioned for $F$ in **km** and $U$ in **m/s**, giving $H$ in **m**. Converting units incorrectly is a 2-mark deduction trap.
> 3. **Using the open-sea wave height directly at the berth** without attenuation leads to gross over-design. The harbour environment dramatically reduces wave energy inside the basin.
> 4. **Mixing block coefficient units** — $C_b$ is dimensionless. Squat is in metres when the formula's constant 100 is treated correctly. Watch for unit errors in the 2-mark table portion.
> 5. **Forgetting to add siltation and tolerance** when the question asks for the **gross dredging depth** vs. the **net depth** — this is a recurring 1-mark deduction across all KTU past papers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Harbour Depth (Module 3)**

### Core Concepts
- [ ] **Harbour depth = Draft + UKC** is the governing equation
- [ ] Depth is **NOT uniform** — it varies across approach channel, entrance, turning basin, and berth
- [ ] **Datum** is typically LLWL (Chart Datum) in Indian practice
- [ ] The **turning basin** requires the maximum depth, NOT the berth

### Formula Arsenal
- [ ] $D_{\text{net}} = T + S_q + S_w + S_t + S_b$ — master equation
- [ ] $S_q = C_b \cdot V^2 / 100$ — Barrass II squat (always in **m** with $V$ in knots)
- [ ] $S_w = 0.5 \cdot H_s$ in exposed channels; $0.3 \cdot H_s$ in sheltered basins
- [ ] $H = 0.34 \cdot \sqrt{F \cdot U}$ — King's formula (deep-water fetch-limited wave)
- [ ] $T_{fw} = T_{sw} \cdot (\rho_{sw} / \rho_{fw})$ — fresh-water draft correction
- [ ] $D_{\text{gross}} = D_{\text{net}} + h_{\text{silt}} + h_{\text{tol}}$ — dredging depth

### Standard Allowances
- [ ] **Bottom clearance:** Mud 0.30 m, Sand 0.45 m, Rock 0.60 m
- [ ] **Siltation allowance:** 0.30–1.0 m/year (tropical harbours)
- [ ] **Dredging tolerance:** 0.20–0.40 m
- [ ] **Trim allowance:** 0.15–0.30 m

### Zone-Wise Multipliers (Quick Reference)
- [ ] Approach channel: $1.15$–$1.20 \times T$
- [ ] Harbour entrance: $1.15$–$1.20 \times T$
- [ ] Turning basin: $1.20$–$1.30 \times T$
- [ ] Alongside berth: $1.10$–$1.15 \times T$

### Common Pitfalls
- [ ] Never use the open-sea wave height inside the harbour without attenuation
- [ ] Always specify the datum in the final answer
- [ ] Round intermediate values to 2 decimal places, final answer to nearest **0.05 m**
- [ ] Distinguish between **net depth** and **gross dredging depth** — these differ by 0.7–1.0 m
- [ ] Fresh-water correction is **mandatory** for riverine ports (Hooghly, Godavari, etc.)

### Real-World Anchors for Answers
- [ ] Vizhinjam Port: 20.1 m depth, accepts 24,000 TEU vessels
- [ ] Mumbai (JNPT): 14–15 m depth, accepts 6,000 TEU Post-Panamax
- [ ] Kolkata (Haldia): ~9 m depth, riverine, fresh-water corrections applicable
- [ ] Cochin Port: ~12–13 m depth, handles Panamax and Capesize

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ship Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET754 - RAILWAY, PORT AND HARBOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ship Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Ship Characteristics — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Ship Characteristics** is the systematic study of the geometric, volumetric, weight-bearing, and hydrodynamic properties that define a vessel's performance at sea and its interaction with port infrastructure. In KTU parlance, these characteristics are the engineering parameters used by naval architects and port engineers to design berths, dry docks, approach channels, and breakwaters that can safely receive and service a vessel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECET754 / Module 3):**
> Ship Characteristics deals with **(i) Principal Dimensions, (ii) Form Coefficients, (iii) Displacement & Tonnage, (iv) Stability, and (v) Power–Speed relationship**. Every port design problem (depth of channel, width of lock, length of berth) begins with these five parameters.

## 1.2 The Five Pillars of Ship Characteristics

| # | Characteristic Group | What it Tells the Port Engineer |
|---|----------------------|----------------------------------|
| 1 | Principal Dimensions | How long, wide, and deep the ship is → sets berth length & channel depth |
| 2 | Form Coefficients | How "fat" or "slender" the hull is → affects resistance & turning |
| 3 | Displacement & Tonnage | How heavy the ship is → sets dredging limits & cargo handling capacity |
| 4 | Stability (GM) | How safely the ship floats → governs crane loading limits |
| 5 | Speed & Power | How fast the ship moves → determines channel traffic & tug needs |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Floating Building"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy:**
> Think of a ship as a **floating multi-storey building** of known length, width, and height. The "basement" sits in water (the *draft*), the "ground floor" is the waterline (the *beam*), and the upper floors are the *freeboard*. The building is hollow (low density), so it floats by Archimedes' principle. The **form** of this hollow box (slim tower vs. fat warehouse) decides how easily it cuts through water — exactly the way a thin knife slices butter more easily than a thick block.

## 1.4 Principal Dimensions — The "Vital Statistics" of a Ship

The standard naval architectural dimensions are:

- **Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP or Lpp):** Distance measured along the design waterline between the forward perpendicular (through the stem at the design waterline) and the aft perpendicular (through the rudder post). This is the **working length** used in all resistance calculations.
- **Length Overall (LOA):** The extreme fore-and-aft length of the ship, including bulbous bow, bow protrusions, and stern overhang. Used for berth allocation.
- **Length at Waterline (LWL):** The actual length of the ship measured along the waterline at the loaded condition. Often slightly greater than LBP.
- **Beam (B):** The **maximum breadth** of the ship, measured amidships at the widest point. Determines lock width and turning basin size.
- **Draft (T):** The vertical distance from the **design waterline** to the lowest point of the keel (or the propeller tip in some definitions). Critical for channel depth and dredging.
- **Depth (D):** The vertical distance from the **keel** to the **main deck** (moulded depth). Determines freeboard and air draft.
- **Freeboard (F):** The vertical distance from the waterline to the main deck. Hence **D = T + F**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Quick Fact — Units & Conversions:**
> - $1$ nautical mile $= 1.852$ km $= 6080$ ft
> - $1$ knot $= 0.5144$ m/s
> - $1$ displacement ton (salt water) $= 35$ cu ft $\approx 0.991$ m³ $\approx 1$ metric tonne
> - Sea water density $\rho_{sw} = 1.025$ t/m³ ; Fresh water density $\rho_{fw} = 1.000$ t/m³

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Form Coefficients — The "Shape Numbers"

Form coefficients are dimensionless ratios that describe how closely the ship's underwater volume approaches a rectangular block. They are derived from three fundamental areas:

- **V** = Volume of displacement (m³)
- **Aw** = Area of the waterplane (m²)
- **Am** = Area of the midship (cross) section (m²)

### 2.1.1 Block Coefficient (Cb)

$$C_b = \frac{V}{L_{pp} \times B \times T}$$

- Represents the **fineness of the underwater hull**.
- Typical range: $0.55$ (fine, fast ship) to $0.85$ (full, bulky tanker).
- Tankers and bulk carriers have **high Cb**; naval destroyers have **low Cb**.

### 2.1.2 Prismatic Coefficient (Cp)

$$C_p = \frac{V}{A_m \times L_{pp}}$$

- Indicates the **degree of "taper"** of the hull ends.
- A high Cp (~0.85) means a relatively prismatic hull (good for tankers).
- A low Cp (~0.55) means a fine entrance and run (good for high-speed ships).

### 2.1.3 Midship Coefficient (Cm)

$$C_m = \frac{A_m}{B \times T}$$

- Describes the **fullness of the midship section** only.
- Always $C_b = C_p \times C_m$ (this identity is **favourite KTU exam trap**).

### 2.1.4 Waterplane Coefficient (Cwp)

$$C_{wp} = \frac{A_w}{L_{WL} \times B}$$

- Indicates the **fullness of the waterplane area**.
- Higher Cwp means more buoyant support along the length → better initial stability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do we need these "shape numbers"?**
> When a port engineer receives a new vessel class (e.g., a Suezmax tanker), they don't have a 3D model. Using just L, B, T and the four coefficients, the entire underwater hull can be approximately reconstructed. This is the basis of **standard series** resistance prediction methods (Taylor, Holtrop).

## 2.2 Displacement and Tonnage

### 2.2.1 Displacement (Δ)

By Archimedes' principle, a floating ship displaces a volume of water whose weight equals the ship's total weight (including cargo, fuel, crew).

$$\Delta = \rho \times V = W_{ship} \text{ (in tonnes)}$$

- $\Delta$ in **tonnes** = $V$ (m³) $\times \rho$ (t/m³)
- For salt water: $\Delta(t) \approx V(m^3) \times 1.025$
- For fresh water: $\Delta(t) \approx V(m^3) \times 1.000$

### 2.2.2 The Three "Tonnages" of Shipping

| Tonnage Type | Symbol | Definition | Engineering Use |
|--------------|--------|------------|-----------------|
| **Displacement Tonnage** | Δ | Weight of water displaced (equals ship weight) | Naval architecture, stability |
| **Deadweight Tonnage** | DWT | Total weight of cargo + fuel + stores + crew a ship can carry | Cargo capacity (commercial) |
| **Gross Tonnage** | GT | Total enclosed volume of all spaces, expressed in tons of 100 ft³ | Port dues, registration |
| **Net Tonnage** | NT | GT minus non-revenue spaces (engine rooms, crew spaces) | Berth dues, canal fees |

**Mathematical relations:**

$$\text{Lightweight (LWT)} + \text{Deadweight (DWT)} = \text{Displacement (}\Delta\text{)}$$

$$NT = GT - \text{deductible spaces}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** "**G**ross is the **G**arage (everything inside), **N**et is the **N**et profit (revenue-earning space only)."

## 2.3 Ship Stability — The Metacentric Concept

Static transverse stability of a floating body is governed by the **Metacentric Height (GM)**. This is the single most important stability parameter.

### 2.3.1 Definitions of Vertical Centres

- **G** = Centre of Gravity (fixed by weight distribution)
- **B** = Centre of Buoyancy (centroid of displaced volume, moves as ship heels)
- **M** = Metacentre (point about which the ship oscillates for small angles)
- **K** = Keel (datum point)

### 2.3.2 The Stability Formula (KTU Gold Equation)

$$GM = KB + BM - KG$$

where:

$$BM = \frac{I_T}{V} = \frac{I_T}{\Delta / \rho}$$

- $I_T$ = Transverse second moment of area of the waterplane about the ship's centreline (m⁴)
- $V$ = Displaced volume (m³)
- $\Delta$ = Displacement (tonnes)
- $KB$ = Height of B above keel = $T - \text{distance of B above keel}$ (or commonly $\approx T/2$ for prismatic hulls)
- $KG$ = Height of G above keel (from weight distribution)

### 2.3.3 Conditions for Stability

| Condition | Result |
|-----------|--------|
| **GM > 0** | Stable (positive righting moment) |
| **GM = 0** | Neutral (no restoring moment) |
| **GM < 0** | Unstable (ship will capsize) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write $GM = KB + BM + KG$. The correct sign is **MINUS** because G lies between K and M for a stable ship. This is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction if wrong.

## 2.4 Speed–Power Relationship

The effective power required to push a ship through water is given by:

$$P_E = R_T \times V_s$$

where:
- $R_T$ = Total resistance (kN) — sum of frictional, wave-making, and eddy resistance
- $V_s$ = Service speed (m/s)
- $P_E$ = Effective power (kW)

The **delivered power** at the propeller is higher due to propulsive efficiency $\eta_D$:

$$P_D = \frac{P_E}{\eta_D}$$

Typical $\eta_D$ values: $0.55$ to $0.70$ for single-screw ships.

The **brake power** at the engine output is:

$$P_B = \frac{P_D}{\eta_S \times \eta_G}$$

with shaft efficiency $\eta_S$ and gearing efficiency $\eta_G$ each around $0.97$–$0.99$.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Range / Units |
|---|---------|---------|------------------------|
| 1 | $C_b = V / (L_{pp} B T)$ | Block coefficient | $0.55$ – $0.85$ |
| 2 | $C_p = V / (A_m L_{pp})$ | Prismatic coefficient | $0.55$ – $0.85$ |
| 3 | $C_m = A_m / (B T)$ | Midship coefficient | $0.90$ – $0.99$ |
| 4 | $C_{wp} = A_w / (L_{WL} B)$ | Waterplane coefficient | $0.65$ – $0.90$ |
| 5 | $C_b = C_p \times C_m$ | Identity (always) | — |
| 6 | $\Delta = \rho V$ (in tonnes) | Displacement | tonnes |
| 7 | $DWT + LWT = \Delta$ | Weight identity | tonnes |
| 8 | $GM = KB + BM - KG$ | Metacentric height | metres |
| 9 | $BM = I_T / V$ | B–M distance | metres |
| 10 | $P_E = R_T V_s$ | Effective power | kW |
| 11 | $P_B = P_E / (\eta_D \eta_S \eta_G)$ | Brake power | kW |
| 12 | $D = T + F$ | Depth–Draft–Freeboard | metres |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Block Coefficient from the Rectangular Block Idealization

**Step 1 — Define a reference rectangular block.**
Imagine the volume of water displaced by the ship were a perfect rectangular box of length $L_{pp}$, breadth $B$, and depth equal to the draft $T$. The volume of this imaginary block is:

$$V_{block} = L_{pp} \times B \times T$$

**Step 2 — Define the actual displaced volume.**
The real ship's underwater volume is $V$, which is some fraction of this block (because the hull is tapered at bow and stern and curved at the bottom).

**Step 3 — Form the dimensionless ratio.**
To compare the actual volume to the ideal block, we take the ratio:

$$C_b = \frac{V}{V_{block}} = \frac{V}{L_{pp} \times B \times T}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**
If $C_b = 1$, the hull is a perfect rectangular box (log!). If $C_b = 0.6$, the hull volume is $60\%$ of the bounding box — a slender, fine ship.

**Step 5 — Express in terms of weight.**
Since $\Delta = \rho V$, we can also write:

$$C_b = \frac{\Delta / \rho}{L_{pp} B T} = \frac{\Delta}{1.025 \times L_{pp} B T} \quad \text{(sea water)}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Identity $C_b = C_p \times C_m$

**Step 1 — Start from the definition of $C_p$.**
$$C_p = \frac{V}{A_m \times L_{pp}}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply numerator and denominator by the same factor.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p \times C_m &= \frac{V}{A_m \times L_{pp}} \times \frac{A_m}{B \times T}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Cancel $A_m$.**
Since $A_m$ appears in the numerator of the second term and the denominator of the first, they cancel:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p \times C_m &= \frac{V}{L_{pp} \times B \times T}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the right-hand side.**
By definition, the right-hand side is exactly the block coefficient $C_b$. Hence:

$$\boxed{C_b = C_p \times C_m}$$

This identity is **always true** for any ship and is a guaranteed KTU exam question.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Metacentric Radius $BM$ from First Principles

**Step 1 — Set up the geometry.**
When a ship heels by a small angle $\theta$, the centre of buoyancy $B$ shifts laterally to $B'$. The original vertical line through $B$ intersects the new vertical line through $B'$ at the metacentre $M$. By definition, $BM$ is the distance from $B$ to $M$ along the centreplane.

**Step 2 — Use the principle of moments.**
The shift in the buoyancy centre is caused by the **wedge of immersion** (added buoyancy on one side) and the **wedge of emersion** (lost buoyancy on the other side). The volume of each small wedge element is:

$$dV = x \, dA$$

where $x$ is the horizontal distance from the centreline to a waterplane element of area $dA$.

**Step 3 — Compute the restoring moment.**
The moment of the added wedge about the original centreline is:

$$M_{righting} = \rho g \int x^2 \, dA = \rho g \, I_T$$

**Step 4 — Equate to restoring moment from buoyancy shift.**

$$M_{righting} = \Delta \cdot g \cdot GM \cdot \theta = \rho V g \cdot GM \cdot \theta$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $BM$ (which approximates $GM$ for small angles).**
Equating the two expressions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho V g \cdot GM \cdot \theta &= \rho g \cdot I_T \cdot \theta \\
GM = BM &= \frac{I_T}{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — For a rectangular waterplane of length $L$ and beam $B$:**

$$I_T = \frac{L \times B^3}{12}$$

Hence:

$$BM = \frac{L \times B^3}{12 \, V}$$

This is the famous **"beam cubed" rule** — a $10\%$ increase in beam increases $BM$ by $33\%$.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Effective Power to Brake Power Chain

**Step 1 — Start with towing force and speed.**
The ship must overcome total resistance $R_T$ at speed $V_s$. The useful power delivered to the water (effective power) is:

$$P_E = R_T \times V_s \quad \text{(kW, with } R_T \text{ in kN and } V_s \text{ in m/s)}$$

**Step 2 — Account for propeller losses.**
The propeller cannot convert $100\%$ of the rotational power into useful thrust. The propulsive efficiency $\eta_D$ captures hull–propeller interaction and propeller open-water efficiency:

$$P_D = \frac{P_E}{\eta_D} \quad \text{(Delivered power at propeller face)}$$

**Step 3 — Account for shaft transmission losses.**

$$P_S = \frac{P_D}{\eta_S}$$

**Step 4 — Account for gear box losses.**

$$P_B = \frac{P_S}{\eta_G} = \frac{P_E}{\eta_D \cdot \eta_S \cdot \eta_G}$$

**Step 5 — Final overall propulsive coefficient.**

$$\eta_{overall} = \eta_D \cdot \eta_S \cdot \eta_G \approx 0.55 \text{ to } 0.65$$

So roughly **half** of the brake power reaches the water as useful thrust — a key reason ships use huge engines (a 100,000 DWT tanker may need 25,000–30,000 kW to cruise at 15 knots).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — How to Pick the Right Coefficient in a Numerical

| Given in the Question | You Need to Find | Use This Formula |
|------------------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| $L, B, T$ and $V$ | Block coefficient $C_b$ | $C_b = V/(LBT)$ |
| $L, B, T$ and $A_m$ | Midship coefficient $C_m$ | $C_m = A_m/(BT)$ |
| $C_b$ and $C_m$ | Prismatic coefficient $C_p$ | $C_p = C_b/C_m$ |
| $A_w, L_{WL}, B$ | Waterplane coefficient $C_{wp}$ | $C_{wp} = A_w/(L_{WL} B)$ |
| $I_T, L, B$ (rect. WP) | $BM$ | $BM = LB^3/(12V)$ |
| $KB, KG, BM$ | $GM$ | $GM = KB + BM - KG$ |
| $R_T, V_s$ | Effective power $P_E$ | $P_E = R_T V_s$ |
| $P_E$ and $\eta$ | Brake power $P_B$ | $P_B = P_E/\eta$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Principal Dimensions of a Ship — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Principal Dimensions of a Ship] --> B[Length]
    A --> C[Breadth]
    A --> D[Vertical Measurements]
    A --> E[Volume and Area]

    B --> B1[Lpp - Length Between Perpendiculars]
    B --> B2[LOA - Length Overall]
    B --> B3[LWL - Length at Waterline]

    C --> C1[B - Maximum Beam at Midships]

    D --> D1[T - Draft]
    D --> D2[D - Depth Moulded]
    D --> D3[F - Freeboard]
    D2 --> D4[Equation D equals T plus F]

    E --> E1[V - Volume of Displacement]
    E --> E2[Aw - Waterplane Area]
    E --> E3[Am - Midship Section Area]
```

## 4.2 Form Coefficients — Interrelationship Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V[Volume V] --> Cb[Block Coefficient Cb]
    Am[Midship Area Am] --> Cm[Midship Coefficient Cm]
    Aw[Waterplane Area Aw] --> Cwp[Waterplane Coefficient Cwp]
    LBT[Lpp times B times T] --> Cb
    BT[B times T] --> Cm
    LWLB[LWL times B] --> Cwp
    AmL[Am times Lpp] --> Cp[Prismatic Coefficient Cp]
    V --> Cp
    Cb --> Identity[Identity Cb equals Cp times Cm]
    Cp --> Identity
    Cm --> Identity
```

## 4.3 Stability Triangle — The GM Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    K[Keel K - Datum] --> KB[KB - Centre of Buoyancy Height]
    K --> KG[KG - Centre of Gravity Height]
    K --> KM[KM - Metacentre Height above Keel]
    KB --> BM[BM - Metacentric Radius]
    KM --> BM
    KG --> GM[GM - Net Metacentric Height]
    KM --> GM
    KB --> GM
    GM --> Stable{Is GM positive?}
    Stable -->|Yes| OK[Stable Ship]
    Stable -->|No| KO[Unstable - Will Capsize]
```

## 4.4 Power Transmission Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PB[Brake Power at Engine] -->|divided by etaG| PS[Shaft Power]
    PS -->|divided by etaS| PD[Delivered Power at Propeller]
    PD -->|divided by etaD| PE[Effective Power to Hull]
    PE -->|equals R times V| RT[Total Resistance multiplied by Speed]
```

## 4.5 Tonnage Relationship — Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TotalWeight[Total Weight of Ship] --> LWT[Lightweight LWT - Hull plus Machinery]
    TotalWeight --> DWT[Deadweight DWT - Cargo plus Fuel plus Stores]
    TotalWeight --> Delta[Displacement Delta equals LWT plus DWT]
    DWT --> Cargo[Cargo Weight]
    DWT --> Fuel[Fuel and Bunkers]
    DWT --> Stores[Stores and Crew]
    GT[Gross Tonnage GT] --> NT[Net Tonnage NT]
    GT --> NonRev[Non-revenue Spaces Subtracted]
    GT --> Rev[Revenue Spaces]
    NT --> Rev
```

## 4.6 Ship Cross-Section Showing All Dimensions

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Vertical
        K1[Keel Line] --> T1[Draft T - Waterline to Keel]
        T1 --> WL1[Design Waterline]
        WL1 --> F1[Freeboard F - Waterline to Deck]
        F1 --> D1[Moulded Depth D]
    end
    subgraph Horizontal
        Stem1[Forward Perpendicular] --> LPP[Length Lpp]
        LPP --> Stern1[Aft Perpendicular]
        BowWL[Bow at Waterline] --> LWL2[Length at Waterline LWL]
        LWL2 --> SternWL[Stern at Waterline]
        B1[Port Side] --> BMid[Midship - Maximum Beam B]
        BMid --> B2[Starboard Side]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Bank]**
**Define the following terms with one example each:**
**(i) Block coefficient $C_b$**
**(ii) Prismatic coefficient $C_p$**
**(iii) Midship coefficient $C_m$**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Block Coefficient ($C_b$):** It is the ratio of the volume of displacement $V$ of a ship to the volume of a rectangular block having the same length $L_{pp}$, beam $B$, and draft $T$.

$$C_b = \frac{V}{L_{pp} \times B \times T}$$

*Example:* A fully loaded oil tanker typically has $C_b \approx 0.80$ to $0.85$.

**(ii) Prismatic Coefficient ($C_p$):** It is the ratio of the volume of displacement $V$ to the volume of a prism of length $L_{pp}$ having a cross-section equal to the midship area $A_m$.

$$C_p = \frac{V}{A_m \times L_{pp}}$$

*Example:* A high-speed container ship has $C_p \approx 0.60$ to $0.65$.

**(iii) Midship Coefficient ($C_m$):** It is the ratio of the actual area of the midship section $A_m$ to the area of a rectangle of breadth $B$ and depth $T$ at the midship.

$$C_m = \frac{A_m}{B \times T}$$

*Example:* Most merchant ships have $C_m \approx 0.95$ to $0.99$, indicating a nearly rectangular midship.

**Identity linking them:** $C_b = C_p \times C_m$.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]**
**Differentiate between Gross Tonnage and Net Tonnage. Why is Deadweight Tonnage (DWT) more important for a port engineer than Gross Tonnage?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Gross Tonnage (GT) | Net Tonnage (NT) |
|-----------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Definition | Total internal volume of all enclosed spaces expressed in units of 100 ft³ | GT minus the volume of non-revenue spaces (engine rooms, crew quarters, ballast) |
| Used For | Registration, IMO identity, port dues slab | Berth dues, Suez/Panama canal fees |
| Spaces Included | All enclosed spaces | Only cargo and passenger spaces |

**Why DWT matters to a Port Engineer:**
Deadweight Tonnage (DWT) directly measures the **weight of cargo** a ship can carry, which determines:
1. **Quay crane selection** (SWL of crane must exceed maximum single-lift × 1.25 safety factor).
2. **Stockyard capacity** required behind the berth.
3. **Approach channel dredging** (DWT correlates strongly with draft).
4. **Bunker and water supply** capacity at berth.

GT is a volumetric, regulatory number, whereas DWT is the **operationally meaningful** parameter for cargo handling.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, every Module Internal Question carries 14 marks split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Two alternative choices are provided below — answer **either A or B**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Model]**

**(a)** A bulk carrier has the following particulars:
$L_{pp} = 200$ m, $B = 32$ m, $T = 12.5$ m, $A_m = 350$ m², $A_w = 5,800$ m², $L_{WL} = 205$ m.

Calculate:
(i) The volume of displacement $V$
(ii) The block coefficient $C_b$
(iii) The prismatic coefficient $C_p$
(iv) The midship coefficient $C_m$
(v) The waterplane coefficient $C_{wp}$

**Also verify the identity $C_b = C_p \times C_m$.** (7 marks)

**(b)** What is **metacentric height**? Derive the expression $BM = I_T / V$ for a ship with a rectangular waterplane. A ship of $L = 150$ m, $B = 20$ m floats at a draft of $T = 8$ m in sea water. The centre of buoyancy is at $KB = 4.2$ m above the keel and the centre of gravity is at $KG = 6.0$ m. Calculate the metacentric height $GM$ and state whether the ship is stable. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Solution:**

Given: $L_{pp} = 200$ m, $B = 32$ m, $T = 12.5$ m, $A_m = 350$ m², $A_w = 5800$ m², $L_{WL} = 205$ m.

**(i) Volume of displacement $V$:**
We must find $V$ first. Use the relationship:
$$C_m = \frac{A_m}{B \times T} = \frac{350}{32 \times 12.5} = \frac{350}{400} = 0.875$$

For a typical bulk carrier, $C_p \approx 0.70$ (assumed standard value, or use identity below). Then:
$$V = C_b \times L_{pp} \times B \times T$$

But $C_b$ is unknown. We use the identity:
$$C_b = C_p \times C_m$$

Assuming $C_p = 0.70$:
$$C_b = 0.70 \times 0.875 = 0.6125$$

**Volume:**
$$V = 0.6125 \times 200 \times 32 \times 12.5$$
$$V = 0.6125 \times 80{,}000 = 49{,}000 \text{ m}^3$$

**(ii) Block Coefficient $C_b$:**
$$\boxed{C_b = 0.6125}$$

**(iii) Prismatic Coefficient $C_p$:**
$$\boxed{C_p = 0.70 \text{ (assumed standard)}}$$

**(iv) Midship Coefficient $C_m$:**
$$\boxed{C_m = 0.875}$$

**(v) Waterplane Coefficient $C_{wp}$:**
$$C_{wp} = \frac{A_w}{L_{WL} \times B} = \frac{5{,}800}{205 \times 32} = \frac{5{,}800}{6{,}560} = 0.884$$

$$\boxed{C_{wp} = 0.884}$$

**Verification of identity:**
$$C_p \times C_m = 0.70 \times 0.875 = 0.6125 = C_b \;\; \checkmark$$

---

**Part (b) — Solution:**

**Definition of Metacentric Height:**
The metacentric height $GM$ is the distance between the **centre of gravity $G$** of the ship and the **metacentre $M$**. It is a measure of the **initial transverse static stability** of a floating vessel.

**Derivation of $BM = I_T / V$ (for rectangular waterplane):**

For a floating body, when heeled by a small angle $\theta$, the centre of buoyancy shifts from $B$ to $B'$. The wedge of immersion on one side adds a moment:

$$M = \rho g \int x^2 \, dA = \rho g \, I_T$$

The same moment is also equal to the righting couple:

$$M = \Delta \cdot g \cdot GM \cdot \theta = \rho g V \cdot GM \cdot \theta$$

Equating both:
$$\rho g I_T = \rho g V \cdot GM \quad \Rightarrow \quad BM = \frac{I_T}{V}$$

For a rectangular waterplane of length $L$ and beam $B$:
$$I_T = \frac{L \times B^3}{12}$$

Therefore:
$$BM = \frac{L \times B^3}{12 \, V}$$

---

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $L = 150$ m, $B = 20$ m, $T = 8$ m, $KB = 4.2$ m, $KG = 6.0$ m, sea water ($\rho = 1.025$ t/m³).

**Step 1 — Find displacement volume $V$.**
For a coarse estimation, assume block coefficient $C_b = 0.70$ (typical for general cargo ship):
$$V = C_b \times L \times B \times T = 0.70 \times 150 \times 20 \times 8 = 16{,}800 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Compute $I_T$ for rectangular waterplane.**
$$I_T = \frac{L \times B^3}{12} = \frac{150 \times 20^3}{12} = \frac{150 \times 8000}{12} = \frac{1{,}200{,}000}{12} = 100{,}000 \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 3 — Compute $BM$.**
$$BM = \frac{I_T}{V} = \frac{100{,}000}{16{,}800} = 5.952 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $GM$.**
$$GM = KB + BM - KG = 4.2 + 5.952 - 6.0 = 4.152 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Stability check.**
Since $GM = +4.152$ m $\; > 0$:

> **The ship is STABLE.** The righting moment for a $1°$ heel would be $\Delta g GM \theta \approx 16{,}800 \times 1.025 \times 9.81 \times 0.01745 \times 4.152 \approx 11{,}980$ kN·m per radian.

A typical safe range of $GM$ for a cargo ship is $0.15$ m to $1.5$ m. A value of $4.15$ m indicates an **extremely stiff ship** — the ship would roll very quickly and uncomfortably. (Note: a 7-mark question expects a comment on whether the value is reasonable.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

**(a)** Explain the difference between **displacement tonnage**, **deadweight tonnage**, and **gross tonnage**. A Panamax bulk carrier has a displacement of $52{,}000$ tonnes in sea water. Its lightweight is $12{,}000$ tonnes. Find (i) its deadweight tonnage, and (ii) the volume of water displaced. (7 marks)

**(b)** What is the **effective power** of a ship? A container ship has a total resistance of $R_T = 850$ kN at a service speed of $V_s = 18$ knots. If the overall propulsive efficiency is $\eta_{overall} = 0.60$, find the **brake power** required from the main engine. Take $1$ knot $= 0.5144$ m/s. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Solution:**

**Conceptual differences (Tabular form):**

| Tonnage | Meaning | Use |
|---------|---------|-----|
| **Displacement ($\Delta$)** | Weight of water displaced (equal to ship weight) | Naval architecture, stability |
| **Deadweight (DWT)** | Total variable load: cargo + fuel + water + stores + crew | Cargo capacity, port planning |
| **Gross (GT)** | Total enclosed volume of ship expressed in 100 ft³ units | IMO registration, port dues |

**Numerical:**

**(i) Deadweight Tonnage:**
$$DWT = \Delta - LWT = 52{,}000 - 12{,}000 = \boxed{40{,}000 \text{ tonnes}}$$

**(ii) Volume of water displaced:**
Using $\Delta = \rho \cdot V$ with $\rho_{sea} = 1.025$ t/m³:
$$V = \frac{\Delta}{\rho} = \frac{52{,}000}{1.025} = \boxed{50{,}731.7 \text{ m}^3}$$

---

**Part (b) — Solution:**

**Definition of Effective Power ($P_E$):**
Effective power is the **useful power** delivered to the water to overcome the total resistance of the ship at a given speed. It is the product of total resistance and ship speed:

$$P_E = R_T \times V_s$$

**Numerical:**

**Step 1 — Convert speed to SI units.**
$$V_s = 18 \text{ knots} \times 0.5144 \text{ m/s/kt} = 9.2592 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute effective power.**
$$P_E = R_T \times V_s = 850 \text{ kN} \times 9.2592 \text{ m/s} = 7{,}870.3 \text{ kW} \approx 7.87 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 3 — Compute brake power using overall efficiency.**
$$P_B = \frac{P_E}{\eta_{overall}} = \frac{7{,}870.3}{0.60} = \boxed{13{,}117.2 \text{ kW} \approx 13.12 \text{ MW}}$$

This is approximately **17,594 BHP** — a typical installed power for a 3,000–4,000 TEU container ship at 18 knots.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks (Module 3 – Ship Characteristics):**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in $GM$ formula:** Writing $GM = KB + KG - BM$ instead of $GM = KB + BM - KG$. Cost: **2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting sea-water density:** Using $\rho = 1.0$ t/m³ when the problem specifies "sea water" (use $1.025$). Cost: **1 mark**.
> 3. **Confusing $L_{pp}$ with $L_{OA}$:** Using overall length in resistance formulas. Cost: **1–2 marks**.
> 4. **Skipping the identity check:** KTU often asks to "verify $C_b = C_p \times C_m$" — students compute but don't show the verification. Cost: **1 mark**.
> 5. **Wrong units of $BM$:** Students sometimes compute $BM$ in m⁴ because they confused $I_T$ with $BM$. Always show the unit cancellation $m^4 / m^3 = m$.
> 6. **Not commenting on practical $GM$:** A calculated $GM$ above $2$ m is "excessively stiff" and below $0.15$ m is "tender" — failing to comment costs **1 mark** in stability problems.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Principal dimensions:** $L_{pp}$ (resistance), $L_{OA}$ (berth), $L_{WL}$ (waterplane), $B$ (beam), $T$ (draft), $D$ (depth), $F$ (freeboard).
- [x] **Identity:** $D = T + F$ and $C_b = C_p \times C_m$ — both are KTU favourites.
- [x] **Form coefficient typical ranges:** $C_b = 0.55$–$0.85$, $C_p = 0.55$–$0.85$, $C_m = 0.90$–$0.99$, $C_{wp} = 0.65$–$0.90$.
- [x] **Tonnage identity:** $\Delta = LWT + DWT$ (and $\Delta = \rho V$ for sea water $\rho = 1.025$).
- [x] **Stability golden formula:** $GM = KB + BM - KG$ (note the **minus** sign for $KG$).
- [x] **Beam cubed rule:** $BM = LB^3 / (12V)$ for rectangular waterplane — small increases in beam greatly increase stability.
- [x] **Power chain:** $P_E = R_T V_s \;\to\; P_D = P_E/\eta_D \;\to\; P_B = P_E/(\eta_D \eta_S \eta_G)$.
- [x] **Typical overall propulsive efficiency:** $0.55$–$0.65$ — only about half the engine power reaches the water.
- [x] **Speed unit:** $1$ knot $= 0.5144$ m/s; remember to convert before SI calculations.
- [x] **DWT matters to ports, GT matters to registration, NT matters to canal dues.**
- [x] **A ship is stable iff $GM > 0$.** Practical safe $GM$ range: $0.15$ m to $2.0$ m for merchant ships.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effects of Natural Phenomena on Marine Structures - Tides
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET754 - RAILWAY, PORT AND HARBOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Effects of Natural Phenomena on Marine Structures - Tides

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Tides** are the periodic rise and fall of sea levels caused by the combined gravitational and centrifugal forces exerted by the Moon, Sun, and the rotation of the Earth. This is a critical phenomenon in **Port and Harbor Engineering** because tidal variations directly govern the design of docks, breakwaters, quay walls, navigation channels, and loading/unloading operations.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Tides are the long-period waves that move through the ocean in response to the forces exerted by the Moon and Sun. The resulting crests and troughs manifest as high and low tides along coastlines.

The principal tidal terminology used in KTU examinations:

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **High Water (HW)** | The highest level reached by the rising tide |
| **Low Water (LW)** | The lowest level reached by the falling tide |
| **Tidal Range** | Vertical difference between successive high and low water |
| **Mean Sea Level (MSL)** | Average level of the sea surface over a long period (~19 years) |
| **Mean High Water Spring (MHWS)** | Average of high water during spring tides |
| **Mean Low Water Spring (MLWS)** | Average of low water during spring tides |
| **Chart Datum (CD)** | Reference level to which depths on nautical charts are reduced |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sitting in the center of a **giant rotating trampoline** with two heavy bowling balls (the Moon and Sun) placed nearby. The trampoline represents the Earth's oceans. As the balls move, they create dimples (gravitational pull), and the rotating center pushes outward (centrifugal effect). Water always flows toward the deepest dimples, creating two opposing bulges on Earth — one facing the Moon, one facing away. **As Earth rotates beneath these bulges, any coastal location experiences the rhythmic rise and fall we call tides.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reality:** A port engineer must design the **dock sill depth** and **quay wall height** based on the **lowest astronomical tide (LAT)** and the **highest astronomical tide (HAT)** — not just average levels. Failing to do so can mean a ship hits the bottom at low tide or the wharf floods at high tide.

## 1.3 Tide-Generating Forces — The Root Cause

Tides are not caused by gravity alone. They are caused by the **differential (tide-generating) force**, which is the *difference* between the gravitational attraction of the Moon/Sun and the local centrifugal force on any particle of the Earth.

- **Earth's Gravity on Sea Water** → provides a constant downward pull (ignorable for tidal analysis since it acts uniformly).
- **Moon's Gravitational Pull** → varies with distance (inverse-square law).
- **Centrifugal Force** → arises from Earth–Moon system revolving around the common barycenter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Exams:** The Moon produces tides that are approximately **2.17 times stronger** than those produced by the Sun, even though the Sun's gravitational pull on Earth is ~179 times greater than the Moon's. This is because tidal force depends on the **gradient** (variation with distance) of gravity, not its absolute value. Since the Sun is ~389 times farther, its gradient is much smaller.

### Mathematical Setup of Tide-Generating Force

Let:
- **M** = Mass of the Moon
- **E** = Mass of the Earth
- **R** = Distance between centers of Earth and Moon = **3.84 × 10⁸ m**
- **r** = Radius of the Earth = **6.37 × 10⁶ m**
- **x** = Distance from Earth's center to the point P on the surface
- **θ** = Angle at Earth's center between the line to the Moon and the line to point P
- **G** = Universal Gravitational Constant = **6.674 × 10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg²**

The horizontal component of the tide-generating force at point P is given by:

$$F_h = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{G M r}{R^3} \sin(2\theta)$$

The vertical component of the tide-generating force at point P is:

$$F_v = \frac{G M r}{R^3} (3\cos^2\theta - 1)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Exams:** Memorize the factor **3/2 (sin 2θ)** in the horizontal component. This explains why maximum horizontal flow (tidal currents) occurs at 45° from the sub-lunar point, not at the poles or equator.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Tides (KTU High-Yield Classification)

### A. Based on Frequency of Tidal Cycles

1. **Semi-diurnal Tides** — Two nearly equal high waters and two nearly equal low waters each lunar day (~24 hours 50 minutes). Example: Most Atlantic coasts.
2. **Diurnal Tides** — Only one high water and one low water per lunar day. Example: Gulf of Mexico, parts of the South China Sea.
3. **Mixed (Semi-diurnal with diurnal inequality)** — Two high and two low waters per day, but with significant differences in heights. Example: Pacific coasts of USA, Indian Ocean.

### B. Based on Sun–Moon–Earth Geometry

1. **Spring Tides** — Occur at **new moon** and **full moon** (Sun + Moon aligned). Highest tidal range.
2. **Neap Tides** — Occur at **first quarter** and **third quarter moon** (Sun ⟂ Moon). Lowest tidal range.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** "**S**pring = **S**uper (largest range)" and "**N**eap = **N**ormal/Near-zero (smallest range)."

## 2.2 The Equilibrium Theory of Tides

The **Equilibrium Theory** (proposed by **Sir Isaac Newton, 1687**) assumes:
- Earth is covered by a uniform ocean (no continents).
- Water responds instantly to tide-generating forces.
- No inertia, no friction, no Coriolis effect.

### Key Predictions of Equilibrium Theory

1. The ocean surface assumes the shape of an **oblate spheroid** stretched along the Earth–Moon axis.
2. The tidal bulge amplitude is theoretically uniform (~0.54 m for Moon alone, ~0.24 m for Sun alone).
3. Spring tides should be ~2.17 times larger than lunar-only tides.
4. Neap tides should have a range of ~0.46 times the lunar-only range.

### Tidal Range Ratio — Spring vs Neap

$$\frac{\text{Range at Spring Tide}}{\text{Range at Neap Tide}} = \frac{M + S}{M - S}$$

where **M = Moon's tide-raising force** and **S = Sun's tide-raising force**.

Since M = 2.17 S, we substitute:

$$\frac{R_{spring}}{R_{neap}} = \frac{2.17S + S}{2.17S - S} = \frac{3.17}{1.17} \approx 2.71$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** Students often confuse **M = 2.17 S** with the Spring–Neap *amplitude* ratio. The **force ratio is 2.17**, but the **range ratio is ~2.71** because the force on each side is added at Spring and subtracted at Neap.

## 2.3 The Dynamic Theory of Tides

Proposed by **Pierre-Simon Laplace (1775)**, this theory considers:
- Continents and ocean basins
- Earth's rotation (Coriolis effect)
- Inertia of water
- Friction at the seabed

> [!NOTE]
> **Equilibrium vs Dynamic:** Equilibrium theory gives the *potential* shape; dynamic theory explains why real tides (e.g., 15 m in Bay of Fundy) can be much larger than the 0.54 m theoretical bulge.

### Resonance in Tidal Basins

Some bays and estuaries have natural periods that match the tidal forcing period (~12 hours). This causes **tidal resonance**, amplifying the tide.

The natural period of oscillation in a rectangular basin is:

$$T = \frac{4L}{\sqrt{g \cdot d}}$$

where:
- **L** = length of the basin
- **d** = average depth of the basin
- **g** = acceleration due to gravity (9.81 m/s²)

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Unit | Application |
|---|---------|----------------|--------------|-------------|
| 1 | $F_h = \tfrac{3}{2} \cdot \tfrac{GMr}{R^3} \sin 2\theta$ | Horizontal tide-generating force | N/kg | Max at $\theta = 45°$ |
| 2 | $F_v = \tfrac{GMr}{R^3}(3\cos^2\theta - 1)$ | Vertical tide-generating force | N/kg | Max at $\theta = 0°$ |
| 3 | $\tfrac{R_{spring}}{R_{neap}} = \tfrac{M+S}{M-S} \approx 2.71$ | Spring–Neap range ratio | — | Exam numerical |
| 4 | $T = \tfrac{4L}{\sqrt{gd}}$ | Natural period of basin oscillation | seconds | Tidal resonance |
| 5 | $\omega = \tfrac{2\pi}{T}$ | Angular frequency | rad/s | Dynamic analysis |
| 6 | $C = \sqrt{gd}$ | Shallow-water wave speed | m/s | Tidal bore speed |
| 7 | $\Delta h = \tfrac{R}{M}\left(1 - 3\sin^2\phi\right)$ | Lunar equilibrium tide elevation | meters | Equilibrium theory |
| 8 | $g_{eff} = g - F_v$ | Effective gravity under tidal influence | m/s² | Tidal g-meter |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formula Safety Rule:** Whenever the syllabus asks for a **magnitude**, the value is always positive. Tide-generating force is attractive on the sub-lunar side and **apparently outward** on the anti-lunar side.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Dredging Operations:** Maintain depths below **Chart Datum (CD)**; dredgers work during high tide to maximize draft.
- **Quay Wall Design:** Crest elevation is set at **HAT + freeboard** (typically 1–1.5 m).
- **Lock & Dock Sill:** Sill depth = **LAT − UKC (Under-Keel Clearance)**.
- **Tidal Stream Energy:** Tidal currents in narrow channels (e.g., Gulf of Cambay) are harnessed for renewable power.
- **Berthing Windows:** Ships can only enter shallow ports during flood tide.
- **Submarine Cable Laying:** Operations are timed with slack water (at HW or LW) to minimize cable stress.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Horizontal Tide-Generating Force

This is the **most important derivation for KTU exams** and must be committed to memory.

### Setup
Let O be the Earth's center, M the Moon's center, and P a point on Earth's surface at angle θ from the Earth–Moon line. The distance from P to M is $D$, and the distance OM = R.

By the **Law of Cosines**:

$$D^2 = R^2 + r^2 - 2Rr\cos\theta$$

Since $r \ll R$, we use the **binomial approximation** $(1-x)^n \approx 1 - nx$ for small x. Here $x = \tfrac{r}{R}\cos\theta$, so:

$$D^2 = R^2\left(1 - \frac{2r}{R}\cos\theta + \frac{r^2}{R^2}\right)$$

$$D \approx R\left(1 - \frac{2r}{R}\cos\theta\right)^{1/2}$$

$$\frac{1}{D^2} \approx \frac{1}{R^2}\left(1 + \frac{2r}{R}\cos\theta\right)$$

### Gravitational Pull at P Toward the Moon
The Moon's gravitational pull on a unit mass at P is:

$$g_M^{(P)} = \frac{GM}{D^2} \approx \frac{GM}{R^2}\left(1 + \frac{2r}{R}\cos\theta\right)$$

### Centrifugal Force at P
The centrifugal force on every particle of the Earth (treating Earth–Moon system as revolving around common barycenter) is constant and equal to $\tfrac{GM}{R^2}$ (acting opposite to the Moon, i.e., along OM direction):

$$f_{cent} = \frac{GM}{R^2}$$

### Total Tide-Generating Force
Subtract the centrifugal force (directed along +OM) from the Moon's pull at P (directed along PM):

$$\vec{F}_{tide} = \vec{g}_M^{(P)} - \vec{f}_{cent}$$

**Resolving into components:**

The Moon's pull has two components at P:
- **Along OP (outward radial):** $\tfrac{GM}{D^2}\cos\alpha$ where $\alpha$ is the angle DPM makes
- **Perpendicular to OP:** $\tfrac{GM}{D^2}\sin\alpha$

For small $\theta$ and using $\cos\alpha \approx \cos\theta - \tfrac{r}{R}$ and $\sin\alpha \approx \sin\theta$:

**Vertical (radial) component:**

$$F_v = \frac{GM}{D^2}\cos\alpha - \frac{GM}{R^2}\cos\theta$$

**Horizontal (tangential) component:**

$$F_h = \frac{GM}{D^2}\sin\alpha$$

Substituting the binomial expansion and simplifying (using $D \approx R$ in the denominator for the leading term):

$$\boxed{F_h = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{GM r}{R^3} \sin(2\theta)}$$

$$\boxed{F_v = \frac{GM r}{R^3} (3\cos^2\theta - 1)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why sin(2θ)?** Because $\sin(2\theta) = 2\sin\theta\cos\theta$, the horizontal force has two zeros per cycle (at $\theta = 0°, 90°, 180°, 270°$) and two maxima at $\theta = 45°$ and $\theta = 135°$. This is why tidal currents are strongest at quarter-points, not at the bulge.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Spring/Neap Ratio

Let $F_M = $ maximum tide-raising force of the Moon and $F_S = $ maximum tide-raising force of the Sun.

$$\frac{F_M}{F_S} = \frac{M_{moon}}{M_{sun}} \times \left(\frac{R_{sun}}{R_{moon}}\right)^3$$

The Moon's tide-raising force is about 2.17 times the Sun's:

$$F_M = 2.17 \cdot F_S$$

**At New Moon (Spring):** Forces are aligned, so total force = $F_M + F_S$.

**At Quarter Moon (Neap):** Forces are perpendicular, so total force = $F_M - F_S$ (vector difference along the axis).

**Range ratio (range is proportional to the sum of forces along the tidal axis):**

$$\frac{R_{spring}}{R_{neap}} = \frac{F_M + F_S}{F_M - F_S} = \frac{2.17 + 1}{2.17 - 1} = \frac{3.17}{1.17} = 2.709$$

$$\boxed{\frac{R_{spring}}{R_{neap}} \approx 2.71}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Question Type:** "If the lunar tide range is 1.2 m, find the solar tide range and the spring/neap ranges."
> - Solar range = 1.2 / 2.17 = 0.553 m
> - Spring range = 1.2 + 0.553 = 1.753 m
> - Neap range = 1.2 − 0.553 = 0.647 m

## 3.3 Derivation of Natural Period of an Oscillating Basin

Consider a long, narrow rectangular basin of length L, depth d, closed at one end and open to the ocean at the other.

**Initial condition:** The water level is tilted at an angle $\alpha_0$ from horizontal (wind or other forcing).

**Forcing:** The water oscillates back and forth due to gravity.

**Energy analysis** (potential → kinetic → potential cycle):

Potential energy at maximum tilt:

$$PE_{max} = \frac{1}{6} \rho g L^3 \alpha_0^2 d$$

Maximum kinetic energy (when all water moves with velocity $u$):

$$KE_{max} = \frac{1}{2} (\rho L d) u_{max}^2$$

Equating PE_max = KE_max (using the linear wave theory result that $u_{max} = \tfrac{L\alpha_0}{2}\sqrt{\tfrac{2g}{d}} \cdot \tfrac{2}{\pi}$ or directly solving the wave equation):

The classical result from shallow-water wave theory gives the natural period of the **fundamental seiche** in a closed basin:

$$\boxed{T = \frac{4L}{\sqrt{gd}}}$$

### Worked Numerical Example for KTU

**Problem:** A harbor basin is rectangular, length L = 50 km, average depth d = 20 m. Find the natural period of oscillation. Will it resonate with semi-diurnal tides (T ≈ 12.4 hours)?

**Solution:**

$$T = \frac{4 \times 50000 \text{ m}}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 20}}$$

$$T = \frac{200000}{\sqrt{196.2}} = \frac{200000}{14.007} = 14278 \text{ seconds}$$

$$T = \frac{14278}{3600} \approx 3.97 \text{ hours}$$

**Conclusion:** Natural period (3.97 h) is much less than semi-diurnal period (12.4 h), so **no resonance** with semi-diurnal tide. However, it may resonate with higher harmonics or quarter-diurnal tides.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always convert units to **SI (meters, seconds)** before substituting in the period formula. Common error: forgetting to convert km → m, giving T in km/s² units.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Tidal Constituents (Harmonic Analysis)

The actual tide at any location is a sum of many **harmonic constituents**, each representing a specific astronomical forcing frequency.

| Symbol | Name | Period (hr) | Relative Amplitude | Origin |
|--------|------|-------------|--------------------| --------|
| M₂ | Principal lunar semi-diurnal | 12.42 | 100.0 | Moon's gravity |
| S₂ | Principal solar semi-diurnal | 12.00 | 46.6 | Sun's gravity |
| N₂ | Larger lunar elliptic | 12.66 | 19.2 | Moon's orbital ellipticity |
| K₂ | Lunisolar declinational semi-diurnal | 11.97 | 12.7 | Moon+Sun declination |
| K₁ | Lunisolar declinational diurnal | 23.93 | 58.4 | Moon+Sun declination |
| O₁ | Principal lunar declinational | 25.82 | 41.5 | Moon's declination |
| P₁ | Principal solar declinational | 24.07 | 19.4 | Sun's declination |
| M₄ | Shallow-water quarter-diurnal | 6.21 | — | Non-linear shallow water |

**Form Factor (used to classify tidal regime):**

$$F = \frac{K_1 + O_1}{M_2 + S_2}$$

- F ≤ 0.25 → **Semi-diurnal**
- 0.25 < F ≤ 1.5 → **Mixed, mainly semi-diurnal**
- 1.5 < F ≤ 3.0 → **Mixed, mainly diurnal**
- F > 3.0 → **Diurnal**
