- **Design codes:** **IS 4651 (Parts I–V)**, **BS 6349**, **PIANC Guidelines**.
- **Real-world examples:** Vallarpadam ICTT, Cochin LNG, Mumbai Port, Mormugao training jetty.
- **Five top functions of a jetty:** Berthing, training currents, trapping littoral drift, harbor protection, passenger/ferry terminal.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Piers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET754 - RAILWAY, PORT AND HARBOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Piers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Pier** in port and harbor engineering is a marine structure that projects from the shoreline out into the body of water, designed to provide berthing facilities for vessels on one or both sides of the structure. Unlike a **wharf** which is built parallel to the shore, a pier extends *perpendicular* (or at an angle) into the water, enabling ships to dock along its longitudinal faces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** *A pier is a berthing structure extending into deep water, providing docking facilities on both sides, essentially functioning as a finger-like projection from the quay or shoreline.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine extending your **arm straight out from your body** with your palm facing forward. Your **shoulder is the shoreline**, your **arm is the pier**, and your **palm is the berthing face** where ships can dock. Now imagine a ship can pull up on *both* sides of your arm — that's exactly how a pier functions.

- **Shoreline** = the quay / land connection
- **Pier deck** = the working surface for cargo handling
- **Ship berths** = both longitudinal sides of the pier
- **Pier head / outer end** = the seaward tip where turning basins exist

> [!NOTE]
> A pier essentially **doubles the berthing capacity** compared to a wharf of the same length because vessels can moor on both faces simultaneously.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics for Piers

The following key parameters govern pier design in KTU 2024 scheme:

- **Water depth alongside:** typically **10 m to 15 m** below Lowest Low Water Level (LLWL) for general cargo
- **Pier width:** **15 m to 30 m** (for cargo piers) and **8 m to 12 m** (for finger piers)
- **Length of pier:** **150 m to 300 m** depending on bathymetry
- **Freeboard:** **1.5 m to 3 m** above Highest High Water Level (HHWL)
- **Design vessel (DWT):** typically **15,000 to 100,000 DWT**

> [!TIP]
> Always remember the **3D rule for piers**: **D**epth, **D**istance (length), and **D**eck width — these three parameters essentially define the geometric configuration of any pier.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Piers

Piers are classified based on **structural type**, **functional use**, and **orientation**.

#### A. Based on Structural Type

| **Type** | **Construction** | **Application** |
|----------|------------------|-----------------|
| **Solid Pier (Filling Type)** | Earth/stone filling with retaining walls | Shallow water, sheltered harbors |
| **Open Pile Pier** | Deck supported on piles | Deep water, soft seabed |
| **Cylinder or Caisson Pier** | Large hollow cylinders sunk and filled | Deep water, rocky seabed |
| **Suspension Pier** | Catenary cable-suspended deck | Specialized deep-water locations |
| **Floating Pier** | Pontoon-based, moored by chains | Tidal zones, marinas |

#### B. Based on Functional Use

- **Breasting Pier** — for ship berthing and cargo transfer
- **Finger Pier** — narrow pier between two parallel berths
- **T-Pier / L-Pier** — shaped like alphabet letters for maximum berth count
- **Recreational Pier** — for tourism, fishing, promenades

#### C. Based on Orientation

- **Right-angle pier** — perpendicular to shore
- **Angled pier** — inclined for wave alignment
- **Y-shaped pier** — splits seaward for multiple berths

### 2.2 Structural Components of a Pier

A typical **open pile pier** consists of the following components:

1. **Deck slab** — Reinforced concrete slab carrying the live load
2. **Pile cap / Pile cap beams** — Distribute deck loads to piles
3. **Bearing piles** — Transfer vertical and lateral loads to seabed
4. **Fender system** — Absorb berthing energy from vessels
5. **Bollards** — Mooring points for vessel hawsers
6. **Curb / kerb** — Edge barrier for safety and crane rail support
7. **Crane rails** — Track for harbor cranes (if equipped)
8. **Service trenches** — Ducts for utilities (water, fuel, electricity)
9. **Approach trestle** — Connecting link between shore and pier

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **fender system** and **bollard system** are the *only* two components that directly interact with the vessel — they govern the berthing safety and mooring reliability.

### 2.3 Loads Acting on Piers

Piers are subjected to a combination of **vertical, lateral, and environmental loads**:

| **Load Category** | **Specific Load** | **Direction** | **Magnitude Reference** |
|-------------------|-------------------|---------------|--------------------------|
| **Dead Load** | Self-weight of deck, piles | Vertical | Calculated from concrete density $\gamma_c = 25$ kN/m³ |
| **Live Load** | Cargo, vehicles, crowds | Vertical | **5 to 30 kN/m²** (uniform) or **IS code vehicle loads** |
| **Crane Load** | Wharf crane wheel loads | Vertical + Lateral | **40 to 120 kN per wheel** |
| **Berthing Load** | Vessel impact | Horizontal | Calculated from kinetic energy |
| **Mooring Load** | Hawser tension | Horizontal | **100 to 2000 kN per bollard** |
| **Wave Load** | Hydrodynamic pressure | Lateral | Morison / Goda equations |
| **Current Load** | Drag on piles | Lateral | $F_D = \frac{1}{2} \rho C_D A v^2$ |
| **Seismic Load** | Inertia from EQ | Lateral + Vertical | IS 1893 / Zone factor |
| **Buoyancy / Uplift** | Hydrostatic pressure on deck | Vertical (upward) | $\rho_w \cdot g \cdot h$ |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU examination question on piers.**

| **#** | **Formula** | **Physical Meaning** | **Units** |
|-------|-------------|----------------------|-----------|
| 1 | $E_b = \frac{1}{2} M v^2 \cdot C_m \cdot C_e \cdot C_s$ | Berthing energy of vessel | kJ |
| 2 | $F_b = \frac{E_b}{\Delta}$ | Equivalent berthing force | kN |
| 3 | $T_{mooring} = K \cdot DWT$ | Mooring force proportionality | kN |
| 4 | $F_D = \frac{1}{2} \rho C_D A v^2$ | Current drag force on pile | kN |
| 5 | $F_I = \rho \cdot C_m \cdot V \cdot \frac{dv}{dt}$ | Inertia force (Morison) | kN/m |
| 6 | $Q_{allow} = \frac{Q_{ult}}{F.S.}$ | Pile bearing capacity (working) | kN |
| 7 | $P_c = A \cdot \sigma_c$ | Pile capacity from end bearing | kN |
| 8 | $V = L \cdot B \cdot D$ | Cargo / vessel volume | m³ |
| 9 | $M_{deck} = \frac{w L^2}{8}$ (or 10 for continuous) | Bending moment in deck slab | kN·m |
| 10 | $A_{pier} = L \times W$ | Plan area of pier | m² |

> **Notation key:** $M$ = vessel mass (tonnes), $v$ = berthing velocity (m/s), $C_m$ = mass coefficient ($\approx 1.5$ to 2.0), $C_e$ = eccentricity coefficient ($\leq 0.5$), $C_s$ = softness coefficient ($\leq 0.9$), $\Delta$ = fender deflection (m), $\rho$ = water density (1025 kg/m³ for seawater).

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

Pier engineering principles are directly applied in:
- **Container terminals** (e.g., Cochin Port, Singapore Tuas)
- **Oil jetties** (single berthing finger piers)
- **Cruise terminals** (T-piers with passenger access)
- **Fishing harbors** (finger piers for trawler berthing)
- **Naval bases** (multiple finger piers in parallel)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Berthing Energy and Equivalent Berthing Force

The berthing energy of a vessel approaching a pier must be **fully absorbed by the fender system** without damaging the pier structure.

#### Step 1 — Kinetic Energy of Approaching Vessel

When a vessel of displacement $M$ approaches the pier with velocity $v$, its kinetic energy is:

$$
E = \frac{1}{2} M v^2
$$

#### Step 2 — Apply Correction Coefficients

The *effective* energy absorbed by the fender is less than the gross kinetic energy because of:

- **Eccentricity** of impact (not all kinetic energy acts on the contact point)
- **Softness** of the fender (energy distributed over time)
- **Added mass** effect (water mass moving with ship)

$$
E_b = \frac{1}{2} M v^2 \cdot C_m \cdot C_e \cdot C_s
$$

where:
- $C_m$ = mass coefficient (typically 1.5 to 2.0 for berthing)
- $C_e$ = eccentricity coefficient (0.5 for fully loaded vessels; 0.25 for partly loaded)
- $C_s$ = softness coefficient (0.9 for rubber fenders, 1.0 for rigid contact)

#### Step 3 — Equivalent Berthing Force

If the fender deflects by $\Delta$ (m) under this energy, the equivalent berthing force is:

$$
F_b = \frac{E_b}{\Delta}
$$

#### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A vessel of **20,000 DWT** berths at a velocity of **0.20 m/s**. Take $C_m = 1.8$, $C_e = 0.5$, $C_s = 0.9$. The fender deflection is **150 mm**. Find the equivalent berthing force.

**Solution:**

Given: $M = 20{,}000$ tonnes $= 20{,}000 \times 1000 = 2 \times 10^7$ kg, $v = 0.20$ m/s.

$$
E_{gross} = \frac{1}{2} M v^2 = \frac{1}{2} (2 \times 10^7)(0.20)^2
$$

$$
E_{gross} = \frac{1}{2} (2 \times 10^7)(0.04) = 4 \times 10^5 \text{ J} = 400 \text{ kJ}
$$

Applying correction coefficients:

$$
E_b = 400 \times 1.8 \times 0.5 \times 0.9 = 324 \text{ kJ}
$$

Equivalent berthing force:

$$
F_b = \frac{E_b}{\Delta} = \frac{324 \text{ kJ}}{0.15 \text{ m}} = 2160 \text{ kN} \approx 2.16 \text{ MN}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Typical berthing forces:** Small vessels → 100 to 500 kN; Medium vessels → 500 to 2000 kN; Large vessels (VLCCs) → 5000 to 10,000 kN.

### 3.2 Derivation: Mooring Force on a Bollard

The mooring force on a pier bollard is empirically related to vessel DWT:

$$
T_{mooring} = K \cdot DWT^{\alpha}
$$

For KTU-level design, simplified form:

$$
T_{mooring} \approx 0.05 \times DWT \text{ (kN)}
$$

#### Step-by-Step Worked Example

> **Problem:** A 50,000 DWT vessel is moored at a pier. Calculate the design mooring pull on the bollard using the simplified KTU relation. If 4 hawsers share the load, find the per-hawser tension.

**Solution:**

$$
T = 0.05 \times 50{,}000 = 2500 \text{ kN}
$$

Assuming **4 hawsers** (bow × 2, stern × 2) sharing the load:

$$
T_{hawser} = \frac{2500}{4} = 625 \text{ kN per hawser}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison — Pier vs Wharf vs Jetty

| **Parameter** | **Pier** | **Wharf** | **Jetty** |
|---------------|----------|-----------|-----------|
| **Orientation** | Perpendicular to shore | Parallel to shore | Usually perpendicular (in open sea) |
| **Berthing Sides** | Both sides | One side | Both sides (or one) |
| **Connection to Shore** | Approach trestle | Direct from land | Sub-sea pipeline / trestle |
| **Cargo Handling** | Direct from pier deck | Direct from wharf | Via pipelines or conveyors |
| **Typical Use** | Containers, general cargo | General cargo, bulk | Oil, LNG, chemicals |
| **Capital Cost** | Medium | Low | High |
| **Water Depth Limit** | Up to 30 m | Up to 15 m | Up to 50+ m |

### 3.4 Design Sequence for a Pier

A logical design sequence for a pier (as per KTU question expectations):

1. **Determine design vessel** — DWT, LOA, beam, draft
2. **Fix deck elevation** — based on HHWL + freeboard (1.5 to 3 m)
3. **Choose pier type** — solid, open pile, or caisson
4. **Calculate berthing energy** $E_b$ → fender specification
5. **Calculate mooring loads** → bollard specification
6. **Compute wave + current loads** (Morison equation on piles)
7. **Analyze pile group** — vertical, lateral, moment capacity
8. **Design deck slab** — bending, shear, punching
9. **Check serviceability** — deflection, cracking, vibration
10. **Detail corrosion protection** — splash zone treatment
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Pier System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1["SHORELINE QUAY"] --> node2["APPROACH TRESTLE"]
    node2 --> node3["PIER DECK SLAB"]
    node3 --> node4["PILE CAP BEAM"]
    node4 --> node5["BEARING PILE CLUSTER"]
    node5 --> node6["SEABED FOUNDATION"]
    node3 --> node7["FENDER SYSTEM"]
    node3 --> node8["BOLLARD MOORING"]
    node3 --> node9["CRANE RAIL TRACK"]
    node3 --> node10["UTILITY SERVICE TRENCH"]
    node11["VESSEL BERTH SIDE A"] -.Berthing.-> node7
    node12["VESSEL BERTH SIDE B"] -.Berthing.-> node7
    node13["HAWSER LINES"] -.Mooring.-> node8
    node14["WAVE + CURRENT LOAD"] --> node5
    node15["SEISMIC LOAD"] --> node5
```

### 4.2 Sectional Schematic — Typical Open Pile Pier

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["HHWL Highest High Water"] --> nodeB["DECK SLAB 300mm RC"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["PILE CAP BEAM"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["RC BEARING PILE"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["SEABED"]
    nodeF["LLWL Lowest Low Water"] --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeG["FENDER UNIT"]
    nodeB --> nodeH["BOLLARD"]
    nodeI["SPLASH ZONE CORROSION COATING"] --> nodeD
    nodeJ["DREDGED WATER DEPTH"] --> nodeE
```

### 4.3 Decision Flowchart: Pier Type Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start{"WATER DEPTH REQUIRED?"} --> shallow{"Less than 8m?"}
    shallow -->|"YES"| solid["SOLID FILL PIER"]
    shallow -->|"NO"| medium{"8m to 15m?"}
    medium -->|"YES"| openPile["OPEN PILE PIER"]
    medium -->|"NO"| deep{"More than 15m?"}
    deep -->|"YES"| caisson["CAISSON PIER"]
    deep -->|"SOFT BED?"}|soft| floating["FLOATING PIER"]
    caisson --> end1["DREDGE + INSTALL"]
    solid --> end1
    openPile --> end1
    floating --> end1
```

### 4.4 Load Transfer Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["DEAD + LIVE LOAD"] --> L2["DECK SLAB"]
    L3["CRANE LOAD"] --> L2
    L4["BERTHING FORCE"] --> L5["FENDER"]
    L5 --> L2
    L6["MOORING FORCE"] --> L7["BOLLARD"]
    L7 --> L2
    L8["WAVE + CURRENT"] --> L9["PILE COLUMN"]
    L2 --> L10["PILE CAP BEAM"]
    L10 --> L9
    L9 --> L11["SOIL STRATUM"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a pier. How is it different from a wharf?**

**Model Answer:**
A **pier** is a marine structure projecting from the shoreline into the water, designed to allow vessels to berth on one or both of its longitudinal sides. It functions as a berthing and cargo-handling platform.

**Difference from a wharf:**

| **Pier** | **Wharf** |
|----------|-----------|
| Projects *perpendicular* into the sea | Built *parallel* to the shore |
| Berthing possible on **both sides** | Berthing only on **one (outer) side** |
| Needs an approach trestle to land | Directly connected to the shore |
| Higher berth capacity per unit length | Lower berth capacity per unit length |

---

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any four types of fender systems used in piers.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Rubber fenders** (cylindrical, cell, cone, arch-type)
2. **Timber fenders** (clusters of piles wrapped with rubber)
3. **Pneumatic fenders** (inflatable Yokohama-type)
4. **Spring fenders** (steel spring assemblies for heavy vessels)
5. **Gravity / hanging fenders** (concrete blocks suspended as buffers)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### ⭐ Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the different **types of piers** based on structural configuration. Mention suitable conditions for each type. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vessel of **15,000 DWT** berths at a pier with a velocity of **0.15 m/s**. If the fender deflection is **100 mm**, calculate the berthing energy and equivalent berthing force. Take $C_m = 1.7$, $C_e = 0.5$, $C_s = 0.9$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Types of piers:**

1. **Solid Pier (Filling Type)**
   - Construction: Stone, rubble, or earth fill retained by masonry/concrete walls
   - Suitable for: Shallow water (< 8 m), firm seabed, sheltered harbors
   - Limitation: Heavy, expensive in deep water

2. **Open Pile Pier**
   - Construction: Reinforced concrete deck supported on vertical and raker piles
   - Suitable for: Soft seabed, water depth 8–20 m, most common modern type
   - Advantage: Economical, allows water flow, minimal dredging

3. **Cylinder / Caisson Pier**
   - Construction: Large hollow cylindrical or box caissons sunk and filled
   - Suitable for: Deep water, weak soil needing large bearing area
   - Use: Outer berths in major ports

4. **Floating Pier**
   - Construction: Pontoon structure held in place by mooring chains
   - Suitable for: Tidal areas, marinas, ferry landings
   - Note: Vertical movement with tide — not for cargo handling

**Model Solution (b):**

Given: $M = 15{,}000 \times 1000 = 1.5 \times 10^7$ kg, $v = 0.15$ m/s, $\Delta = 0.10$ m.

Gross kinetic energy:

$$
E = \frac{1}{2} M v^2 = \frac{1}{2} (1.5 \times 10^7)(0.15)^2 = 168{,}750 \text{ J} = 168.75 \text{ kJ}
$$

Effective berthing energy:

$$
E_b = E \cdot C_m \cdot C_e \cdot C_s = 168.75 \times 1.7 \times 0.5 \times 0.9
$$

$$
E_b = 168.75 \times 0.765 = 129.09 \text{ kJ}
$$

Equivalent berthing force:

$$
F_b = \frac{E_b}{\Delta} = \frac{129.09}{0.10} = 1290.9 \text{ kN} \approx 1291 \text{ kN}
$$

**Final Answer:** $E_b \approx 129.1$ kJ, $F_b \approx 1291$ kN.

---

#### ⭐ Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss the **various loads acting on a pier**. How is the berthing energy of a vessel estimated? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design the **mooring bollard capacity** for a 30,000 DWT vessel using the KTU empirical relation. If **6 hawsers** are used, calculate the per-hawser load. Also list the components of an open pile pier. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Loads on piers:**

- **Vertical Loads:** Self-weight (dead), live load (cargo, vehicles, crowds), crane wheel loads, uplift due to buoyancy
- **Horizontal Loads:** Berthing impact force, mooring line tension, wind on vessel, current on submerged parts
- **Environmental Loads:** Wave forces (Morison equation), seismic forces, hydrostatic water pressure
- **Special Loads:** Accidental loads (collision, fire), thermal effects (deck expansion)

**Berthing energy estimation:**

The berthing energy is the kinetic energy of the vessel at the moment of impact, modified by correction coefficients:

$$
E_b = \frac{1}{2} M v^2 \cdot C_m \cdot C_e \cdot C_s
$$

- $C_m$ — mass coefficient accounts for hydrodynamic added mass (1.5–2.0)
- $C_e$ — eccentricity coefficient reduces energy for off-center impact
- $C_s$ — softness coefficient accounts for elastic contact

The equivalent berthing force is then:

$$
F_b = \frac{E_b}{\Delta}
$$

This force governs fender design and pier impact resistance.

**Model Solution (b):**

Using KTU empirical relation:

$$
T = 0.05 \times DWT = 0.05 \times 30{,}000 = 1500 \text{ kN}
$$

This is the design bollard pull capacity.

Per-hawser load (6 hawsers):

$$
T_{hawser} = \frac{1500}{6} = 250 \text{ kN}
$$

**Components of open pile pier:**

1. Deck slab (RC, 250–400 mm thick)
2. Pile cap beams
3. Bearing piles (RC precast, typically 400–800 mm diameter)
4. Fender system
5. Bollards and mooring hooks
6. Curb / kerb at deck edges
7. Service / utility trenches
8. Approach trestle
9. Handrails and edge protection

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Forgetting correction coefficients** $C_m, C_e, C_s$ — results in 3 to 4 mark deduction in Part B numerical questions
> 2. **Unit mismatch** in berthing energy — kJ vs J is the most common error
> 3. **Confusing pier with wharf** in definition questions — loses 1 full mark
> 4. **Drawing without labels** in sketches — fender, bollard, pile, deck must be marked
> 5. **Wrong empirical relation** for mooring — always state $T = 0.05 \times DWT$ explicitly
> 6. **Neglecting added mass** in Morison equation derivation — important for current load on piles

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Piers**

- ✅ A **pier** projects *perpendicular* to the shore; a **wharf** runs *parallel*
- ✅ Piers offer **double berthing** — vessels dock on both longitudinal sides
- ✅ **Three structural types:** solid fill, open pile, caisson; selection based on water depth and seabed
- ✅ **Open pile pier** is the most common modern construction in KTU problems
- ✅ **Berthing energy formula** $E_b = \frac{1}{2} M v^2 C_m C_e C_s$ is **mandatory** for numericals
- ✅ **Berthing force** $F_b = E_b / \Delta$ — depends on fender deflection
- ✅ **Mooring force** $T = 0.05 \times DWT$ (kN) — empirical KTU relation
- ✅ **Morison equation** for current force on pile: $F_D = \frac{1}{2} \rho C_D A v^2$
- ✅ **Freeboard** of pier: 1.5 m to 3 m above HHWL
- ✅ **Pile corrosion protection** is essential in the **splash zone**
- ✅ **Fender system** is the *first line of defense* against berthing impact
- ✅ **Bollards** are *strategic mooring points* placed at intervals along the pier edge
- ✅ **Approach trestle** connects the pier to the mainland quay
- ✅ **Key components** to always mention: deck, pile cap, pile, fender, bollard, curb, service trench
- ✅ **Design sequence:** vessel → loads → fender → bollard → pile → deck → detailing
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pier Heads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET754 - RAILWAY, PORT AND HARBOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pier Heads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Pier Head

A **Pier Head** is the outermost, shoreward-end (or seaward-end) structural extension of a pier, jetty, wharf, or breakwater that projects into deep water. It is the section of a marine structure specifically designed and reinforced to facilitate the **mooring and berthing of vessels**, support **navigation aids (lights, signals)**, and sometimes host **cargo-handling equipment** like cranes or loading platforms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Pier heads are the "interface structures" between the vessel and the shore facility. They are engineered to absorb enormous dynamic impact (berthing) and sustained pull (mooring) loads, making them one of the most heavily loaded components in port engineering.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a pier head may be:
- The **head of a finger pier** (a T-shaped or L-shaped jetty extending into the harbor).
- The **outer end of a breakwater** pier used for vessel mooring.
- A **dedicated mooring dolphin cluster** connected to the shore via an access trestle.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are parking a large car in a narrow garage. You cannot just ram it in. You need:
1. **Bumpers** (fenders) to absorb the impact so neither the wall nor the car is damaged.
2. **Hooks/cleats** (bollards, mooring hooks) to tie the car down once parked so it doesn't drift away.
3. **A reinforced corner wall** (the pier head structure) that is strong enough to handle the impact and the pulling force.
4. **A light on top** (navigation light) so drivers in the dark know exactly where the edge is.

A **Pier Head** in a port does exactly this — but on a massive scale. A ship weighing **50,000 to 300,000 tonnes** moving at **0.1 to 0.5 m/s** is being stopped by rubber or pneumatic fenders mounted on the pier head, while **steel mooring hooks** rated at **100 to 200 tonnes** hold the vessel against wind, current, and wave pull. The pier head is essentially a **heavily armored docking station** built at the water's edge.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Berthing velocity** of design vessel: **0.10 to 0.50 m/s** (typical design value **0.15 m/s** for large vessels).
- **Mooring pull (bollard load)**: **50 to 200 tonnes** per hook for ocean-going vessels.
- **Berthing energy absorption**: **10 to 1000+ tonne-metres** depending on vessel size.
- **Tidal range considered**: typically **±0.5 m to ±5 m** depending on the port location.
- **Concrete grade** for pier head: minimum **M40** (KTU standard for marine RCC); high-performance structures use **M50–M60**.
- **Steel reinforcement cover** in marine splash zone: minimum **75 mm** (BS 6349 / IS 4651 norms).
- **Design life**: **50 to 100 years** for major port structures.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why These Numbers Matter in KTU Exams:** Numerical design problems in KTU 2024 ESE often revolve around berthing energy (E = ½ m v²) and fender selection. Memorizing the **typical range of design berthing velocity (0.1–0.5 m/s)** is critical.

## 1.4 Functions of a Pier Head

A pier head must simultaneously perform **five primary functions**:

1. **Berthing Function:** Absorb the kinetic energy of an approaching vessel via fender systems without damage to the hull or the structure.
2. **Mooring Function:** Provide secure anchoring points (bollards, hooks) for the vessel's mooring lines to resist environmental pull-out forces.
3. **Navigation Function:** Support lighthouses, signal masts, leading lights, and fog signals.
4. **Access Function:** Provide safe personnel access via ladders, stairways, and fender access walkways.
5. **Loading Function (if applicable):** Support cranes, conveyors, or chocking points for cargo transfer.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Pier Head

A pier head typically consists of the following **sub-components**, working as an integrated system:

### 2.1.1 Deck Slab
- A **reinforced concrete slab** (thickness **0.4 m to 1.0 m**) supported on piles or piers.
- Must be designed for **wheel loads** from cargo equipment and **patch loads** from mooring bollards.

### 2.1.2 Pile Cap / Pile System
- **Vertical, raker (battered), and sometimes fender piles** driven into the seabed.
- **Vertical piles** carry **gravity (dead + live) loads**.
- **Raker piles** at an angle of **1:4 to 1:6** (horizontal:vertical) resist **horizontal berthing and mooring forces**.

### 2.1.3 Fender System
The fender is the **energy-absorbing buffer** between the vessel hull and the pier head. Common types:

| Fender Type | Energy Absorption | Reaction Force | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Timber fender** | Low (5–50 kJ) | High | Small craft, fishing harbors |
| **Rubber cylindrical** | Medium (10–100 kJ) | Medium | General cargo berths |
| **Rubber cell (chevron)** | High (50–500 kJ) | Low | Large vessel berths |
| **Pneumatic / Foam-filled** | Very High (100–3000 kJ) | Very Low | VLCC, naval berths |
| **Buckling rubber cell** | High (200–1500 kJ) | Constant | Container, bulk berths |

### 2.1.4 Bollards and Mooring Hooks
- **Bollards**: Short, single-cast-iron or steel posts with a curved horn. Rated **10 to 200 tonnes**.
- **Mooring hooks (Quick-release hooks)**: Allow **single-handed line release** from the deck. Rated **50 to 200 tonnes**.
- Spaced at **15 m to 30 m** along the pier head.

### 2.1.5 Capstans and Winches
- **Capstans**: Vertical rotating drums for hauling mooring lines.
- **Winches**: Used for adjusting mooring line tension on the vessel.

### 2.1.6 Ladders, Stairways, and Access Walkways
- Provide personnel access from boat to pier head.
- Must have **non-slip surfaces** and **safety rails** (height **1.1 m** as per IS 4651).

### 2.1.7 Navigation Aids
- **Lighthouse / Beacon light**: For night-time vessel approach.
- **Leading lights**: For channel alignment.
- **Fog signal / Bell**: For low-visibility conditions.

## 2.2 Loads Acting on a Pier Head

A pier head is subjected to a **complex multi-directional load system**. The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes them as follows:

### 2.2.1 Vertical Loads
- **Dead Load (DL)**: Self-weight of deck, beams, pile cap, and utilities.
- **Live Load (LL)**: Surcharge from cranes, cargo, vehicles, personnel. Typically **5 to 10 kN/m²**.
- **Mooring vertical load**: Small downward pull from moored vessel lines (usually negligible).

### 2.2.2 Horizontal Loads
- **Berthing Impact Load (F_b)**: Sudden impact when a vessel strikes the fender.
- **Mooring Pull Load (F_m)**: Sustained horizontal pull from mooring lines due to wind, current, and wave action on the vessel.
- **Wave Load (F_w)**: Direct wave impact on the pier head structure.
- **Current Load (F_c)**: Drag force from tidal currents.
- **Seismic Load (F_e)**: Inertial force during earthquake events.
- **Wind Load (F_wind)**: Direct wind on the superstructure.

### 2.2.3 Environmental / Indirect Loads
- **Temperature stresses** (thermal expansion/contraction of deck).
- **Shrinkage and creep** in concrete.
- **Corrosion-related degradation** (long-term).
- **Scour around piles** (reduces lateral capacity over time).

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Load Category

- **Berthing forces are dynamic** — they must be computed using energy methods, not just statics.
- **Mooring forces are quasi-static** — they are sustained and may cause fatigue in mooring hardware.
- **Wave forces can dominate** in exposed locations — Morison's equation (for slender piles) or Goda's formula (for large structures) applies.
- **Seismic forces** are particularly critical in **Kerala's seismic zone III** — relevant to KTU 2024 case studies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Pier head design directly determines the **maximum vessel size** a berth can handle. Under-designing the fender system leads to ship hull damage (litigation); over-designing wastes capital. KTU problems often test the *balance* between energy absorption and reaction force.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes / Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Berthing Kinetic Energy | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v^{2}$ | kJ or tonne·m | $m$ = vessel displacement (tonnes), $v$ = berthing velocity (m/s) |
| 2 | Effective Berthing Energy | $E_{eff} = E \cdot C_{m} \cdot C_{e} \cdot C_{s}$ | kJ | $C_m$ = mass coeff., $C_e$ = eccentricity coeff., $C_s$ = softness coeff. |
| 3 | Eccentricity Coefficient | $C_{e} = 1.0$ (for $\vert K \vert \leq 0.5$) | dimensionless | $K$ = longitudinal position factor |
| 4 | Berthing Force on Fender | $F_{b} = E_{eff} / \delta$ | kN | $\delta$ = fender deflection (m) |
| 5 | Fender Reaction Force | $R = f(\delta, \text{fender type})$ | kN | From fender manufacturer's curve |
| 6 | Mooring Pull per Line | $F_{line} = (P_{total}) / (n \cdot \cos\theta)$ | kN | $n$ = number of lines, $\theta$ = line angle with horizontal |
| 7 | Wind Force on Vessel | $P_{w} = C_{d} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot V^{2} \cdot A$ | kN | $C_d$ ≈ 1.0–1.6 for broadside |
| 8 | Current Force on Vessel | $P_{c} = C_{d} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_{w} \cdot v^{2} \cdot A_{sub}$ | kN | $C_d$ ≈ 0.6–1.2 for submerged hull |
| 9 | Morison's Wave Force on Pile | $F = C_{d} \tfrac{1}{2}\rho D u \vert u \vert + C_{m} \tfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \dot{u}$ | kN/m | $D$ = pile diameter, $u$ = water particle velocity |
| 10 | Fender Energy (Rubber Cell) | $E_{fender} = \tfrac{1}{2} R \cdot \delta$ | kJ | Energy = ½ × Reaction × Deflection |
| 11 | Number of Fenders Required | $N = E_{total} / E_{one}$ | dimensionless | Round up to nearest integer |
| 12 | Pile Lateral Capacity (Broms) | $H_{u} = \text{function of } M_{y},\, \gamma,\, D,\, L$ | kN | Used for raker pile design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Tip:** In Part A (3-mark) questions, you are often asked to **state the formula** for berthing energy or fender reaction. Memorize Equations 1, 2, and 4 from the cheat sheet above.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of Pier Head Design

- Determines the **tonnage class of vessels** that a port can accommodate.
- Directly affects **cargo handling efficiency** at berth (downtime from damaged fenders).
- Critical for **naval berths** (submarine, aircraft carrier) requiring extreme energy absorption.
- Important for **LNG / oil jetties** where fire safety and quick-release mooring are mandatory.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Berthing Energy

A vessel of displacement mass $m$ moving at a berthing velocity $v$ must be brought to rest by the fender system. The kinetic energy is converted into potential (elastic) energy stored in the deformed fender.

### Step 1: Kinetic Energy of the Moving Vessel

The kinetic energy of a vessel of mass $m$ (in tonnes, equivalent to $1000 \cdot m$ kg) moving with velocity $v$ (m/s) is given by Newton's second law integrated over distance.

$$
E = \frac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v^{2}
$$

**Logic:** Kinetic energy $= \tfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$ is fundamental mechanics. We convert $m$ from tonnes to kg for SI consistency: $m_{kg} = 1000 \cdot m_{tonnes}$. Thus:

$$
E = \frac{1}{2} \cdot (1000 \cdot m) \cdot v^{2} \quad [\text{Joules}]
$$

### Step 2: Conversion to tonne-metres (Engineering Unit)

In port engineering, energy is expressed in **tonne-metres (tm)** or **kilojoules (kJ)**. Using $1 \text{ tm} = 9806.65 \text{ J} \approx 9.81 \text{ kJ}$:

$$
E \,[\text{tm}] = \frac{m \cdot v^{2}}{2 \times 9.81 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{m \cdot v^{2}}{0.01962}
$$

Or equivalently, in kJ:

$$
E \,[\text{kJ}] = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1000 \cdot m \cdot v^{2} = 500 \cdot m \cdot v^{2}
$$

### Step 3: Introduction of Correction Coefficients

The actual energy absorbed by the fender is less than the gross kinetic energy because:

- **Mass Coefficient ($C_m$)**: The displaced water moves with the ship, so the effective mass is greater than the ship's mass. $C_m$ ranges from **1.5 to 2.0** for large vessels.
- **Eccentricity Coefficient ($C_e$)**: Berthing is rarely a pure head-on impact; usually the vessel strikes at an angle or off-center. $C_e$ depends on the position of impact along the ship's length.

$$
C_{e} = \frac{1}{1 + \left(\frac{r}{R}\right)^{2}}
$$

where $r$ is the distance from the ship's center of gravity to the point of impact, and $R$ is the radius of gyration of the ship (typically $R \approx 0.25 \cdot L$, where $L$ is the vessel's length).

- **Softness Coefficient ($C_s$)**: The fender, hull, and pier together act as a spring system. $C_s$ is typically **0.9 to 1.0**.

### Step 4: The Effective Berthing Energy

$$
E_{eff} = E \cdot C_{m} \cdot C_{e} \cdot C_{s}
$$

**Logic:** Multiplying by these three coefficients gives the realistic energy that a single fender (or set of fenders) must absorb. This is the **design energy** used to size the fender system.

### Step 5: Converting Energy to Force via Fender Deflection

The fender acts as a spring. Energy stored in a linear spring is:

$$
E = \frac{1}{2} \cdot R \cdot \delta
$$

where $R$ is the fender reaction force and $\delta$ is the fender deflection. Rearranging for $R$:

$$
R = \frac{2 \cdot E_{eff}}{\delta}
$$

For non-linear fenders (e.g., rubber cell), the relationship is read from the manufacturer's load-deflection curve.

### Step 6: Distributed vs. Concentrated Energy

If the berthing energy is to be distributed over $N$ fenders equally:

$$
E_{per\,fender} = \frac{E_{eff}}{N}
$$

This is used to select the **fender rating** (kJ per fender) from the manufacturer's catalogue.

### Step 7: Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style)

**Given:**
- Vessel displacement $m = 50{,}000$ tonnes
- Berthing velocity $v = 0.20$ m/s
- Mass coefficient $C_m = 1.8$
- Eccentricity coefficient $C_e = 0.7$
- Softness coefficient $C_s = 1.0$

**Find:** (a) Effective berthing energy. (b) Reaction force if fender deflection $\delta = 0.30$ m and 3 fenders share the load.

**Solution:**

(a) Gross kinetic energy:
$$
E = \frac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 50{,}000 \times (0.20)^{2} = 1000 \text{ tm}
$$

Effective energy:
$$
E_{eff} = 1000 \times 1.8 \times 0.7 \times 1.0 = 1260 \text{ tm} \;(\approx 12{,}360 \text{ kJ})
$$

(b) Energy per fender:
$$
E_{per\,fender} = \frac{1260}{3} = 420 \text{ tm}
$$

Reaction force per fender:
$$
R = \frac{2 \cdot 420}{0.30} = 2800 \text{ tm/m} = 2800 \text{ tonnes force per metre deflection}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students forget to convert tonnes to kg in the kinetic energy formula. Always state units explicitly to earn full marks. The examiner will deduct if $E$ is given as "1000" without units.

## 3.2 Derivation of Mooring Force Distribution

When a vessel is moored, the total horizontal force $P_{total}$ (from wind + current + wave on the moored ship) is resisted by $n$ mooring lines. If each line makes an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal in the longitudinal direction and $\phi$ with the horizontal in the transverse direction, then:

### Step 1: Force Equilibrium — Longitudinal Direction

The longitudinal component of force in each mooring line is $F_{line} \cdot \cos\theta$. If $n_L$ lines resist longitudinal force:

$$
F_{line} = \frac{P_{long}}{\sum_{i=1}^{n_L} \cos\theta_i}
$$

### Step 2: Force Equilibrium — Transverse Direction

Similarly, with $n_T$ breast lines:

$$
F_{line} = \frac{P_{trans}}{\sum_{i=1}^{n_T} \cos\phi_i}
$$

### Step 3: The Vertical Reaction at Bollard

Each mooring line exerts a vertical pull (downward) on the bollard equal to:

$$
V_{bollard} = F_{line} \cdot \sin\alpha
$$

where $\alpha$ is the angle of the line with the horizontal. The bollard foundation must resist both **horizontal pull-out** and **vertical uplift**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Insight:** When computing mooring line tensions, students commonly ignore the **angle effect**. In reality, a line at 30° carries 1.155 times the horizontal force, and a line at 45° carries 1.414 times. Always include $\cos\theta$ in your calculation.

## 3.3 Pile Foundation Reactions for Pier Head

### Step 3.3.1: Vertical Pile Capacity

For a single vertical pile, the ultimate axial capacity is:

$$
Q_{u} = Q_{b} + Q_{s} = q_{b} \cdot A_{b} + \sum f_{s,i} \cdot A_{s,i}
$$

where:
- $q_{b}$ = unit end bearing (kN/m²)
- $A_{b}$ = pile base area
- $f_{s,i}$ = unit skin friction in layer $i$
- $A_{s,i}$ = surface area of pile in layer $i$

### Step 3.3.2: Lateral Pile Capacity (Broms' Method)

For a **free-head pile** in cohesive soil, the ultimate lateral load is:

$$
H_{u} = \frac{M_{y}}{e + 0.67 f}
$$

For a **fixed-head pile**:

$$
H_{u} = \frac{2 M_{y}}{e + 0.67 f}
$$

where $M_y$ is the yield moment of the pile section, $e$ is the eccentricity of load above ground, and $f$ is the depth of failure (often taken as $f = 3D$ in soft clays).

### Step 3.3.3: Group Efficiency

For a pile group with $n$ piles spaced at $s$ diameters center-to-center, the efficiency is given by **Converse-Labarre formula**:

$$
\eta = 1 - \alpha \cdot \frac{(n-1)m + (m-1)n}{90 \cdot m \cdot n}
$$

where $\alpha = \tan^{-1}(D/s)$ in degrees, and $m, n$ are rows and columns. For pier heads, $\eta$ is typically **0.6 to 0.8**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for Fender Selection

```
Vessel Displacement (m) — known
        ↓
Berthing Velocity (v) — assumed from design norms (0.10 to 0.50 m/s)
        ↓
Gross Kinetic Energy E = ½mv²
        ↓
Apply Coefficients (Cm, Ce, Cs) → E_eff
        ↓
Distribute E_eff over N fenders → E_per_fender
        ↓
Match E_per_fender to fender catalogue (rubber cell / pneumatic / hydraulic)
        ↓
Check Reaction Force R ≤ allowable hull pressure (≤ 200 kN/m² for steel hulls)
        ↓
If R too high → use softer fender (cell-type) or add more fenders
```

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value | KTU Reference |

| $C_m$ | Mass coefficient | 1.5 – 2.0 | BS 6349 |
| $C_e$ | Eccentricity coefficient | 0.4 – 1.0 | BS 6349 |
| $C_s$ | Softness coefficient | 0.9 – 1.0 | BS 6349 |
| $C_d$ | Drag coefficient (vessel) | 0.6 – 1.2 | — |
| $C_d$ | Drag coefficient (pile) | 0.7 – 1.2 | Morison's equation |
| $C_m$ | Inertia coefficient (pile) | 2.0 (circular) | Morison's equation |
| $\rho_{air}$ | Air density | 1.226 kg/m³ | Standard |
| $\rho_{w}$ | Seawater density | 1025 kg/m³ | Standard |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Pier Head and list its primary functions. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A **Pier Head** is the seaward-most reinforced structural extension of a pier, jetty, or wharf that projects into navigable water to facilitate vessel berthing and mooring operations. It is engineered to safely absorb the dynamic impact of approaching vessels via fender systems and to provide secure mooring points.

**Primary Functions (must list at least 4 for full marks):**

1. **Berthing**: Absorbs the kinetic energy of a vessel approaching the berth through fender systems.
2. **Mooring**: Provides anchored points (bollards, quick-release hooks) for the vessel's mooring lines.
3. **Navigation Support**: Houses lighthouses, beacons, leading lights, and fog signals.
4. **Access**: Provides safe personnel access to/from the vessel via ladders and stairways.
5. **Cargo Loading (where applicable)**: Supports cranes, conveyors, and chocking points.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often confuse a "Pier Head" with a "Breakwater Head" or a "Dolphin". Remember: A pier head is structurally continuous with a pier, while a dolphin is a standalone mooring structure.

---

### Question 2: What are the different types of fenders used on pier heads? Mention the energy absorption range. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Fenders are energy-absorbing devices mounted on the face of the pier head to protect both the vessel hull and the structure during berthing. The principal types are:

| Fender Type | Energy Absorption Range | Reaction Force | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Timber fender** | 5 – 50 kJ | High | Small craft, fishing harbors |
| **Rubber cylindrical** | 10 – 100 kJ | Medium | General cargo berths |
| **Rubber cell (chevron)** | 50 – 500 kJ | Low (constant) | Large vessel berths |
| **Pneumatic / foam-filled** | 100 – 3000 kJ | Very low | VLCC, naval, LNG berths |
| **Buckling rubber cell** | 200 – 1500 kJ | Constant | Container and bulk berths |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse "energy absorption" with "reaction force". A good fender has **high energy absorption with low reaction force**. Many students incorrectly state that fenders increase impact force — the opposite is true.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1): Berthing Energy and Fender Design

**(a)** A vessel of displacement **40,000 tonnes** berths at a velocity of **0.18 m/s** at a pier head. The mass coefficient $C_m = 1.75$, eccentricity coefficient $C_e = 0.65$, and softness coefficient $C_s = 1.0$. The pier head uses **4 identical rubber cell fenders**, each with a deflection of **0.25 m** at design load. Determine:
1. The gross kinetic energy of the vessel.
2. The effective berthing energy.
3. The energy absorbed per fender.
4. The fender reaction force per fender.

`[7 Marks]`

**(b)** Explain in detail the **load combinations** to be considered in the design of a pier head. With neat sketches, describe the **components of a pier head** and their functions.

`[7 Marks]`

---

#### Model Solution to A(a):

**Given:** $m = 40{,}000$ tonnes, $v = 0.18$ m/s, $C_m = 1.75$, $C_e = 0.65$, $C_s = 1.0$, $N = 4$ fenders, $\delta = 0.25$ m.

**Step 1: Gross kinetic energy**

$$
E = \frac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 40{,}000 \times (0.18)^{2}
$$

$$
E = \frac{1}{2} \times 40{,}000 \times 0.0324 = 648 \text{ tm}
$$

**Step 2: Effective berthing energy**

$$
E_{eff} = E \cdot C_m \cdot C_e \cdot C_s = 648 \times 1.75 \times 0.65 \times 1.0
$$

$$
E_{eff} = 737.1 \text{ tm} \quad (\approx 7{,}228 \text{ kJ})
$$

**Step 3: Energy per fender**

$$
E_{per\,fender} = \frac{E_{eff}}{N} = \frac{737.1}{4} = 184.3 \text{ tm}
$$

**Step 4: Fender reaction force per fender**

$$
R = \frac{2 \cdot E_{per\,fender}}{\delta} = \frac{2 \times 184.3}{0.25} = 1474.4 \text{ tm/m} \approx 1474 \text{ tonnes force/m deflection}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part A-a):** The most common error is forgetting the three correction coefficients. If the student computes only $E = 648$ tm and stops, they lose **3 out of 7 marks**. Always show the full expression for $E_{eff}$ explicitly.

---

#### Model Solution to A(b):

The following **load combinations** must be considered in pier head design (as per IS 4651 / BS 6349):

1. **Dead Load + Live Load (DL + LL):** Routine operating condition. Factor of safety = 1.5 on materials.

2. **DL + LL + Berthing Force (Fb):** Single fender impact at design velocity.

3. **DL + LL + Mooring Force (Fm):** Worst-case mooring line pull due to combined wind + current + wave on moored vessel.

4. **DL + LL + Wave Force (Fw):** Direct wave attack on the pier head superstructure.

5. **DL + LL + Seismic Force (Fe):** Earthquake inertial force (relevant for KTU Kerala zone III).

6. **DL + Berthing Force + Mooring Force (unusual combination):** Considered only for special cases (e.g., emergency berthing with moored ship).

**Components of a Pier Head (with schematic labels):**

1. **Deck Slab** — Distributes loads to pile cap; resists local wheel and bollard loads.
2. **Pile Cap** — RCC beam transferring loads from deck to piles.
3. **Vertical Piles** — Carry gravity loads to deep strata.
4. **Raker Piles** — Inclined piles resisting horizontal berthing and mooring loads.
5. **Fender System** — Rubber cell / pneumatic units absorbing berthing energy.
6. **Mooring Bollards and Quick-Release Hooks** — Anchor points for vessel lines.
7. **Ladders and Stairways** — Personnel access.
8. **Navigation Light / Beacon** — For night-time vessel approach.
9. **Capstans and Winches** — For hauling and adjusting mooring lines.
10. **Crane Rails (if applicable)** — For cargo handling equipment.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part A-b):** Students often draw a vague rectangle and label it "pier head" without showing fenders, bollards, piles, and rakers. The examiner awards marks based on **labeled components**. A clear hand-drawn sketch with at least **6 labeled parts** is essential.

---

### Question B (Choice 2): Mooring System and Pile Design

**(a)** A moored vessel at a pier head is subjected to the following environmental forces:
- Wind force on broadside: $P_w = 600$ kN
- Current force on submerged hull: $P_c = 350$ kN
- Wave force on moored ship: $P_{wave} = 200$ kN

The mooring system has **6 breast lines** at 30° to the horizontal. Determine the tension in each breast line assuming the total force is shared equally.

`[7 Marks]`

**(b)** Describe with neat sketches the **various types of fender systems** used on pier heads. Compare their energy absorption, reaction force, and typical applications in a tabulated form.

`[7 Marks]`

---

#### Model Solution to B(a):

**Given:**
- $P_w = 600$ kN, $P_c = 350$ kN, $P_{wave} = 200$ kN
- Number of breast lines $n = 6$
- Angle with horizontal $\theta = 30°$

**Step 1: Total horizontal force on vessel**

$$
P_{total} = P_w + P_c + P_{wave} = 600 + 350 + 200 = 1150 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 2: Force equilibrium in transverse direction**

For breast lines, the horizontal component of tension in each line is $F_{line} \cdot \cos\theta$. Total transverse resistance is $n \cdot F_{line} \cdot \cos\theta$.

$$
n \cdot F_{line} \cdot \cos\theta = P_{total}
$$

$$
F_{line} = \frac{P_{total}}{n \cdot \cos\theta}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute values**

$$
F_{line} = \frac{1150}{6 \cdot \cos 30°} = \frac{1150}{6 \times 0.866} = \frac{1150}{5.196}
$$

$$
F_{line} = 221.3 \text{ kN per line}
$$

**Step 4: Vertical pull on bollard (additional check)**

$$
V_{bollard} = F_{line} \cdot \sin 30° = 221.3 \times 0.5 = 110.65 \text{ kN}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part B-a):** A common mistake is dividing by 6 directly without the $\cos\theta$ term, giving $F_{line} = 191.67$ kN — **which is incorrect**. The mooring line tension is always higher than the horizontal force share because the line is inclined. Full marks only if the angle effect is explicitly included.

---

#### Model Solution to B(b):

**Types of Fender Systems:**

1. **Timber Fenders:** Vertical timber piles or wales bolted to the pier face. Simple, low cost, low energy absorption (5–50 kJ). Used in small craft harbors.

2. **Rubber Cylindrical Fenders:** Hollow rubber cylinders hung along the berthing face. Simple, moderate energy absorption (10–100 kJ), moderate reaction.

3. **Rubber Cell (Chevron) Fenders:** V-shaped rubber blocks with internal steel plates. High energy absorption (50–500 kJ) with low and constant reaction. Most common for general cargo.

4. **Pneumatic Fenders:** Inflated rubber cylinders filled with compressed air. Very high energy absorption (100–3000 kJ) with very low reaction force. Used for VLCC and naval berths.

5. **Buckling Rubber Cell Fenders:** Rubber cells that buckle under load, giving a constant reaction over large deflection. Used at container and bulk berths.

6. **Hydraulic Fenders:** Piston-cylinder units with hydraulic fluid. Adjustable reaction; used at very large berths.

7. **Foam-Filled Fenders:** Closed-cell foam inside rubber skin. Submersible; used between ship-to-ship (STS) operations.

**Tabulated Comparison:**

| Fender Type | Energy Range (kJ) | Reaction Force | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Timber | 5 – 50 | High | Fishing, small craft |
| Rubber cylindrical | 10 – 100 | Medium | General cargo |
| Rubber cell (chevron) | 50 – 500 | Low (constant) | Large vessels |
| Pneumatic | 100 – 3000 | Very low | VLCC, naval, LNG |
| Buckling cell | 200 – 1500 | Constant | Container, bulk |
| Hydraulic | 500 – 5000 | Adjustable | Very large berths |
| Foam-filled | 50 – 500 | Low | STS, emergency |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part B-b):** Students often describe only **timber** and **rubber** fenders. To score full 7 marks, you must describe **at least 4–5 types** with proper tabulated comparison. A diagram showing a rubber cell or pneumatic fender in section view is highly recommended.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Common Mark-Loss Points (Consolidated)

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP 5 MARK-LOSS MISTAKES IN PIER HEAD QUESTIONS (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting correction coefficients** ($C_m$, $C_e$, $C_s$) in berthing energy — costs **3 marks** typically.
> 2. **Ignoring the angle of mooring lines** — gives wrong mooring line tension.
> 3. **Confusing "energy" and "force"** — fender selection is based on energy, not force.
> 4. **Not labeling components** in the sketch — at least **6 labels** needed for full marks.
> 5. **Using wrong units** — mixing SI (kN, kJ) with engineering (tonnes, tm) without conversion factors. Always state conversion if used.

---

> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — PIER HEADS (PECET754 / Module 3)**

- ☐ **Definition:** Pier head = seaward extension of pier/jetty for berthing + mooring + navigation.
- ☐ **Primary Functions:** Berthing, Mooring, Navigation, Access, Loading.
- ☐ **Fender Formulae:**
  - $E = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$
  - $E_{eff} = E \cdot C_m \cdot C_e \cdot C_s$
  - $R = 2 E_{per\,fender} / \delta$
- ☐ **Standard Coefficient Values:**
  - $C_m = 1.5$ to $2.0$
  - $C_e = 0.4$ to $1.0$
  - $C_s = 0.9$ to $1.0$
  - $v = 0.10$ to $0.50$ m/s
- ☐ **Mooring Force:** $F_{line} = P_{total} / (n \cdot \cos\theta)$. Never forget the $\cos\theta$ term.
- ☐ **Pile Types:** Vertical (gravity), Raker (horizontal), Fender (impact cushion).
- ☐ **Fender Selection Criterion:** Highest energy absorption + lowest reaction force within hull pressure limit (≤ 200 kN/m²).
- ☐ **Bollard Ratings:** 10 t (small) → 200 t (VLCC). Quick-release hooks are used for fire-safety and rapid departure.
- ☐ **Pile Capacity (Broms):** $H_u = M_y / (e + 0.67f)$ for free-head piles in clay.
- ☐ **Pile Group Efficiency (Converse-Labarre):** $\eta = 0.6$ to $0.8$ for typical pier head pile groups.
- ☐ **Concrete Grade for Pier Head:** M40 minimum; M50–M60 preferred in splash zone.
- ☐ **Design Code References:** IS 4651 (Parts I, II, III, IV), BS 6349, PIANC guidelines.
- ☐ **Loads to Combine:** DL + LL + Berthing / Mooring / Wave / Seismic (as relevant).
- ☐ **Critical KTU 2024 Keywords:** "Berthing energy", "Effective energy", "Fender reaction", "Mooring tension", "Raker pile", "Bollard", "Quick-release hook".

> **End of Section 5 — All five sections of the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 output are complete.**

topics.title: Dolphin
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET754 - RAILWAY, PORT AND HARBOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dolphin

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — DOCKS AND HARBOURS: DOLPHIN STRUCTURES

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DOLPHIN (KTU 2024 Official Definition):**
> A **Dolphin** is an isolated marine structure, consisting of a cluster of piles, sheet piles, or a single large cylinder (caisson), driven or constructed into the seabed and projecting above the water surface, primarily used for **berthing**, **mooring**, or **leading/guide** of vessels at a berth, jetty, wharf, or lock approach. Dolphins transfer horizontal loads (impact, pull, breasting) from vessels to the foundation soil through a combination of vertical and batter piles.

In KTU 2024 scheme parlance, dolphins are auxiliary berthing structures that are **structurally independent of the main wharf/quay** but functionally integrated with it through catwalks or access platforms. They are employed when the water depth adjacent to the existing structure is insufficient or when the cost of extending a solid berth is uneconomical.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Lone Tree Trunk in a River"**
> Imagine a wide river where boats need to dock. Instead of building an entire concrete wall along the riverbank (a wharf), the port engineer drives several long wooden poles deep into the riverbed and ties their tops together with a wooden cap. This little island of poles — sticking out of the water like a single tree trunk — is what a *dolphin* looks like.
> A boat can tie its rope (mooring) to this structure or gently lean against it (berthing/breasting), exactly as it would against a quay wall. Multiple such "tree trunks" lined up along the berth form a complete berthing facility without building a continuous wall.
> In nature, dolphins (the marine mammals) swim in formation beside ships — and that is the etymological root of the name: **the structures swim (project) alone, beside the main jetty**, guiding or mooring the vessel.

### 1.3 Functional Role in a Port Layout

A dolphin system typically performs **three critical functions** in a berth:

| Function | Description | Typical Location |
|----------|-------------|------------------|
| **Mooring** | Holds the vessel against horizontal drift (wind, current, tide) | Bow & stern of vessel |
| **Breasting** | Resists the lateral berthing impact of the approaching vessel | Middle of vessel |
| **Leading/Guiding** | Aligns the vessel during approach to the berth | Approach channel side |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Whenever a question mentions "Isolated berthing structures" or "Berths beyond the apron of a wharf" — the default answer keyword is **DOLPHIN**.

### 1.4 Classification of Dolphins (KTU Syllabus Mapping)

Dolphins are classified based on **structural form** and **functional purpose**:

**A. Based on Structural Type:**

1. **Pile Cluster Dolphin** — Most common; group of vertical + batter piles with RCC pile cap.
2. **Sheet Pile Dolphin** — Cellular or diaphragm type using interconnected steel sheet piles filled with earth.
3. **Caisson Dolphin** — Large hollow cylinder (concrete/steel) floated into position, sunk, and filled.
4. **Floating Dolphin** — Pontoon-type, restrained by chains/anchors; used where seabed is too deep for piling.

**B. Based on Function:**

1. **Mooring Dolphin** — Carries only horizontal pull (tension in ropes).
2. **Breasting Dolphin** — Carries the impact (berthing energy) of the vessel.
3. **Leading (Guide) Dolphin** — Aligns the vessel during approach.
4. **Combination Dolphin** — Performs two or more of the above functions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Typical Pile Cluster Dolphin

A pile cluster dolphin is composed of the following engineering components:

1. **Pile Cap (Deck Slab):** A heavily reinforced RCC slab that **distributes** the concentrated point load from the bollard or fender into the pile group.
2. **Vertical Piles:** Carry the **predominant share of vertical load** (self-weight + live load on cap) and a portion of horizontal load through bending.
3. **Batter (Raker) Piles:** Inclined piles (typically 1:3 to 1:5 batter) that **resist the major share of horizontal load** through axial compression/tension.
4. **Fenders:** Energy-absorbing devices (rubber, timber, pneumatic) fixed to the seaward face of the breasting dolphin.
5. **Bollards (Mooring Posts):** Steel castings on top of mooring dolphins to which the vessel's hawsers are tied.
6. **Catwalk/Access Bridge:** A narrow pedestrian walkway connecting the dolphin to the main wharf.
7. **Wearing Surface & Handrails:** Safety features for the operating personnel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why batter piles are essential:** A pure vertical pile resists lateral load only by **bending**, and a long slender pile in soft marine clay has very low lateral stiffness. A batter pile resists the same horizontal load through **axial compression along its length**, which is far more efficient. A typical dolphin uses **2 vertical piles + 4 to 6 batter piles** per cluster.

## 2.2 Forces Acting on a Dolphin

The design of a dolphin requires consideration of the following force systems:

| Force | Source | Direction | Magnitude Reference |
|-------|--------|-----------|---------------------|
| **Berthing Impact (E_kinetic)** | Vessel approaching the berth | Horizontal, normal to berth face | $E_k = \frac{1}{2} m V_n^2 C_d C_m C_s$ |
| **Mooring Pull (T)** | Hawsers under wind/current | Horizontal, along berth | From windage area of vessel |
| **Wave Force (F_w)** | Breaking/non-breaking waves | Horizontal, variable | Depends on $H, d, T$ |
| **Current Force (F_c)** | Drag on submerged hull | Horizontal | $F_c = \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A V_c^2$ |
| **Wind Force (F_wind)** | Windage on exposed hull | Horizontal, above water | $F_{wind} = C_f \rho_a A V_w^2$ |
| **Self-weight + Live Load** | Pile cap + personnel + equipment | Vertical | Constant |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** the KTU board examiner expects a student to reproduce for the Dolphin topic. Memorize the structure, not the constants.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Kinetic Energy of Berthing Vessel | $E = \frac{1}{2} m V_n^2 C_d C_m C_s$ | kJ | Energy to be absorbed by fender |
| 2 | Normal Approach Velocity | $V_n = V \sin\theta$ | m/s | Component perpendicular to berth |
| 3 | Berthing Energy (Mass form) | $E = \frac{W}{2g} V_n^2 C_d C_m C_s$ | kJ | When W (displacement) is given in tonnes |
| 4 | Hydrodynamic Mass Coefficient | $C_m = 1 + \frac{2 \pi h}{B} \cdot \frac{\rho_w}{\rho_s}$ | — | Deep draft correction |
| 5 | Eccentricity Coefficient | $C_e = \frac{K^2 + R^2 \cos^2\gamma}{K^2 + R^2}$ | — | For berthing at distance $K$ from midship |
| 6 | Softness Coefficient | $C_s = 0.5$ (rigid) to $1.0$ (soft) | — | Depends on fender type |
| 7 | Fender Reaction | $R_f = f(E, deflection)$ | kN | Read from fender performance curve |
| 8 | Mooring Force (Wind on vessel) | $F_w = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} C_f A V_w^2$ | kN | Empirical PIANC formula |
| 9 | Pile Axial Capacity | $Q_u = Q_b + Q_s$ | kN | Static formula |
| 10 | Group Efficiency | $\eta = 1 - \theta \cdot \frac{(n-1)m + (m-1)n}{90 mn}$ | — | Converse-Labarre formula |
| 11 | Deflection of Pile Group | $\delta = \frac{H h^3}{3 E_p I_p N}$ | m | For lateral load H |
| 12 | Wave Force (Morison) | $f = \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d D u \vert u \vert + \rho C_m A \dot{u}$ | N/m | Per unit length of pile |

**Where the key symbols are:**

- $m$ = Mass of vessel (tonnes) ; $V_n$ = Normal berthing velocity (m/s)
- $C_d$ = Deformation coefficient (≈ 0.9 for open sea, 0.7 for sheltered)
- $C_m$ = Mass (virtual) coefficient (1.5 to 2.0 typical)
- $C_s$ = Softness coefficient (≈ 1.0 for rubber fenders)
- $W$ = Displacement of vessel (tonnes) ; $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$
- $A$ = Windage area (m²) ; $C_f$ = Wind force coefficient
- $V_w$ = Wind speed (m/s) ; $V_c$ = Current speed (m/s)
- $\rho$ = Density of water (1025 kg/m³ for seawater)

## 2.4 Design Philosophy — Why Dolphins are Designed Differently from Wharves

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Concept: Independent vs. Continuous Structure**
> A **wharf** is a continuous wall/structure — it distributes loads over a long length.
> A **dolphin** is a **point structure** — it must resist the entire vessel force at a single "point" in the sea. This makes the design philosophy fundamentally different:
> 1. **Local intensity of force is high** → requires heavy fendering and a rigid pile cap.
> 2. **No redundancy in horizontal direction** → must have batter piles for lateral stability.
> 3. **Scour and seabed conditions dominate design** → pile length often governed by scour depth, not by structural capacity.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Dolphin systems are the workhorses of the following maritime infrastructure:

- **Oil Jetties** — Where tankers of 100,000+ DWT berth at single-point mooring (SPM) or multi-buoy mooring.
- **Container Terminals** — Breasting dolphins at the seaward end of the apron.
- **LNG/LPG Terminals** — Where the cryogenic hazard mandates the loading arms be away from the main wharf.
- **Lock Entrances** — Guide dolphins for vessels negotiating lock approach.
- **Shipyards** — Where outfitting berths use dolphins to support hull sections.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation I: Berthing Energy of a Vessel Against a Dolphin

This is the **single most important derivation** for the KTU board exam on dolphins. We derive the kinetic energy that the fender system of the dolphin must absorb.

### Step 1 — Identify the source of energy

When a vessel of mass $m$ approaches the berth with a velocity $V$ at an angle $\theta$ to the berthing face, the **component of velocity normal to the dolphin** is what produces the impact.

$$V_n = V \sin\theta$$

### Step 2 — Apply classical kinetic energy formula

The kinetic energy associated with the **normal component** is:

$$E = \frac{1}{2} m V_n^2$$

Substituting the normal velocity:

$$E = \frac{1}{2} m (V \sin\theta)^2 = \frac{1}{2} m V^2 \sin^2\theta$$

### Step 3 — Introduce correction coefficients

The actual energy absorbed by the fender is **less** than the theoretical kinetic energy because:

- The water mass moves with the hull → effective mass is **higher** → $C_m > 1$
- The vessel is **not perfectly rigid** → energy is lost in internal deformation → $C_d < 1$
- The fender has finite stiffness → $C_s$ accounts for energy radiated back

$$E_{design} = \frac{1}{2} m V^2 \sin^2\theta \cdot C_d C_m C_s$$

### Step 4 — Express in terms of displacement (more common in practice)

Vessel displacement is given in tonnes (1 tonne = 1000 kg), so $m = W/g$:

$$E_{design} = \frac{W}{2g} V_n^2 C_d C_m C_s$$

### Step 5 — Substituting typical values (worked example)

> **Numerical: A 50,000 DWT vessel approaches a breasting dolphin at $V = 0.20 \, m/s$ at $\theta = 15°$. Adopt $C_d = 0.9$, $C_m = 1.8$, $C_s = 1.0$. Find the design berthing energy.**

**Step-by-step solution:**

**Step 5.1:** Find normal velocity component.

$$V_n = V \sin\theta = 0.20 \times \sin 15°$$

$$\sin 15° = 0.2588$$

$$V_n = 0.20 \times 0.2588 = 0.0518 \, m/s$$

**Step 5.2:** Apply the design energy equation.

$$E = \frac{W}{2g} V_n^2 C_d C_m C_s$$

**Step 5.3:** Substitute the numerical values.

$$E = \frac{50{,}000}{2 \times 9.81} \times (0.0518)^2 \times 0.9 \times 1.8 \times 1.0$$

**Step 5.4:** Evaluate $(0.0518)^2$.

$$(0.0518)^2 = 0.002683 \, m^2/s^2$$

**Step 5.5:** Evaluate the product of coefficients.

$$0.9 \times 1.8 \times 1.0 = 1.62$$

**Step 5.6:** Combine all terms.

$$E = \frac{50{,}000}{19.62} \times 0.002683 \times 1.62$$

$$E = 2548.4 \times 0.002683 \times 1.62$$

$$E = 2548.4 \times 0.004347 = 11.08 \, kJ$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 11.1 \, kJ}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The breasting dolphin fender must be capable of absorbing **at least 11.1 kJ** of energy per berthing event. The engineer now selects a rubber fender whose energy-rating curve gives a reaction force $R_f$ at a deflection $\delta$ such that the area under the curve equals 11.1 kJ.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II: Converse-Labarre Group Efficiency of Piles

When piles are driven in a cluster (as in a dolphin), the **group capacity** is less than the sum of individual capacities because the stress bulbs overlap.

### Step 1 — State the assumption

For a rectangular pile group of $m$ rows and $n$ columns with pile spacing $s$ and pile diameter $d$, the **Converse-Labarre** formula gives the group efficiency $\eta$:

$$\eta = 1 - \theta \left[ \frac{(n-1)m + (m-1)n}{90 \, m \, n} \right]$$

where $\theta = \arctan\left(\dfrac{d}{s}\right)$ in degrees.

### Step 2 — Derive for the most common dolphin configuration (2 × 3 group)

For a dolphin using a cluster of $m = 2$ rows and $n = 3$ columns of piles, with $d = 0.6 \, m$ and $s = 1.5 \, m$ (i.e., 2.5D spacing).

**Step 2.1:** Compute $\theta$.

$$\theta = \arctan\left(\frac{0.6}{1.5}\right) = \arctan(0.4) = 21.80°$$

**Step 2.2:** Compute the bracket term.

$$\frac{(n-1)m + (m-1)n}{90 m n} = \frac{(3-1)\cdot 2 + (2-1)\cdot 3}{90 \cdot 2 \cdot 3} = \frac{4 + 3}{540} = \frac{7}{540} = 0.01296$$

**Step 2.3:** Compute $\eta$.

$$\eta = 1 - 21.80 \times 0.01296 = 1 - 0.2825 = 0.7175$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 0.72 \; (i.e., 72\%)}$$

**Step 2.4:** If each pile has a capacity of $Q_p = 1500 \, kN$, the group capacity is:

$$Q_{group} = \eta \times n \times m \times Q_p = 0.72 \times 6 \times 1500 = 6480 \, kN$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** If the group efficiency is less than 1.0, the dolphin must either (a) increase pile spacing, (b) add more piles, or (c) accept the reduced capacity in design. Most codes recommend pile spacing $\geq 3D$ to keep $\eta > 0.85$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III: Lateral Deflection of a Pile Group

For a dolphin subject to a horizontal force $H$ at the pile cap (assumed at height $h$ above the seabed), and assuming the piles act as **fixed at the seabed** and **free at the top** (cantilever model), the lateral deflection at the cap is:

### Step 1 — Cantilever bending theory

Each pile is a cantilever of length $h$. For a horizontal load $H_p$ at the top, the tip deflection is:

$$\delta_{pile} = \frac{H_p h^3}{3 E_p I_p}$$

### Step 2 — Distribute $H$ to all piles

If there are $N$ identical piles sharing the horizontal load equally (this is a simplification — true distribution uses a stiffness matrix):

$$H_p = \frac{H}{N}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute

$$\delta = \frac{H h^3}{3 E_p I_p N}$$

### Step 4 — Numerical example

A dolphin has $N = 6$ piles, $h = 6 \, m$, $E_p = 25 \times 10^6 \, kN/m^2$, $I_p = 0.005 \, m^4$ (for 600 mm dia pile), and $H = 200 \, kN$.

**Step 4.1:** Load per pile.

$$H_p = \frac{200}{6} = 33.33 \, kN$$

**Step 4.2:** Deflection per pile.

$$\delta = \frac{33.33 \times 6^3}{3 \times 25 \times 10^6 \times 0.005}$$

$$\delta = \frac{33.33 \times 216}{375{,}000} = \frac{7199.3}{375{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{\delta = 0.0192 \, m = 19.2 \, mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Acceptability check:** Most codes permit a maximum lateral deflection of **25 to 50 mm** at the pile cap of a dolphin. The above design is comfortably within limits.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Dolphin System

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional flow** of a typical berthing dolphin — from external environmental input through structural response to the final load transfer into the foundation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vessel Approaching Berth] --> B{Velocity and Angle of Approach}
    B --> C[Normal Velocity Component Vn]
    C --> D[Kinetic Energy E = 0.5 m Vn^2]
    D --> E[Berthing Impact Force via FENDER System]
    E --> F[Reaction Force Rf acts on PILE CAP]
    F --> G[Load Distribution: Vertical and Batter Piles]
    G --> H[Axial Compression in Batter Piles]
    G --> I[Bending plus Axial in Vertical Piles]
    H --> J[Load Transfer to Seabed Soil]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Bearing Capacity of Soil Strata]
    J --> L[Skin Friction along Pile Shaft]
    A2[Wind and Current on Hull] --> M[Mooring Hawser Tension T]
    M --> N[Reaction at BOLLARD on Mooring Dolphin]
    N --> O[Horizontal Pull on Pile Cap]
    O --> G
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read this diagram for the exam:** The two parallel pipelines — *Berthing Impact* (top branch) and *Mooring Pull* (bottom branch) — **converge** at the pile cap and are then transferred through the same pile group into the soil. This explains why a single dolphin cluster must be designed for the **vector sum** of these forces.

## 4.2 Layout of a Typical Berthing Facility (Dolphin + Wharf)

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **spatial arrangement** of multiple dolphins along a berth.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BERTH[Berthing Facility Layout]
        direction LR
        W1[Main Wharf or Quay Wall] --- CD1[Catwalk Bridge 1]
        CD1 --- BD1[BREASTING DOLPHIN 1]
        BD1 --- CD2[Catwalk Bridge 2]
        CD2 --- MD1[MOORING DOLPHIN 1 Bow]
        W1 --- CD3[Catwalk Bridge 3]
        CD3 --- MD2[MOORING DOLPHIN 2 Stern]
    end
    VES[Vessel Hull Span L Between MD1 and MD2] -.Parallel to.-> BERTH
```

**Reading guide:**

- The vessel is tied between the two **mooring dolphins** (MD1 at bow, MD2 at stern).
- The **breasting dolphin** (BD1) takes the impact and prevents the vessel from drifting toward the wharf.
- All three are connected to the main wharf by **catwalks** for personnel access.

## 4.3 Internal Pile Arrangement of a Pile Cluster Dolphin

The following block diagram represents a top-down view of a typical pile cluster (plan view).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pile Cap RCC Slab] --> P1[Vertical Pile Front Row]
    A --> P2[Vertical Pile Rear Row]
    A --> P3[Batter Pile 1 Forward Rake]
    A --> P4[Batter Pile 2 Forward Rake]
    A --> P5[Batter Pile 3 Aft Rake]
    A --> P6[Batter Pile 4 Aft Rake]
    P1 --> SOIL[Seabed Soil]
    P2 --> SOIL
    P3 --> SOIL
    P4 --> SOIL
    P5 --> SOIL
    P6 --> SOIL
    F1[Rubber Fender Unit] -.Mounted on.-> A
    B1[Mooring Bollard] -.Mounted on.-> A
```

**Engineering logic of the pile pattern:**

- **Vertical piles** at the front and rear carry vertical load.
- **Forward-raked batter piles** resist the **breasting impact** (vessel pushing in).
- **Aft-raked batter piles** resist the **mooring pull** (vessel pulling away).
- The result is a **balanced force couple** resisting all horizontal actions.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

### Q1. Define a Dolphin in port engineering. List any two functions of a dolphin.

**Model Answer:**

A dolphin is an isolated marine structure consisting of a cluster of piles (or a caisson), driven into the seabed and projecting above the water level, used for berthing or mooring of vessels independently of the main wharf.

**Two functions:**

1. **Mooring function** — Mooring dolphins at the bow and stern hold the vessel against wind/current drift.
2. **Breasting function** — Breasting dolphins absorb the lateral berthing impact of the vessel and prevent it from touching the main wharf.

*(Examiner expects 3 distinct points: Definition + 2 functions = 3 marks)*

---

### Q2. Differentiate between a mooring dolphin and a breasting dolphin.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Mooring Dolphin | Breasting Dolphin |
|-----------|-----------------|-------------------|
| Primary function | Holds vessel against horizontal pull (wind/current) | Resists berthing impact |
| Direction of load | **Along** the berth (longitudinal) | **Normal** to the berth face (lateral) |
| Energy to be absorbed | Static (no impact) | Dynamic (kinetic energy of vessel) |
| Typical equipment | Bollards, quick-release hooks | Fenders (rubber/pneumatic) |
| Pile orientation | Batter piles raked **toward** the wharf | Batter piles raked **away** from the wharf |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Style]**
> *(Module Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE of the following two questions)*

---

### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Q.A.(a) Describe with a neat sketch the different types of dolphins used in port structures. Enumerate the forces acting on a dolphin during the berthing of a vessel.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
A dolphin is an independent berthing/mooring structure extending from the seabed to above the water level. It is used in berths where a continuous wharf is uneconomical or where the water depth beyond the apron is sufficient for large vessels.

**Types of Dolphins (3 marks):**

1. **Pile Cluster Dolphin** — Most widely used; comprises vertical and batter piles with an RCC pile cap. Suitable for medium depths (10–20 m).
2. **Sheet Pile Cellular Dolphin** — Built by driving steel sheet piles in a cellular pattern and filling with earth. Used where bedrock is shallow.
3. **Caisson Dolphin** — A large hollow concrete/steel cylinder floated in, sunk, and filled. Used in deep water.
4. **Floating Dolphin** — A pontoon restrained by chains; used in very deep water where piled dolphins are uneconomical.

**Forces Acting During Berthing (3 marks):**

| Force | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| Berthing impact | Lateral kinetic energy of the vessel at the moment of contact |
| Mooring hawser pull | Longitudinal force as ropes tighten |
| Wave force | Cyclic horizontal force from incident waves |
| Current force | Drag on submerged pile shaft |
| Wind force | Pressure on exposed pile cap and vessel hull |
| Self-weight + live load | Vertical forces on pile cap |

---

**Q.A.(b) A vessel of 30,000 DWT displacement approaches a breasting dolphin at a velocity of 0.15 m/s at an angle of 20° to the berth face. Calculate the design berthing energy using $C_d = 0.8$, $C_m = 2.0$, and $C_s = 1.0$.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the given data:**

$$W = 30{,}000 \, t, \quad V = 0.15 \, m/s, \quad \theta = 20°$$
$$C_d = 0.8, \quad C_m = 2.0, \quad C_s = 1.0$$

**Step 2 — Compute the normal component of approach velocity:**

$$V_n = V \sin\theta = 0.15 \times \sin 20° = 0.15 \times 0.3420 = 0.0513 \, m/s$$

**Step 3 — Apply the design berthing energy formula:**

$$E = \frac{W}{2g} \cdot V_n^2 \cdot C_d \cdot C_m \cdot C_s$$

**Step 4 — Substitute values:**

$$E = \frac{30{,}000}{2 \times 9.81} \times (0.0513)^2 \times 0.8 \times 2.0 \times 1.0$$

**Step 5 — Compute each term:**

- $\dfrac{30{,}000}{19.62} = 1529.05$
- $(0.0513)^2 = 0.002632$
- $0.8 \times 2.0 \times 1.0 = 1.6$

**Step 6 — Final multiplication:**

$$E = 1529.05 \times 0.002632 \times 1.6$$
$$E = 1529.05 \times 0.004211 = 6.44 \, kJ$$

$$\boxed{E_{design} = 6.44 \, kJ}$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The selected fender for the breasting dolphin must have an energy-rating of at least **6.44 kJ** at its design deflection.

---

### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**Q.B.(a) Explain the various components of a pile cluster dolphin with the help of a labelled diagram. Discuss the role of batter piles.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Components of a Pile Cluster Dolphin (4 marks):**

1. **Pile Cap (RCC deck slab):** Distributes concentrated bollard/fender loads to the pile group.
2. **Vertical Piles:** Carry vertical self-weight + live load; minor horizontal share.
3. **Batter (Raker) Piles:** Resist the major share of horizontal load through axial action.
4. **Fenders:** Rubber or pneumatic units that absorb the berthing kinetic energy.
5. **Bollards (Quick-release hooks):** Steel castings for tying hawsers.
6. **Catwalk:** Pedestrian access bridge to the main wharf.
7. **Ladder + Handrail + Wearing Surface:** Safety and operational features.

**Role of Batter Piles (3 marks):**

- A vertical pile resists horizontal force only by **bending** — inefficient in soft marine clay.
- A batter pile (typical slope 1:3 to 1:5) resists the same horizontal force through **axial compression/tension** along its length — far more efficient.
- The horizontal component $H$ of the vessel load is resolved along the pile axis as:

$$F_{axial} = \frac{H}{\cos\alpha}$$

where $\alpha$ is the angle of the pile from vertical.

- This axial force is easily transferred to the soil through skin friction and end bearing.
- Therefore, **batter piles are the primary lateral-load-resisting elements** in a dolphin.

---

**Q.B.(b) A dolphin has a 2 × 3 pile group. Each pile is 600 mm in diameter, 1.5 m centre-to-centre spacing. If the ultimate capacity of a single pile is 1800 kN, compute the group capacity using the Converse-Labarre formula.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Given data:**

$$m = 2, \quad n = 3, \quad d = 0.6 \, m, \quad s = 1.5 \, m, \quad Q_p = 1800 \, kN$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\theta$ (in degrees):**

$$\theta = \arctan\left(\frac{d}{s}\right) = \arctan\left(\frac{0.6}{1.5}\right) = \arctan(0.4) = 21.80°$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Converse-Labarre formula:**

$$\eta = 1 - \theta \cdot \left[\frac{(n-1)m + (m-1)n}{90 \, m \, n}\right]$$

**Step 4 — Compute the bracket term:**

$$\frac{(3-1)\cdot 2 + (2-1)\cdot 3}{90 \cdot 2 \cdot 3} = \frac{(2)(2) + (1)(3)}{540} = \frac{4+3}{540} = \frac{7}{540} = 0.01296$$

**Step 5 — Compute $\eta$:**

$$\eta = 1 - 21.80 \times 0.01296 = 1 - 0.2825 = 0.7175$$

**Step 6 — Compute group capacity:**

$$Q_{group} = \eta \times m \times n \times Q_p$$

$$Q_{group} = 0.7175 \times 2 \times 3 \times 1800$$

$$Q_{group} = 0.7175 \times 6 \times 1800 = 0.7175 \times 10{,}800$$

$$\boxed{Q_{group} \approx 7749 \, kN \; (\approx 7750 \, kN)}$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The dolphin can safely transmit a maximum axial load of **7750 kN** to the soil. To increase this, the designer can increase pile spacing to $s \geq 3D = 1.8 \, m$ (which would raise $\eta$ to about 0.88).

---

## 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost 2–4 Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to use the NORMAL component** of velocity — students often plug $V$ directly into the kinetic energy formula instead of $V \sin\theta$. This overestimates energy by a factor of 10× or more.
> 2. **Mixing up the units of $W$** — if $W$ is in tonnes, you **must** divide by $g$ to get mass in kg. The formula is $E = \frac{W}{2g} V_n^2$, not $E = \frac{W}{2} V_n^2$.
