**Step 2: Width of dock**

$$
W_{dock} = 25 + 2 \times 2 = 29 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3: Depth of dock**

$$
D_{dock} = 10.5 + 0.6 + 1.8 + 0.9 = 13.8 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4: Sill depth below HWL**

$$
S_{depth} = 10.5 + 0.6 = 11.1 \text{ m below HWL}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students commonly confuse **"sill depth below HWL"** with **"dock depth"**. Remember: **Sill depth is set by the vessel's loaded draft + keel block height**, while **dock depth includes tidal variation and freeboard**. Both are different quantities. Also, ensure you **state the units** (m) at every step — losing the unit costs 0.5 marks per step.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Model Question]

**(a)** Classify **fixed and floating navigation aids** with neat diagrams. Explain the working of a **lighthouse and a leading light system**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A dock gate retains seawater to a depth of 8 m. The gate width is 20 m. Calculate the **total hydrostatic thrust** acting on the gate and the **depth of the center of pressure** from the water surface. Take $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Classification and Working (7 marks):**

**Classification of Navigation Aids:**

**Fixed Aids:** Lighthouses, beacons, leading lights, light towers, daymarks.
**Floating Aids:** Light buoys, sound buoys, lightships, LANBY, spar buoys.

**Working of a Lighthouse:**
A lighthouse is a tall tower (height 20 m to 60 m) erected at a strategic coastal location. It carries a powerful light source (originally oil lamps, now electric/LED) at its summit, focused by **Fresnel lenses** (rotating or fixed) into a horizontal beam visible up to 15–30 nautical miles. The light is characterized by a unique **rhythm** (e.g., flash every 5 seconds) for identification. Modern lighthouses are equipped with **RACON** transponders that reply to ship radars with a coded Morse signal, enabling radar identification.

**Working of Leading Lights:**
Leading lights are two fixed lights — a **front light** (lower) and a **rear light** (higher) — placed along the centerline of a navigation channel, separated by a known distance. A vessel navigating the channel must keep both lights in **vertical alignment** (one directly above the other). If the vessel deviates, the lights appear laterally displaced, prompting corrective steering. Leading lights are a simple, low-cost, and highly reliable method for channel navigation.

**Part (b) — Hydrostatic Thrust Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Write the hydrostatic thrust formula**

$$
F = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot W \cdot d^{2}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute values**

$$
F = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 20 \times (8)^{2}
$$

**Step 3: Compute step by step**

$$
F = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 20 \times 64
$$

$$
F = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 1280
$$

$$
F = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 12{,}556.8
$$

$$
F = 0.5 \times 12{,}870{,}720
$$

$$
F = 6{,}435{,}360 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F \approx 6435.36 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4: Depth of center of pressure from water surface**

$$
h^{*}_{cp} = \dfrac{2d}{3} = \dfrac{2 \times 8}{3} = 5.33 \text{ m below water surface}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students use $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (freshwater) instead of **1025 kg/m³** (seawater) in coastal and dock problems, leading to a 2.5% error. Always check whether the problem specifies **seawater or freshwater** and use the corresponding density. Also, the **center of pressure** is at $\dfrac{2d}{3}$ from the top (or $\dfrac{d}{3}$ from the bottom) — not at the centroid $d/2$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Navigation aids** are split into **fixed** (lighthouses, beacons) and **floating** (buoys, lightships). India follows the **IALA Region A** buoyage system.
- A **wet dock** retains water at near HWL using **dock gates** on a **sill**, providing a stable berthing environment independent of tides.
- **Dock length** $L = L_{ship} + 2C_L$ (clearance 3–5 m on each side).
- **Dock width** $W = B_{ship} + 2C_W$ (clearance 1.5–3 m on each side).
- **Dock depth** $D = d_{loaded} + h_{keel} + T_{tide} + F_{freeboard}$.
- **Sill depth below HWL** $S = d_{loaded} + h_{keel}$.
- **Hydrostatic thrust** on a vertical gate: $F = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho g W d^2$.
- **Center of pressure** on a vertical gate: $\dfrac{2d}{3}$ below water surface.
- **Mitre gates** are the most common dock gate type; they transfer thrust to dock wall abutments.
- **Seawater density** is **1025 kg/m³** — always use this in port and dock problems.
- **Lighthouses** have a range of **15–30 nautical miles**; leading lights work in pairs (front + rear).
- For Part B numericals, always show: **(1) Formula, (2) Substitution, (3) Step-by-step arithmetic, (4) Final answer with units.**
- **Common pitfall:** Confusing dock depth with sill depth — they are different quantities.
- **Squat and trim** of moving vessels require additional depth clearance of **0.3 m to 0.5 m**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET754 - RAILWAY, PORT AND HARBOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Classification - Different Types - Design Considerations - Operation of Lock Gates and Passage - Dry Dock - Graving Dry Dock - Design Aspects - Floating Dry Dock - Design Aspects - Port Facilities - Port Building Facilities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Lock Gates, Dry Docks & Port Facilities

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Lock** is a hydraulic engineering structure comprising a water-tight chamber equipped with **Lock Gates** at both ends, used to transfer vessels between two water bodies (or stretches of the same waterway) having differing water levels. The gates are massive, engineered, movable barriers that resist the hydrostatic pressure head of the impounded water.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lock Gate (KTU Definition):** A pair of structural barriers (usually hinged leaves) installed at the entrance of a navigation lock or dry dock, designed to withstand hydrostatic load, ship impact, and self-weight while permitting controlled entry/exit of vessels.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🧠 Think of a Lock as a "Water Elevator" for Ships!**
> Imagine a staircase water tank that a boat must climb. When a boat enters from a low-level river, the upstream gate (like a door) closes behind it. Water is then poured INTO the chamber until the level matches the upstream canal/river level. The upstream gate opens, and the boat sails out at the higher level. The reverse happens when descending. The **lock gates** act as the "water-tight doors" of this elevator, sealing the chamber at each end.

### Why This Topic Matters for KTU Examinations

| Real-World Application | Engineering Relevance |
|---|---|
| **Panama Canal Locks** | One of the largest lock systems in the world (Gatun, Pedro Miguel, Miraflores) |
| **Kochi Port (Cochin), Kerala** | India's natural harbor; uses **dry docks for ship repair** |
| **Inland Waterways (NW-3, Kerala)** | Uses locks for navigation through the West Coast Canal |
| **Cochin Shipyard Limited (CSL)** | Operates one of India's largest **graving dry docks** |

### Standard Physical Constants & Design Metrics

- **Density of seawater:** **ρ = 1025 kg/m³**
- **Density of freshwater:** **ρ = 1000 kg/m³**
- **Acceleration due to gravity:** **g = 9.81 m/s²**
- **Standard dock floor live load (ship blocks):** **40–80 kN/m²**
- **Standard ship impact load (berthing):** **up to 2000 kN** (small vessels) to **10000+ kN** (VLCCs)

---

## 1.2 Classification of Locks

Locks are classified based on **number of chambers**, **alignment**, and **operational mode**:

| Classification Basis | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **By Number of Chambers** | **Single Lock (Single Lift)** | One chamber; vessel lifted/dropped in a single operation |
| | **Flight of Locks (Staircase)** | Multiple consecutive locks (e.g., Panama Canal has flights) |
| | **Lateral Locks (Side-by-side)** | Two parallel chambers to handle traffic in both directions simultaneously |
| **By Alignment** | **Straight Lock** | Chamber axis aligned with waterway |
| | **Bent Lock** | Chamber axis offset (saves space but reduces usable length) |
| **By Operational Mode** | **Mitre Lock** | Gates mitred at 18°–22° when closed |
| | **Swing Bridge Lock** | Gates swing horizontally on vertical pivots |
| | **Sliding (Caisson) Lock** | Gates slide horizontally or are floated into position |

---

## 1.3 Classification of Dry Docks

A **Dry Dock** is a structure used for the **construction, inspection, repair, and maintenance of ships** by removing water to expose the underwater hull.

```
DRY DOCKS
   ├── GRAVING DRY DOCK
   │     ├── Dry Dock (Conventional)
   │     ├── Wet Dock
   │     └── Slipway (Marine Railway)
   ├── FLOATING DRY DOCK
   │     ├── Self-Docking Floating Dock
   │     ├── Sectional Floating Dock
   │     └── Pontoon + Side Walls
   └── HYDROSTATIC DRY DOCK (Modern Hybrid)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** The difference between a *Graving Dry Dock* and a *Floating Dry Dock* is the **location of the ship relative to the water body** — Graving Docks are built on **land** (fixed structure) while Floating Docks **float on water** and can be moved.

---

## 1.4 Port Facilities vs. Port Buildings — The Distinction

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024):** *Port Facilities* refer to the **engineering infrastructure** (wharves, quays, jetties, piers, breakwaters, moorings), while *Port Buildings* are the **architectural/functional superstructures** (transit sheds, warehouses, customs houses, administrative offices, passenger terminals).

Analogy: **Port Facilities = the skeleton; Port Buildings = the organs.** The skeleton holds ships, the organs process cargo and people.

---

## 1.5 Sub-Topic Roadmap for Module 4

| Section | Sub-Topic | KTU Marks Weightage (Typical) |
|---|---|---|
| 1.1 – 1.4 | Lock Gates — Types, Design, Operation | High (4–6 marks) |
| 1.5 – 1.6 | Dry Docks — Graving & Floating, Design | High (5–7 marks) |
| 1.7 – 1.8 | Port Facilities (Quays, Wharves, Jetties) | Medium (3–4 marks) |
| 1.9 | Port Building Facilities | Medium (3–4 marks) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Lock Gates — Detailed Classification

Lock gates must satisfy **three fundamental design requirements**:
1. **Strength** to resist hydrostatic head
2. **Tightness** to prevent leakage (especially at mitre and heel posts)
3. **Operability** — easy to open/close in **< 60 seconds** typically

### 2.1.1 Types of Lock Gates (KTU-Focused)

| Gate Type | Construction | Best For | Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Mitre Gates** | Two leaves meeting at angle (18°–22°) | Low/medium head (≤ 10 m) | Self-bearing arch action; economical | High head causes large thrust at hinge |
| **Sliding Gates (Caisson)** | Hollow steel box; slides on rails | Wide entrances; shipyards | Acts as bridge when open | Heavy machinery; costly |
| **Flap (Fall) Gates** | Hinged at bottom; rotates down | Low heads; canal locks | Simple structure | Cannot take high head |
| **Sector Gates** | Curved leaf rotating about a pivot | Large locks (sea locks) | Bi-directional thrust; small hinge force | Complex civil works |
| **Rolling Gates** | Cylindrical; rolls on a rack | Wide locks (≤ 30 m) | Supports own weight | Bulky; precision engineering |
| **Lift Gates** | Vertical upward movement | High head differences | Effective sealing | Slow operation; heavy machinery |
| **Tumble Gates** | Hollow; fills with water to rotate | Entrance to graving docks | Saves dock floor space | Limited to one direction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mitre Angle Rule (KTU):** The mitre angle (θ) is given by:
> $$\theta = 2 \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{B}{2L}\right)$$
> where $B$ = gate leaf length (along the wall) and $L$ = chamber width.
> **Standard mitre angle = 18° to 22°** (commonly **20°**).

### 2.1.2 Forces on Lock Gates

The primary load on a lock gate is **hydrostatic pressure**, which increases linearly with depth:

$$P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h \;\; \left[\text{kN/m}^2\right]$$

Total horizontal thrust per unit width on a vertical gate retaining water depth $H$:

$$P_{total} = \frac{1}{2} \rho g H^2 \;\; \left[\text{kN/m}\right]$$

For a **mitre gate**, the horizontal thrust on each leaf at the mitre joint is amplified by the geometry:

$$P_{leaf} = \frac{P_{total}}{2 \sin \theta} \;\; \left[\text{kN/m}\right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **arch action** of mitre gates converts the horizontal water pressure into **compressive forces along the gate length** — this is why the leaves lean against each other at the mitre joint, transferring load to the hinge posts.

---

## 2.2 Operation of Lock Gates and Passage

### 2.2.1 Sequence of Lock Operation (Upstream Passage)

A vessel moving from **low water (downstream)** to **high water (upstream)** follows this **9-step KTU sequence**:

1. **Approach** — Vessel enters the lock chamber at low water level.
2. **Closing Lower Gate** — Lower mitre/sliding gate closes; lock is sealed downstream.
3. **Filling** — Water admitted through **culverts** (filling valves open). Chamber level rises.
4. **Equalization** — Water level in chamber matches upstream level.
5. **Opening Upper Gate** — Upper gate opens.
6. **Exit** — Vessel sails out at upstream level.
7. **Closing Upper Gate** — Upper gate closes.
8. **Emptying** — Valves open; water released through emptying culverts.
9. **Ready for next passage** — Chamber drained; lower gate opens for incoming vessel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Time of Filling (KtU Design Rule):** The filling time $T_f$ for a lock chamber is given by:
> $$T_f = \frac{2A_c \sqrt{H}}{C_d A_v \sqrt{2g}} \;\; \left[\text{seconds}\right]$$
> where $A_c$ = chamber cross-sectional area, $H$ = head difference, $A_v$ = valve area, $C_d$ = discharge coefficient (≈ 0.6–0.8).

### 2.2.2 Methods of Lock Filling & Emptying

| Method | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct (Through Gates)** | Water passes through openings in the gates | Low head, small locks |
| **Wall Culvert System** | Culverts in lock walls; discharge through ports in chamber floor | Modern, high-head locks |
| **Central Culvert with Lateral Ports** | Central culvert feeds side ports along floor | Uniform filling, low turbulence |
| **Bottom Longitudinal Culvert** | Single large culvert in chamber floor | Large locks, complex systems |

> [!WARNING]
> **Turbulence Hazard:** Uneven filling causes a **hydraulic jump** that can jerk moored vessels violently. Wall culvert systems with multiple side ports are preferred to maintain **< 1.8 m/min vertical velocity**.

---

## 2.3 Graving Dry Dock — Design Aspects

### 2.3.1 Components of a Graving Dry Dock

A **Graving Dry Dock** is essentially a rectangular basin with a **floor, side walls, an entrance (mouth), and a gate** (often a caisson or flap gate).

```
        ENTRANCE (MOUTH) — closed by a CAISSON or FLAP GATE
        ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
        │                                         │
        │   ALTITUDE STAGE (working platform)     │
        │                                         │
        │   SIDE STEPS (berthing steps)           │
        │                                         │
        │   DOCK FLOOR (keel blocks)              │
        │                                         │
        │   PUMPING WELL + DRAIN PIPE              │
        └─────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### 2.3.2 Design Considerations for Graving Docks (KTU 2024 — Critical)

| Design Parameter | KTU Specification | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| **Dock Length** | L = L_ship + 2 × 5 m (min) | Working clearance fore & aft |
| **Dock Width (at top)** | B = B_ship + 2 × 5 m | Crane access; staging |
| **Dock Depth (below water)** | D = d_ship + freeboard + block height | Allows ship to sit on keel blocks |
| **Keel Block Load** | 40–80 kN/m² | Concentrated ship weight on small blocks |
| **Side Wall Pressure** | Active earth pressure + surcharge | Retains earth when dock is empty |
| **Floor Uplift** | Sub-soil water pressure | When dock is dry, water tries to lift floor |
| **Crane Load on Altitude Stage** | 20–40 kN/m² | Heavy repair work |

### 2.3.3 Critical Design Equations for Graving Dock

**(a) Floor Uplift Check**

The floor must resist upward water pressure when dock is empty:

$$\sigma_{uplift} = \gamma_w \cdot h_{sub} - \gamma_c \cdot t_{floor}$$

For **safety**, floor thickness $t_{floor}$:

$$t_{floor} \geq \frac{\gamma_w h_{sub}}{\gamma_c} \;\; [\text{m}]$$

where $h_{sub}$ = subsoil water head above floor soffit, $\gamma_c$ = unit weight of concrete (≈ 24 kN/m³).

**(b) Side Wall Design (Cantilever Sheet Pile or Mass Concrete)**

Earth pressure on back of wall (Rankine active):

$$p_a = K_a \cdot \gamma_s \cdot H - 2c\sqrt{K_a}$$

For sandy backfill (c = 0), lateral pressure per metre run:

$$P_a = \frac{1}{2} K_a \cdot \gamma_s \cdot H^2 \;\; [\text{kN/m}]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Stability Check:** Overturning factor of safety ≥ **1.5**; Sliding FOS ≥ **1.4**; Floor must be checked for **uplift + bending + punching shear** simultaneously.

---

## 2.4 Floating Dry Dock — Design Aspects

### 2.4.1 Working Principle

A **Floating Dry Dock** is a **U-shaped (or rectangular pontoon + side walls)** steel structure that **submerges itself** to allow a ship to float in, then **deballasts** (pumps water out of ballast tanks) to lift the ship out of the water.

```
   [Stage 1: SUBMERGED — Ship enters]
   ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ ← water level
   ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░ ← Side wall
   ░░░░░░░ SHIP ░░░░░░░░░░░░░
   ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░
   ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓ PONTOON ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓

   [Stage 2: LIFTED — Ship clear of water]
                                  ← water level
   ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░
   ░░░░░░░ SHIP ░░░░░░░░░░░░░
   ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░
   ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓ PONTOON ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓
```

### 2.4.2 Types of Floating Dry Docks

| Type | Description | KTU Note |
|---|---|---|
| **Single Section (Open-bottomed Pontoon)** | One-piece U-shaped structure | Most common |
| **Sectional (Pontoon + Side Walls)** | Side walls removable from pontoon | Allows **self-docking** (the dock can dock itself) |
| **Self-Docking Floating Dock** | Any one section can be lifted for repair of the rest | Used for the **largest floating docks** |

### 2.4.3 Design Equations for Floating Dry Dock

**(a) Required Lifting Force (Buoyancy Requirement)**

The pontoon must generate sufficient buoyancy to lift the ship:

$$F_{buoyancy} = (W_{ship} + W_{dock}) \cdot SF$$

For a rectangular pontoon of plan area $A_p$ and immersion depth $d$:

$$F_{buoyancy} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot A_p \cdot d \;\; [\text{kN}]$$

**(b) Ballast Water Volume to be Pumped Out**

To lift the ship by height $h_{lift}$, the water displaced must equal the ship's weight:

$$V_{ballast} = \frac{W_{ship}}{\rho_{sw} \cdot g} \;\; [\text{m}^3]$$

**(c) Stability of Floating Dock (Metacentric Height)**

The dock must remain stable during all stages of operation:

$$GM = I/V - BG \;\; [\text{m}]$$

where $I$ = second moment of waterplane area, $V$ = displaced volume, $BG$ = distance between centre of buoyancy and centre of gravity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Stability Rule:** For a floating dry dock, $GM$ must be **≥ 1.5 m** at all stages of operation (submerged + lifted). Negative GM causes **capsizing** — a critical design failure.

**(d) Pumping Time**

$$T_{pump} = \frac{V_{ballast}}{Q_{pump}} \;\; [\text{seconds}]$$

where $Q_{pump}$ = total discharge of ballast pumps (m³/s).

---

## 2.5 Port Facilities

### 2.5.1 Key Engineering Structures (KTU 2024)

| Structure | Definition | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Wharf** | Vertical/steep wall structure along the shore, parallel to ship | Berthing on one side |
| **Quay** | Synonymous with wharf; sometimes denotes solid-fill structure | Berthing + cargo handling |
| **Jetty** | Structure projecting **perpendicular** into water; berths on **both sides** | Multi-vessel berthing |
| **Pier** | Jetty connected to shore at one end (sometimes synonymous with jetty) | Deep water access |
| **Berth** | Designated location where a vessel moors | One ship position |
| **Mooring Dolphin** | Isolated structure with bollards for mooring lines | Independent mooring |
| **Breastting Dolphin** | Dolphin that takes the **breast (lateral) thrust** of the ship | Berthing + mooring |
| **Fender System** | Energy-absorbing material on berth face | Protects ship & structure from impact |
| **Bollard** | Mooring post (cast iron) on quay edge | Secures mooring lines |
| **Capstan** | Vertical rotating drum for hauling ropes | Mooring assistance |

### 2.5.2 Types of Berthing Structures

1. **Solid-fill (Gravity) Wharf** — Large mass of masonry/concrete; resists earth + water pressure.
2. **Open-type (Piled) Wharf** — Platform on piles; minimal fill; suitable for deep water & soft soil.
3. **Suspended Deck Wharf** — Deck hangs from anchor piles; minimizes seabed disturbance.
4. **Cellular Cofferdam Wharf** — Steel sheet pile cells filled with earth; quasi-gravity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Selection Criterion (KTU):** Open-type piled wharves are preferred when the **seabed is soft** (low bearing capacity); solid-fill is used for **firm foundations**.

### 2.5.3 Fender Design — Energy Absorption Equation

The kinetic energy of a berthing ship must be absorbed by the fender:

$$E = \frac{1}{2} M V_n^2 \cdot C_e \cdot C_s \cdot C_c \;\; [\text{kJ}]$$

where $M$ = ship mass (tonnes), $V_n$ = normal berthing velocity (m/s), $C_e$ = eccentricity factor, $C_s$ = softness factor, $C_c$ = configuration factor.

> [!WARNING]
> **V_n is NOT the ship speed!** It is the **normal approach velocity** (perpendicular to berth) — typically **0.10 to 0.25 m/s** for large vessels. Confusing it with the ship's cruising speed is a common KTU error.

---

## 2.6 Port Building Facilities

### 2.6.1 Functional Classification of Port Buildings

| Building Type | Function | Typical KTU Note |
|---|---|---|
| **Transit Shed** | Short-term storage of cargo awaiting clearance | Adjacent to quay |
| **Warehouse** | Long-term storage of cargo | Inland of transit shed |
| **Customs House** | Customs clearance, examination, duty collection | Near port entrance |
| **Passenger Terminal** | Embarkation/disembarkation of passengers | Separate from cargo area |
| **Administrative Office** | Port authority operations | Central port area |
| **Fire Station** | Emergency response | Strategically located |
| **Power Sub-station** | Power distribution to cranes, lighting | Distributed across port |
| **Water Supply Station** | Potable + technical water to ships | At berths |
| **Bunkering Station** | Fuel supply to ships | Designated berths |
| **Repair Workshop** | Maintenance of port equipment | Inland area |
| **Garage & Marshalling Yard** | Truck/rail cargo assembly | Behind warehouses |
| **Quay Shed** | Same as transit shed (UK terminology) | Adjacent to quay |

### 2.6.2 Transit Shed — Design Parameters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Design Rule:** Transit shed area = **1.2 to 1.5 × the area occupied by one ship's cargo**. This accounts for sorting, segregation, and waiting time.

The transit shed must be:
- **Tall** (≥ 6 m clear height for crane handling)
- **Wide** (≥ 30 m to allow truck maneuvering)
- **Located** within **50–100 m of the berth edge** (for efficient transfer)
- Equipped with **fire-fighting systems** and **drainage for oil spills**

### 2.6.3 Layout Principle (KTU)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Port Building Layout (Landward Sequence):**
> **Berth → Apron → Transit Shed → Open Storage → Warehouse → Rail/Road Siding**
> This progressive flow moves cargo from ship to inland transport with **minimum back-tracking**.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Module 4)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print and memorize this table before the exam.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Unit | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$ | $P = \rho g h$ | kN/m² | Lock gate design |
| 2 | Total thrust on vertical gate | $P_t = 0.5 \rho g H^2$ | kN/m | Lock gate sizing |
| 3 | Mitre gate leaf thrust | $P_{leaf} = P_t / (2 \sin\theta)$ | kN/m | Mitre gate design |
| 4 | Mitre angle | $\theta = 2 \tan^{-1}(B/2L)$ | degrees | Lock geometry |
| 5 | Lock filling time | $T_f = 2A_c \sqrt{H} / (C_d A_v \sqrt{2g})$ | s | Operation |
| 6 | Floor uplift thickness | $t = \gamma_w h_{sub} / \gamma_c$ | m | Graving dock floor |
| 7 | Active earth pressure (Rankine) | $p_a = K_a \gamma_s H$ | kN/m² | Side wall |
| 8 | Buoyancy on floating dock | $F_b = \rho_{sw} g A_p d$ | kN | Floating dock |
| 9 | Metacentric height | $GM = I/V - BG$ | m | Stability |
| 10 | Berthing energy (fender) | $E = 0.5 M V_n^2 C_e C_s C_c$ | kJ | Fender design |
| 11 | Ballast pump time | $T = V_{ballast} / Q_{pump}$ | s | Floating dock |
| 12 | Transit shed area | $A = 1.2$ to $1.5 \times$ ship cargo area | m² | Port buildings |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constants (KTU standard values to use in problems):**
> $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³ (seawater); $\rho_{fw} = 1000$ kg/m³; $g = 9.81$ m/s²; $C_d$ (valve) = 0.6–0.8; $K_a$ (sand) = 0.33; $\gamma_c = 24$ kN/m³.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** Module 4 problems typically involve **berthing energy calculation**, **floating dock buoyancy**, or **floor uplift check**. The derivations below are what examiners expect you to reproduce.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hydrostatic Thrust on a Lock Gate

### Problem Setup
A vertical lock gate retains water to a depth of $H$ metres. Find the **total horizontal thrust per metre width** and the **depth at which it acts**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Pressure at depth $h$**

At any depth $h$ below the free water surface, the hydrostatic pressure is:

$$P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h \;\; \left[\text{N/m}^2\right]$$

**Step 2 — Pressure on a strip of gate**

Consider a horizontal strip at depth $h$ with thickness $dh$ and unit width. The force on the strip is:

$$dF = P \cdot dh = \rho g h \cdot dh \;\; \left[\text{N/m}\right]$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from $h = 0$ to $h = H$**

The total thrust per unit width:

$$F = \int_{0}^{H} \rho g h \cdot dh = \rho g \left[\frac{h^2}{2}\right]_0^H = \frac{1}{2} \rho g H^2 \;\; \left[\text{N/m}\right]$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kN/m**

$$F = \frac{1}{2} \rho g H^2 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \left[\text{kN/m}\right]$$

**Step 5 — Location of resultant (centre of pressure)**

The resultant thrust acts at the **centroid of the triangular pressure distribution**, i.e., at a depth:

$$\bar{h} = \frac{2H}{3} \;\; \text{from the free surface}$$

$$\bar{h}_{from\ bottom} = H - \frac{2H}{3} = \frac{H}{3}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The total horizontal thrust on a vertical lock gate is $\dfrac{1}{2} \rho g H^2$ kN/m, and it acts at **H/3 from the bottom** (or 2H/3 from the top).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Floor Uplift in a Graving Dry Dock

### Problem Setup
A graving dry dock floor is at elevation $-7.0$ m below the natural ground water table. The dock floor is made of reinforced concrete (γc = 24 kN/m³). Find the **minimum floor thickness** to prevent uplift when the dock is dry.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the problem**

When the dock is empty (dry for ship repair), the **subsoil water pressure pushes UP** on the floor with intensity:

$$P_{up} = \gamma_w \cdot h_{sub} \;\; \left[\text{kN/m}^2\right]$$

**Step 2 — The concrete floor resists uplift by its self-weight**

For a floor of thickness $t$, the downward self-weight is:

$$P_{down} = \gamma_c \cdot t \;\; \left[\text{kN/m}^2\right]$$

**Step 3 — Equate downward and upward pressures (just-stable condition)**

For safety against uplift:

$$\gamma_c \cdot t \cdot FOS = \gamma_w \cdot h_{sub}$$

Using FOS = 1.0 (just-stable) to find minimum thickness:

$$t = \frac{\gamma_w \cdot h_{sub}}{\gamma_c}$$

**Step 4 — Plug in values**

$$t = \frac{9.81 \times 7.0}{24} = \frac{68.67}{24} = 2.86 \;\; \text{m}$$

**Step 5 — Apply safety factor**

Using FOS = 1.2 (standard for dock floors):

$$t_{design} = 1.2 \times 2.86 = 3.43 \;\; \text{m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Forgetting to convert $\gamma_w$ from kN/m³ (9.81) to a consistent unit, OR using FOS of 1.0 instead of **1.2 to 1.5** as required by IS codes.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Berthing Energy for Fender Design

### Problem Setup
A vessel of displacement $W = 50{,}000$ tonnes berths at a normal velocity $V_n = 0.20$ m/s. Eccentricity factor $C_e = 0.5$, softness factor $C_s = 1.0$, configuration factor $C_c = 0.95$. Find the **kinetic energy to be absorbed by the fender**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Mass of the ship**

$$M = \frac{W}{g} = \frac{50{,}000 \times 1000}{9.81} = 5.097 \times 10^6 \;\; \text{kg}$$

(Here, weight in Newtons: $W = 50{,}000 \times 9.81 = 4.905 \times 10^8$ N; mass in kg = weight/g.)

**Step 2 — Base kinetic energy (no correction factors)**

$$E_0 = \frac{1}{2} M V_n^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 5.097 \times 10^6 \times (0.20)^2 = 1.019 \times 10^5 \;\; \text{J} = 101.9 \;\; \text{kJ}$$

**Step 3 — Apply correction factors**

$$E = E_0 \cdot C_e \cdot C_s \cdot C_c$$

$$E = 101.9 \times 0.5 \times 1.0 \times 0.95 = 48.4 \;\; \text{kJ}$$

**Step 4 — Select a fender**

From the **Trelleborg Super Cell Fender Catalogue**, a **SCN 800 E2.3** fender absorbs **~50 kJ at 50% deflection**. Hence, the SCN 800 is selected.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The fender must absorb **at least 48.4 kJ**; the next standard fender size is selected from the manufacturer's catalogue (always round up for safety).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Floating Dock Pumping Time

### Problem Setup
A floating dry dock must lift a ship of weight $W = 30{,}000$ tonnes out of water. The dock has three ballast pumps, each rated $Q = 0.50$ m³/s. Find the **minimum pumping time**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Total ballast water volume**

$$V_{ballast} = \frac{W_{ship}}{\rho_{sw} \cdot g} = \frac{30{,}000 \times 9.81}{1025 \times 9.81} = \frac{30{,}000}{1025} = 29.27 \;\; \text{m}^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that the $g$ cancels! In tidal/buoyancy problems, **weight (kN) ÷ density (kN/m³) = volume (m³)**. This shortcut saves calculation time in the KTU exam.

**Step 2 — Total pump discharge**

$$Q_{total} = 3 \times 0.50 = 1.50 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Pumping time**

$$T_{pump} = \frac{V_{ballast}}{Q_{total}} = \frac{29.27}{1.50} = 19.51 \;\; \text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Apply efficiency factor**

Real pumps have $\eta \approx 0.85$:

$$T_{actual} = \frac{19.51}{0.85} = 22.96 \;\; \text{s}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Using ship *displacement* (m³) instead of weight (kN) OR forgetting to convert tonnes to kN. **1 tonne weight = 9.81 kN** (NOT 1 kN — that's a force-mass confusion).

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Stability (Metacentric Height) of Floating Dock

### Problem Setup
A floating dry dock has waterplane area $A_w = 2000$ m² and length of waterline $L = 80$ m. The distance between the centre of buoyancy (B) and centre of gravity (G) is $BG = 1.2$ m (B is above G). Find the **metacentric height GM**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Second moment of waterplane area (I)**

For a rectangular waterplane of length $L$ and width $B$:

$$I_T = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12} \;\; \left[\text{m}^4\right]$$

(Since stability is governed by the **narrower** axis, we use $B^3$.)

**Step 2 — Displaced volume V**

For dock at draft $d = 4$ m, length $L = 80$ m, width $B = 25$ m:

$$V = L \times B \times d = 80 \times 25 \times 4 = 8000 \;\; \text{m}^3$$

**Step 3 — BM (distance from B to M)**

$$BM = \frac{I}{V} = \frac{L B^3 / 12}{L B d} = \frac{B^2}{12 d} = \frac{(25)^2}{12 \times 4} = \frac{625}{48} = 13.02 \;\; \text{m}$$

**Step 4 — Metacentric height GM**

$$GM = BM - BG = 13.02 - 1.2 = 11.82 \;\; \text{m}$$

**Step 5 — Check against KTU criterion**

$$GM = 11.82 \;\; \text{m} \geq 1.5 \;\; \text{m} \;\; ✅ \;\;\text{Stable}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The large GM (11.82 m) indicates **very stiff stability**. For human comfort during operation, designers often deliberately add ballast to lower GM to a more **comfortable range of 3–5 m** for floating docks (excessively high GM causes jerky roll).

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Scenario | KTU-Suggested Approach | Key Equation |
|---|---|---|
| **Gate design (load)** | Hydrostatic thrust | $F = 0.5 \rho g H^2$ |
| **Gate design (geometry)** | Mitre angle | $\theta = 2 \tan^{-1}(B/2L)$ |
| **Lock operation (time)** | Filling/emptying time | $T_f = 2A_c \sqrt{H}/(C_d A_v \sqrt{2g})$ |
| **Graving dock (floor)** | Uplift check | $t = \gamma_w h_{sub}/\gamma_c$ |
| **Graving dock (wall)** | Rankine earth pressure | $P_a = 0.5 K_a \gamma_s H^2$ |
| **Floating dock (lift)** | Buoyancy requirement | $F_b = \rho_{sw} g A_p d$ |
| **Floating dock (stability)** | Metacentric height | $GM = I/V - BG$ |
| **Fender (energy)** | Berthing energy | $E = 0.5 M V_n^2 C_e C_s C_c$ |
| **Transit shed (area)** | Empirical ratio | $A = 1.2$–$1.5 \times$ ship cargo area |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define a Mitre Lock Gate. State the typical mitre angle used in practice.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Mitre Lock Gate** consists of **two leaves** hinged at the lock walls and meeting at an angle in the middle (the *mitre joint*) when closed. The horizontal water pressure on each leaf is transferred as **compressive axial force** through the mitre joint, with the leaves behaving as a **three-hinged arch** (hinge at mitre + two wall hinges). This converts the bending moment into axial compression, making it highly efficient.

The **typical mitre angle is 18° to 22°** (commonly 20°) measured between the closed leaf and the lock wall centreline. Beyond 25°, thrust at the mitre joint becomes excessive; below 15°, the leaves interfere during opening.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marking Note:** 1 mark for definition; 1 mark for arch action explanation; 1 mark for angle range.

---

### **Question 2: Differentiate between a Graving Dry Dock and a Floating Dry Dock.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer (3 marks — Tabular Format):**

| Parameter | Graving Dry Dock | Floating Dry Dock |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | Built on land as a permanent basin | Floats on water; can be relocated |
| **Gate Type** | Caisson, flap, or sliding gate | None required (ballast tanks control sinking) |
| **Dewatering** | Pumping water out using pumps | Pumping water out of ballast tanks only |
| **Construction** | Excavation + concrete; long lead time | Built in a shipyard; shorter lead time |
| **Cost** | Higher (civil works) | Lower (steel fabrication) |
| **Mobility** | Fixed | Can be towed to new locations |
| **Self-Docking** | Not possible | Possible (sectional type) |

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, divided into **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): Lock Gates and Passage — Comprehensive Design Problem**

`[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]`

**(a)** With the help of a **neat sketch**, explain the **operation of a navigation lock** for lifting a vessel from a lower water level to a higher water level. List the **types of lock gates** commonly used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **mitre lock gate** retains water to a depth of **6 m**. Each leaf has a length of **5 m** and the lock width is **12 m**. The mitre angle is **20°**. Calculate:
  (i) The **total hydrostatic thrust** on the gate per metre width.
  (ii) The **thrust on each leaf** at the mitre joint.
  (iii) The **depth at which the resultant thrust acts**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

**Operation Sequence (4 marks):**

A vessel ascending from low to high water level follows these steps:

1. **Vessel enters** the lock chamber at low water.
2. **Lower (downstream) gate closes** behind the vessel — lock sealed.
3. **Filling valves open** — water enters via culverts in the lock walls.
4. **Chamber water level rises** gradually until equal to upstream level.
5. **Upper (upstream) gate opens** outward.
6. **Vessel exits** into the upstream channel.
7. **Upper gate closes**; **emptying valves** open; chamber drains.
8. **Lower gate opens** — lock ready for next vessel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sketch should show:** Lock chamber, two mitre gates (one at each end), filling/emptying culverts, valve positions, water levels on both sides. (3 marks)

**Types of Lock Gates (3 marks):**
1. **Mitre Gates** (most common for low-medium heads)
2. **Sliding Gates / Caissons**
3. **Flap (Fall) Gates**
4. **Sector Gates**
5. **Rolling Gates**
6. **Lift Gates**
7. **Tumble Gates**

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $H = 6$ m; $L = 5$ m; $B = 12$ m; $\theta = 20°$; $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³; $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**(i) Total hydrostatic thrust per metre width (3 marks):**

$$F = \frac{1}{2} \rho g H^2$$

$$F = \frac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times (6)^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 36$$

$$F = \frac{1}{2} \times 361{,}989 = 180{,}994.5 \;\; \text{N/m} \approx 181 \;\; \text{kN/m}$$

**(ii) Thrust on each leaf at the mitre joint (2 marks):**

Total thrust on full gate = $181 \times 12 = 2172$ kN (since B = 12 m)

Each leaf carries half the total thrust along its length. The thrust at the mitre joint (horizontal component) is:

$$F_{mitre} = \frac{F_{total}}{2 \sin \theta} = \frac{2172}{2 \times \sin 20°} = \frac{2172}{2 \times 0.3420} = \frac{2172}{0.6840}$$

$$F_{mitre} = 3175.4 \;\; \text{kN}$$

**(iii) Depth of resultant thrust (2 marks):**

The pressure distribution is triangular; its centroid (and hence the resultant) acts at $\dfrac{2H}{3}$ from the free surface:

$$\bar{h} = \frac{2 \times 6}{3} = 4 \;\; \text{m from the free surface}$$

$$\bar{h}_{from\ bottom} = H - 4 = 6 - 4 = 2 \;\; \text{m from the bottom}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A common student error is using $\rho_{sw} = 1000$ kg/m³ (freshwater value) when the problem is for a **sea-lock** or **harbour**. Always check the problem context. The standard exam value is **$\rho = 1025$ kg/m³ for seawater** and **$1000$ kg/m³ for freshwater**. (Lose **1 mark** for wrong density.)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Dry Dock Design — Alternative Choice**

`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **components and design considerations** of a **graving dry dock** with the help of a **sketch**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A floating dry dock of **plan dimensions 80 m × 25 m** has a **pontoon depth of 4 m**. The dock is required to lift a ship of weight **30,000 tonnes**. The **distance between centre of buoyancy and centre of gravity is 1.5 m** (B above G). The waterplane is rectangular. Calculate:
  (i) The **buoyancy generated** when the pontoon is fully submerged.
  (ii) The **ballast water volume** to be pumped out.
  (iii) The **metacentric height GM** (assume $I$ for the narrow axis). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

**Components of a Graving Dry Dock (4 marks):**
1. **Entrance (Mouth)** — opening at one end, closed by a gate
2. **Caisson / Flap Gate** — watertight barrier
3. **Side Walls** — concrete or masonry, resist earth + water pressure
4. **Dock Floor** — RCC designed against uplift and bending
5. **Keel Blocks** — short timber/concrete blocks on which the ship rests
6. **Altitude Stage** — upper working platform on the side walls
7. **Side Steps** — steps along the wall for ship berthing support
8. **Altar Steps** — inner steps inside the dock
9. **Pumping Well & Sump** — at the lowest point
10. **Crane Rails** — on top of side walls for mobile cranes

**Design Considerations (3 marks):**
- **Dimensions:** Length = L_ship + 2(5 m); Width = B_ship + 2(5 m); Depth = ship draft + block height + 1.5 m.
- **Floor Uplift:** Subsoil water pressure; floor thickness ≥ 2.5–4 m for safety.
- **Side Wall:** Designed as cantilever to resist earth pressure + hydrostatic pressure.
- **Loads:** Keel block load 40–80 kN/m²; crane loads 20–40 kN/m².
- **Dewatering System:** Pumps sized to empty the dock in 4–6 hours.
- **Drainage:** Floor slope 1:200 toward the pumping well.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** L = 80 m; B = 25 m; d = 4 m; W_ship = 30,000 tonnes; BG = 1.5 m; $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³.

**(i) Buoyancy when fully submerged (3 marks):**

$$F_b = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot L \cdot B \cdot d$$

$$F_b = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 80 \times 25 \times 4$$

$$F_b = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 8000 = 1025 \times 78{,}480 = 80.44 \times 10^6 \;\; \text{N}$$

$$F_b \approx 80{,}442 \;\; \text{kN} \approx 80.4 \;\; \text{MN}$$

Convert to tonnes weight: $80{,}442 / 9.81 \approx 8200$ tonnes lifting capacity (far more than needed — confirms feasibility).

**(ii) Ballast water volume to be pumped out (2 marks):**

The water volume displaced must equal the weight of the ship (in compatible units):

$$V_{ballast} = \frac{W_{ship}}{\rho_{sw} \cdot g} = \frac{30{,}000 \times 9.81}{1025 \times 9.81} = \frac{30{,}000}{1025} = 29.27 \;\; \text{m}^3$$

**(iii) Metacentric height (2 marks):**

Second moment of waterplane area (narrow axis):

$$I = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \frac{80 \times (25)^3}{12} = \frac{80 \times 15{,}625}{12} = 104{,}166.67 \;\; \text{m}^4$$

Displaced volume: $V = L \times B \times d = 80 \times 25 \times 4 = 8000$ m³

$$BM = \frac{I}{V} = \frac{104{,}166.67}{8000} = 13.02 \;\; \text{m}$$

$$GM = BM - BG = 13.02 - 1.5 = 11.52 \;\; \text{m}$$

Since $GM = 11.52$ m $\gg 1.5$ m → **Stable** (in fact, *very* stable).

---

## 📊 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in Module 4 (Loss of 2–3 marks each):**
> 1. **Confusing freshwater and seawater density** — Always use **1025 kg/m³ for sea**, **1000 kg/m³ for fresh**. Marks deducted: **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting correction factors in berthing energy** — Students often compute $E = 0.5 M V^2$ without $C_e, C_s, C_c$. Loss: **2 marks**.
> 3. **Using ship speed instead of berthing velocity** — $V_n$ is **0.10 to 0.25 m/s**, NOT the cruising speed of the ship.
> 4. **Floor uplift without FOS** — Always apply **FOS = 1.2 to 1.5** on the calculated minimum thickness.
> 5. **Confusing BM with GM** — BM is fixed by hull shape; GM = BM − BG is what the designer controls.
> 6. **Wrong unit conversions** — **1 tonne = 1000 kg = 9.81 kN weight**; do not equate tonnes with kN directly.
> 7. **Skipping sketches** — A neat, labelled diagram carries **2–3 marks** by itself in descriptive parts. **Always draw.**
> 8. **Omitting sequence numbers** in lock operation questions — Examiners expect a **numbered step-by-step flow**.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 5-Minute Revision Bulletins — Memorize Before the Exam!**

### 🔹 Lock Gates
- Lock gate = **movable barrier** at lock entrance; resists hydrostatic pressure.
- **Mitre angle** = 18°–22°; standard 20°.
- **Mitre gate** = two leaves meeting at angle; arch action; ideal for low–medium heads.
- **Sliding gate/caisson** slides on rails; for wide entrances.
- **Sector gate** = curved leaf, bi-directional thrust; for large locks.
- **Flap (fall) gate** hinges at bottom; for low heads.
- **Tumble gate** fills with water to rotate; used at graving docks.
- Hydrostatic thrust on vertical gate: $F = 0.5 \rho g H^2$ acting at $H/3$ from bottom.

### 🔹 Lock Operation
- **Filling time:** $T_f = 2A_c \sqrt{H} / (C_d A_v \sqrt{2g})$.
- **Fill via culverts** in walls (preferred) or directly through gates.
- Always specify **wall culvert system** to avoid turbulence.
- Lock operation sequence is a **9-step process** (master it!).

### 🔹 Graving Dry Dock
- Fixed on land; rectangular basin; closed by caisson or flap gate.
- Length = L_ship + 2(5 m); Width = B_ship + 2(5 m).
- **Floor uplift:** $t = \gamma_w h_{sub} / \gamma_c$; FOS 1.2–1.5.
- **Keel block load:** 40–80 kN/m².
- **Side wall:** Rankine active earth pressure; designed as cantilever.

### 🔹 Floating Dry Dock
- Floats on water; ballasts down, then de-ballasts to lift ship.
- **Buoyancy:** $F_b = \rho_{sw} g A_p d$.
- **Ballast volume:** $V_{ballast} = W_{ship} / (\rho_{sw} g)$.
- **Stability:** $GM = BM - BG \geq 1.5$ m.
- **Self-docking** possible for sectional floating docks.

### 🔹 Port Facilities
- **Wharf/Quay** = parallel to shore; **Jetty/Pier** = perpendicular to shore.
- **Solid-fill wharf** for firm ground; **Open piled wharf** for soft ground.
- **Dolphins:** Mooring (rope) or Breasting (lateral thrust).
- **Fender design:** $E = 0.5 M V_n^2 C_e C_s C_c$.
- $V_n$ (normal velocity) = **0.10 to 0.25 m/s**, NOT ship cruising speed.

### 🔹 Port Buildings
- **Transit shed** = short-term; adjacent to quay; area = 1.2 to 1.5 × ship cargo area.
- **Warehouse** = long-term; further inland.
- **Sequence from sea to land:** Berth → Apron → Transit Shed → Open Storage → Warehouse → Rail/Road.
- **Customs, passenger terminal, bunkering, fire station** are essential buildings.

### 🔹 KTU Quick Values
- $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³; $\rho_{fw} = 1000$ kg/m³; $g = 9.81$ m/s².
- $C_d$ (lock valve) = 0.6–0.8; $K_a$ (sand) = 0.33; $\gamma_c = 24$ kN/m³.
- 1 tonne weight = 9.81 kN; 1 kN = 1000 N.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 4 Notes — PECET754**
> **All the best for your KTU ESE examination!** 🎓

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transit Sheds
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET754 - RAILWAY, PORT AND HARBOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transit Sheds

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Transit Shed** is a single-storey covered waterfront structure constructed alongside or in close proximity to a quay/wharf in a port, specifically designed and engineered for the **temporary storage, sorting, inspection, and transfer of break-bulk and bagged cargo** during the operational interval between its discharge from a vessel and its eventual delivery to the inland transport mode (railway, road, or inland waterway), or vice versa.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECET754 Module 4):**
> Transit Sheds form a critical component of the **Port Terminal Infrastructure** sub-module. They are classified as **operational storage structures** (as opposed to long-term warehouses) and their design directly influences the **berth productivity**, **turnaround time** of ships, and the **overall throughput efficiency** of a port.

Mathematically expressed, a transit shed is the **physical interface node** where the **sea-leg** of the transport chain synchronizes with the **land-leg**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Cargo Hotel"**
> Imagine a busy international airport. When passengers deplane, they don't immediately rush into taxis. They pass through a **terminal building** — a transitional space where they wait, collect baggage, sort documents, and prepare for the next leg of their journey. The **transit shed performs the exact same function for cargo**. Goods arriving by ship are "checked in" to the transit shed, held there briefly (typically **3 to 7 days** for break-bulk cargo), then "checked out" to trucks or trains. The shed is *not* a permanent warehouse; it is a **high-turnover staging facility**.

## 1.3 Primary Functions of a Transit Shed

The transit shed serves **four cardinal engineering functions**:

1. **Physical Protection Function:**
   Shields cargo from **rain, sun, wind, salt spray, and dust** during the loading/unloading cycle. Break-bulk cargo (e.g., tea, rice, coffee, machinery) is highly susceptible to moisture damage.

2. **Security & Custodial Function:**
   Provides **enclosed, supervised, lockable space** to prevent pilferage, theft, and unauthorized handling between the vessel and the consignee.

3. **Operational Function:**
   Acts as a **working platform** for tally clerks, customs officers, cargo inspectors, and stevedores to perform **marking, sorting, grading, and documentation** of goods.

4. **Buffer Storage Function:**
   Decouples the **high-rate ship discharge** (e.g., 500 tonnes/hour) from the comparatively **slower land transport** (e.g., 80 tonnes/hour by road), absorbing the mismatch in throughput rates.

## 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Range / Standard Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Dwell Time (Storage Period) | **3 to 7 days** (break-bulk); **1 to 3 days** (modern sheds) |
| Clear Height (Eaves) | **6.0 m to 8.0 m** (minimum 6 m per IS codes) |
| Floor Area per Tonne (Mixed Cargo) | **1.0 m² to 1.5 m² per tonne** |
| Floor Area per Tonne (Bagged Cargo) | **1.4 m² to 2.0 m² per tonne** |
| Door Spacing (Along Quay) | **20 m to 30 m centre-to-centre** |
| Door Width | **3.5 m to 5.0 m** (truck access); **6.0 m to 8.0 m** (rail access) |
| Peak Factor | **1.2 to 1.5** (accounts for non-uniform arrival of cargo) |
| Floor Load Capacity | **2.0 tonnes/m² to 5.0 tonnes/m²** |
| Life Expectancy | **50 to 75 years** (reinforced concrete) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Philosophy: The "Just-in-Time" Cargo Concept

A transit shed operates on the **principle of minimal dwell time**. The entire engineering design — from the size of doors to the column spacing — is optimized to **maximize the cargo turnover rate (kg/m²/day)**. This is fundamentally different from a warehouse, which is optimized for **maximum storage density (kg/m³)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cognitive Hook:** Think of a transit shed as a **3-lane highway interchange** (high throughput, low dwell) versus a warehouse, which is a **multi-storey parking garage** (high density, long dwell).

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Theoretical Framework

The design of a transit shed follows a **5-step logical chain**:

### Step 1: Demand Estimation
Estimate the **annual cargo tonnage** $T_a$ (in tonnes/year) that the berth is expected to handle. This is derived from trade forecasts and port master plan data.

### Step 2: Cargo Mix Analysis
Categorize cargo by **type** (liquid, dry bulk, break-bulk, containerized) and **stowage factor** (m³/tonne). Transit sheds *primarily* serve **general cargo (break-bulk)**.

### Step 3: Dwell Time Determination
Select an **appropriate average dwell time** $D$ (in days). Forced dwell time arises from:
- Documentation and customs clearance delays
- Working hours of inland transport
- Availability of trucks/wagons
- Market-side demand variability

### Step 4: Peak Factor Application
Multiply the steady-state cargo volume by a **peak factor** $P_f$ to handle the **non-uniform arrival pattern** of ships. Ships arrive in **clusters** (e.g., 3 ships in a week followed by a 2-week gap), creating peaks.

### Step 5: Geometric Sizing
Compute the **required floor area** $A_f$ and select a **structural configuration** (column grid, span, height) that satisfies the area with efficient land use.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **core KTU-board-exam-ready formulas** for transit shed design. Master these thoroughly.

| S.No | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Cargo Dwelled at Any Time | $W = \dfrac{T_a \cdot D}{365} \cdot P_f$ | tonnes | $T_a$ = Annual tonnage, $D$ = Dwell time (days), $P_f$ = Peak factor |
| 2 | Required Floor Area | $A_f = \dfrac{W \cdot s}{H \cdot \rho_c}$ | m² | $s$ = Stowage factor (m³/t), $H$ = Stack height (m), $\rho_c$ = Conversion factor |
| 3 | Simplified Area (by Tonnage) | $A_f = W \cdot a_t$ | m² | $a_t$ = Specific area per tonne (m²/t) — empirical value |
| 4 | Length of Transit Shed | $L = \dfrac{A_f}{b}$ | m | $b$ = Width of shed (typically 25 m to 40 m) |
| 5 | Number of Doors | $N_d = \dfrac{L}{S_d}$ | integer | $S_d$ = Door spacing (20–30 m) |
| 6 | Throughput Capacity | $C_{sh} = \dfrac{A_f \cdot H \cdot \rho_c}{D \cdot s}$ | tonnes/day | Inverse of dwell-time logic |
| 7 | Volume of Shed | $V_{sh} = A_f \cdot H$ | m³ | Used for ventilation/lighting design |
| 8 | Floor Load | $q_f = \rho_c \cdot H$ | tonnes/m² | Drives structural design |
| 9 | Quay-to-Shed Setback | $S_q = 15$ to $30$ m | m | Provides apron for rail/road |
| 10 | Door-to-Berth Ratio | $\dfrac{N_d}{B_{length}}$ | doors/m | Operational accessibility metric |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Notation Alert:** The stowage factor $s$ has units of **m³/tonne** (volume per unit mass). It is **NOT** the density. Density $\rho = 1/s$. Mixing these up is the most common KTU exam error on this topic.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The transit shed concept is foundational to:
- **Berth Capacity Planning:** A transit shed that is too small causes **congestion at the quay** — ships idle at berth (incurring demurrage), trucks queue outside, and port efficiency collapses.
- **Container Terminal Evolution:** With the rise of **containerization post-1960s**, traditional transit sheds have been **partially replaced** by **Container Freight Stations (CFS)** and **open storage yards** with rubber-tyred gantry cranes (RTGs). However, transit sheds remain essential for **non-containerizable general cargo** (machinery, project cargo, steel coils).
- **Multimodal Integration:** Modern transit sheds incorporate **rail siding inside the building** ("through sheds") for direct ship-to-train transfer, eliminating one handling step.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Floor Area of a Transit Shed (From First Principles)

### Statement
Derive an expression for the **required floor area** of a transit shed, given the annual cargo throughput, dwell time, peak factor, stowage factor, and stack height.

### Given Data (Notation)
- $T_a$ = Total annual cargo handled by the berth (tonnes/year)
- $D$ = Average dwell time of cargo in the shed (days)
- $P_f$ = Peak factor (dimensionless, $> 1$)
- $s$ = Stowage factor of the cargo (m³/tonne)
- $H$ = Effective stack height of cargo (m)
- $W$ = Weight of cargo present in the shed at any instant (tonnes)
- $A_f$ = Required floor area of the transit shed (m²)
- $V_c$ = Volume occupied by the cargo (m³)

### Step 1: Compute the Average Mass of Cargo Present Per Day

If $T_a$ tonnes of cargo pass through the berth per year, and on average each tonne spends $D$ days in the shed, then the **average quantity present** at any instant is:

$$
\overline{W} = \dfrac{T_a \cdot D}{365}
$$

**Logic:** Multiplying annual tonnage by dwell time (and dividing by 365 to convert year to days) gives the **tonne-days of storage required per year**. Dividing by 365 converts this to the **average tonnes stored per day**.

### Step 2: Apply the Peak Factor

Cargo does not arrive uniformly. A **peak factor** $P_f$ inflates the average to account for clustering of ship arrivals:

$$
W = \overline{W} \cdot P_f = \dfrac{T_a \cdot D \cdot P_f}{365}
$$

**Logic:** $P_f$ typically ranges from **1.2 to 1.5**. If $P_f = 1.3$, the shed must be sized for **30% more cargo** than the annual average suggests.

### Step 3: Convert Mass to Volume Using Stowage Factor

The **stowage factor** $s$ (m³/tonne) gives the volume occupied by 1 tonne of cargo, including voids in packaging:

$$
V_c = W \cdot s
$$

Substituting the expression for $W$:

$$
V_c = \dfrac{T_a \cdot D \cdot P_f \cdot s}{365}
$$

### Step 4: Compute the Required Floor Area

The cargo is stacked to a height $H$ (the **effective stack height**, accounting for safe stacking, ventilation, and sprinkler clearances). The floor area must accommodate the volume $V_c$ within height $H$:

$$
A_f = \dfrac{V_c}{H}
$$

### Step 5: Final Consolidated Formula

Substituting $V_c$ from Step 3:

$$
A_f = \dfrac{T_a \cdot D \cdot P_f \cdot s}{365 \cdot H}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result (Board-Ready Form):**
> $$\boxed{A_f = \dfrac{T_a \cdot D \cdot P_f \cdot s}{365 \cdot H}}$$
> This is the **master formula** for transit shed sizing in the KTU syllabus. Dimensions: $[m^2] = \dfrac{[\text{tonnes/yr}] \cdot [\text{days}] \cdot [1] \cdot [\text{m}^3/\text{t}]}{[\text{days/yr}] \cdot [\text{m}]}$

### Verification with Typical Values
Let $T_a = 200{,}000$ t/yr, $D = 5$ days, $P_f = 1.3$, $s = 1.5$ m³/t, $H = 4$ m.

$$
A_f = \dfrac{200000 \cdot 5 \cdot 1.3 \cdot 1.5}{365 \cdot 4}
$$

$$
A_f = \dfrac{1{,}950{,}000}{1460} = 1335.6 \; \text{m}^2
$$

This is a **realistic area** for a single berth handling ~200,000 tonnes/year of general cargo.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Throughput Capacity of a Transit Shed

### Statement
Derive the **daily throughput capacity** of an existing transit shed of known area, given its height, cargo stowage factor, and average dwell time.

### Logic
The capacity $C_{sh}$ is the inverse of the area formula. If the shed has area $A_f$, height $H$, and cargo has stowage factor $s$ with dwell time $D$, then:

$$
C_{sh} = \dfrac{A_f \cdot H}{D \cdot s} \quad \text{(tonnes/day, steady state)}
$$

For annual capacity (multiply by 365 and divide by peak factor):

$$
T_{a,\text{max}} = \dfrac{365 \cdot A_f \cdot H}{D \cdot P_f \cdot s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is a **limiting capacity**. The berth can only handle this much cargo *if the transit shed is the bottleneck*. In practice, the **ship's gear, quay cranes, or hinterland transport** may limit throughput before the shed does.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Design Variables vs. Their Effects

| Design Variable | Increase Effect on $A_f$ | Increase Effect on $C_{sh}$ | Engineering Trade-off |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Dwell time $D \uparrow$ | $A_f \uparrow$ proportionally | $C_{sh} \downarrow$ proportionally | Operators want low $D$; consignees want high $D$ |
| Peak factor $P_f \uparrow$ | $A_f \uparrow$ proportionally | $C_{sh} \downarrow$ proportionally | Higher $P_f$ = more resilience, more cost |
| Stack height $H \uparrow$ | $A_f \downarrow$ inversely | $C_{sh} \uparrow$ proportionally | Limited by cargo crushability & sprinkler code |
| Stowage factor $s \uparrow$ | $A_f \uparrow$ proportionally | $C_{sh} \downarrow$ proportionally | Bulky cargo (low density) needs more shed area |
| Specific area $a_t \downarrow$ | $A_f \downarrow$ | $C_{sh} \uparrow$ | Achieved by better stevedoring, not by design alone |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

### Problem
A port berth is designed to handle **300,000 tonnes** of bagged general cargo per year. The average dwell time is **6 days**, the peak factor is **1.4**, the stowage factor of bagged cargo is **1.8 m³/tonne**, and the effective stack height is **3.5 m**. Compute:
(a) The required transit shed floor area.
(b) The length of the shed if the width is fixed at **30 m**.
(c) The number of doors required if door spacing is **25 m centre-to-centre**.

### Solution

**Part (a): Floor Area**

$$
A_f = \dfrac{T_a \cdot D \cdot P_f \cdot s}{365 \cdot H}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
A_f = \dfrac{300000 \cdot 6 \cdot 1.4 \cdot 1.8}{365 \cdot 3.5}
$$

Numerator:

$$
300000 \cdot 6 = 1{,}800{,}000
$$

$$
1{,}800{,}000 \cdot 1.4 = 2{,}520{,}000
$$

$$
2{,}520{,}000 \cdot 1.8 = 4{,}536{,}000
$$

Denominator:

$$
365 \cdot 3.5 = 1277.5
$$

Therefore:

$$
A_f = \dfrac{4536000}{1277.5} = 3550.69 \; \text{m}^2
$$

> **Result:** $A_f \approx \mathbf{3551 \; m^2}$

**Part (b): Length of Shed**

$$
L = \dfrac{A_f}{b} = \dfrac{3550.69}{30} = 118.36 \; \text{m}
$$

> **Result:** $L \approx \mathbf{118.4 \; m}$ (round up to **120 m** for construction)

**Part (c): Number of Doors**

$$
N_d = \dfrac{L}{S_d} = \dfrac{118.36}{25} = 4.73
$$

> **Result:** Round up to **5 doors** (you cannot have a fractional door; rounding down would violate the maximum spacing requirement).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Transit Shed Cargo Flow

The following **Mermaid block diagram** maps the **functional architecture** of cargo movement through a transit shed, including the inputs, processes, decision nodes, and outputs.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Vessel Arrives at Berth] --> B1[Ship-to-Shore Crane Discharge]
    B1 --> C1[Quay Apron Staging Zone]
    C1 --> D1{Document Verification and Customs Check}
    D1 --> D2[Rejected Cargo Route to Inspection Bay]
    D2 --> E1[Returned to Shed after Clearance]
    D1 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[Storage in Transit Shed Bay Zones]
    F1 --> F2[Sorting and Marking Stations]
    F2 --> G1[Inland Transport Loading Dock]
    G1 --> H1{Transport Mode Selection}
    H1 --> H2[Road Truck Dispatch]
    H1 --> H3[Rail Wagon Loading]
    H1 --> H4[Inland Barge Transfer]
    H1 --> H5[Pipeline for Liquid Cargo]
    H2 --> I1[Final Consignee Delivery]
    H3 --> I1
    H4 --> I1
    H5 --> I1
    F1 --> J1[Inventory Management System]
    J1 --> K1[Daily Throughput and Dwell Time Reports]
    K1 --> L1[Port Authority Performance Dashboard]
```

## 4.2 Comparative Block Diagram — Types of Transit Sheds

The following Mermaid block diagram classifies the **four principal types of transit sheds** based on their relationship to the quay and inland transport corridors.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[Transit Shed Classification] --> T2[Type 1 Finger Type Shed]
    T1 --> T3[Type 2 Through Type Shed]
    T1 --> T4[Type 3 Marginal Wharf Shed]
    T1 --> T5[Type 4 Open Sided Shed]
    
    T2 --> T2A[Parallel to Quay]
    T2 --> T2B[Single Side Access]
    T2 --> T2C[Land Side Doors Only]
    
    T3 --> T3A[Spans Quay and Siding]
    T3 --> T3B[Double Side Access]
    T3 --> T3C[Direct Ship to Rail Transfer]
    
    T4 --> T4A[On Piles Over Water]
    T4 --> T4B[Used for Light Draft Berths]
    T4 --> T4C[Cargo Loaded via Gangway]
    
    T5 --> T5A[Roof with Columns No Walls]
    T5 --> T5B[Used for Steel and Forest Products]
    T5 --> T5C[Low Value Weather Tolerant Cargo]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Block Diagram — Transit Shed Component Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Transit Shed Structural System] --> P2[Substructure]
    P1 --> P3[Superstructure]
    P1 --> P4[Envelope]
    P1 --> P5[MEP Systems]
    
    P2 --> P2A[Foundation Piles or Raft]
    P2 --> P2B[Plinth Beam]
    P2 --> P2C[Anti Corrosive Floor Treatment]
    
    P3 --> P3A[Column Grid 6m to 12m]
    P3 --> P3B[Roof Trusses or Portal Frames]
    P3 --> P3C[Crane Beams if Internal Crane Used]
    
    P4 --> P4A[Side Sheeting with Louvers]
    P4 --> P4B[Roof Deck with Waterproofing]
    P4 --> P4C[Rolling Shutters and Hinged Doors]
    
    P5 --> P5A[Fire Sprinkler Network]
    P5 --> P5B[High Bay LED Lighting]
    P5 --> P5C[Mechanical Ventilation]
    P5 --> P5D[CCTV and Access Control]
```

## 4.4 Process Flow — Transit Shed Sizing Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start with Annual Cargo Forecast Ta] --> S2[Determine Cargo Mix and Stowage Factor s]
    S2 --> S3[Estimate Average Dwell Time D]
    S3 --> S4[Apply Peak Factor Pf]
    S4 --> S5[Compute Average Stored Mass W]
    S5 --> S6[Convert Mass to Volume using s]
    S6 --> S7[Apply Stack Height H Constraint]
    S7 --> S8[Calculate Required Floor Area Af]
    S8 --> S9[Select Shed Width b from Site Constraints]
    S9 --> S10[Compute Length L equals Af divided by b]
    S10 --> S11[Determine Number of Doors Nd]
    S11 --> S12[Verify Structural and Code Compliance]
    S12 --> S13[Final Transit Shed Design]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** These functional block diagrams are explicitly substituted for physical drawings (as mandated by the protocol) and represent the **engineering process flow** governing transit shed design under the KTU 2024 syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define a transit shed. State any four of its primary functions.**

**Model Answer:**

A transit shed is a covered, single-storey waterfront structure constructed along the quay in a port for the **temporary storage, handling, and transfer of break-bulk and bagged cargo** between the ship and the inland transport system.

The four primary functions are:
1. **Protection** of cargo from weather (rain, sun, salt, dust).
2. **Security** against pilferage and unauthorized handling.
3. **Operational workspace** for sorting, inspection, and documentation.
4. **Buffer storage** to decouple the high-rate ship discharge from the slower land transport.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write "warehouse" instead of "transit shed" and lose marks. A transit shed is for **short-term (3–7 days) staging**, NOT long-term storage. Always emphasize the **temporary** nature.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**What is meant by "dwell time" of cargo in a transit shed? List the factors that influence it.**

**Model Answer:**

**Dwell time** is the average number of days that cargo remains stored inside the transit shed between the time of its discharge from the vessel and the time of its dispatch via inland transport.

**Factors influencing dwell time:**
- Documentation and customs clearance procedures
- Working hours and availability of inland transport (trucks/rail)
- Labor strikes or holidays
- Consignee's urgency in taking delivery
- Availability of storage space
- Type of cargo (perishable cargo has lower dwell time)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**A port berth is expected to handle 250,000 tonnes of mixed general cargo per annum. The average dwell time is 5 days, the peak factor is 1.3, and the average stowage factor of the cargo is 1.6 m³/tonne. The effective stack height permissible inside the shed is 4.0 m. The shed is to be constructed with a clear width of 30 m between the centre lines of the outermost columns.**

**(a)** Calculate the **required floor area** of the transit shed.
**(b)** Determine the **length** of the transit shed and the **number of doors** required if the centre-to-centre spacing of doors is 25 m.

#### Solution to Part (a) — Floor Area [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $T_a = 250{,}000$ t/yr
- $D = 5$ days
- $P_f = 1.3$
- $s = 1.6$ m³/t
- $H = 4.0$ m

**Step 1:** Average cargo mass stored per day:

$$
\overline{W} = \dfrac{T_a \cdot D}{365} = \dfrac{250000 \cdot 5}{365} = \dfrac{1250000}{365} = 3424.66 \; \text{t}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply peak factor:

$$
W = \overline{W} \cdot P_f = 3424.66 \cdot 1.3 = 4452.05 \; \text{t}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert mass to volume:

$$
V_c = W \cdot s = 4452.05 \cdot 1.6 = 7123.28 \; \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 4:** Compute floor area:

$$
A_f = \dfrac{V_c}{H} = \dfrac{7123.28}{4.0} = 1780.82 \; \text{m}^2
$$

> **Result:** $\boxed{A_f \approx 1781 \; \text{m}^2}$

#### Solution to Part (b) — Length and Number of Doors [7 Marks]

**Step 5:** Compute length:

$$
L = \dfrac{A_f}{b} = \dfrac{1780.82}{30} = 59.36 \; \text{m}
$$

Round up to **60 m** for constructability.

**Step 6:** Compute number of doors:

$$
N_d = \dfrac{L}{S_d} = \dfrac{60}{25} = 2.4
$$

Round up to **3 doors** (you cannot have 2.4 doors; rounding down would leave a 35 m unsupported stretch of wall without access).

> **Result:** $L = \mathbf{60 \; m}$, $N_d = \mathbf{3 \; doors}$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **classification of transit sheds** based on their location and accessibility. State the relative advantages of each type.
**(b)** A transit shed has a floor area of 2500 m², a clear height of 5 m, and handles cargo with an average stowage factor of 1.4 m³/tonne. If the average dwell time is 4 days, calculate the **annual throughput capacity** of the shed. Assume a peak factor of 1.25.

#### Solution to Part (a) — Classification of Transit Sheds [7 Marks]

Transit sheds are classified into the following four types:

**1. Finger Type Transit Shed:**
- Constructed **parallel to the quay** with a single row of doors opening onto the **land side** for road/rail access.
- **Advantages:** Simple structural design; efficient land use; suitable when the quay length exceeds the shed length.
- **Disadvantages:** Cargo must be moved the full length of the shed to exit; single-sided access limits operational flexibility.

**2. Through Type Transit Shed:**
- The shed is constructed to **span across both the quay-side and land-side**, with doors on **both faces**. Often, a **rail siding runs through the interior** of the shed.
- **Advantages:** Direct ship-to-rail transfer without intermediate handling; double-sided access dramatically reduces internal travel distance.
- **Disadvantages:** Higher construction cost; structural complexity due to internal rail track.

**3. Marginal Wharf Type Transit Shed:**
- Constructed on **piles extending over the water** at locations where the foreshore is shallow. Cargo is moved into the shed via a **gangway or lighters**.
- **Advantages:** Allows berthing at shallow sites; saves valuable land.
- **Disadvantages:** Limited floor area; not suitable for heavy cargo.

**4. Open-Sided Transit Shed:**
- A **roof supported on columns** with **no enclosing walls** (or removable side panels).
- **Advantages:** Low construction cost; excellent natural ventilation; suitable for weather-tolerant cargo (steel, timber).
- **Disadvantages:** No protection from rain or theft; cannot store moisture-sensitive cargo.

#### Solution to Part (b) — Annual Throughput Capacity [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $A_f = 2500$ m²
- $H = 5$ m
- $s = 1.4$ m³/t
- $D = 4$ days
- $P_f = 1.25$

**Step 1:** Compute the daily steady-state storage capacity (mass):

$$
W = \dfrac{A_f \cdot H}{s} = \dfrac{2500 \cdot 5}{1.4} = \dfrac{12500}{1.4} = 8928.57 \; \text{t}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the steady-state daily throughput:

$$
C_{day} = \dfrac{W}{D} = \dfrac{8928.57}{4} = 2232.14 \; \text{t/day}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to annual capacity (accounting for peak factor):

$$
T_{a,\text{max}} = \dfrac{365 \cdot C_{day}}{P_f} = \dfrac{365 \cdot 2232.14}{1.25} = \dfrac{814{,}731}{1.25}
$$

$$
T_{a,\text{max}} = 651{,}785 \; \text{t/yr}
$$

> **Result:** $\boxed{T_{a,\text{max}} \approx 6.52 \times 10^5 \; \text{tonnes/year}}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Stowage Factor Direction:** The biggest error in this problem is dividing $A_f \cdot H$ by $s$ correctly vs. multiplying. Remember: **Volume = Mass × Stowage Factor**, so **Mass = Volume / Stowage Factor**.
> 2. **Peak Factor Placement:** Some students apply $P_f$ in the wrong direction. The peak factor *reduces* annual capacity because the shed must reserve space for peak days. So **divide** by $P_f$, do not multiply.
> 3. **Rounding Rule:** Always round UP the number of doors and length, never down. This is the KTU-recommended practice for safety and operational adequacy.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — Master These Bullets:**

- [x] **Definition:** Transit shed = temporary (3–7 day) covered storage on the quayside for break-bulk cargo, NOT a warehouse.
- [x] **Four Functions:** Protection, Security, Operational Workspace, Buffer Storage.
- [x] **Master Formula:** $A_f = \dfrac{T_a \cdot D \cdot P_f \cdot s}{365 \cdot H}$ — memorize verbatim.
- [x] **Dwell Time:** $D = 3$ to $7$ days for break-bulk; lower with modern CFS operations.
- [x] **Peak Factor:** $P_f = 1.2$ to $1.5$; always $> 1$.
- [x] **Stowage Factor:** $s$ is in **m³/tonne**; the inverse of density.
- [x] **Stack Height:** $H = 3$ to $5$ m typical; limited by cargo crushability and fire code.
- [x] **Shed Width:** $b = 25$ to $40$ m typical; constrained by column-free span economics.
- [x] **Door Spacing:** $S_d = 20$ to $30$ m centre-to-centre; always round UP the door count.
- [x] **Four Types:** Finger, Through, Marginal Wharf, Open-Sided — know advantages/disadvantages.
- [x] **Modern Trend:** Containerization has reduced (but not eliminated) transit shed demand; CFS has partially replaced them for containerized cargo.
- [x] **Specific Area Rule of Thumb:** $a_t = 1.0$ to $1.5$ m²/t for mixed general cargo — useful for quick estimation in exams.
- [x] **Conversion Gotcha:** $1$ year $= 365$ days (not 360); using 360 is a common KTU error.
- [x] **Always Include Units** in every step of the solution; KTU examiners deduct marks for unit-less final answers.
- [x] **Annual Capacity:** $T_{a,\text{max}} = \dfrac{365 \cdot A_f \cdot H}{D \cdot P_f \cdot s}$ — the inverse of the area formula.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Warehouses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET754 - RAILWAY, PORT AND HARBOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Warehouses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Warehouses

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **warehouse** is a planned, engineered, and enclosed physical structure used for the **systematic storage, handling, protection, and distribution** of goods, raw materials, semi-finished products, and finished commodities along the supply chain. In the domain of Railway, Port and Harbor Engineering, warehouses act as critical **intermodal buffer nodes** that bridge the gap between bulk transport (ships, trains, trucks) and the final consumer or manufacturing unit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECET754 Module 4):** Warehousing in the transport-engineering context refers to the **strategic placement, geometric design, and operational management** of storage depots that interface with rail sidings, port transit sheds, container freight stations (CFS), and inland container depots (ICD).

The governing authorities for warehouse design and operations in India include:
- **Central Warehousing Corporation (CWC)**
- **State Warehousing Corporations (SWCs)**
- **Food Corporation of India (FCI)** — for foodgrain godowns
- **Directorate General of Foreign Trade (DGFT)** — for bonded warehouses
- **Warehousing Development and Regulatory Authority (WDRA)** — established under the Warehousing (Development and Regulation) Act, 2007

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hotel for Goods"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a warehouse as a **"hotel for goods"** — just as a hotel receives guests (check-in), provides secure rooms with storage (lockers/beds), serves food (cross-docking), and eventually checks guests out (dispatch), a warehouse receives inbound cargo, stores it safely in designated zones, processes orders, and dispatches goods outward.

| Hotel Component | Warehouse Equivalent | Function |
|:----------------|:---------------------|:---------|
| Reception desk | Loading/Unloading Bay | Inbound acceptance |
| Rooms | Storage racks/aisles | Holding inventory |
| Housekeeping | Material handling equipment | Internal movement |
| Billing/Accounts | Inventory management system (IMS) | Tracking and accounting |
| Check-out counter | Dispatch bay | Outbound shipment |

## 1.3 Primary Functions of a Warehouse

The six core engineering functions are:
1. **Storage** — Holding goods in optimum conditions over time
2. **Protection** — Shielding cargo from weather, theft, pests, and damage
3. **Consolidation & Break-bulk** — Combining small consignments or splitting bulk loads
4. **Buffering** — Smoothing demand-supply fluctuations (decoupling)
5. **Value-Added Services** — Labeling, kitting, packaging, assembly
6. **Information Hub** — Real-time data on stock levels, location, and movement

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (Bolded KTU Key Terms)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following metrics are **frequently asked in KTU examinations**:
> - **Floor Space Index (FSI) / Storage Density:** typically **0.5 to 0.7**
> - **Aisle Width:** **3.0 m to 4.5 m** for forklift operations
> - **Ceiling Height:** **6 m to 12 m** (modern automated warehouses up to 30 m)
> - **Loading Bay Height:** **1.2 m to 1.3 m** (matching standard truck bed)
> - **Door Width:** **3.0 m minimum** for forklift passage
> - **Floor Load Capacity:** **50 kN/m² to 100 kN/m²** (heavy-duty industrial)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Warehouses (Hierarchical Logic)

### A. Based on Ownership & Operational Control

| Type | Owner | User | Strategic Purpose |
|:-----|:------|:-----|:------------------|
| **Private Warehouse** | Manufacturer/Trader | Self | Exclusive company use |
| **Public Warehouse** | CWC/SWC/Private operator | Rent-paying multiple clients | Multi-tenancy storage |
| **Bonded Warehouse** | Customs-licensed operator | Importers/Exporters | Deferred duty payment |
| **Co-operative Warehouse** | Farmer/Producer co-ops | Members | Agricultural produce |
| **Government Warehouse** | FCI/CWC/State | Public distribution | Food security & PSU needs |

### B. Based on Commodity Type (Critical for Port & Harbor)

1. **General Cargo Warehouse** — Mixed goods, palletized, boxed
2. **Bulk Warehouse / Silo** — Grains, cement, sugar (cylindrical/rectangular)
3. **Cold Storage Warehouse** — Temperature-controlled (-25°C to +15°C)
4. **Hazardous Goods Warehouse** — Chemicals, fuels (explosion-proof, ATEX-rated)
5. **Container Freight Station (CFS)** — Container stuffing/destuffing
6. **Duty-Free Bonded Warehouse** — Under customs supervision
7. **Automated/ASRS Warehouse** — Robotic retrieval systems

### C. Based on Material Handling Configuration

- **Single-aisle vs. Multi-aisle** layouts
- **Block stacking** (no racks) vs. **Rack-supported** systems
- **Drive-in / Drive-through** racks for high-density LIFO/FIFO
- **Mezzanine-supported** multi-level warehouses

## 2.2 Site Selection Criteria (Stepwise Logic)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Site Selection Matters:** A poorly sited warehouse can increase logistics costs by **20–30%** even if the structure itself is well-designed.

| Step | Parameter | Ideal Condition | Engineering Rationale |
|:-----|:----------|:----------------|:----------------------|
| 1 | Proximity to port/rail terminal | **5–15 km** | Reduces drayage cost |
| 2 | Road connectivity | NH/SH access within **1 km** | Smooth truck movement |
| 3 | Topography | Flat, well-drained | Reduces civil cost |
| 4 | Soil bearing capacity | **\ge 100 kN/m²** | Supports floor loading |
| 5 | Water table | **> 3 m below GL** | Avoids dampness in storage |
| 6 | Power supply | **Reliable 3-phase HT line** | Powers material handling |
| 7 | Firefighting infrastructure | Hydrants within **100 m** | Statutory NFPA compliance |
| 8 | Future expansion | **\ge 50%** adjacent open land | Scalability |

## 2.3 Functional Layout of a Modern Warehouse

A standard warehouse consists of the following engineered zones:
- **Receiving Bay (Inbound Dock)**
- **Inspection / Quality Check Area**
- **Put-away Zone (Staging)**
- **Storage Zone (Bulk / Rack / Bin)**
