- **Order Picking Zone**
- **Packing & Labeling Zone**
- **Dispatch Bay (Outbound Dock)**
- **Utility Zone (Office, Restrooms, Battery Charging)**

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Quantitative Tools)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are examinable. Note the **\vert** symbol used to denote absolute value or modulus (this avoids breaking markdown table syntax).

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|:--|:--------|:------------|:------|
| 1 | $A_t = A_s + A_a + A_o + A_d$ | Total area = Storage + Aisle + Operational + Duct | m² |
| 2 | $A_s = \dfrac{Q \cdot t}{H \cdot \rho \cdot k}$ | Required storage area | m² |
| 3 | $\eta = \dfrac{A_s}{A_t} \times 100\%$ | Space utilization efficiency | % |
| 4 | $T_c = \dfrac{U}{Q \cdot n}$ | Inventory turnover cycle | days |
| 5 | $N_r = \dfrac{Q}{q_r}$ | Number of racks/bins required | integer |
| 6 | $C_{th} = \dfrac{U_{out}}{T_{op}}$ | Warehouse throughput capacity | units/hour |
| 7 | $L_{f} = \dfrac{P \cdot L_r}{A_f}$ | Floor loading requirement | kN/m² |
| 8 | $h_{eff} = h_{cl} - h_{cl} \cdot f_{cl}$ | Effective clear height (deducting clearance) | m |
| 9 | $V_{use} = A_s \cdot h_{eff}$ | Usable storage volume | m³ |
| 10 | $d_{iso} = \dfrac{\lambda_{ins}}{R_{th}}$ | Insulation thickness (cold storage) | m |

### Symbol Glossary (used in the table above):

- $A_t$ = Total warehouse area
- $A_s$ = Net storage area
- $A_a$ = Aisle area
- $A_o$ = Operational area
- $A_d$ = Duct/utility area
- $Q$ = Quantity of goods to be stored
- $t$ = Storage duration
- $H$ = Stack height
- $\rho$ = Storage density (kg/m³ or units/m²)
- $k$ = Space utilization factor (0.6 to 0.8)
- $U$ = Annual usage/dispatch volume
- $n$ = Number of cycles per year
- $q_r$ = Capacity per rack/bin
- $U_{out}$ = Units dispatched
- $T_{op}$ = Operating time
- $P$ = Point load from pallet
- $L_r$ = Load factor
- $A_f$ = Footprint area
- $h_{cl}$ = Clear height
- $f_{cl}$ = Clearance fraction (typically 0.15)
- $\lambda_{ins}$ = Thermal conductivity of insulator
- $R_{th}$ = Thermal resistance required

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Port Container Yards (CY)** are specialized warehouses using **RTG/RMG cranes** with stacking densities of **0.5–0.7 TEU/m²**.
- **Inland Container Depots (ICD)** at Concor (India) facilities use **rail-mounted gantry** systems achieving **25+ moves/hour**.
- **Amazon Fulfillment Centers** use **ASRS + Kiva robots** with slot densities of **>1.5 units/m²**.
- **FCI godowns** are engineered with **grains at 1.4 m height** to maintain moisture < 14%.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Storage Area Calculation (Core KTU Numericals)

**Problem Framework:** A warehouse must store **Q units** of cargo for a duration of **t days**. Each unit has a footprint of $a$ m² and a maximum stackable height of $H$ m. Determine the minimum net storage area required.

### Step-by-Step Deduction:

**Step 1:** The volumetric footprint per unit (allowing stacking) is the area occupied at the base multiplied by the number of stackable layers.

$$
n_{stack} = \dfrac{H}{h_u}
$$

where $h_u$ is the unit height.

**Step 2:** The number of unit columns (lateral footprint) is:

$$
N_{col} = \dfrac{Q}{n_{stack}}
$$

**Step 3:** The total base footprint required (without aisle/operational allowance) is:

$$
A_{s,ideal} = N_{col} \cdot a = \dfrac{Q \cdot a \cdot h_u}{H}
$$

**Step 4:** Introduce the **space utilization factor** $k$ (accounting for packing inefficiencies, gaps, and orientation losses):

$$
A_s = \dfrac{A_{s,ideal}}{k} = \dfrac{Q \cdot a \cdot h_u}{H \cdot k}
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute the volumetric storage density $\rho = \dfrac{1}{a \cdot h_u}$ (units per cubic meter when packed perfectly):

$$
A_s = \dfrac{Q}{H \cdot \rho \cdot k}
$$

This is **Formula #2** in the KTU formula sheet — derived rigorously.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Total Warehouse Area (Including Aisles & Operations)

**Step 1:** Define auxiliary areas:

- $A_a$ = Aisle area = (Length of aisle) × (Width of aisle) × (Number of aisles)
- $A_o$ = Operational area (≈ 15–25% of $A_s$)
- $A_d$ = Duct/utility area (≈ 5–10% of $A_s$)

**Step 2:** Express the total:

$$
A_t = A_s + A_a + A_o + A_d
$$

**Step 3:** Express operational and duct areas as fractions of $A_s$:

$$
A_o = f_o \cdot A_s, \quad A_d = f_d \cdot A_s
$$

where $f_o \in [0.15, 0.25]$ and $f_d \in [0.05, 0.10]$.

**Step 4:** Substituting:

$$
A_t = A_s + A_a + f_o A_s + f_d A_s = A_s(1 + f_o + f_d) + A_a
$$

**Step 5:** The space utilization efficiency becomes:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{A_s}{A_t} = \dfrac{A_s}{A_s(1 + f_o + f_d) + A_a}
$$

**Step 6:** A typical KTU target is $\eta \ge 0.45$ (45%). For an ideal system with $A_a \to 0$, $f_o = 0.20$, $f_d = 0.05$:

$$
\eta_{max} = \dfrac{1}{1.25} = 0.80 \;\; (80\%)
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Inventory Turnover and Reorder Cycle

**Step 1:** The **inventory turnover ratio (ITR)** is:

$$
ITR = \dfrac{COGS}{A_{inv}}
$$

where $COGS$ = Cost of Goods Sold per year, $A_{inv}$ = Average inventory value.

**Step 2:** The **cycle time** $T_c$ (days between consecutive reorder deliveries) is:

$$
T_c = \dfrac{365}{ITR} = \dfrac{365 \cdot A_{inv}}{COGS}
$$

**Step 3:** Equivalently, in terms of units:

$$
T_c = \dfrac{U_{avg}}{U_{out,day}}
$$

where $U_{avg}$ = average units in stock, $U_{out,day}$ = units dispatched per day.

**Step 4:** The reorder point (when stock reaches critical level) is:

$$
ROP = d_{avg} \cdot L_t + S_s
$$

where $d_{avg}$ = average daily demand, $L_t$ = lead time (days), $S_s$ = safety stock.

**Step 5:** Safety stock derivation (using service-level $z$-score):

$$
S_s = z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L_t}
$$

where $\sigma_d$ = standard deviation of daily demand, and $z$ is from the normal distribution (e.g., 1.65 for 95% service level).

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem)

> **Problem:** A port warehouse must store **8,000 tonnes** of bagged cement. Each bag weighs **50 kg**, dimensions **0.6 m × 0.4 m × 0.2 m**. Maximum stack height allowed = **5 m**. Space utilization factor = **0.70**. Operational area = **22%** of storage area; duct area = **8%**; total aisle area = **450 m²**. Determine:
> (a) Net storage area
> (b) Total warehouse floor area
> (c) Space utilization efficiency

### Part (a): Net Storage Area

**Step 1:** Number of bags = $\dfrac{8{,}000{,}000}{50} = 160{,}000$ bags.

**Step 2:** Stackable height per column = $\dfrac{5}{0.2} = 25$ bags/column.

**Step 3:** Number of columns = $\dfrac{160{,}000}{25} = 6{,}400$ columns.

**Step 4:** Footprint per column (top-down view) = $0.6 \times 0.4 = 0.24$ m².

**Step 5:** Ideal storage area = $6{,}400 \times 0.24 = 1{,}536$ m².

**Step 6:** Apply utilization factor $k = 0.70$:

$$
A_s = \dfrac{1{,}536}{0.70} = 2{,}194.29 \;\; \text{m}^2
$$

### Part (b): Total Warehouse Floor Area

**Step 1:** Operational area = $0.22 \times 2{,}194.29 = 482.74$ m².

**Step 2:** Duct area = $0.08 \times 2{,}194.29 = 175.54$ m².

**Step 3:** Aisle area = 450 m² (given).

**Step 4:** Total area:

$$
A_t = 2{,}194.29 + 482.74 + 175.54 + 450 = 3{,}302.57 \;\; \text{m}^2
$$

### Part (c): Space Utilization Efficiency

$$
\eta = \dfrac{A_s}{A_t} = \dfrac{2{,}194.29}{3{,}302.57} \times 100\% = 66.44\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Interpretation:** A utilization of **66%** is **good** for a bulk bagged warehouse (typical range: 55–70%). Lower than 50% indicates over-spacing; above 80% indicates congestion.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Warehouse Type

| Decision Criterion | Public WH | Private WH | Bonded WH | Cold Storage | Automated WH |
|:-------------------|:---------:|:----------:|:---------:|:------------:|:------------:|
| Capital cost (lower is better) | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★ | ★ | ✗ |
| Operational flexibility | ★★★ | ★ | ★★ | ★★ | ★★ |
| Inventory turnover speed | ★★ | ★★★ | ★ | ★ | ★★★ |
| Customs-related suitability | ✗ | ✗ | ★★★ | ✗ | ✗ |
| Temperature-controlled cargo | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ★★★ | ★★ |
| High SKU variety | ★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ★ | ★★★ |
| E-commerce readiness | ★★ | ★ | ✗ | ★ | ★★★ |

(★★★ = Best, ★★ = Average, ★ = Limited, ✗ = Not suitable)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Warehouse Operational Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inbound Truck or Rail Wagon Arrives] --> B[Receiving Bay Unloading]
    B --> C[Quality Inspection and Weighment]
    C --> D{Quality OK?}
    D -- No --> E[Quarantine or Reject Zone]
    D -- Yes --> F[Put-away Staging Area]
    F --> G[Storage Zone Selection]
    G --> G1[Block Stack Bulk Area]
    G --> G2[Pallet Rack Aisle Zone]
    G --> G3[Bin Shelf Zone]
    G --> G4[Climate Controlled Cold Zone]
    G1 --> H[Order Picking Zone]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    H --> I[Packing and Labeling]
    I --> J[Dispatch Bay Loading]
    J --> K[Outbound Truck or Rail Departure]
    K --> L[Inventory Update in WMS]
    E --> L
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each rectangular box represents a **physical process zone** in the warehouse. Diamond boxes are **decision nodes** (e.g., quality check). The flow moves from inbound arrival at the top to outbound dispatch at the bottom, with a feedback loop to the **Warehouse Management System (WMS)**.

---

## 4.2 Warehouse Zonal Layout (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A1[Inbound Zone]
        A11[Receiving Dock]
        A12[Inspection Bay]
        A13[Weighbridge]
    end
    subgraph A2[Storage Zone]
        A21[Bulk Block Stack]
        A22[Pallet Racks]
        A23[Cold Storage]
        A24[Hazardous Cabinet]
    end
    subgraph A3[Operations Zone]
        A31[Order Picking]
        A32[Packing]
        A33[Labeling]
    end
    subgraph A4[Outbound Zone]
        A41[Dispatch Dock]
        A42[Loading Bay]
        A43[Dispatch Office]
    end
    subgraph A5[Utility Zone]
        A51[Admin Office]
        A52[Battery Charging]
        A53[Fire Pump Room]
    end
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> A4
    A5 --- A1
    A5 --- A2
    A5 --- A3
    A5 --- A4
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this layout matters:** The **U-shaped flow** (Inbound → Storage → Operations → Outbound) minimizes cross-traffic, reduces handling time, and aligns with **KTU-emphasized best practices** for port-adjacent warehouses.

---

## 4.3 Warehouse Racking System Comparison (Schematic Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Selective Pallet Rack] --> R1A[FIFO Access per Aisle]
    R2[Drive-In Rack] --> R2A[LIFO High Density]
    R3[Drive-Through Rack] --> R3A[FIFO High Density]
    R4[Push Back Rack] --> R4A[LIFO Medium Density]
    R5[Pallet Flow Rack] --> R5A[FIFO Gravity Fed]
    R6[Cantilever Rack] --> R6A[Long Goods Storage]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Application:** In port warehouses handling palletized import cargo, **Selective Racks (R1)** are most common due to FIFO compliance with customs regulations.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define a bonded warehouse. Mention any two of its key features.*

**Model Answer:**

A **bonded warehouse** is a customs-licensed storage facility where imported goods can be stored **without payment of customs duty** until they are withdrawn for domestic consumption, re-export, or transshipment.

**Key Features (any two):**
1. Operated under customs supervision; duty payment is **deferred** until goods exit for consumption.
2. Goods can be stored for a **stipulated period** (typically up to **3 years**, extendable).
3. Allows **value-added operations** (repacking, relabeling, partial removal) under customs oversight.
4. Strategically located near **ports, airports, or ICDs** to facilitate import-export trade.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*List any six functions of a warehouse in the logistics chain.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Storage** of raw materials, work-in-progress, and finished goods.
2. **Consolidation** of small consignments into bulk shipments.
3. **Break-bulk** of large shipments into smaller customer-specific loads.
4. **Protection** of goods from weather, theft, pests, and damage.
5. **Buffering / Decoupling** — smooths demand-supply mismatches.
6. **Value-added services** — labeling, kitting, packaging, light assembly.
7. **Information hub** — provides real-time inventory data via WMS.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Classify warehouses based on **ownership, commodity type, and material handling** systems. Explain the features of any **four** types in detail. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A grain silo must store **12,000 tonnes** of wheat. Each bag is **75 kg**, bag size **0.9 m × 0.5 m × 0.3 m**, maximum stack height **4.5 m**, space utilization factor **0.65**, operational area = **25%** of storage, duct area = **10%**, aisle area = **600 m²**. Calculate the **total warehouse floor area** and the **space utilization efficiency**. **[7 Marks]**

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Classification

**Based on Ownership:**

1. **Private Warehouse** — Owned and operated by a single company for its exclusive use. High control, no sharing of costs, but capital-intensive.
2. **Public Warehouse** — Operated by CWC/SWC or private parties, rented to multiple users on a tariff basis. Ideal for SMEs.
3. **Bonded Warehouse** — Customs-licensed; allows duty deferment on imported goods.
4. **Government Warehouse** — Owned by state (e.g., FCI for foodgrains).

**Based on Commodity Type:**

5. **General Cargo Warehouse** — Mixed bagged, boxed, palletized goods (most common in ports).
6. **Bulk Warehouse / Silo** — Grains, cement, sugar; often cylindrical.
7. **Cold Storage Warehouse** — Insulated + refrigeration (fruits, pharma, meat).
8. **Hazardous Warehouse** — ATEX-certified, explosion-proof, separate ventilation.

**Based on Material Handling:**

9. **Block Stack** — Floor stacking in rows; lowest cost, lowest density.
10. **Rack-Supported** — Pallet racks, drive-in, cantilever, etc.

**Detailed Features of Four Types (any 4):**

- **General Cargo WH:** Slab-on-grade PCC floor, roller shutter doors, fire sprinklers, FIFO racking, dock levelers.
- **Bonded WH:** Triple-lock system (customs + warehouse + customs broker), CCTV, restricted access, computerised inventory.
- **Cold Storage WH:** PUF-insulated panels, vapor barrier, refrigeration plant (-25°C to +15°C), humidity control, ethylene scrubbers for horticulture.
- **Automated WH (ASRS):** Robotic cranes, conveyors, totes, WMS-controlled, slot density 1.5+ units/m², minimal human intervention.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical

**Step 1:** Number of bags:

$$
Q = \dfrac{12{,}000{,}000}{75} = 160{,}000 \;\; \text{bags}
$$

**Step 2:** Stack height per column:

$$
n_{stack} = \dfrac{4.5}{0.3} = 15 \;\; \text{bags/column}
$$

**Step 3:** Number of columns:

$$
N_{col} = \dfrac{160{,}000}{15} = 10{,}666.67 \;\; \text{columns}
$$

**Step 4:** Footprint per column:

$$
a = 0.9 \times 0.5 = 0.45 \;\; \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 5:** Ideal storage area:

$$
A_{s,ideal} = 10{,}666.67 \times 0.45 = 4{,}800 \;\; \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 6:** Apply $k = 0.65$:

$$
A_s = \dfrac{4{,}800}{0.65} = 7{,}384.62 \;\; \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 7:** Operational area:

$$
A_o = 0.25 \times 7{,}384.62 = 1{,}846.15 \;\; \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 8:** Duct area:

$$
A_d = 0.10 \times 7{,}384.62 = 738.46 \;\; \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 9:** Total warehouse floor area:

$$
A_t = 7{,}384.62 + 1{,}846.15 + 738.46 + 600 = 10{,}569.23 \;\; \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 10:** Space utilization efficiency:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{7{,}384.62}{10{,}569.23} \times 100\% = 69.86\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Interpretation:** A 70% space utilization is **acceptable** for grain warehouses. Note that grain godowns typically have lower rack-supported density but high volumetric density (loose grain in silos achieves 850 kg/m³).

---

### Question B (Option 2 — Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Discuss the **site selection criteria** for a port-adjacent warehouse. Explain how each criterion affects cost and operational efficiency. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A CFS must handle **5,000 TEU** annually. Each TEU stays on average **6 days**. Storage density = **0.65 TEU/m²**. Aisle area = **35%** of storage area. Determine the **storage area** and **total area** of the CFS yard. **[7 Marks]**

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Site Selection Criteria

1. **Proximity to Port/Rail Terminal** — Drayage (local transport) cost drops by ~₹2/km/TEU. A site within **10 km** is optimal.
2. **Road & Rail Connectivity** — Direct NH/SH access; rail siding feasibility is checked (loading gauge, curve radius).
3. **Topography & Drainage** — Flat land (slope < 1%) reduces earthwork; good drainage prevents flooding of stored cargo.
4. **Soil Bearing Capacity (SBC)** — Should be $\ge$ 100 kN/m² for container stacking; weak soils need raft/pile foundations, increasing cost by 20–40%.
5. **Water Table Depth** — Below 3 m prevents capillary rise and dampness in bagged goods.
6. **Power & Water Supply** — Reliable HT line (≥ 11 kV) for cranes, reefer plugs; treated water for firefighting.
7. **Environmental & Statutory Clearances** — Coastal Regulation Zone (CRZ) clearance, pollution board NOC, fire NOC.
8. **Future Expansion Potential** — Adjacent land availability for 50% growth over 10 years.
9. **Labor Availability & Law-and-Order** — Skilled forklift operators, IT staff, security personnel availability.
10. **Distance from Habitation** — Hazardous goods warehouses need ≥ 500 m buffer from residential areas.

**Cost-Efficiency Trade-off:** A site close to port (≤ 5 km) is **30–40% costlier** in land price but saves **15–20%** in transport cost over the project life.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical (CFS Yard)

**Step 1:** Average TEU in storage at any time:

$$
TEU_{avg} = \dfrac{5{,}000 \times 6}{365} = 82.19 \;\; \text{TEU}
$$

**Step 2:** Required storage area:

$$
A_s = \dfrac{TEU_{avg}}{\rho} = \dfrac{82.19}{0.65} = 126.45 \;\; \text{m}^2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Correction:** The above gives a small area because the storage density 0.65 TEU/m² is high. For a peak-factor design (3× the average), use peak TEU:

$$
TEU_{peak} = 82.19 \times 2.5 = 205.48 \;\; \text{TEU}
$$

$$
A_s = \dfrac{205.48}{0.65} = 316.12 \;\; \text{m}^2
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a realistic CFS design, peak TEU is used. KTU problems may either explicitly state "peak" or expect students to apply a peak factor of 2.0–3.0.

**Step 3:** Aisle area = 35% of $A_s$:

$$
A_a = 0.35 \times 316.12 = 110.64 \;\; \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 4:** Operational area (assume 20% of $A_s$):

$$
A_o = 0.20 \times 316.12 = 63.22 \;\; \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 5:** Total CFS yard area:

$$
A_t = 316.12 + 110.64 + 63.22 = 489.98 \;\; \text{m}^2
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - Students often **forget to convert** the time units (annual → daily) in inventory problems.
> - Confusing **storage density** units (TEU/m² vs. m²/TEU) leads to order-of-magnitude errors.
> - **Not applying the peak factor** results in an under-designed (too small) yard, which fails the valuation key's "design adequacy" check.
> - Forgetting to **include duct and operational areas** loses 2 marks easily.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Rapid Revision Checklist — Warehouses**

- ✅ A warehouse is a **storage buffer node** in the transport chain — **not a manufacturing facility**.
- ✅ The **6 core functions** are: Storage, Protection, Consolidation, Break-bulk, Buffering, Information Hub.
- ✅ **Bonded warehouses** = customs-licensed, **duty-deferred** storage (up to 3 years).
- ✅ **CWC** = Central Warehousing Corporation (public sector, multi-client).
- ✅ **FCI** = Food Corporation of India (foodgrain godowns).
- ✅ **ICD** = Inland Container Depot (off-dock container facility).
- ✅ **CFS** = Container Freight Station (customs clearance + stuffing).
- ✅ **ASRS** = Automated Storage & Retrieval System (robotic, high-density).
- ✅ **Space utilization efficiency** target: **45–70%** (higher in automated systems).
- ✅ **Aisle width** for forklift: **3.0–4.5 m**; for reach trucks: **2.7–3.0 m**.
- ✅ **Floor load capacity**: **50–100 kN/m²** (heavy industrial).
- ✅ **Clear height**: **6–12 m** (modern automated up to 30 m).
- ✅ **Space utilization factor** $k$: typically **0.60 to 0.80** (depends on packaging regularity).
- ✅ **Peak factor** for inventory: **2.0 to 3.0** (when average is given).
- ✅ **WMS** = Warehouse Management System (IT backbone of modern warehouses).
- ✅ **Reorder Point** $ROP = d_{avg} \cdot L_t + S_s$.
- ✅ **Safety Stock** $S_s = z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L_t}$ (service-level based).
- ✅ **Bonded warehouse** = under **Section 57 of Customs Act, 1962**.
- ✅ **WDRA** regulates negotiable warehouse receipts (NWR) in India.
- ✅ **FIFO** = First In First Out (perishables, customs goods) → Selective/Pallet Flow racks.
- ✅ **LIFO** = Last In First Out (non-perishable, bulk) → Drive-in/Push-back racks.
- ✅ **NFPA** = National Fire Protection Association (fire safety standards).

> **End of Module 4 — Warehouses Notes (PECET754 | KTU 2024 Scheme)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cargo Handling Facility
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET754 - RAILWAY, PORT AND HARBOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cargo Handling Facility

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cargo Handling Facility** at a port refers to the integrated system of fixed installations, mobile equipment, storage structures, and auxiliary services designed to transfer cargo efficiently and safely between ship, shore transport (road/rail/pipeline), and storage areas. It encompasses the entire chain of reception, identification, weighing, sorting, stacking, loading, and dispatch of various cargo categories — bulk (dry & liquid), break-bulk, unitized (containers, pallets), and neo-bulk.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Spirit):**
> "Cargo handling facility comprises the quay-side, transit shed, warehouse, storage yards, mechanical handling equipment (cranes, conveyors, forklifts, pipelines), and the inter-modal transfer systems that allow movement of goods from the ship's hook to the inland transport mode, ensuring minimum dwell time and maximum throughput."

The facility is governed by:
- **UN/IMO standards** for safety
- **IS 4651 (Part V)** for planning of port facilities
- **BIS codes** for structural and mechanical design

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Think of a port as a **large, multi-lingual hotel lobby** where guests (ships) arrive carrying heavy luggage (cargo) in different shapes and sizes. The lobby must have:
- **Conveyor belts and carts** to move luggage from the entrance door (ship's hatch) to the cloakroom (warehouse),
- **Specialized racks** for different luggage types (silk, liquids, steel, grain),
- **Trained porters with trolleys** (cranes, forklifts) to lift and stack,
- **A clear path** from cloakroom to the car park (trucks/trains) for the next journey.

Just as a poorly designed hotel lobby creates long queues, poor cargo handling creates **port congestion, demurrage charges, and shipment delays**. Hence, every port invests in a tailored **Cargo Handling Facility (CHF)**.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

- **Throughput (T)** — Total cargo moved per year in **million tonnes (MT)** or **TEU** (Twenty-foot Equivalent Unit for containers).
- **Berth Occupancy (BO)** — Should ideally be **40–60%** for efficient working.
- **Dwell Time** — Time cargo remains in port storage; ideally **3–7 days** for general cargo.
- **Loading Rate** — In **tonnes/hour** (e.g., coal: 1000–3000 TPH; oil: 5000–10000 TPH).

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Cargo Categories — "D-B-L-C"**
> **D**ry Bulk (coal, ore, grain)
> **B**reak-Bulk (machinery, bagged goods)
> **L**iquid Bulk (crude, LNG, chemicals)
> **C**ontainerized (TEUs)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Cargo Handling Facilities

Cargo facilities are classified based on the **nature of cargo** and the **handling technology** employed:

### A. By Type of Cargo

| Cargo Type | Examples | Handling Mode | Typical Equipment |
|------------|----------|---------------|-------------------|
| **Dry Bulk** | Coal, iron ore, grain, fertilizer | Continuous / Cyclic | Grab cranes, belt conveyors, ship unloaders, stacker-reclaimers |
| **Liquid Bulk** | Crude oil, LNG, petroleum | Pipeline (continuous) | Marine loading arms, submarine pipelines, storage tanks |
| **Break-Bulk** | Machinery, steel, bagged sugar | Discontinuous (cyclic) | Cranes, forklifts, trailers, slings |
| **Unitized/Container** | TEUs, pallets, Ro-Ro | Discontinuous | Quay cranes (STS), RTGs, reach stackers, AGVs |

### B. By Functional Zone

1. **Water-side (Quayside / Jetty / Wharf)** — Interface with ship.
2. **Apron / Transit Area** — Staging zone behind quay.
3. **Storage Zone** — Transit shed, warehouse, open stack yard, silo, tank farm.
4. **Landside (Hinterland Connectivity)** — Road, rail, pipeline, conveyor gallery.

## 2.2 Core Handling Equipment — Logic & Operation

### 2.2.1 Quay-side Cranes
- **Level-Luffing Cranes**: Hook moves horizontally while load is raised/lowered — ideal for general cargo.
- **Ship-to-Shore (STS) Gantry Cranes**: Span 30–70 m outreach, lift **40–65 tonnes** under spreader, productivity **25–35 moves/hr** for containers.
- **Mobile Harbour Cranes (MHC)**: Rail/tyre-mounted, versatile, used for bulk and project cargo.

### 2.2.2 Unloaders (for Dry Bulk)
- **Grab-type ship unloaders** — Cranes with clamshell buckets; capacity **500–2500 TPH**.
- **Continuous unloaders** — Screw-type or bucket-elevator; capacity up to **4000 TPH**.
- **Pneumatic unloaders** — For fine powders like cement; **200–500 TPH**.

### 2.2.3 Conveyors
- **Belt conveyors** — Most common; capacity governed by:
$$Q = A \cdot v \cdot \rho \cdot 3600 \quad [\text{tonnes/hr}]$$
where $A$ = cross-sectional area of load (m²), $v$ = belt speed (m/s), $\rho$ = bulk density (t/m³).

### 2.2.4 Pumping Systems (Liquid Bulk)
- **Centrifugal pumps** for crude; **positive displacement** for viscous oils.
- Loading arm flow governed by:
$$Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 g H}$$
where $C_d$ = discharge coefficient (~0.6–0.8), $A$ = nozzle area, $H$ = effective head.

## 2.3 Storage Facilities

- **Transit Shed**: Open-sided steel/concrete structure adjacent to quay for short-term storage (1–3 days).
- **Warehouse**: Enclosed for longer-term or value-added cargo.
- **Open Stack Yard**: For coal, ore, timber (high volumetric cargo).
- **Silos**: For grain, cement — vertical steel/concrete cylinders.
- **Tank Farm**: Cluster of cylindrical storage tanks for liquids; bund walls for spill containment.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` instead of `|` to avoid breaking markdown tables.

| # | Parameter / Concept | Formula | Units | Typical Range / KTU Value |
|---|---------------------|---------|-------|---------------------------|
| 1 | **Belt Conveyor Capacity** | $Q = 3600 \cdot A \cdot v \cdot \rho$ | tonnes/hr | A: 0.02–0.4 m², v: 1.5–5 m/s |
| 2 | **Berth Occupancy** | $BO = \dfrac{\sum t_{berth}}{T_{period}} \times 100$ | % | Optimum: 50–65% |
| 3 | **Annual Throughput per Berth** | $T_{annual} = \dfrac{24 \cdot 365 \cdot R \cdot U}{1000}$ | million tonnes/yr | R: handling rate, U: utilization 0.6–0.7 |
| 4 | **Number of Berths Required** | $N = \dfrac{Q_{annual} \cdot t_{ship}}{T_{period} \cdot R \cdot U}$ | dimensionless | Used in master planning |
| 5 | **Dwell Time** | $D_t = T_{out} - T_{in}$ | days | 3–7 days general cargo |
| 6 | **Crane Productivity** | $P_c = \dfrac{3600 \cdot W_{avg}}{t_{cycle}}$ | tonnes/hr | $t_{cycle}$: 30–90 s |
| 7 | **Pump Flow (Orifice/Loading Arm)** | $Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$ | m³/s | $C_d$ = 0.6–0.8 |
| 8 | **TEU Throughput Conversion** | $1 \text{ TEU} \approx 10\text{–}14 \text{ tonnes}$ | t/TEU | Container cargo density |
| 9 | **Stockyard Capacity** | $V_s = L \cdot W \cdot H \cdot \rho_{stacked}$ | tonnes | Function of stack height |
| 10 | **Ship Turnaround Time** | $T_{turn} = T_{berthing} + T_{loading} + T_{deberthing}$ | hours | Bulk: 2–4 days; Container: 12–24 hr |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Master Plan of Ports (e.g., Cochin, Vizhinjam, JNPT)**: The cargo mix dictates facility selection — Vizhinjam is container-major, whereas Paradip is dry-bulk dominant.
- **CapEx Optimization**: Selecting the right facility reduces unit handling cost from **₹800–1200/tonne** (manual) to **₹150–400/tonne** (mechanized).
- **Environmental Compliance**: Modern facilities use **closed conveyors, dust suppression, vapour recovery** to comply with **MOEFCC norms**.
- **Smart Port Integration**: IoT-enabled cranes, automated stacking cranes (ASCs), and digital twin platforms optimize facility utilization.

## 2.6 Selection Criteria (Decision Flow Logic)

1. **Identify cargo mix** → Dry bulk? Liquid? Container?
2. **Estimate volume & frequency** → Annual tonnage, peak season variation.
3. **Vessel size & turnaround** → Capesize, Panamax, Feeder.
4. **Site constraints** → Water depth, hinterland access, land area.
5. **Select handling system** → Continuous (pipeline/belt) vs Cyclic (cranes).
6. **Size the storage** → Dwell time × daily throughput.
7. **Integrate inter-modal links** → Road, rail, conveyor, pipeline.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Belt Conveyor Capacity

### Statement
Derive an expression for the mass flow rate of a troughed belt conveyor carrying bulk material of bulk density $\rho$ at belt speed $v$.

### Given
- Belt width: $B$ (m)
- Troughing angle: $\theta$ (typically $20°$ or $30°$)
- Belt speed: $v$ (m/s)
- Bulk density: $\rho$ (t/m³)

### Step 1 — Cross-Sectional Area of Load
The troughed belt carries material in a shape approximated as a segment of a circle plus two side segments. For standard troughing at angle $\theta$:

$$A \approx 0.25 \cdot \left(0.9 B^{2} + B \cdot B \tan\theta \right) \approx k B^{2}$$

For $\theta = 20°$, $k \approx 0.0487$. For $\theta = 30°$, $k \approx 0.0587$.

A simplified KTU-acceptable form is:

$$A = k \cdot B^{2}$$

### Step 2 — Volumetric Throughput
The volume of material passing a cross-section per unit time is:

$$Q_{vol} = A \cdot v \quad [\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}]$$

### Step 3 — Mass Throughput
Multiplying by bulk density $\rho$:

$$Q_{mass} = A \cdot v \cdot \rho \quad [\text{tonnes}/\text{s}]$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Tonnes per Hour
Since 1 hour = 3600 seconds:

$$Q_{thr} = 3600 \cdot A \cdot v \cdot \rho \quad [\text{tonnes}/\text{hr}]$$

### Final Equation

$$\boxed{Q_{thr} = 3600 \cdot k \cdot B^{2} \cdot v \cdot \rho \quad [\text{tonnes/hr}]}$$

### Numerical Example (KTU Practice)
A 1200 mm wide belt, troughed at 20° ($k = 0.0487$), runs at 2.5 m/s, carrying coal of bulk density 0.85 t/m³.

$$A = 0.0487 \times (1.2)^{2} = 0.0701 \text{ m}^{2}$$

$$Q_{thr} = 3600 \times 0.0701 \times 2.5 \times 0.85$$

$$Q_{thr} = 536.3 \text{ tonnes/hr}$$

> **Interpretation:** A single 1.2 m belt can shift ~536 TPH — sufficient to load a Handysize bulk carrier (~35,000 DWT) in ~65 hours.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Number of Berths Required

### Statement
Derive the formula to determine the number of berths needed at a port to handle an estimated annual cargo throughput.

### Given
- $Q$ = Annual cargo throughput (tonnes/year)
- $R$ = Loading/unloading rate per berth (tonnes/hr)
- $U$ = Berth utilization factor (0.5–0.7)
- $T$ = Total time available per year (hours)
- $t_{s}$ = Average service time per ship (hours)

### Step 1 — Effective Service Time per Berth
A berth can be used productively only for fraction $U$ of total time:

$$T_{eff} = U \cdot T$$

### Step 2 — Total Ship Service Hours Required

$$T_{ship} = \frac{Q}{R}$$

### Step 3 — Number of Berths

$$N = \frac{T_{ship}}{T_{eff}} = \frac{Q}{R \cdot U \cdot T}$$

### Step 4 — If Average Ship Service Time $t_s$ is Given

$$N = \frac{Q \cdot t_{s}}{U \cdot R \cdot T \cdot (\text{cargo per ship})}$$

The classical KTU form is:

$$\boxed{N = \frac{Q \cdot t_{s}}{U \cdot q \cdot T_{period}}}$$

where $q$ = average cargo handled per ship call (tonnes).

### Numerical Example
A port expects 10 MT of dry bulk per year. Each ship carries 50,000 t; service time = 60 hours; berth utilization = 0.65; T = 8760 hr/yr.

$$N = \frac{10 \times 10^{6} \times 60}{0.65 \times 50{,}000 \times 8760}$$

$$N = \frac{6 \times 10^{8}}{2.847 \times 10^{8}} = 2.11 \Rightarrow 3 \text{ berths (round up)}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Crane Productivity (Container Handling)

### Given
- Crane move cycle: $t_c$ (seconds) = hoist up + trolley out + lower + release + return
- Average container payload: $W_{avg}$ (tonnes)

### Step 1 — Moves per Hour

$$N_{moves} = \frac{3600}{t_c}$$

### Step 2 — Mass Handled per Hour

$$P_{crane} = N_{moves} \cdot W_{avg} \quad [\text{tonnes/hr}]$$

### Step 3 — TEU per Hour
Since one container ≈ 1 TEU (for 20 ft):

$$P_{TEU} = N_{moves} \quad [\text{TEU/hr}]$$

### Numerical Example
STS gantry crane: $t_c = 90$ s, average load = 18 t (mixed 20 ft + 40 ft; convert 40 ft to 2 TEU each).

$$N_{moves} = 3600/90 = 40 \text{ moves/hr}$$

$$P_{TEU} = 40 \times 1.6 = 64 \text{ TEU/hr (weighted)}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Facility vs Cargo

| Facility Feature | Dry Bulk | Liquid Bulk | Break-Bulk | Container |
|------------------|:--------:|:-----------:|:----------:|:---------:|
| Quay structure | Open berth | Jetty with breasting dolphins | Open berth | Reinforced apron |
| Prime mover | Grab crane / unloader | Marine loading arm | Mobile crane | STS gantry crane |
| Horizontal transport | Belt conveyor | Pipeline | Forklift + trailer | Straddle / AGV |
| Vertical transport | Bucket elevator / chute | Pump | Forklift | RTG / RMG |
| Storage | Stockyard / silo | Tank farm | Transit shed | Container yard |
| Dust/Vapour control | Sprinklers, enclosures | Vapour recovery | None | None |
| Throughput/berth | 5–15 MT/yr | 20–50 MT/yr | 0.5–2 MT/yr | 0.4–1.0 M TEU/yr |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Cargo Handling Facility

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **end-to-end flow** of cargo from ship to hinterland, with all functional sub-systems, intermediate buffers, and decision logic — replacing what would otherwise be a complex physical port layout.

## 4.2 Equipment Hierarchy Sub-System

```mermaid

    EQ --> L2[Horizontal Transport]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tips for KTU Exam:**
> 1. The **diamond decision nodes** (`{Type of Cargo}` and `{Storage Mode Selection}`) are critical — examiners often test whether students can branch the flow correctly.
> 2. **Weighbridge and Inspection Bay** are mandatory choke points — drawing them correctly earns bonus marks in layouts.
> 3. The **Hinterland Transport Hub** is the multi-modal convergence point — link to road, rail, pipeline, and conveyor.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between dry bulk cargo and break-bulk cargo handling facilities. List two equipment used in each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Dry Bulk | Break-Bulk |
|--------|----------|------------|
| Nature | Loose, granular, powdery | Packaged, palletized, individual items |
| Examples | Coal, iron ore, grain, cement | Machinery, steel coils, bagged sugar |
| Handling Mode | Continuous (conveyor) or cyclic (grab) | Cyclic (crane + forklift) |
| Equipment 1 | Grab-type ship unloader | Level-luffing quay crane |
| Equipment 2 | Belt conveyor system | Forklift truck with trailer |
| Storage | Open stack yard / silo | Transit shed / warehouse |

**(1 mark for definition difference, 1 mark for each correct equipment pair → 3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define 'Berth Occupancy'. What is its optimum range and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Berth Occupancy (BO) is the ratio of the total time a berth is occupied by ships for cargo operations to the total available time in a given period, expressed as a percentage.

$$BO = \frac{\sum t_{berth-occupied}}{T_{period}} \times 100$$

**Optimum Range (1 mark):** **50% – 65%** for general cargo berths; up to 70% for container berths.

**Reason (1 mark):** Below 50% indicates under-utilization and economic loss; above 70% causes congestion, ship queuing, and demurrage. The optimum ensures a balance between revenue and operational flexibility.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, internal choice is mandatory. **Attempt either Question A or Question B.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **various types of cargo handling facilities provided at a modern port** with the help of neat block diagrams. Differentiate between open stack yard and transit shed storage.

**(b) [7 Marks)** A port handles **8 million tonnes/year** of thermal coal through a dedicated dry bulk berth. The average ship size is **60,000 DWT**, average service time per ship is **72 hours**, and the berth utilization factor is **0.65**. Determine:
   (i) The number of berths required.
   (ii) The annual revenue at ₹120 per tonne handling charge.

---

#### **Solution to A(a) — Facilities at a Modern Port (7 marks)**

1. **Quay-side facilities (2 marks):** Berths, jetties, breasting dolphins, mooring buoys, fenders. Equipped with cranes (grab, MHC, STS) sized 5–80 tonne SWL.

2. **Cargo transfer facilities (2 marks):** Belt conveyors (width 800–2000 mm), pipelines (diameter 200–1200 mm), rail-mounted gantries, road trailers.

3. **Storage facilities (2 marks):**
   - **Open Stack Yard**: Outdoor, used for weather-resistant bulk (coal, ore, limestone). Allows stacker-reclaimer operation, low CapEx, large footprint.
   - **Transit Shed**: Covered, near quay, short dwell (1–3 days), higher CapEx per m², protects cargo from rain/pilferage.

4. **Auxiliary facilities (1 mark):** Weighbridges, sampling labs, customs, dust suppression, fire fighting, bunkering, fresh water supply.

*Block diagram:* Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes — KTU accepts hand-drawn equivalent.

---

#### **Solution to A(b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $Q = 8 \times 10^6$ t/yr
- $q = 60{,}000$ t/ship
- $t_s = 72$ hr
- $U = 0.65$
- $T = 8760$ hr/yr

**Step 1 (3 marks):** Apply the berth formula:

$$N = \frac{Q \cdot t_s}{U \cdot q \cdot T}$$

$$N = \frac{8 \times 10^{6} \times 72}{0.65 \times 60{,}000 \times 8760}$$

$$N = \frac{5.76 \times 10^{8}}{3.4164 \times 10^{8}}$$

$$N = 1.686$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Round up → **2 berths required**.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Revenue calculation:

$$\text{Annual Revenue} = 8 \times 10^{6} \times 120 = ₹96 \times 10^{7} = ₹96 \text{ crore/yr}$$

**Final Answer:** **2 berths**, **₹96 crore/year revenue**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with sketches the working of **(i) Ship-to-Shore (STS) Gantry Crane** and **(ii) Marine Loading Arm**. State the typical capacity range of each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 1500 mm wide troughed belt conveyor ($\theta = 20°$, $k = 0.0487$) operates at 3.0 m/s carrying iron ore of bulk density 2.4 t/m³. Calculate the **mass throughput in TPH**. If a ship of 75,000 DWT is to be loaded, determine the **loading time in hours** (assume 95% conveyor efficiency).

---

#### **Solution to B(a) — Equipment Description (7 marks)**

**(i) STS Gantry Crane (4 marks):**
- **Structure:** Portal frame on rails parallel to quay, boom outreach 30–70 m, lift height 30–45 m above quay.
- **Components:** Trolley, spreader (twist-lock for containers), hoist drums, operator cabin.
- **Working:** Trolley moves along boom → spreader lowered into ship's cell → twist-locks engage container corners → hoist lifts → trolley retracts → spreader lowered onto apron or yard.
- **Capacity range:** **40–65 tonnes** under spreader; **25–35 moves/hour**; outreach up to **70 m** for ultra-large container vessels (ULCV).

**(ii) Marine Loading Arm (3 marks):**
- **Structure:** Counterbalanced articulated arm mounted on jetty manifold.
- **Working:** Inner and outer arms swivel on vertical and horizontal axes; inboard end connects to shore pipeline, outboard end has a loading head with Emergency Release Coupling (ERC).
- **Capacity range:** Diameter **200–600 mm**; flow **1000–10,000 m³/hr**; suited for crude, refined products, LNG (with cryogenic variants).

---

#### **Solution to B(b) — Belt Conveyor Numerical (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Cross-sectional area:

$$A = k \cdot B^{2} = 0.0487 \times (1.5)^{2} = 0.1096 \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Theoretical throughput:

$$Q_{thr} = 3600 \cdot A \cdot v \cdot \rho = 3600 \times 0.1096 \times 3.0 \times 2.4$$

$$Q_{thr} = 2842 \text{ TPH}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Effective throughput (95% efficiency):

$$Q_{eff} = 0.95 \times 2842 = 2700 \text{ TPH}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Loading time for 75,000 DWT ship:

$$T_{load} = \frac{75{,}000}{2700} = 27.78 \text{ hours} \approx 28 \text{ hours}$$

**Final Answer:** **Throughput ≈ 2842 TPH (theoretical), 2700 TPH (effective); Loading time ≈ 28 hours.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in Cargo Handling Facility Questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit confusion in belt conveyor problem:** Converting m/s to TPH via the 3600 factor is the single most-missed step. **Always write "× 3600" explicitly.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting to round up berth count:** $N = 1.68$ → 2 berths, NOT 1.6 or 1.68. **Always round UP to next whole integer.**
>
> 3. **Mixing TEU and tonnes:** In container problems, 1 TEU ≠ 1 tonne. Use **~10–14 t/TEU** based on stowage factor.
>
> 4. **Skipping the diagram in 7-mark theory questions:** A neat block diagram (even hand-drawn) carries **1–1.5 marks** as visual aid. Always include it.
>
> 5. **Ignoring berth utilization factor $U$:** Many students use $T$ directly without multiplying by $U$ — instant loss of 2 marks.
>
> 6. **Confusing "service time" with "turnaround time":** Service time = berth occupancy time per ship. Turnaround = service + waiting + transit. KTU questions specify — read carefully.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Cargo Handling Facility**

- ☐ **Four cargo categories:** Dry Bulk, Liquid Bulk, Break-Bulk, Container — memorize examples and equipment.
- ☐ **Belt conveyor capacity formula:** $Q = 3600 \cdot k \cdot B^{2} \cdot v \cdot \rho$ — TPH output.
- ☐ **Berth occupancy formula:** $BO = (\Sigma t_{berth} / T_{period}) \times 100$ — Optimum 50–65%.
- ☐ **Number of berths formula:** $N = (Q \cdot t_s) / (U \cdot q \cdot T)$ — Always round UP.
- ☐ **Crane cycle time:** Typical 60–90 s for STS; productivity in moves/hr.
- ☐ **Open stack yard vs Transit shed:** Outdoor vs covered; bulk vs break-bulk; stacker-reclaimer vs forklift.
- ☐ **Marine Loading Arm components:** Inner arm, outer arm, swivel joints, ERC.
- ☐ **Pipeline flow formula:** $Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$ — used for liquid loading arms.
- ☐ **Dwell time benchmark:** 3–7 days general cargo; <24 hours containers.
- ☐ **TEU standard:** 1 TEU = 20 ft container; 2 TEU = 40 ft container; ~10–14 t/TEU.
- ☐ **Indian port standards:** IS 4651, IS 9527 (cranes), MOEFCC environmental norms.
- ☐ **Hinterland connectivity modes:** Road, Rail, Pipeline, Conveyor — multi-modal is mandatory.
- ☐ **Recent trends:** Automated stacking cranes (ASCs), AGVs, digital twin, Vapour Recovery Units (VRUs).
- ☐ **Always include a block diagram** in 7-mark theory answers — earns visual aid marks.
- ☐ **Always state units** in numerical answers — partial marking is strict without units.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Services for Shipping Terminals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET754 - RAILWAY, PORT AND HARBOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Services for Shipping Terminals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Services for Shipping Terminals** are the **auxiliary operational, navigational, logistical, and welfare facilities** provided within and around a port or harbor to ensure the **safe, efficient, and economical handling of vessels, cargo, and personnel**. These services form the *soft infrastructure* complementing the *hard infrastructure* (quay walls, breakwaters, jetties).

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, shipping terminal services are classified under **three functional domains**:
- **Marine Services** — relating to vessel movement and safety (pilotage, towage, mooring).
- **Cargo Services** — relating to loading, unloading, and storage (stevedoring, warehousing).
- **Auxiliary/Hospitality Services** — supporting crew, vessel, and trade (bunkering, water supply, fumigation, customs).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — A Shipping Terminal is like a 5-Star Hotel + Hospital + Fuel Station for Giant Ships.**

Think of a port terminal as a **resort-cum-service-station for ships**:
- Just as a hotel offers **check-in (pilotage), valet parking (mooring), room service (bunkering), laundry (fumigation), and concierge (cargo handling)**, a shipping terminal provides a *basket of services* that a vessel needs during its short stay.
- A ship arriving at port is like a tired traveler — it needs to be *guided in (pilot)*, *parked safely (mooring/berthing)*, *refueled (bunkering)*, *fed water (water supply)*, *unloaded (cargo handling)*, *repaired if needed (dry dock/slipway)*, and finally *escorted out (towage)*.
- Without these services, even the most beautiful port (with great breakwaters and quays) is **useless**, like a hotel without staff.

### 1.3 Why These Services are Critical

- A modern **Very Large Crude Carrier (VLCC)** of ~**3,00,000 DWT** cannot berth, unload, or even enter the harbor basin without coordinated services.
- Downtime in port costs shipping lines approximately **USD 50,000–1,50,000 per day** (industry benchmark).
- KTU 2024 emphasizes that **~60% of total port operational revenue** comes from service charges, not just cargo throughput.
- Standards referenced: **IMCO (now IMO)**, **ISGOTT** (International Safety Guide for Oil Tankers and Terminals), **ILO conventions** on seafarer welfare, **IALA** for aids to navigation.

### 1.4 Physical / Standard Metrics at a Glance

- **Standard navigation light range** for major ports: **15–25 nautical miles (NM)**.
- **Pilot boarding ground (PBG)** distance: typically **2–5 NM** outside breakwater.
- **Bunker fuel delivery rate**: **100–500 m³/hour** via pipeline or barge.
- **Fresh water supply rate**: **50–200 tonnes/hour** via shore hydrant or barge.
- **Standard tug power** for a VLCC berthing: **60–80 tonne bollard pull (TBP)** per tug, typically 2–3 tugs per ship.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Services for Shipping Terminals (Hierarchical)

#### A. Marine / Vessel-Related Services

1. **Pilotage Service**
   - *Definition*: A **licensed marine pilot** boards the ship at the **Pilot Boarding Ground (PBG)** and assumes *navigational command* of the vessel to safely guide it through the harbor channel, turning basin, and to the berth.
   - *Legal status*: Compulsory in most countries for foreign vessels; regulated under **Merchant Shipping Act** of the flag/coastal state.
   - *Why needed*: The ship's captain is unfamiliar with local depths, currents, traffic, and regulations.

2. **Towage Service**
   - *Definition*: Use of **tugboats** to assist large vessels in **berthing, un-berthing, and maneuvering** in confined waters.
   - *Why needed*: A fully loaded ship has poor maneuverability at low speeds; tugs provide the lateral thrust (via **bollard pull**) that the ship's own propeller cannot generate.

3. **Mooring & Berthing Service**
   - *Definition*: Securing the vessel to the berth using **mooring lines, bollards, bitts, and fenders**.
   - *Components*: Forward, aft, and spring lines — total **8–12 lines** typically.
   - *Equipment*: Capstans, winches (on ship), bollards and bitts (on quay), fenders (energy absorbers between ship and quay).

4. **Navigation Aids (Aids to Navigation — AtoN)**
   - *Lighthouses, light buoys, beacons, leading lights, radar reflectors, DGPS stations*.
   - Managed by IALA (International Association of Marine Aids to Navigation and Lighthouse Authorities).

5. **Bunkering Service**
   - *Definition*: Supply of **fuel oil (IFO 380, VLSFO), marine diesel oil (MDO), and lubricating oil** to the vessel.
   - Delivered via **pipeline from shore storage** or **bunker barge**.

6. **Fresh Water Supply**
   - Critical for boiler feed, drinking, cargo (e.g., grain washing), and ballast adjustments.

7. **Ship Repair & Maintenance**
   - *Floating docks, dry docks, slipways, repair quays*.
   - Classified into **hull cleaning, propeller polishing, engine overhaul, electrical works**.

#### B. Cargo-Related Services

1. **Stevedoring**
   - *Definition*: Loading and unloading of cargo from the vessel using **cranes, derricks, conveyors, or ship's gear**.
   - Performed by licensed labor force under a stevedoring company.

2. **Cargo Handling & Storage**
   - *Includes*: Warehousing, open stacking yards, container yards (CY), tank farms (for liquid cargo), silos (for grain), cold storage (for perishables).

3. **Weighing, Sampling, and Inspection**
   - Quality (inspection) and quantity (draft survey, weighbridge) verification.

#### C. Auxiliary / Welfare Services

1. **Fumigation & Disinfection**
   - Mandatory for cargo holds carrying grain, timber, etc., per **phytosanitary regulations**.

2. **Waste Reception & Garbage Disposal**
   - **MARPOL Annex V** compliance: ports must provide reception facilities for ship-generated waste.

3. **Customs, Immigration, and Quarantine (CIQ)**
   - One-stop clearance for crew, cargo, and vessel.

4. **Crew Welfare**
   - Crew change, medical facilities, transport, victualling (provisions supply).

5. **Fire-fighting & Pollution Control**
   - Dedicated **fire tugs, oil spill response barges, dispersant stockpiles**.

6. **Communication & IT Services**
   - **VHF radio, port communication system (PCS), Vessel Traffic Service (VTS), AIS** coverage.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Parameter | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|---------------------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Number of tugs required | $N_t = \dfrac{L_{ship}}{200} \pm 1$ | dimensionless | Empirical; for $L_{ship} > 200$ m use 2–3 tugs |
| 2 | Bollard pull (BP) requirement | $BP \ge 0.012 \times DWT$ | tonnes | For VLCCs $DWT \approx 3 \times 10^5$ t |
| 3 | Bunker fuel quantity | $Q_b = P_{aux} \times T_{stay} \times f_{sfc}$ | tonnes | $f_{sfc}$ = specific fuel consumption (g/kWh) |
| 4 | Fresh water demand | $Q_w = N_{crew} \times d_{per} + Q_{cargo}$ | m³/day | $d_{per} \approx 0.15$ m³/person/day |
| 5 | Pilotage charge | $P_p = K_p \times GT + C_p$ | currency | $GT$ = Gross Tonnage; $K_p$ = rate/GT |
| 6 | Berth occupancy ratio | $\eta = \dfrac{T_{occupied}}{T_{available}} \times 100$ | % | Ideal $\eta = 60$–$70$ % |
| 7 | Fender reaction energy | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} m_{eff} v^2 C_m C_e$ | kJ | $C_m$ = added mass coefficient |
| 8 | Mooring line tension | $T_{line} = \dfrac{F_{env}}{\sin\theta \cdot n}$ | kN | $n$ = number of effective lines |
| 9 | Cargo throughput | $Th = \dfrac{W_{cargo}}{T_{turnaround}}$ | tonnes/day | Turnaround = berth + handling time |
| 10 | Port dues | $D = D_{light} + D_{berth} + D_{pilot} + D_{tug}$ | currency | Component-wise billing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU 2024 Exam Note**: When numerical problems involve **tug requirements**, the examiner expects you to (a) compute **bollard pull**, (b) state the **minimum number of tugs**, AND (c) justify with the **vessel size class**.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tanker Terminals (e.g., Kochi, Singapore)**: Require **continuous bunkering, oil-spill response, and inert gas services** for safe crude transfer.
- **Container Terminals (e.g., Vallarpadam ICTT)**: Demand **24×7 stevedoring, on-dock rail, IT-based tracking, and reefer power supply**.
- **Cruise Terminals (e.g., Cochin Cruise Terminal)**: Need **CIQ in minutes, passenger gangways, customs baggage clearance, provisioning for 3,000+ passengers**.
- **Bulk Terminals (e.g., New Mangalore)**: Focus on **dust suppression, weighbridges, stockyard management, and conveyor services**.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Service-by-Service Evaluation Framework

For any service at a shipping terminal, KTU 2024 expects students to evaluate across **five parameters**: **Purpose → Provider → Equipment → Standard → Cost**.

| Service | Primary Purpose | Provider | Key Equipment | Governing Standard | Cost Basis |
|---------|-----------------|----------|---------------|--------------------|------------|
| Pilotage | Safe navigation | Port Authority (licensed pilots) | Pilot launch, VHF | IMO Resolution A.485 | GT-based |
| Towage | Maneuvering assist | Port Authority / private | Tugboats, tow lines | IACS, classification societies | Bollard pull × hours |
| Mooring | Securing vessel | Port Authority | Bollards, bitts, capstans, fenders | PIANC, BS 6349 | Per berthing |
| Bunkering | Fuel supply | Oil majors, barge operators | Pipeline, barge, manifold | ISO 8217 (fuel standards) | Per tonne |
| Stevedoring | Cargo transfer | Licensed stevedore co. | Cranes, forklifts, spreaders | ILO Convention 152 | Per ton / per TEU |
| Fumigation | Pest control | Certified agencies | Gas cylinders, applicators | FAO ISPM-15, IMDG | Per cargo lot |
| Fresh water | Potable supply | Port utility wing | Hydrants, hoses, meters | WHO + IS 10500 | Per m³ |
| Waste reception | MARPOL compliance | Port + private | Barges, drums, incinerators | MARPOL 73/78 | Per m³ |
| CIQ | Statutory clearance | Govt. agencies (single window) | Computers, scanners | Customs Act, Immigration rules | Per call |
| Fire/pollution | Safety | Port fire service | Fire tugs, foam tenders, booms | SOLAS, OPA 90 | Included in dues |

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Number of Tugs Required

> **Problem Context**: A bulk carrier of **Length Overall (LOA) = 250 m** and **Deadweight Tonnage (DWT) = 80,000 tonnes** is approaching a port. Compute the **minimum number of tugs** required and the **minimum bollard pull** each must provide.

**Step 1 — Establish vessel size class**

From the LOA:

$$L_{ship} = 250 \text{ m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Vessel class} = \text{Handymax to Panamax}$$

From the DWT:

$$DWT = 80{,}000 \text{ t} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Class} = \text{Handymax Bulk Carrier}$$

**Step 2 — Empirical rule for number of tugs**

The rule of thumb used in KTU 2024 reference materials is:

$$N_t = \dfrac{L_{ship}}{200} \pm 1$$

Substituting the value:

$$N_t = \dfrac{250}{200} \pm 1$$

$$N_t = 1.25 \pm 1$$

Taking the upper bound for a safe design (because the vessel is in confined waters during berthing):

$$N_t = 1.25 + 1 = 2.25 \approx 3 \text{ tugs}$$

Therefore, **minimum 3 tugs** must be deployed for safe berthing of this vessel.

**Step 3 — Minimum Bollard Pull (BP) per tug**

The empirical formula relating bollard pull to deadweight is:

$$BP_{total} = 0.012 \times DWT$$

$$BP_{total} = 0.012 \times 80{,}000$$

$$BP_{total} = 960 \text{ tonnes (combined bollard pull)}$$

**Step 4 — Distribute bollard pull across tugs**

Dividing the total bollard pull equally among the 3 tugs:

$$BP_{per\,tug} = \dfrac{BP_{total}}{N_t} = \dfrac{960}{3} = 320 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 5 — Apply safety factor**

Per **OCIMF (Oil Companies International Marine Forum)** guidelines, a **safety factor of 1.5** must be applied to account for environmental loads (wind, current, wave):

$$BP_{design} = 1.5 \times BP_{per\,tug}$$

$$BP_{design} = 1.5 \times 320 = 480 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 6 — Select standard commercial tug**

Standard tugs are manufactured in **30, 50, 60, 80, 100 tonne** bollard pull ratings. Therefore, the selected tug is:

$$\boxed{\text{3 tugs of } 480 \text{ tonne BP (or 3 commercial tugs of 500 tonne BP class)}}$$

**Step 7 — Validation against PIANC guidelines**

PIANC recommends that total bollard pull should be at least **3% of DWT** for very large vessels. Check:

$$BP_{check} = 0.03 \times 80{,}000 = 2{,}400 \text{ tonnes}$$

Since our design is **2,400 tonnes combined** (from 3 tugs of 800 tonnes each), we are well within the safe limit when using larger commercial tugs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion for KTU Answer Writing**: Always present (a) the rule used, (b) the numerical substitution, (c) the upper-bound selection, (d) the safety factor, and (e) the final commercial selection. This **5-step pattern** fetches full marks.

### 3.3 Derivation — Berth Occupancy Ratio (BOR)

**Step 1 — Define BOR**

$$\eta = \dfrac{\text{Actual time berth is occupied by ships}}{\text{Total time berth is available}} \times 100 \text{ %}$$

**Step 2 — Express numerically**

$$\eta = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{berth,i}}{T_{available}} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Example**

If a berth handled **30 ships in 90 days**, with each ship occupying it for an average of **2.5 days**:

$$\eta = \dfrac{30 \times 2.5}{90} \times 100 = \dfrac{75}{90} \times 100 = 83.33 \text{ %}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An **ideal BOR** is **60–70%**. Values above **75%** indicate the berth is **over-utilized** and needs expansion; below **40%** indicates **under-utilization** and inefficient infrastructure.

### 3.4 Derivation — Port Dues Component (Composite Charge)

A ship calling at a port typically pays:

$$D_{total} = D_{light\,dues} + D_{pilotage} + D_{towage} + D_{berth\,hire} + D_{cargo\,dues}$$

**Step 1 — Light Dues**

$$D_{light} = R_l \times GT \times N_{factors}$$

Where $R_l$ is the rate per GT, $N_{factors}$ adjusts for special zones.

**Step 2 — Pilotage Charge**

$$D_{pilot} = R_p \times GT + C_{pilot,base}$$

**Step 3 — Towage Charge**

$$D_{tug} = R_t \times BP \times T_{tug} + C_{tug,base}$$

**Step 4 — Berth Hire**

$$D_{berth} = R_b \times LOA \times T_{berth}$$

**Step 5 — Cargo Dues**

$$D_{cargo} = R_c \times Q_{cargo}$$

**Step 6 — Composite (Example)**: For a 30,000 GT vessel, 4 hours tug time at 60 tonne BP, 1.5 days berth, 5000 tonnes cargo:

$$D_{total} = 0.05 \times 30{,}000 + 1.5 \times 30{,}000 + 5 \times 60 \times 4 + 0.4 \times 200 \times 1.5 + 2.5 \times 5000$$

$$D_{total} = 1{,}500 + 45{,}000 + 1{,}200 + 120 + 12{,}500 = 60{,}320 \text{ currency units}$$

(Using example rates; actual values vary by port.)
