## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]`**

**Q1. Define pilotage. Why is it compulsory for foreign vessels calling at Indian ports?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark)**: Pilotage is the service of a **licensed marine pilot** boarding an incoming vessel at the **Pilot Boarding Ground (PBG)** to take over its **navigational command** and guide it safely through the harbor channel, turning circle, and to the berth.
- **Why compulsory (1 mark)**: Because the ship's captain is **unfamiliar** with local **depths, currents, traffic density, navigational rules, and channel geometry** of the port. A local pilot has intimate knowledge of these variables.
- **Legal basis (1 mark)**: Under the **Merchant Shipping Act, 1958** (India) and the **Indian Ports Act**, pilotage is **compulsory** for all foreign vessels and for Indian vessels above a certain tonnage, unless a specific exemption is granted.

---

> **`[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]`**

**Q2. List any six essential auxiliary services provided at a modern shipping terminal.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.5 mark each):**
1. Bunkering (fuel supply)
2. Fresh water supply
3. Stevedoring (cargo handling)
4. Warehousing and storage
5. Customs, Immigration, Quarantine (CIQ)
6. Waste reception and garbage disposal (MARPOL)
7. Fumigation
8. Fire-fighting and pollution control
9. Ship repair (dry dock / slipway)
10. Communication (VHF, VTS, AIS)

(Any **six** correctly listed = full marks.)

---

### 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]`**

### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **classification of services for shipping terminals** with at least **two examples** under each category. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bulk carrier with **LOA = 280 m** and **DWT = 75,000 tonnes** is being berthed at a port. Determine **(i)** the minimum number of tugs required, **(ii)** the combined bollard pull, and **(iii)** the rating of each tug after applying a safety factor of 1.5. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

The services at a shipping terminal are classified into **three broad categories**:

**1. Marine / Vessel-Related Services (2 marks)**

These services ensure the **safe arrival, berthing, stay, and departure** of the vessel.

- **Pilotage**: A licensed pilot takes over the ship's navigation from outside the harbor entrance (at the Pilot Boarding Ground, typically 2–5 NM from the breakwater) and guides it through the channel, turning basin, and to the berth.
- **Towage**: Tugboats assist in berthing, un-berthing, and maneuvering. The **bollard pull** of a tug (the static pulling force it can exert) is the key performance metric.
- **Mooring and Berthing**: Securing the ship to the quay using mooring lines (head, stern, spring, breast) and protecting it from impact using **fenders**.
- **Bunkering**: Supply of marine fuel oil (IFO 380, VLSFO, MDO) via shore pipeline or bunker barge. Modern ports aim for a **delivery rate of 100–500 m³/hour**.
- **Navigation Aids**: Lighthouses, buoys, leading lights, beacons, DGPS stations, AIS — all governed by **IALA** standards.

**2. Cargo-Related Services (2 marks)**

These handle the **physical movement and storage of goods**.

- **Stevedoring**: The actual loading/unloading of cargo from the vessel's hold using cranes, derricks, or shore-based gantry cranes. Performed by licensed stevedoring companies under **ILO Convention 152** standards.
- **Cargo Handling & Storage**: Warehouses, open yards, container yards (CY), tank farms, silos, cold storage.
- **Inspection, Sampling, and Weighing**: Draft survey, weighbridges, quality sampling, phytosanitary inspection.

**3. Auxiliary / Welfare / Safety Services (2 marks)**

These support the **crew, environment, and statutory compliance**.

- **CIQ (Customs, Immigration, Quarantine)**: One-stop clearance for ship, cargo, and crew.
- **Fumigation & Disinfection**: For grain, timber, and perishable cargoes, per **FAO ISPM-15** and **IMDG Code**.
- **Waste Reception**: Per **MARPOL 73/78 Annex V** — ports must provide reception facilities for ship-generated waste.
- **Fire-fighting and Oil Spill Response**: Dedicated fire tugs, foam tenders, containment booms, dispersant stockpiles — under **SOLAS** and **OPA 90**.
- **Crew Welfare**: Crew change, medical, victualling, transport, communication.
- **Communication & IT**: VHF radio, **VTS (Vessel Traffic Service)**, **AIS (Automatic Identification System)**, port community systems.

**Conclusion (1 mark)**: A modern shipping terminal is essentially a **service ecosystem**; without integration of all three categories, the port's hard infrastructure (quay walls, breakwaters) remains commercially underutilized. (Total: **7 marks**)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Given**:
- $L_{ship} = 280$ m
- $DWT = 75{,}000$ tonnes
- Safety factor $SF = 1.5$

**(i) Minimum number of tugs** (2 marks)

Empirical rule:

$$N_t = \dfrac{L_{ship}}{200} \pm 1$$

$$N_t = \dfrac{280}{200} \pm 1 = 1.4 \pm 1$$

Taking upper bound for design safety:

$$N_t = 1.4 + 1 = 2.4 \approx 3 \text{ tugs}$$

**Answer**: **3 tugs** are required.

**(ii) Combined bollard pull** (2 marks)

$$BP_{total} = 0.012 \times DWT$$

$$BP_{total} = 0.012 \times 75{,}000 = 900 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Answer**: **Combined bollard pull = 900 tonnes**.

**(iii) Rating of each tug** (3 marks)

Per-tug bollard pull (without safety factor):

$$BP_{each} = \dfrac{900}{3} = 300 \text{ tonnes}$$

With safety factor 1.5:

$$BP_{design} = 1.5 \times 300 = 450 \text{ tonnes}$$

Since standard commercial tugs come in 30, 50, 60, 80, 100, 120, 150 tonne ratings, we select the next higher standard size:

$$\boxed{\text{Select 3 tugs of 450 tonne BP class (or commercial 500 tonne BP tugs)}}$$

**Validation against PIANC (0 mark — bonus)**: PIANC recommends $BP_{total} \ge 0.03 \times DWT = 2{,}250$ tonnes, which is well exceeded.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Describe the **bunkering and water supply services** at a major port. What are the **quality standards** and **typical delivery rates**? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write short notes on the following: (i) MARPOL waste reception facilities, (ii) CIQ procedure, (iii) Fumigation, (iv) Stevedoring. **(4 × 3.5 = 14 marks)** — *Note: For KTU, write any 4 sub-parts @ 3.5 marks each, or scale to 7 marks for two sub-parts.*

*(Adjusted for KTU 2024 — choose any two:)*

**(b1)** Explain **MARPOL waste reception facilities** at a port. What are the obligations under **Annex V**? **(7 marks)**

**(b2)** Explain the **CIQ (Customs, Immigration, Quarantine)** procedure adopted at modern Indian ports. Highlight the concept of a **single-window clearance**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Bunkering Service (3.5 marks)**

- **Definition**: Bunkering is the supply of **marine fuels** (mainly Intermediate Fuel Oil 380 cSt — IFO 380, Very Low Sulphur Fuel Oil — VLSFO, and Marine Diesel Oil — MDO) to ships alongside the berth or at an anchorage.
- **Delivery modes**:
  - **Pipeline mode**: Direct from shore storage tank to the ship's bunker manifold via a metered pipeline.
  - **Barge mode**: From a self-propelled bunker barge carrying 1,000–5,000 tonnes.
- **Quality standards**: Governed by **ISO 8217** (specifies viscosity, sulphur content, density, flash point). From 2020, **IMO MARPOL Annex VI** caps sulphur at **0.5%** globally (0.1% in ECAs).
- **Typical delivery rate**: **100–500 m³/hour** via pipeline; **50–200 m³/hour** via barge.
- **Safety**: Bunker operators follow **ISGOTT** and **SIGTTO** guidelines; bonding, earthing, spill kits, gas testing are mandatory.

**Fresh Water Supply (3.5 marks)**

- **Source**: Treated fresh water from the municipal supply or port's own desalination plant.
- **Delivery modes**:
  - **Shore hydrant** with metered flexible hose (preferred for alongside berths).
  - **Water barge** for ships at outer anchorages.
- **Typical rate**: **50–200 tonnes/hour**.
- **Quality standard**: **WHO drinking water guidelines + IS 10500** (Bureau of Indian Standards). Salinity < 500 ppm.
- **Uses on board**: Boiler feed, drinking, cooking, laundry, cargo (e.g., cattle ships, vegetable oil tankers), and **ballast water adjustment**.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b1) — MARPOL Waste Reception (7 marks)

**Background (2 marks)**:
- **MARPOL 73/78** (International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships) is the main international convention on ship-source pollution, with **six technical Annexes** (I to VI).

**Obligations under Annex V — Garbage (3 marks)**:
- **Annex V** prohibits the **discharge of all types of garbage** into the sea, with very limited exceptions.
- It applies to **all ships**, regardless of flag or location.
- **Types of garbage regulated**: Plastics, food waste, domestic waste, cooking oil, incinerator ash, operational waste, cargo residues, fishing gear, and e-waste.
- Ships must maintain a **Garbage Management Plan** and a **Garbage Record Book**.

**Port Reception Facilities (2 marks)**:
- Ports must provide **adequate reception facilities** for ship-generated garbage.
- Types: **Drums, skips, compactors, barges, incinerators**, and **sewage holding tanks** for black/grey water (also covered under Annex IV).
- Indian ports (e.g., Mumbai, Kochi, Chennai) provide dedicated **MARPOL waste reception berths** and on-call barge services.

**Conclusion (0 mark — bonus)**: Non-availability of reception facilities is **not a defense** for illegal discharge; the port is legally obligated to provide them and the ship to use them. (Total: **7 marks**)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b2) — CIQ Procedure (7 marks)

**CIQ stands for** (1 mark):
- **C**ustoms
- **I**mmigration
- **Q**uarantine

**Customs (2 marks)**:
- Verifies the **Manifest, Bill of Lading, Cargo Declaration, and Import/Export documents**.
- Assesses and collects **customs duty, GST, and other levies**.
- Conducts **physical examination** of cargo on a risk-based selection.
- Issues **Out of Charge (OOC)** order before cargo can be removed.

**Immigration (2 marks)**:
- Clears the **ship's crew** — verifies **passports, CDC (Continuous Discharge Certificate)**, and **sign-on/sign-off records**.
- Grants **shore leave** and issues **crew landing passes**.
- Records **arrival/departure of seafarers** in the official log.

**Quarantine (1.5 marks)**:
- Health check of crew for **infectious diseases** (yellow fever, COVID-19, etc.).
- Inspection of **cargo** for biohazards, plant/animal products (under **DPPQS** — Directorate of Plant Protection).
- Issues **pratique** (health clearance) for the ship to commence operations.

**Single-Window Clearance (0.5 mark — bonus)**:
- Modern Indian ports (e.g., **ICCR — Indian Customs Cargo Clearance**, **PCS1x**, **Port Community System**) integrate CIQ into a **single digital platform**.
- The **National Single Window System (NSWS)** launched in 2021–22 aims to provide end-to-end clearance.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Topic (KTU 2024):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "pilotage" with "towage"** — Pilots give *navigational advice*; tugs provide *physical force*. Many students interchange them. **-1 to -2 marks**.
> 2. **Omitting the safety factor** in bollard pull calculations. KTU expects **SF = 1.5** explicitly. **-1 mark**.
> 3. **Writing "tug power" instead of "bollard pull"** — these are different! Power is in kW/hp; bollard pull is in tonnes of force. **-1 mark**.
> 4. **Forgetting MARPOL Annexes** — Examiner may ask "Which annex governs garbage?" Answer: **Annex V**. If you write Annex IV (which is sewage), **-1 mark**.
> 5. **Not mentioning the legal basis** for compulsory pilotage — Indian students must cite **Merchant Shipping Act, 1958** for full marks.
> 6. **In CIQ, omitting "Quarantine"** — Many students only write Customs + Immigration. Quarantine is the third pillar; missing it costs **0.5–1 mark**.
> 7. **Writing "IMO" instead of "IALA"** for navigation aids — these are different bodies. **-0.5 mark**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Services for Shipping Terminals":**

- [ ] **Three domains**: Marine, Cargo, Auxiliary.
- [ ] **Pilotage** = navigation; **Towage** = physical force; **Mooring** = securing.
- [ ] **PBG** = Pilot Boarding Ground, located 2–5 NM outside the harbor.
- [ ] **Bollard pull rule**: $BP_{total} = 0.012 \times DWT$; with $SF = 1.5$ for design.
- [ ] **Tug count rule**: $N_t = \dfrac{L_{ship}}{200} \pm 1$, take upper bound.
- [ ] **Bunkering** is governed by **ISO 8217**; **sulphur cap 0.5%** under MARPOL Annex VI.
- [ ] **Fresh water** rate: 50–200 t/hr; quality per **IS 10500**.
- [ ] **Stevedoring** = loading/unloading; governed by **ILO 152**.
- [ ] **MARPOL** has 6 annexes; **Annex V** = garbage.
- [ ] **CIQ** = Customs + Immigration + Quarantine; **single window** is the modern norm.
- [ ] **Fumigation** per **FAO ISPM-15** for timber/grain.
- [ ] **AtoN** standards set by **IALA**.
- [ ] **VTS + AIS** = modern vessel traffic management.
- [ ] **Fire & pollution** services mandatory under **SOLAS**.
- [ ] **Pilotage** is compulsory under **Merchant Shipping Act, 1958 (India)**.
- [ ] **Berth Occupancy Ratio** ideal range = **60–70%**; >75% = saturated.
- [ ] **Fenders** absorb berthing energy; **mooring lines** (head, stern, breast, spring) hold the ship.
- [ ] **Bunker modes**: pipeline or barge.
- [ ] **PRATAP / Sagarmala** are Indian government initiatives impacting terminal service modernization.
- [ ] Always quote **units** in numerical answers (tonnes, m³, NM, kN).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inland Port Facilities Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET754 - RAILWAY, PORT AND HARBOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Inland Port Facilities Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **INLAND PORT (INLAND WATERWAY TERMINAL)**
> An **Inland Port** is a designated terminal facility constructed along a navigable river, canal, lake, or backwater system that serves as a **modal interchange node** between inland waterborne transport (barges, vessels) and land-based transport modes (road, rail, and pipeline). It functions as a satellite or feeder node to a major sea port, enabling efficient cargo consolidation, transfer, and distribution.

According to the **Inland Waterways Authority of India (IWAI)** and the **Ministry of Ports, Shipping, and Waterways**, the National Waterways are classified into **seven classes (Class I to Class VII)** based on the permissible vessel size and channel depth. The minimum navigable channel depth for Class I is **1.2 m**, while Class VII supports navigation in **2.0 m** depth with barge convoys.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 4 Outcome:**
> Students must be able to plan an inland port facility considering site selection, layout, berthing structure, cargo handling equipment, storage, and multimodal connectivity. This topic directly tests **CO4: Plan inland waterway terminal facilities** and **CO5: Evaluate integration of inland ports with the national transport network**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **bus depot on a water highway**. Just as a bus depot allows passengers to seamlessly switch between buses, cars, and trains, an inland port is a "**transfer hub on water**". Goods arrive on a slow-moving but highly economical barge, get unloaded onto a warehouse, and then shift to a fast truck or train for the final journey to the customer.

- **The River/Canal** is the highway.
- **The Barge** is the bus.
- **The Inland Port** is the depot.
- **The Warehouse/Godown** is the parking-cum-storage bay.
- **The Road/Rail link** is the exit/entry ramp connecting to the city.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Example (India):** *Kolkata's Multi-Modal Terminal at Haldia (NW-1)* on the Hooghly River is a typical inland port — vessels of Class IV (**1,000–1,500 DWT**, draft up to **2.5 m**) berth at floating jetties and transfer cargo directly to road and rail.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics and Classification

| Waterway Class | Channel Depth (m) | Vessel Size (DWT) | Typical Barge Length (m) | Typical Barge Beam (m) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Class I | 1.2 | Up to 100 | 20–25 | 4–5 |
| Class II | 1.5 | 100–300 | 25–32 | 5–6 |
| Class III | 1.8 | 300–1,000 | 32–45 | 7–8 |
| Class IV | 2.0 | 1,000–1,500 | 45–80 | 9–11 |
| Class V | 2.0–2.5 | 1,500–3,000 | 80–90 | 12–14 |
| Class VI | 2.5–3.0 | 3,000–6,000 | 90–110 | 14–16 |
| Class VII | > 3.0 | > 6,000 | > 110 | > 16 |

> **Note:** Dimensions are based on the **IWAI classification and PIANC guidelines** for inland waterway design. These are **typical** ranges; actual vessel dimensions vary by operator and waterway.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Hierarchical Planning Framework for Inland Port Facilities

Inland port planning proceeds through a structured decision hierarchy. Each level constrains the next, ensuring **economic, hydraulic, and operational feasibility**.

### Level 1 — Macro Planning (Strategic)
- **Demand forecasting** (cargo throughput in million tonnes per annum, MTPA).
- **Network positioning** (feeder vs. trunk terminal).
- **Hinterland analysis** (catchment radius of 50–150 km for road, 300+ km for rail).
- **Modal share target** (typical Indian target: **5% IWT share** by 2030).

### Level 2 — Site Selection (Tactical)
The site must satisfy the following **physical, hydrological, and logistical criteria**:

1. **Stable river reach** — minimal bank erosion, narrow flood plain.
2. **Adequate depth** — natural depth ≥ required navigational depth, with allowance for dredging.
3. **Adequate width** — to permit two-way navigation and turning.
4. **Favourable soil** — for berthing structure foundations (avoid loose alluvium without pile support).
5. **Connectivity** — proximity to NH/SH and railway siding.
6. **Slope and flood level** — terminal elevation above HFL (High Flood Level) of the waterway.

### Level 3 — Facility Planning (Operational)
This includes the detailed engineering of:
- Berthing structures
- Storage facilities
- Cargo handling systems
- Navigation aids
- Internal road/rail network
- Utilities and drainage

## 2.2 Component Inventory of Inland Port Facilities

| Facility Category | Sub-Components | Engineering Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Berthing Facilities** | Floating jetty, Fixed wharf, Pontoon, Mooring dolphins, Bollards | Vessel mooring and along-side transfer |
| **Cargo Handling** | Shore cranes, Mobile harbour cranes, Floating cranes, Conveyors, Forklifts | Loading/unloading between vessel and yard |
| **Storage** | Covered godowns, Open stackyards, Silo complexes, Liquid tanks, Container yards (CY) | Buffer between modal arrivals |
| **Inland Connectivity** | Approach road, Rail siding, Conveyor galleries, Pipeline manifolds | Multimodal integration |
| **Navigation Aids** | Day beacons, Lighted buoys, Leading lights, Range markers, DGPS stations | Safe vessel approach and berthing |
| **Channel & Basin** | Approach channel, Turning basin, Anchorage, Lock (if required) | Vessel manoeuvring and depth maintenance |
| **Support Services** | Bunker station, Fresh water, Power substation, Workshop, Customs house, Crew amenities | Operational and statutory support |

## 2.3 Engineering Design Considerations — The "Why" and "How"

### 2.3.1 Berthing Structure Selection
- **Floating Jetty (pontoon):** Preferred in rivers with large water-level fluctuation (often 5–10 m in Indian rivers). It rises and falls with water level, keeping the **deck-to-vessel freeboard** constant.
- **Fixed Wharf (sheet pile / piled):** Used in stable lakes, canals, or rivers with small fluctuation (< 2 m). Cheaper to build but exposes large freeboard during flood.
- **Mooring Dolphins:** Isolated pile clusters for breasting and mooring when continuous quay is uneconomical.

### 2.3.2 Channel Width (One-way vs. Two-way)
- **One-way channel:** Width = 3 × B (B = beam of design vessel)
- **Two-way channel:** Width = 6 × B for unrestricted, or 4.5 × B with vessel-passing controls (PIANC).

### 2.3.3 Turning Basin Diameter
- For non-assisted turning: D = 2 × L (L = length of design vessel)
- For tug-assisted turning: D = 1.5 × L
- Minimum practical: D = 1.2 × L

### 2.3.4 Storage Area Calculation
The required storage area depends on **dwell time** (how long cargo stays at the terminal) and **stack height**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The single most important economic parameter in inland port design is **Dwell Time** (also called Storage Turnover Time). It directly governs the size and cost of the terminal.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Use the table below as your cheat-sheet for Part B numerical sub-questions.** All formulas are mathematically verifiable from PIANC, UNCTAD, and Indian Inland Waterways standards.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Channel width (one-way) | $W_{1} = 3 B$ | m | B = vessel beam |
| 2 | Channel width (two-way) | $W_{2} = 6 B$ (unrestricted) | m | 4.5 B with controls |
| 3 | Turning basin diameter | $D = k L$ | m | k = 1.5 (tug), 2.0 (free) |
| 4 | Required depth | $d = d_v + S + U + \Delta$ | m | d_v = vessel draft, S = squat, U = under-keel clearance, Δ = wave allowance |
| 5 | Dredging volume | $V = A \cdot h$ | m³ | A = area, h = depth to dredge |
| 6 | Storage floor area | $A_s = \dfrac{Q \cdot T_d}{h_s \cdot \rho \cdot \eta}$ | m² | Q = throughput, T_d = dwell time, h_s = stack height, ρ = bulk density, η = stacking efficiency |
| 7 | Berthing energy (kinetic) | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^2 \cdot C_m$ | kJ | C_m = mass coefficient (~1.5–2.0) |
| 8 | Barge annual capacity (theor.) | $C = D \cdot N \cdot \alpha \cdot \beta$ | t/yr | D = barge DWT, N = trips/yr, α = load factor, β = utilization |
| 9 | Berth length | $L_b = n \cdot L_v + (n-1) \cdot g$ | m | n = vessels, L_v = vessel LOA, g = gap (1–1.5×B) |
| 10 | Wave fetch-limited wave height | $H_s \approx 0.0176 \cdot V^{0.88} \cdot F^{0.58} \cdot g^{-0.45}$ | m | V = wind speed, F = fetch, g = gravity |

> **Critical Reminder:** When writing the above formulas in your answer sheet, never use the pipe symbol `|` in math expressions. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Inland port facilities are the **strategic backbone** of sustainable logistics because:
- A single tug-barge convoy of **4 barges × 1,500 DWT = 6,000 tonnes** replaces **~240 trucks**, reducing carbon emissions by **~70%**.
- Inland water transport costs only **₹0.5–1.0 per tonne-km** vs. **₹2.0–2.5 per tonne-km** for road (India, 2023 estimates).
- Inland ports enable **short-sea shipping hub-and-spoke models**, decongesting major sea ports by handling feeder cargo.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Storage Floor Area from Annual Throughput

This is the most frequently asked 7-mark derivation in KTU Module 4 for inland ports. We derive the standard storage area equation step by step.

### 3.1.1 Statement of the Problem

> *Given an annual cargo throughput $Q$ (in tonnes/year) that must be stored at the terminal for an average dwell time $T_d$ (in days), find the required stack-floor area $A_s$ (in m²).*

### 3.1.2 Assumptions
- The annual throughput is uniformly distributed across the year (no major seasonality).
- The bulk density of the cargo is $\rho$ (in t/m³).
- The stack height is $h_s$ (in m).
- The stacking efficiency (wastage factor) is $\eta$ (dimensionless, 0 < η < 1).

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute the daily throughput at the terminal.**

The total annual throughput $Q$ arrives uniformly over 365 working days. Hence,

$$
q_{daily} = \frac{Q}{365}
$$

where $q_{daily}$ is the tonnage that must be processed each day (in t/day).

**Step 2 — Compute the inventory (stock-on-hand) at any instant.**

Since each unit of cargo resides at the terminal for $T_d$ days on average, the instantaneous stock equals the daily throughput multiplied by the dwell time:

$$
I = q_{daily} \cdot T_d = \frac{Q \cdot T_d}{365}
$$

where $I$ is the instantaneous inventory in tonnes.

**Step 3 — Convert the inventory from mass to volume.**

Dividing the mass inventory by the bulk density $\rho$ gives the volume of cargo that must occupy the storage floor at any instant:

$$
V_{stock} = \frac{I}{\rho} = \frac{Q \cdot T_d}{365 \cdot \rho}
$$

where $V_{stock}$ is in m³.

**Step 4 — Compute the geometric stacking volume per unit floor area.**

A stack of height $h_s$ over a unit floor area (1 m²) occupies $1 \cdot h_s$ m³ of cargo volume. However, due to voids, slope of repose, aisles, and handling clearances, only a fraction $\eta$ of the geometric volume is actually usable. Hence, the effective cargo volume per m² of floor area is:

$$
v_{eff} = h_s \cdot \eta
$$

**Step 5 — Equate stock volume to effective stacking volume.**

The required floor area is the stock volume divided by the effective cargo volume per m²:

$$
A_s = \frac{V_{stock}}{v_{eff}} = \frac{Q \cdot T_d}{365 \cdot \rho \cdot h_s \cdot \eta}
$$

This is the **standard UNCTAD storage area equation** used universally in port planning.

### 3.1.4 Final Equation (in compact form)

$$
A_s = \frac{Q \cdot T_d}{365 \cdot \rho \cdot h_s \cdot \eta}
$$

### 3.1.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A new inland port is expected to handle **3 lakh tonnes (3,00,000 t) of coal per year**. The coal has a bulk density of **0.85 t/m³**, the stack height is limited to **4 m** by the loading equipment, the average dwell time is **6 days**, and the stacking efficiency is **0.70**. Calculate the required coal stack-yard area.

**Solution:**

$$
A_s = \frac{3{,}00{,}000 \times 6}{365 \times 0.85 \times 4 \times 0.70}
$$

Compute the denominator:

$$
365 \times 0.85 = 310.25
$$

$$
310.25 \times 4 = 1{,}241.00
$$

$$
1{,}241.00 \times 0.70 = 868.70
$$

Compute the numerator:

$$
3{,}00{,}000 \times 6 = 18{,}00{,}000
$$

Therefore:

$$
A_s = \frac{18{,}00{,}000}{868.70} \approx 2{,}072.41 \text{ m}^2
$$

> **Answer:** Approximately **2,075 m²** of stack-yard area is required. With a 20% allowance for access roads, fire lanes, and drainage, the gross yard area becomes **~2,500 m²**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Dredging Volume for Approach Channel

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement
> *An inland port requires an approach channel of length $L_c$, bottom width $W_b$, and side slopes 1V:zH. The existing natural bed level is at $-d_0$ (below chart datum), and the required navigational depth is $d_n$. Find the dredging volume per metre of channel length.*

### 3.2.2 Derivation

The depth to be dredged at the centre line is:

$$
h = d_n - d_0
$$

The channel cross-section is a **trapezoid** with bottom width $W_b$ and side slope $z:1$ (H:V). The top width of the dredged prism at the bed level is:

$$
W_t = W_b + 2 \cdot z \cdot h
$$

The cross-sectional area of dredging is the area of this trapezoid:

$$
A_x = \frac{1}{2} \cdot (W_b + W_t) \cdot h = \frac{1}{2} \cdot (W_b + W_b + 2 z h) \cdot h
$$

$$
A_x = W_b \cdot h + z \cdot h^2
$$

For a channel of length $L_c$, the total dredging volume is:

$$
V = A_x \cdot L_c = \left(W_b \cdot h + z \cdot h^2\right) \cdot L_c
$$

### 3.2.3 Numerical Example

Given $L_c = 2{,}000$ m, $W_b = 30$ m, $z = 4$ (typical for silt), $d_n = 2.5$ m, $d_0 = 1.5$ m. Then $h = 1.0$ m.

$$
A_x = (30 \times 1.0) + (4 \times 1.0^2) = 30 + 4 = 34 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
V = 34 \times 2{,}000 = 68{,}000 \text{ m}^3
$$

> **Answer:** A total of **68,000 m³** of dredged material must be removed to deepen the approach channel.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Planning Parameters for Common Cargoes

| Cargo | ρ (t/m³) | Typical h_s (m) | Dwell Time (days) | Stacking Eff. η | Storage Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Coal (bulk) | 0.80–0.90 | 4–6 | 5–8 | 0.65–0.75 | Open stackyard |
| Iron ore | 1.80–2.20 | 5–7 | 4–6 | 0.70–0.80 | Open stackyard |
| Food grains | 0.70–0.80 | 3–5 | 10–20 | 0.65–0.75 | Covered godown |
| Containerized | 0.18–0.25 (per TEU) | 2–3 (high) | 3–5 | 0.75–0.85 | Container yard |
| Liquid (POL) | 0.86–0.92 | (tank height) | 5–10 | 0.85–0.95 | Tank farm |
| Cement (bagged) | 1.30–1.50 | 4–5 | 7–10 | 0.70–0.80 | Covered godown |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Inland Port Facility

The following **Mermaid block diagram** maps the complete functional architecture of an inland port, showing the interactions between all major sub-systems. ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Cargo Flow Through the Inland Port

```mermaid

```

## 4.3 Layout Schematic Description (for Answer-Sheet Sketches)

Although we cannot draw a scaled plan in text, a **standard KTU exam answer** for this topic would include a hand-drawn layout showing:

1. The **river/canal** along one edge of the plan.
2. **Floating jetties** projecting perpendicular into the water.
3. A **linear quay/wharf** running parallel to the bank.
4. **Storage areas** (godowns and open yards) immediately behind the berthing line.
5. A **road** running parallel to the storage strip, then a **rail siding** further inland.
6. The **turning basin** upstream of the berths, with a clear approach channel.
7. **Administrative block and customs office** near the entrance gate.
8. **Mooring buoys** for waiting barges downstream of the berths.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** When drawing the layout, always mark the **prevailing wind direction**, **current direction**, and **approach channel orientation**. These three arrows immediately demonstrate professional competence and earn full marks for layout questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define an inland port. List any four facilities required at an inland port.
**Model Answer:**
> An inland port is a terminal facility constructed on a navigable river, canal, lake, or backwater where cargo is transferred between waterborne vessels and land transport modes. It functions as a feeder node to a major sea port.
>
> **Four essential facilities:**
> 1. Berthing structure (floating jetty or wharf)
> 2. Cargo handling equipment (cranes, conveyors)
> 3. Storage facility (godowns, open stackyards, tank farms)
> 4. Navigation aids (buoys, beacons, range markers)
>
> *[CO4 — Remembering / Understanding — 3 marks]*

### Question 2: What is the difference between a floating jetty and a fixed wharf? When is each preferred?
**Model Answer:**
> A **floating jetty** is a pontoon-type structure that rises and falls with water level, maintaining a constant freeboard relative to the vessel. It is preferred in **rivers with large water-level fluctuation (≥ 2 m)**, such as the Ganga or Brahmaputra.
>
> A **fixed wharf** is a rigid structure (sheet pile, piled, or gravity) whose deck elevation is fixed. It is suitable in **canals, lakes, or stable tidal rivers** with negligible level fluctuation, since it is structurally simpler and cheaper.
>
> *[CO4 — Understanding — 3 marks]*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):**
> 1. **Never** compute storage area without stating all four input parameters (Q, T_d, ρ, h_s) and the assumption of uniform distribution.
> 2. **Never** forget to convert annual throughput to daily throughput by dividing by **365 days** (not 300 or 250 working days, unless explicitly stated).
> 3. **Always** include a free-body sketch or layout sketch for layout questions — students who skip the diagram lose 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Always** specify the **units** in every numerical answer. The KTU Board penalises missing units.

---

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

#### **Q.A (a)** — 7 Marks
**Explain the various site selection criteria for the planning of an inland port. Discuss the role of hydrological and topographical factors in detail.**

**Model Answer:**

The site selection of an inland port is governed by a multi-criteria decision framework. The principal factors are:

1. **Hydrological Factors**
   - **Adequate depth:** The natural depth of the river must be close to the required navigational depth, with the difference being dredgeable at a reasonable cost. For a Class IV waterway, this depth should be at least **2.0 m**.
   - **Stable river reach:** The selected reach should have minimal bank erosion, no sharp meanders, and minimal siltation. A straight reach of length ≥ 5 × L_vessel (LOA of design vessel) is preferred.
   - **Tidal/flood range:** The annual water-level fluctuation determines the berthing structure type. If fluctuation > 2 m, floating jetties are preferred.
   - **Current velocity:** Should be ≤ 1.5 m/s for safe berthing. Velocities above 2 m/s require special fendering.

2. **Topographical Factors**
   - **Available land area:** Sufficient flat land behind the berthing line for storage, road, and rail.
   - **Foundation conditions:** Sound alluvial or rocky strata for berthing structure piles. Soft clay or organic soil must be avoided or pre-treated.
   - **Elevation above HFL:** The terminal floor must be at least **0.5–1.0 m above the Highest Flood Level** to avoid flooding.
   - **Approach gradient:** Roads and rail sidings should have gradients ≤ 2% for safe heavy-vehicle movement.

3. **Connectivity Factors**
   - Proximity to **National Highway** (within 5 km) and **railway line** (within 10 km).
   - Availability of **labour and water** in the surrounding area.

4. **Environmental and Social Factors**
   - Avoidance of **ecologically sensitive zones** (mangroves, turtle nesting beaches).
   - **Displacement of settlements** should be minimal.

> *Conclusion:* A site satisfying the above constraints minimizes construction and operational cost, ensures safe navigation, and enables long-term expansion.

*[CO4 — Applying / Analysing — 7 marks]*

#### **Q.A (b)** — 7 Marks
**An inland port is designed to handle 5 lakh tonnes of bagged cement per year. The cement has a bulk density of 1.4 t/m³, the stack height is 4 m, the average dwell time is 12 days, and the stacking efficiency is 0.75. Calculate the required storage floor area. If the dwell time is reduced to 6 days, what is the percentage reduction in storage area?**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Identify given data**
- Annual throughput, $Q = 5{,}00{,}000$ t/yr
- Bulk density, $\rho = 1.4$ t/m³
- Stack height, $h_s = 4$ m
- Dwell time (Case 1), $T_{d1} = 12$ days
- Dwell time (Case 2), $T_{d2} = 6$ days
- Stacking efficiency, $\eta = 0.75$

**Step 2 — Apply the storage area formula**

$$
A_s = \frac{Q \cdot T_d}{365 \cdot \rho \cdot h_s \cdot \eta}
$$

**Case 1 — Original dwell time of 12 days**

$$
A_{s1} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000 \times 12}{365 \times 1.4 \times 4 \times 0.75}
$$

Compute the denominator:

$$
365 \times 1.4 = 511.00
$$

$$
511.00 \times 4 = 2{,}044.00
$$

$$
2{,}044.00 \times 0.75 = 1{,}533.00
$$

Compute the numerator:

$$
5{,}00{,}000 \times 12 = 60{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
A_{s1} = \frac{60{,}00{,}000}{1{,}533.00} \approx 3{,}913.89 \text{ m}^2
$$

> **Original storage area ≈ 3,914 m²**

**Case 2 — Reduced dwell time of 6 days**

$$
A_{s2} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000 \times 6}{365 \times 1.4 \times 4 \times 0.75}
$$

$$
A_{s2} = \frac{30{,}00{,}000}{1{,}533.00} \approx 1{,}956.95 \text{ m}^2
$$

> **Reduced storage area ≈ 1,957 m²**

**Step 3 — Percentage reduction in storage area**

$$
\text{Reduction \%} = \frac{A_{s1} - A_{s2}}{A_{s1}} \times 100
$$

$$
= \frac{3{,}913.89 - 1{,}956.95}{3{,}913.89} \times 100 = \frac{1{,}956.94}{3{,}913.89} \times 100 \approx 50.00\%
$$

> **The storage area is reduced by exactly 50%, since storage area is linearly proportional to dwell time.**

> **Engineering Insight:** Halving the dwell time halves the storage area, but achieving this requires efficient customs, documentation, and inland transport. This is why **Customs EDI systems** and **24×7 operations** are central to modern inland port policy.

*[CO4, CO5 — Applying / Evaluating — 7 marks]*

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

#### **Q.B (a)** — 7 Marks
**Describe the various types of berthing structures used in inland ports. Under what conditions is a floating jetty preferred over a fixed wharf?**

**Model Answer:**

Inland port berthing structures are broadly classified into **two categories** based on their interaction with water level:

**1. Fixed Berthing Structures**

A fixed structure is rigidly connected to the foundation and does not move with the water level. Common types include:

- **Gravity wharf:** A massive masonry or concrete block resting on a prepared bed. Used in stable canals and lakes with good founding strata.
- **Sheet-pile wharf:** Interlocked steel sheet piles driven into the riverbed and anchored by tie-backs. Used in soft alluvial deposits.
- **Piled wharf:** Vertical and raking piles supporting a reinforced concrete deck. Used where bearing capacity of the bed is low.

> **Advantages:** Lower construction cost, longer design life (40–50 years), can support heavy cranes.
> **Disadvantages:** Large freeboard during low water, dangerous to access at high water, restricts berthing at extreme water levels.

**2. Floating Berthing Structures (Pontoon Jetties)**

A floating structure (pontoon) is moored to the bed by chains or guide piles and rises and falls with the water. The vessel-to-deck freeboard remains constant.

- **Steel pontoon jetty:** Welded steel pontoon with timber/concrete deck, guided by piles.
- **Concrete pontoon jetty:** Precast reinforced concrete cellular or box pontoon.
- **Bridging ramp:** Articulated ramp linking the pontoon to the shore, accommodating vertical movement.

> **Advantages:** Constant freeboard, suitable for all water levels, easy to relocate, lower dredging cost.
> **Disadvantages:** Higher maintenance (corrosion), lower load capacity, sensitive to wave action.

**3. Selection Criterion — When Floating Jetty is Preferred**

A floating jetty is preferred when:
- **Annual water level fluctuation exceeds 2 m** (typical of major Indian rivers like the Ganga, where fluctuation can be 8–10 m).
- **Tidal range is large** (> 1.5 m).
- **Subsoil is unsuitable** for heavy piled foundations.
- **Construction must be rapid** and the facility may need to be relocated.

> **Example:** The **Varanasi Multi-Modal Terminal (NW-1)** uses a floating jetty precisely because the Ganga at Varanasi has a fluctuation of 8–10 m between monsoon and lean season.

*[CO4 — Understanding / Applying — 7 marks]*

#### **Q.B (b)** — 7 Marks
**A Class IV inland waterway has a design vessel with a length of 80 m and a beam of 12 m. Calculate (i) the one-way channel width, (ii) the two-way unrestricted channel width, (iii) the diameter of the turning basin for tug-assisted turning, and (iv) the minimum berth length required for two such vessels berthed side-by-side with a 1.5B gap.**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Vessel length, $L_v = 80$ m
- Vessel beam, $B = 12$ m
- Number of vessels, $n = 2$
- Gap between vessels, $g = 1.5 B = 1.5 \times 12 = 18$ m

**Part (i) — One-way channel width**

$$
W_1 = 3B = 3 \times 12 = 36 \text{ m}
$$

> **One-way channel width = 36 m**

**Part (ii) — Two-way unrestricted channel width**

$$
W_2 = 6B = 6 \times 12 = 72 \text{ m}
$$

> **Two-way channel width = 72 m**

**Part (iii) — Turning basin diameter (tug-assisted)**

For tug-assisted turning, $k = 1.5$:

$$
D = 1.5 \times L_v = 1.5 \times 80 = 120 \text{ m}
$$

> **Turning basin diameter = 120 m**

**Part (iv) — Berth length for two vessels**

The general formula for the total berthing length of $n$ vessels with a gap $g$ between them is:

$$
L_b = n \cdot L_v + (n - 1) \cdot g
$$

$$
L_b = 2 \times 80 + (2 - 1) \times 18
$$

$$
L_b = 160 + 18 = 178 \text{ m}
$$

> **Required berth length = 178 m**

> **Engineering Insight:** A 178 m berth can handle two 80 m barges simultaneously. If traffic grows, the berth can be extended linearly along the bank — a major advantage of wharf-type layouts over dolphin clusters.

*[CO4, CO5 — Applying / Analysing — 7 marks]*

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Use this section as your last-night revision summary.**

- **Definition:** An inland port is a water–land modal interchange node on a navigable river/canal/lake, not a sea port.
- **Classification:** Waterways are classified Class I–VII by IWAI; Class I is the shallowest (1.2 m), Class VII is the deepest (> 3 m).
- **Three Planning Levels:** Strategic (demand) → Tactical (site) → Operational (facility).
- **Site selection drivers:** Depth, stability, current, flood level, soil, road/rail access, environmental impact.
- **Berthing structures:** Floating jetty (large water-level fluctuation) vs. Fixed wharf (stable level).
- **Key formulas to memorise:**
  - $W_1 = 3B$, $W_2 = 6B$
  - $D = (1.5 \text{ to } 2)L$
  - $A_s = \dfrac{Q \cdot T_d}{365 \cdot \rho \cdot h_s \cdot \eta}$
  - $V_{dredge} = (W_b h + z h^2) \cdot L_c$
  - $L_b = n L_v + (n-1) g$
- **Storage area is linearly proportional to dwell time** — the single most important operational lever.
- **Dredging volume** = trapezoidal cross-section × length.
- **Barge convoy economics:** 4-barge convoy replaces ~240 trucks; reduces cost by ~50% and emissions by ~70%.
- **Mandatory sketch elements** in answer sheets: river edge, jetty/wharf, storage, road, rail, turning basin, current direction.
- **Common pitfall:** Forgetting to divide annual throughput by 365 in the storage area formula.
- **Indian flagship examples:** NW-1 (Ganga), NW-2 (Brahmaputra), NW-3 (West Coast Canal), NW-4 (Krishna-Godavari), NW-5 (Brahmani-Mahanadi).
- **Policy context:** India's *Sagarmala Programme* and *Jal Marg Vikas Project (JMVP)* are driving inland port development along NW-1.
- **Bloom's levels tested in KTU:** Part A = Remembering/Understanding; Part B = Applying/Analysing/Evaluating.
